<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define hand tools. List any four categories of hand tools with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Hand tools are manually operated implements that require only the muscular effort of the worker — without any external power source (electrical, pneumatic, hydraulic, or fuel-based) — to perform material manipulation tasks such as cutting, striking, holding, or turning.

**Four Categories with Examples (2 Marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Striking Tools:** e.g., Claw Hammer — used for driving nails or striking chisels.
2. **Cutting Tools:** e.g., Cold Chisel — used for cutting metal by impact.
3. **Holding/Gripping Tools:** e.g., Slip-Joint Pliers — used for gripping round or flat objects.
4. **Turning/Rotating Tools:** e.g., Socket Wrench — used for tightening or loosening nuts and bolts.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: State the three main causes of hand-tool accidents in industries and briefly explain any one.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Three Causes (1½ Marks — ½ mark each):**
1. Improper selection of tool for the task.
2. Use of defective or poorly maintained tools.
3. Failure to use appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE).

**Explanation of Any One (1½ Marks):**

**Cause 1 — Improper Tool Selection:**
Using a chisel as a screwdriver, or a wrench as a hammer, leads to tool slippage, breakage, and flying fragments. The tool is not designed for the applied load type, causing failure at the stress-concentration points. OSHA regulations (29 CFR 1910.132) require that the **right tool be used for the right job**.

---

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Classify hand tools used in material handling and explain the safety precautions to be observed while using striking and cutting tools.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a claw hammer. Discuss the common defects that render a hammer unsafe for use.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Classification of Hand Tools (3 Marks):**

Hand tools may be classified based on the type of mechanical action they perform:

| **Category** | **Function** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| Striking Tools | Apply impact force | Hammer, Mallet |
| Cutting Tools | Sever material | Chisel, Hacksaw |
| Holding Tools | Grip workpieces | Pliers, Vise |
| Turning Tools | Apply torque | Wrench, Screwdriver |
| Prying Tools | Provide leverage | Crowbar, Pry Bar |
| Measuring Tools | Measure dimensions | Tape, Caliper |

**Safety Precautions for Striking Tools (2 Marks):**

1. Ensure the hammer head is **securely wedged** to the handle (no loose head).
2. Inspect the striking face — **no mushrooming or cracks** are permitted.
3. Wear **ANSI Z87.1 safety glasses** to protect against flying chips or nail fragments.
4. Use the **correct hammer weight** for the job — never use a sledgehammer for finish work.
5. Replace **splintered or cracked handles** immediately.

**Safety Precautions for Cutting Tools (2 Marks):**

1. Keep **cutting edges sharp** — a dull edge requires excessive force and slips.
2. Ensure **chisels have proper handle fitting** to prevent hand impact injuries.
3. Direct cutting tools **away from the body** during use.
4. **Never use a chisel with a mushroomed head** — grind it back to shape before use.
5. Always use a **face shield** for heavy cutting operations (e.g., chisel work on metal).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Construction of a Claw Hammer (3 Marks):**

A claw hammer consists of:
1. **Head:** Made of forged high-carbon steel, hardened to **HRC 50–55**. Contains two ends:
   - **Striking Face (Peen):** Flat circular surface for driving nails.
   - **Claw:** Curved, bifurcated end with a V-slot for pulling nails.
2. **Eye Hole:** Tapered hole through the head where the handle is fitted.
3. **Handle:** Typically made of hickory wood, fibreglass, or steel. Provides grip and lever action.
4. **Grip:** Often wrapped in rubber or leather for ergonomic comfort.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**

When swung, the hammer head gains kinetic energy. This energy is transferred to the nail head upon impact, driving the nail into the wood. The claw end acts as a **Class 1 lever** — the head acts as the load, the handle as the effort arm, and the contact point with the wood as the fulcrum, allowing nails to be pried out.

**Common Defects Rendering Hammer Unsafe (2 Marks):**

1. **Loose Head:** Caused by handle shrinkage or improper wedging.
2. **Mushroomed Striking Face:** Metal deformation at the edge — fragments can fly off.
3. **Cracked or Splintered Handle:** Wooden handles can develop splinters or cracks.
4. **Bent or Broken Claw:** From prying excessively large nails.
5. **Worn Grip Surface:** Reduces hand control and increases slip risk.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a) Explain the ergonomic considerations in the design of hand tool handles. Discuss why the handle diameter of 32–38 mm is recommended.** *(7 Marks)*

**(b) Discuss the safety guidelines issued by OSHA (Occupational Safety and Health Administration) for the use of hand tools in industries.** *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Ergonomic Considerations in Handle Design (5 Marks):**

Ergonomics in hand tool design aims to **maximize efficiency, comfort, and safety** while minimizing worker fatigue, musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs), and accidents. Key considerations include:

1. **Handle Diameter:** Should match the average grip span of the operator's hand. Too thin → excessive grip force required; too thick → restricted blood flow and nerve compression.
2. **Handle Length:** Adequate to allow full hand grip without contacting the head.
3. **Handle Shape:** Contoured (anatomically shaped) handles distribute pressure across the palm, reducing localized stress.
4. **Surface Texture:** Non-slip but not abrasive — promotes secure grip even with oily or sweaty hands.
5. **Material:** Should provide thermal insulation (prevent cold-burn in winter), electrical insulation (for electricians), and vibration damping.
6. **Weight Balance:** Centre of gravity should be near the grip to minimize wrist torque.

**Why 32–38 mm is Recommended (2 Marks):**

Research in occupational biomechanics has established that the **optimal handle diameter for a power grip** (used for striking and turning tools) ranges between **32 mm and 38 mm** for adult male workers. This range corresponds to the **average internal hand breadth** when the fingers are flexed in a power grip posture. Handles within this diameter:
- Allow the fingers to wrap fully around the handle.
- Maximize grip strength (force output).
- Minimize fatigue during prolonged use.
- Fall within the **ANSI/ASSP A1264.2** ergonomic standard for portable hand tools.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**OSHA Safety Guidelines for Hand Tools (7 Marks):**

The **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** under **29 CFR 1910, Subpart P** provides the following mandatory guidelines:

1. **Right Tool for the Right Job (1910.132):** Employers shall ensure that employees use the correct tool for each operation. Improvised tools are prohibited.

2. **Inspection Before Use:** All hand tools must be inspected before each use. Defective tools must be removed from service immediately (tagged "DO NOT USE").

3. **Tool Condition:** Tools must be maintained in good working condition. Worn, cracked, or broken tools must be replaced.

4. **Proper Handling:** Sharp tools must be carried in sheaths or tool boxes with points downward. Tools must never be thrown from one location to another.

5. **PPE Requirements:** Appropriate PPE must be worn — safety glasses, gloves, hard hats, or face shields depending on the tool and task.

6. **Guarding:** Where applicable, tool guards (e.g., on abrasive wheel hand tools) must be in place and functional.

7. **Training:** Employees must be trained in the proper use, inspection, and maintenance of each hand tool they operate.

8. **Storage:** Tools must be stored in dry, organized locations — not left on floors, walkways, or elevated surfaces where they may fall.

9. **Housekeeping:** Work areas must be kept clean and free of debris that could cause slips or falls while carrying tools.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **lose 2–3 marks** in Part B questions by failing to cite the specific OSHA regulation number (e.g., **29 CFR 1910.132**). Always quote the regulatory citation wherever possible. Additionally, students often confuse OSHA standards with IS (Indian Standard) codes — the KTU syllabus specifically references OSHA, so use it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hand Tools (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Hand tools = manual muscular operation only; no external power.
- **Six Categories:** Striking, Cutting, Holding, Turning, Prying, Measuring.
- **Mechanical Principles:** Levers (MA = Le/Ll), Impact (KE = ½mv²), Shear cutting.
- **Ergonomic Handle Diameter:** **32–38 mm** (KTU favourite value).
- **Max Sustained Grip Force:** **220 N**.
- **Hammer Face Hardness:** **HRC 50–55**.
- **Top 3 Accident Causes:** Wrong tool, defective tool, missing PPE.
- **Bare File Rule:** Always fit handle before use (top puncture injury cause).
- **Chisel Mushrooming:** Must be ground off — mushroomed head causes chip ejection.
- **OSHA Citation:** **29 CFR 1910.132** for hand and PPE protection.
- **Safety Glass Standard:** **ANSI Z87.1** for impact resistance.
- **Loose Hammer Head:** Causes "fly-off" — check wedge security before use.
- **Crowbar Classification:** **Class 1 Lever** (fulcrum between effort and load).
- **PPE Matrix:** Striking/Cutting → Face shield + Z87.1 glasses; Holding → Cut-resistant gloves; Prying → Hard hat + boots.
- **Coefficient of Restitution:** Steel-on-steel ≈ **0.85** — high fragmentation risk.
- **Rule of Thumb:** Safety zone radius (m) ≈ **0.5 × √KE (in Joules)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Jacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jack (Engineering Definition):** A *jack* is a mechanical or hydraulic lifting device used to apply a large force, lift heavy loads, or support structures vertically over a short working stroke. In the context of **material handling safety**, a jack is classified as a *portable load-lifting appliance* governed by the **Factories Act 1948 (India)** and the **Indian Standard IS 4682 / IS 6166** for safe lifting operations, particularly for automobile servicing, structural maintenance, and industrial load handling.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, jacks are categorized as **manually operated (mechanical)** or **power-operated (hydraulic/pneumatic)** lifting devices used where a controlled, low-speed, high-force vertical displacement of loads is required.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Understanding):**
> Imagine you are trying to lift a parked **car (~1500 kg)** with just your bare hands — impossible, right? Now imagine inserting a small metal scissor-frame (a *mechanical jack*) and turning a small handle. The handle moves a long distance with little effort, but the car rises slowly with great force. This is the **trade-off between distance and force** — the same principle a child uses on a seesaw: a small weight on a long arm balances a heavy weight on a short arm. A *hydraulic jack* is even more magical — it uses an incompressible fluid (oil) to transmit pressure, so a small push on a thin piston produces a huge push on a thick piston. The fluid acts like a rigid, invisible "rod" of water that obeys Pascal's principle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Values (must be remembered for KTU exams):**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
> - **Density of standard hydraulic oil:** $\rho \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (mineral-based)
> - **Standard coefficient of friction** for steel-on-steel screw threads: $\mu \approx 0.10$ to $0.18$
> - **Standard safe working load (SWL) ranges:** Domestic car jacks $\to$ **2 to 5 tonnes**; Industrial hydraulic jacks $\to$ **10 to 100 tonnes**; Heavy-duty bottle jacks $\to$ **up to 200 tonnes**.
> - **Helix angle (α)** for self-locking screw jacks: typically $\alpha < 6°$ to prevent back-driving.

### 1.4 Classification of Jacks (Syllabus-Relevant Types)

| # | Type | Driving Force | Typical Capacity | KTU Weightage |
|---|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Screw Jack** | Manual (lever) | 5 – 50 tonnes | Very High |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Jack** | Oil pressure (Pascal's Law) | 2 – 200 tonnes | Very High |
| 3 | **Pneumatic Jack** | Compressed air | 1 – 25 tonnes | Moderate |
| 4 | **Bottle Jack** | Hydraulic (compact) | 2 – 50 tonnes | High |
| 5 | **Floor Jack (Trolley)** | Hydraulic + wheels | 2 – 10 tonnes | High |
| 6 | **Scissor Jack** | Mechanical (linkage) | 1 – 3 tonnes | High |
| 7 | **Toe Jack / Low-Profile Jack** | Hydraulic | 5 – 80 tonnes | Moderate |
| 8 | **Air-Hydraulic Jack** | Pneumo-hydraulic | 10 – 100 tonnes | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **Screw Jacks** and **Hydraulic Jacks** as the two primary topics, with derivations of *Mechanical Advantage*, *Velocity Ratio*, and *Efficiency* being the most frequently asked derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of a Screw Jack

A screw jack converts a **small rotational effort** applied at the end of a long lever into a **large vertical lifting force** on a load. The conversion is achieved through the inclined-plane principle wrapped around a cylinder (the screw thread).

**Step-by-step logic of operation:**
1. The operator applies a horizontal effort $P$ at the end of a lever arm of length $L$.
2. The lever rotates the screw about its axis through one full revolution ($2\pi$ radians).
3. In one revolution, the screw (and hence the load cup) rises by an axial distance equal to the **pitch $p$** of the thread.
4. The friction between the threads absorbs a portion of the input effort, reducing efficiency.
5. For **self-locking** (load does not back-drive), the helix angle $\alpha$ must be less than the friction angle $\phi$ (i.e., $\alpha < \phi$).

**The "Why" behind each step:**
- A long lever arm $L$ **multiplies torque** ($T = P \cdot L$) → so a small force produces a large turning moment.
- A small pitch $p$ **trades distance for force** → for each revolution, the screw advances very little, but with massive force.
- Friction is *desirable* for self-locking but *undesirable* for efficiency.

### 2.2 Operational Concept of a Hydraulic Jack

A hydraulic jack operates on **Pascal's Law (1653)**: pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions.

**Step-by-step logic of operation:**
1. Operator pumps a small **hand lever**, displacing oil from a small-diameter **pump piston (area $a$)**.
2. This generates pressure $P = \dfrac{F}{a}$ in the hydraulic oil.
3. The pressurized oil flows through a one-way check valve into a **larger cylinder** with a **ram piston (area $A$)**.
4. By Pascal's law, the same pressure acts on the ram, producing an output force $W = P \cdot A = \dfrac{F \cdot A}{a}$.
5. The **force multiplication ratio** is $\dfrac{A}{a}$, which can be 10× to 100× in industrial jacks.
6. A **release valve** opens to allow oil to flow back to the reservoir, lowering the load under controlled gravity descent.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-critical for KTU 2024 Scheme. Memorize the symbols, the units, and the boundary conditions.**

#### A. SCREW JACK FORMULAS

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Helix angle** | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi d_m}$ | radians | $d_m$ = mean thread diameter |
| 2 | **Friction angle** | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | radians | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction |
| 3 | **Tangential force on thread** (raising load) | $P_t = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | N | $W$ = load lifted |
| 4 | **Effort at lever end** (raising) | $P = \dfrac{W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot d_m/2}{L}$ | N | $L$ = lever length |
| 5 | **Effort (lowering load)** | $P = \dfrac{W \tan(\phi - \alpha) \cdot d_m/2}{L}$ | N | Only if $\alpha < \phi$ |
| 6 | **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** | $MA = \dfrac{W}{P} = \dfrac{2L}{d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 7 | **Velocity Ratio (VR)** | $VR = \dfrac{2\pi L}{p}$ | dimensionless | $p$ = pitch |
| 8 | **Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | % (×100) | $0 < \eta < 0.5$ typically |
| 9 | **Condition for self-locking** | $\alpha \leq \phi$ | — | Essential for screw jacks |
| 10 | **Maximum efficiency helix angle** | $\alpha_{max} = 45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | degrees | Optimum design point |
| 11 | **Maximum efficiency** | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi}$ | % | Theoretical upper bound |
| 12 | **Torque required (raising)** | $T_{raise} = \dfrac{W \cdot d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | N·m | — |
| 13 | **Torque required (lowering)** | $T_{lower} = \dfrac{W \cdot d_m}{2} \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | N·m | Positive only if $\alpha < \phi$ |
| 14 | **Friction torque (collar bearing)** | $T_{collar} = \mu_c \cdot W \cdot R_m$ | N·m | $R_m$ = mean collar radius |

#### B. HYDRAULIC JACK FORMULAS

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Pascal's Law** | $P_1 = P_2$ | Pa | Static fluid |
| 2 | **Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa or N/m² | — |
| 3 | **Force multiplication** | $W = P \cdot A = F \cdot \dfrac{A}{a}$ | N | $A > a$ (ram vs pump) |
| 4 | **Mechanical Advantage** | $MA = \dfrac{W}{F} = \dfrac{A}{a} = \dfrac{D^2}{d^2}$ | dimensionless | $D$ = ram dia., $d$ = pump dia. |
| 5 | **Volume conservation** | $A \cdot h = a \cdot s$ | m³ | Incompressible fluid |
| 6 | **Stroke velocity ratio** | $\dfrac{v_{load}}{v_{pump}} = \dfrac{a}{A}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of force ratio |
| 7 | **Pump lever effort** | $F = \dfrac{W \cdot a}{A \cdot \eta_{pump}}$ | N | $\eta_{pump}$ = pump efficiency |
| 8 | **Overall efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{W \cdot H}{F \cdot S}$ | % | $H$ = lift height, $S$ = pump stroke |

#### C. SAFETY / STANDARDS — IMPORTANT FOR OESFT833

| # | Standard | Coverage |
|---|----------|----------|
| 1 | **IS 4682** | Specification for hydraulic jacks |
| 2 | **IS 6166** | Specification for screw jacks |
| 3 | **Factories Act 1948, Sec. 28 & 29** | Lifting machinery, safety of lifts |
| 4 | **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.244** | Vehicle jack safety (US reference) |
| 5 | **Safe Working Load (SWL)** | Maximum load; must be marked on the jack |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in industry:**
> - **Automotive workshops** use trolley jacks (hydraulic) for vehicle service — load capacities of 2 to 5 tonnes.
> - **Civil construction** uses hydraulic jacks for **post-tensioning** of bridges and **lifting heavy slabs**.
> - **Shipyards** use bottle jacks and air-hydraulic jacks for hull-section alignment.
> - **Disaster response** uses hydraulic jacks to lift collapsed structures.
> - **Foundries and heavy machinery erection** use screw jacks for **precise vertical alignment** (e.g., aligning a 500-tonne turbine shaft).
> - Understanding **efficiency and self-locking** prevents the catastrophic back-driving failure of screw jacks under load.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Velocity Ratio (VR) of a Screw Jack

> **Given:** A screw jack has a lever arm of length $L$ and a screw thread of pitch $p$.
> **Find:** Velocity Ratio $VR$.

**Step 1 — Distance moved by effort in one full revolution:**
The effort $P$ is applied at the end of the lever of length $L$. When the lever completes **one full revolution**, the effort moves along a circular path of circumference:

$$d_{effort} = 2\pi L$$

**Step 2 — Distance moved by the load in one full revolution:**
In one complete revolution of the screw, the screw advances axially by an amount equal to the **pitch $p$** of the thread (for a single-start thread; for an $n$-start thread, the lead = $n \cdot p$).

$$d_{load} = p$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of Velocity Ratio:**

$$\boxed{VR = \dfrac{\text{Distance moved by effort}}{\text{Distance moved by load}} = \dfrac{2\pi L}{p}}$$

> **Engineering insight:** For a screw jack with $L = 400$ mm and $p = 10$ mm, $VR = \dfrac{2\pi \times 400}{10} = 251.3$. This is a *very high* mechanical advantage — that is why a small effort can lift a car!

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Mechanical Advantage (MA) of a Screw Jack — Raising the Load

> **Given:** A square-threaded screw of mean diameter $d_m$, helix angle $\alpha$, friction angle $\phi$, supporting a load $W$. Effort $P$ applied at lever end of length $L$.
> **Find:** $MA = W/P$.

**Step 1 — Unwrap the thread onto an inclined plane:**
Imagine cutting the screw thread along a vertical line and unrolling it flat. The thread becomes a **rectangular block of weight $W$** resting on an **inclined plane** of slope angle $\alpha$. The pitch $p$ becomes the vertical height; the circumference $\pi d_m$ becomes the horizontal base.

**Step 2 — Resolve forces on the inclined plane (raising):**
When the load is being raised, friction acts **down the incline** (opposing motion up the plane). The forces on the block are:
- Weight $W$ (vertically downward)
- Normal reaction $R_N$ (perpendicular to the plane)
- Applied tangential force $P_t$ (up the plane, parallel to incline)
- Friction force $\mu R_N$ (down the plane, opposing motion)

**Step 3 — Resolve $W$ into components along and perpendicular to the incline:**

$$\text{Component along plane (down)} = W \sin \alpha$$
$$\text{Component perpendicular to plane} = W \cos \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium equations:**

Perpendicular to plane:
$$R_N = W \cos \alpha$$

Along the plane (raising, friction down):
$$P_t = W \sin \alpha + \mu R_N = W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Introduce friction angle $\phi$ where $\tan \phi = \mu$:**

$$P_t = W \sin \alpha + W \cos \alpha \tan \phi = W \dfrac{\sin \alpha \cos \phi + \cos \alpha \sin \phi}{\cos \phi}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(\alpha + \phi) = \sin \alpha \cos \phi + \cos \alpha \sin \phi$:

$$P_t = W \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos \phi} = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Step 6 — Convert tangential force to effort at lever end:**
The tangential force $P_t$ acts at the mean radius $d_m / 2$ of the screw. The effort $P$ at lever end $L$ must produce the same torque:

$$P \cdot L = P_t \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} = W \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2} \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $P$ and invert to get $MA$:**

$$P = \dfrac{W \cdot d_m \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2 L}$$

$$\boxed{MA = \dfrac{W}{P} = \dfrac{2L}{d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}}$$

---

### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Efficiency of a Screw Jack

> **Given:** Ideal (frictionless) and actual (with friction) mechanical advantage of the screw jack.
> **Find:** Efficiency $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Definition of efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Ideal load (no friction)}}{\text{Actual load (with friction)}} = \dfrac{W_{ideal}}{W_{actual}}$$

Equivalently, since $VR$ is fixed by geometry (independent of friction):

$$\eta = \dfrac{MA}{VR} = \dfrac{W/P}{2\pi L / p}$$

**Step 2 — Express helix angle in terms of pitch and mean diameter:**

From the geometry of the unwrapped thread:

$$\tan \alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi d_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p = \pi d_m \tan \alpha$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $p$ into the VR expression:**

$$VR = \dfrac{2\pi L}{\pi d_m \tan \alpha} = \dfrac{2L}{d_m \tan \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute MA (from derivation 2):**

$$MA = \dfrac{2L}{d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

**Step 5 — Divide to obtain efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{MA}{VR} = \dfrac{2L \Big/ d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2L \Big/ d_m \tan \alpha} = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 6 — Self-locking condition:**
For the screw jack to be **self-locking** (i.e., the load does not unwind the screw when effort is removed), the helix angle must be less than the friction angle:

$$\alpha < \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan \alpha < \tan \phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha < \phi$$

> **KTU board exam trick:** If a numerical problem asks "what is the condition for the load to remain in place after effort is removed?" — the answer is $\alpha \leq \phi$ (or equivalently, $\alpha < \tan^{-1}(\mu)$).

---

### 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Maximum Efficiency of a Screw Jack

> **Given:** Efficiency formula $\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$.
> **Find:** The value of helix angle $\alpha$ for which efficiency is maximum.

**Step 1 — Take the derivative of efficiency with respect to helix angle:**

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = \dfrac{d}{d\alpha} \left[ \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} \right] = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the quotient rule:**

$$\dfrac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = \dfrac{\sec^2 \alpha \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) - \tan \alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha + \phi)}{\tan^2(\alpha + \phi)} = 0$$

For maximum efficiency, the numerator must equal zero:

$$\sec^2 \alpha \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) = \tan \alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using $\sec^2 \theta = 1 + \tan^2 \theta$:**

$$\dfrac{1 + \tan^2 \alpha}{\tan \alpha} = \dfrac{1 + \tan^2(\alpha + \phi)}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the cotangent relationship (or use the standard result):**

The standard textbook result (which KTU expects students to state) is:

$$\alpha_{max} = 45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into efficiency formula:**

Using $\alpha_{max} = 45° - \phi/2$:

$$\alpha_{max} + \phi = 45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}$$

We use the identity $\tan(45° - \phi/2) = \dfrac{1 - \tan(\phi/2)}{1 + \tan(\phi/2)}$ and the half-angle formula $\tan(\phi/2) = \dfrac{\sin \phi}{1 + \cos \phi}$. After algebraic simplification (covered in textbooks):

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi} \times 100\%}$$

> **Numerical example:** If $\mu = 0.15$, then $\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.15) = 8.53°$, and $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{1 - \sin(8.53°)}{1 + \sin(8.53°)} = \dfrac{1 - 0.1483}{1 + 0.1483} = 0.7416 = 74.16\%$. This is the **theoretical upper limit** of efficiency for a given friction coefficient.

---

### 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Mechanical Advantage of a Hydraulic Jack

> **Given:** A hydraulic jack with a pump piston of diameter $d$ and a ram piston of diameter $D$. Effort $F$ applied on the pump piston.
> **Find:** Load $W$ that can be lifted.

**Step 1 — Pressure generated at the pump piston:**

$$P = \dfrac{F}{a} = \dfrac{F}{\pi d^2 / 4} = \dfrac{4F}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2 — By Pascal's Law, the same pressure acts on the ram piston:**

$$P = \dfrac{W}{A} = \dfrac{W}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \dfrac{4W}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two pressure expressions:**

$$\dfrac{4F}{\pi d^2} = \dfrac{4W}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the load $W$:**

$$W = F \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{d^2}$$

$$\boxed{MA = \dfrac{W}{F} = \dfrac{D^2}{d^2} = \dfrac{A}{a}}$$

> **Numerical example:** If $D = 50$ mm and $d = 10$ mm, then $MA = (50/10)^2 = 25$. So a 200 N effort lifts a $W = 200 \times 25 = 5000$ N = 5 kN load. In a real jack, the **hand lever** further multiplies the human effort (a hand lever with mechanical advantage of 5 turns the 200 N hand force into a 1000 N force on the pump piston, giving an *overall* $MA = 5 \times 25 = 125$).

---

### 3.6 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX

| Parameter | Screw Jack | Hydraulic Jack |
|-----------|------------|----------------|
| Driving principle | Inclined plane (thread) | Pascal's law (fluid pressure) |
| Energy storage | None (rigid) | Pneumatic accumulator (optional) |
| Self-locking | Yes (if $\alpha < \phi$) | No (requires check valve) |
| Typical efficiency | 30% – 50% | 70% – 90% |
| Maximum load | Up to 50 tonnes | Up to 200+ tonnes |
| Speed of lifting | Very slow (mm/turn) | Slow to moderate |
| Maintenance | Low (no seals) | High (seals, oil, valves) |
| Safety risk | Mechanical failure | Hydraulic fluid leak, sudden release |
| KTU marks weightage | ~8 marks in 14-mark question | ~6 marks in 14-mark question |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Screw Jack — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LE[Operator Hand Effort P] --> LV[Levers: arm length L]
    LV --> TR[Transmit torque to screw shaft]
    TR --> SC[Screw threads: pitch p, mean dia dm]
    SC --> IP[Inclined plane unwrapping: helix angle alpha]
    IP --> FR[Friction angle phi = arctan mu]
    FR --> RA[Raise load if alpha greater than zero and effort applied]
    SC --> CC[Collar bearing friction mu_c]
    CC --> RD[Reduce or lower load: effort required if alpha less than phi]
    RA --> LD[Load W supported on cup]
    RD --> LD
    LD --> SL{Self locking check: alpha less than phi}
    SL --> YES[Load held without effort]
    SL --> NO[Load back drives: jack fails]
```

### 4.2 Hydraulic Jack — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OP[Operator hand pump lever] --> FP[Force F on small piston area a]
    FP --> CHK1[One way check valve 1 opens]
    CHK1 --> RES[Hydraulic oil reservoir supply]
    FP --> P[Pressure P equals F by a]
    P --> OIL[Pressurized oil in cylinder]
    OIL --> CHK2[One way check valve 2 closes ram path]
    OIL --> RP[Acts on large ram piston area A]
    RP --> RL[Raise load W equals P times A equals F times A by a]
    RL --> AC[Optional accumulator stores pressure]
    RL --> SWL[Marked SWL: never exceed]
    OIL --> RV[Release valve for controlled descent]
    RV --> RES
```

### 4.3 Jack Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    JK[Material Handling Jacks]
    JK --> MJ[Mechanical Jacks]
    JK --> HJ[Hydraulic Jacks]
    JK --> PJ[Pneumatic Jacks]
    MJ --> SJ[Screw Jack]
    MJ --> SCJ[Scissor Jack]
    MJ --> RJ[Rack and Pinion Jack]
    HJ --> BJ[Bottle Jack]
    HJ --> FJ[Floor or Trolley Jack]
    HJ --> TJ[Toe or Low Profile Jack]
    HJ --> AHJ[Air Hydraulic Jack]
    PJ --> ABJ[Air Bag Jack]
    PJ --> ACJ[Air Cylinder Jack]
    SJ --> SWL1[SWL 5 to 50 tonnes]
    BJ --> SWL2[SWL 2 to 50 tonnes]
    FJ --> SWL3[SWL 2 to 10 tonnes]
```

### 4.4 Safety Decision Logic for Jack Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{Load weight W?}
    Q1 --> L1[Less than 3 tonnes]
    Q1 --> L2[3 to 20 tonnes]
    Q1 --> L3[More than 20 tonnes]
    L1 --> S1{Static or moving load?}
    S1 --> ST1[Static: Scissor or screw jack]
    S1 --> MV1[Moving: Floor trolley jack]
    L2 --> S2{Working clearance?}
    S2 --> HI2[High clearance: Bottle jack]
    S2 --> LO2[Low clearance: Toe jack]
    L3 --> S3{Critical alignment?}
    S3 --> Y3[Use precision screw jack]
    S3 --> N3[Use high tonnage hydraulic jack]
    HI2 --> SF[Safety check: SWL, base plate, slope, safety stands]
    LO2 --> SF
    Y3 --> SF
    N3 --> SF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define a screw jack. State any two applications of a screw jack in material handling.**

**Model Answer:**
A screw jack is a mechanical device used to lift heavy loads by applying a small effort at the end of a lever that rotates a screw thread. It works on the principle of the inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder, converting rotational motion into linear lifting motion.

**Two applications:**
1. **Automobile lifting** in service stations for changing tyres and undercarriage work (capacity 2–5 tonnes).
2. **Leveling and supporting heavy machinery** (e.g., turbines, generators, bridge girders) during installation and alignment.

---

#### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**State Pascal's Law. How is it used in a hydraulic jack?**

**Model Answer:**
**Pascal's Law (1653):** The pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid.

**Application in a hydraulic jack:**
- A small force $F$ is applied on a small piston of area $a$, generating pressure $P = F/a$.
- This pressure is transmitted through the hydraulic oil to a larger piston of area $A$.
- The output lifting force on the larger piston is $W = P \cdot A = F \cdot (A/a)$.
- The force is multiplied by the ratio $(A/a)$, which is the **mechanical advantage** of the hydraulic jack.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by forgetting to convert helix angle from degrees to radians (or vice versa) in numerical problems. Always double-check units.
> - Students **often confuse the "raising" and "lowering" formulas** — remember: friction *aids* lowering (subtracts from $\alpha$ in the tangent), so effort required to *lower* is much less than effort required to *raise*.
> - In hydraulic jack derivations, students frequently **forget to square the diameter ratio** (since area scales as $D^2$).
> - Always state the **self-locking condition** $\alpha < \phi$ explicitly — it is a compulsory 1-mark addition in 14-mark screw jack questions.
> - Always state the **assumptions** at the start of derivations (square thread, uniform load, no collar friction unless specified) — KTU examiners award 1 mark for this.

---

#### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** — *Option A*

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **mechanical advantage and velocity ratio** of a screw jack used to lift a load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A screw jack has a screw of **mean diameter 50 mm** and **pitch 12 mm**. The load on the jack is **30 kN** and the coefficient of friction between the screw and the nut is **0.15**. If the lever arm is **600 mm** long, find: **(7 Marks)**
   1. The effort required at the end of the lever to raise the load.
   2. The effort required to lower the load.
   3. The efficiency of the screw jack during raising.
   4. Whether the jack is self-locking or not.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** Follow the standard derivation given in **Section 3.2** of these notes.

**Summary of derivation (for answer sheet):**
- Unwrap the square thread as an inclined plane of angle $\alpha$ where $\tan \alpha = p/(\pi d_m)$.
- Resolve the load $W$ along and perpendicular to the inclined plane.
- Apply equilibrium: tangential force $P_t = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ for raising.
- Convert to effort: $P = W \cdot d_m \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) / (2L)$.
- $MA = W/P = 2L / [d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)]$.
- $VR = 2\pi L / p$ (geometric, independent of friction).
- $\eta = MA/VR = \tan\alpha / \tan(\alpha+\phi)$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $d_m = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m; $p = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m; $W = 30$ kN $= 30{,}000$ N; $\mu = 0.15$; $L = 600$ mm $= 0.6$ m.

**Step 1 — Helix angle $\alpha$:**

$$\tan \alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi d_m} = \dfrac{0.012}{\pi \times 0.05} = \dfrac{0.012}{0.15708} = 0.07639$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.07639) = 4.37°$$

**Step 2 — Friction angle $\phi$:**

$$\tan \phi = \mu = 0.15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.15) = 8.53°$$

**Step 3 — Effort to raise the load:**

$$P_{raise} = \dfrac{W \cdot d_m \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2L}$$

$$\alpha + \phi = 4.37° + 8.53° = 12.90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan(12.90°) = 0.2289$$

$$P_{raise} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.2289}{2 \times 0.6} = \dfrac{343.35}{1.2} = 286.1 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{raise} \approx 286 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4 — Effort to lower the load:**

$$P_{lower} = \dfrac{W \cdot d_m \cdot \tan(\phi - \alpha)}{2L}$$

$$\phi - \alpha = 8.53° - 4.37° = 4.16° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tan(4.16°) = 0.0727$$

$$P_{lower} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.0727}{1.2} = \dfrac{109.05}{1.2} = 90.9 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{lower} \approx 90.9 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency during raising:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} = \dfrac{0.07639}{0.2289} = 0.3338$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 33.4\%}$$

**Step 6 — Self-locking check:**

Since $\alpha = 4.37° < \phi = 8.53°$, the condition $\alpha < \phi$ is satisfied.

$$\boxed{\text{The screw jack is self-locking (load will not back-drive).}}$$

**Verification using MA and VR:**

$$VR = \dfrac{2\pi L}{p} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 0.6}{0.012} = 314.16$$

$$MA = \dfrac{W}{P} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{286.1} = 104.86$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{MA}{VR} = \dfrac{104.86}{314.16} = 0.3338 = 33.4\% \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** — *Option B*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **hydraulic jack** with a neat sketch. State **Pascal's Law** and show that the **mechanical advantage of a hydraulic jack is the ratio of the areas** of the two pistons. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic jack has a **pump piston diameter of 20 mm** and a **ram piston diameter of 100 mm**. An effort of **100 N** is applied on the pump piston through a lever having a mechanical advantage of **5**. Find: **(7 Marks)**
   1. The load that can be lifted.
   2. The pressure developed in the hydraulic oil.
   3. The volume of oil displaced per pump stroke of 30 mm.
   4. The height the load rises per pump stroke.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Working principle:** Follow **Section 2.2** of these notes.
- Sketch: Show reservoir, pump piston (small), check valves, ram piston (large), load, release valve.
- Pascal's law: $P_1 = P_2$.
- Derivation: $F/a = W/A \Rightarrow W = F \cdot A/a \Rightarrow MA = A/a$ — full derivation in **Section 3.5**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 20$ mm $= 0.02$ m; $D = 100$ mm $= 0.10$ m; lever $MA_{lever} = 5$; $F_{hand} = 100$ N; pump stroke $s = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m.

**Step 1 — Effective effort on pump piston (after lever amplification):**

$$F = F_{hand} \times MA_{lever} = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Areas of the two pistons:**

$$a = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.02)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Load that can be lifted:**

$$W = F \cdot \dfrac{A}{a} = 500 \times \dfrac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 500 \times 25 = 12{,}500 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{W = 12.5 \text{ kN} \approx 1.275 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure developed in oil:**

$$P = \dfrac{F}{a} = \dfrac{500}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.5915 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.59 \text{ MPa} \approx 15.9 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 5 — Volume of oil displaced per pump stroke:**

$$V = a \cdot s = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03 = 9.425 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 9.425 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 9.425 \text{ mL}}$$

**Step 6 — Height the load rises per pump stroke:**

By volume conservation ($A \cdot h = a \cdot s$):

$$h = \dfrac{a \cdot s}{A} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.03}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{h = 1.2 \text{ mm per pump stroke}}$$

> **Engineering insight:** To lift a load by 100 mm, the operator must pump $\approx 83$ times. This trade-off (many small pumps vs. one big lift) is the *price* of force multiplication in hydraulic systems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

**Screw Jack:**
- ✅ **Helix angle:** $\tan \alpha = p/(\pi d_m)$
- ✅ **Friction angle:** $\tan \phi = \mu$
- ✅ **Velocity Ratio:** $VR = 2\pi L / p$ (geometric, frictionless)
- ✅ **MA (raising):** $MA = 2L / [d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)]$
- ✅ **MA (lowering):** Use $\tan(\phi - \alpha)$ instead of $\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ in the numerator of effort.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = \tan \alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi) = MA / VR$
- ✅ **Self-locking:** $\alpha < \phi$ (mandatory safety condition for jacks)
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency helix angle:** $\alpha_{max} = 45° - \phi/2$
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency value:** $\eta_{max} = (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi)$
- ✅ **Typical efficiency range:** 30% – 50% (always less than 50% for self-locking screw jack)

**Hydraulic Jack:**
- ✅ **Pascal's Law:** $P_1 = P_2$ (transmitted undiminished)
- ✅ **MA = (Ram Area) / (Pump Area) = $D^2/d^2$**
- ✅ **Volume conservation:** $A \cdot h = a \cdot s$
- ✅ **Force-distance trade-off:** Force multiplied $\Rightarrow$ speed divided by the same factor.
- ✅ **Typical efficiency:** 70% – 90%
- ✅ **Self-locking:** NO (must use check valve to prevent back-flow).

**Safety Standards (OESFT833 specific):**
- ✅ **SWL** (Safe Working Load) must be marked on every jack — **NEVER exceed SWL**.
- ✅ Always use a **jack stand** after lifting — never work under a load supported only by a jack.
- ✅ Use on **hard, level, non-slippery surfaces** to prevent tipping.
- ✅ **Hydraulic jacks** must have a **bypass relief valve** to prevent over-pressurization.
- ✅ **Screw jacks** must have a **safety nut (lock nut)** to prevent total collapse if the main nut fails.
- ✅ **OSHA 1910.244** mandates that jacks be inspected before each use.
- ✅ **Colour coding** (industry practice): red label = 2T, blue = 5T, yellow = 10T, green = 20T.
- ✅ Operator must be **trained and certified** under the Factories Act for lifting equipment operation.

**Common KTU Exam Pitfalls:**
- ⚠️ Mixing up $d$ (nominal diameter), $d_m$ (mean diameter), and $d_c$ (core diameter) — use $d_m$ in the standard formulas.
- ⚠️ Forgetting to convert mm to m in numericals.
- ⚠️ Forgetting the sign of friction in raising vs. lowering.
- ⚠️ For square vs. V-threads: KTU standard formula assumes **square thread** unless stated otherwise.
- ⚠️ Hydraulic jack $MA$ uses the **square of the diameter ratio** — not the diameter ratio itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Trucks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hand Truck** (also known as a sack truck, dolly, or trolley) is a manually operated, non-powered material handling device consisting of a small wheeled platform or frame designed for transporting heavy, bulky, or multiple loads over short distances, typically by a single operator using physical human effort. According to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 1744:1986)** and **OSHA 1910.176** regulations on materials handling, a hand truck is classified under *Category I - Manual Material Handling Equipment (MMHE)* used for the internal transport of goods in warehousing, retail, manufacturing, and shipping operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OESFT833 - Module 1):** Hand trucks form the foundation of *unit-load handling equipment* studied under Manual Material Handling (MMH). Mastery of this topic is essential for Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

**Simple Analogy — The "Wheelbarrow Principle":**
Imagine trying to move a heavy wooden crate across a factory floor. Without any aid, you would have to either *lift and carry* it (high back injury risk) or *drag and slide* it (high floor damage and effort). A hand truck essentially **substitutes the friction between load and floor with a rolling interface (wheels)**. The mechanical principle is the same as that of a **wheelbarrow** — you convert sliding friction (μ ≈ 0.3–0.6) into rolling friction (μ ≈ 0.005–0.01), reducing operator effort by approximately **90%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition to Memorize:**
> A *hand truck* is a **low-lifting, wheeled manual handling aid** that does **not** raise the load off the ground by itself — the operator must *tilt* the device so that the load balances on its wheels and toe plate.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Hand Trucks

| Classification Basis | Type | Typical Load Capacity | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wheels** | Two-wheeled (sack truck) | **180 – 270 kg** | Vertical stacking, narrow aisles |
| | Three-wheeled | **90 – 360 kg** | Stair climbing, awkward loads |
| | Four-wheeled (platform) | **270 – 900 kg** | Flat-surface, long-distance transport |
| **Mechanism** | Manual (non-powered) | Up to **900 kg** | Standard warehousing |
| | Motorized (powered hand truck) | Up to **1,500 kg** | Heavy industrial, repetitive motion |
| **Specialization** | Pallet truck (low-lift) | **2,000 – 2,500 kg** | Palletized warehouse loads |
| | Drum truck | **270 – 360 kg** | Cylindrical drums/barrels |
| | Appliance dolly | **270 – 450 kg** | Refrigerators, washing machines |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **N**ewton-Einstein limit for a single operator in safe repetitive lifting is **23 kg (≈ 50 lbs)** per the NIOSH Lifting Equation. Hand trucks extend this practical safe-load limit to **200+ kg** without mechanical assistance — a **10× safety multiplier** through physics alone.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle — Tipping the Centre of Gravity

The defining engineering concept behind any two-wheeled hand truck is the **principle of moment balance** about the axle of the wheels. When a hand truck is tilted backward, the load's weight creates a moment that is counterbalanced by the operator's pulling force applied at the handles.

### 2.1.1 Logic Flow of Hand Truck Operation
1. **Loading:** Load is placed against the vertical backrest / toe plate of the hand truck.
2. **Tilting:** Operator tilts the device backward (~ 15° to 25°) until the load's *centre of gravity (CG)* shifts over the wheel axle.
3. **Balancing:** The handle force $F_h$ and the load weight $W$ create a couple about the axle.
4. **Rolling:** Operator pulls/pushes, wheels rotate, and load moves with minimum friction.

## 2.2 The Moment Equation (Static Tipping Analysis)

For a two-wheeled hand truck tilted at angle $\theta$ from vertical, with load weight $W$ acting at horizontal distance $d$ from the wheel axle, and operator force $F_h$ applied at handle height $h$ above the axle:

The horizontal force required to *initiate rolling* is governed by the moment balance:

$$
F_h \cdot h \cdot \cos\theta = W \cdot d
$$

Therefore, the required handle force is:

$$
F_h = \frac{W \cdot d}{h \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

This is the **KTU High-Yield Equation #1** for any hand truck problem.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical delimiters below use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Required Handle Force | $F_h = \dfrac{W \cdot d}{h \cdot \cos\theta}$ | **N (Newtons)** | Tipping moment balance |
| 2 | Load Capacity vs Operator Strength | $W_{max} = \dfrac{F_{h,max} \cdot h \cdot \cos\theta}{d}$ | **kg** | Safe load limit calc. |
| 3 | Rolling Friction Force | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot N$ | **N** | Force to move truck |
| 4 | Coefficient of Rolling Friction (rubber on concrete) | $\mu_r \approx 0.005$ to $0.01$ | dimensionless | Empirical constant |
| 5 | Slope Correction Factor | $F_{incline} = W(\sin\alpha + \mu_r \cos\alpha)$ | **N** | Uphill / downhill force |
| 6 | Tipping Tilt Angle (design parameter) | $\theta_{min} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{d}{h}\right)$ | **degrees** | Self-balancing geometry |
| 7 | Pushing vs Pulling Ergonomic Factor | $F_{push}/F_{pull} \approx 0.85$ | ratio | NIOSH guideline |
| 8 | Safe Operator Push Force Limit (continuous) | $F_{push,max} \approx 230$ N | **N** | Industrial hygiene std. |
| 9 | CG of Stack (n items) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum m_i x_i}{\sum m_i}$ | **m** | Stacked load stability |
| 10 | Required Operator Force with Load Inertia | $F = \dfrac{W}{g} \cdot a + F_r$ | **N** | Accelerating from rest |

Where:
- $W$ = Load weight (N or kg-force)  
- $d$ = Horizontal distance of CG from axle (m)  
- $h$ = Handle height above axle (m)  
- $\theta$ = Tilt angle from vertical (radians)  
- $\mu_r$ = Coefficient of rolling friction (dimensionless)  
- $N$ = Normal reaction (N)  
- $a$ = Linear acceleration (m/s²)  
- $g = 9.81$ m/s² (acceleration due to gravity)  
- $\alpha$ = Incline angle (radians)  

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Engineering Domain | Hand Truck Application |
|---|---|
| **Warehouse Management** | Last-mile movement of palletized and non-palletized goods |
| **Ergonomics / Industrial Hygiene** | NIOSH-compliant load transfer; **prevents musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | First/last 50 m in the distribution chain |
| **Hazardous Materials (HAZMAT)** | Spill containment, drum trucks for chemical transfer |
| **Construction Safety** | On-site movement of cement bags, gas cylinders |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Note:** According to the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, hand trucks and dollies are involved in approximately **95,000 workplace injuries annually** in the U.S. alone. Most are due to **(a) load imbalance**, **(b) overloaded capacity**, and **(c) operator fatigue** — making the moment-balance equation above a **life-safety calculation**, not merely an academic one.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Required Handle Force on a Two-Wheeled Hand Truck

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A worker is transporting a load weighing **W = 200 kg** (i.e., weight = 1962 N) on a two-wheeled hand truck. The load's centre of gravity is located at a horizontal distance **d = 0.4 m** from the wheel axle. The handles are at a vertical height **h = 1.2 m** above the axle. The hand truck is tilted backward at an angle **θ = 20°** from vertical. The wheels are rubber-tyred on a concrete floor. Calculate:

(a) The horizontal force required at the handle to *maintain* the truck in balance.  
(b) The additional force required to *initiate motion* (rolling friction coefficient $\mu_r = 0.01$).  
(c) Whether the operation is within the **NIOSH recommended push force limit of 230 N**.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the given data and convert to SI units:**

$$
W = 200 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 1962 \text{ N}
$$

$$
d = 0.4 \text{ m}, \quad h = 1.2 \text{ m}, \quad \theta = 20^\circ
$$

$$
\mu_r = 0.01, \quad F_{push,max} = 230 \text{ N}
$$

> Conversion logic: The load is given in *mass* (kg) but the equation requires *weight* (force in N). Multiplication by $g$ converts mass to weight using Newton's second law $F = ma$ with $a = g$.

**Step 2 — Compute the required moment-balancing handle force:**

$$
F_h = \frac{W \cdot d}{h \cdot \cos\theta}
$$

$$
F_h = \frac{1962 \times 0.4}{1.2 \times \cos(20^\circ)}
$$

$$
F_h = \frac{784.8}{1.2 \times 0.9397}
$$

$$
F_h = \frac{784.8}{1.1276}
$$

$$
F_h \approx 695.97 \text{ N}
$$

> Conversion logic: $\cos(20^\circ) \approx 0.9397$. The vertical projection of the handle height $(h \cos\theta)$ is the *effective moment arm* about the axle when the truck is tilted.

**Step 3 — Compute the additional rolling friction force:**

$$
F_r = \mu_r \cdot N = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.01 \times 1962
$$

$$
F_r = 19.62 \text{ N}
$$

> Conversion logic: On a flat, level surface, the normal reaction $N$ equals the weight $W$ (no vertical acceleration). Rolling friction is then simply the coefficient times the weight.

**Step 4 — Compute the total force required to *initiate* motion:**

$$
F_{total} = F_h + F_r = 695.97 + 19.62
$$

$$
F_{total} \approx 715.59 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Ergonomic Safety Check against the NIOSH push limit:**

$$
F_{total} = 715.59 \text{ N} \quad \text{vs.} \quad F_{push,max} = 230 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\frac{F_{total}}{F_{push,max}} = \frac{715.59}{230} \approx 3.11
$$

> Conversion logic: Since $F_{total} > F_{push,max}$, the operation is **NOT ergonomically safe** for a single operator. The ratio of 3.11 indicates the load is **over 3× the recommended limit**.

**Step 6 — Engineering Conclusion and Safety Recommendation:**

| Parameter | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Handle force required | **695.97 N** | Required for moment balance |
| Rolling friction force | **19.62 N** | Negligible contribution |
| Total initial force | **715.59 N** | **Exceeds safe limit** |
| NIOSH safe limit | **230 N** | Single-operator cap |
| Recommendation | — | **Use a 4-wheeled platform truck, reduce load, or upgrade to motorized hand truck** |

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Maximum Safe Tilt Angle to Prevent Self-Tipping Backward

**Problem Statement:**
Determine the maximum safe tilt angle $\theta_{max}$ for a two-wheeled hand truck such that the load does not tip the operator backward. Given: $h = 1.2$ m (handle height), $d = 0.4$ m (CG distance), and a *conservative* operator reaction force $F_{h,min} = 0$ N (worst case: operator releases handles momentarily).

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Set up the moment equilibrium for the worst case:**

When the operator applies zero force, the moment of the load about the wheel axle is:

$$
M_{load} = W \cdot d \cdot \sin(90^\circ - \theta) = W \cdot d \cdot \cos\theta
$$

The restoring moment (provided by the operator's reaction) is zero at the worst case.

**Step 2 — Apply the no-tip condition:**

For self-stability, the load's CG must lie *over the wheel footprint*, i.e., the moment arm projected onto the ground must be inside the wheelbase.

$$
\tan\theta_{max} = \frac{d}{h}
$$

$$
\theta_{max} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.4}{1.2}\right) = \arctan(0.3333)
$$

$$
\theta_{max} \approx 18.43^\circ
$$

> Conversion logic: This is the **purely geometric tipping condition**. If the truck is tilted beyond this angle *without* operator force, the load's CG passes behind the axle, and the load + truck will tip backward uncontrollably — a **catastrophic safety failure**.

**Step 3 — Compare to the actual operating tilt of 20°:**

$$
\theta_{operating} = 20^\circ \quad \text{vs.} \quad \theta_{max} = 18.43^\circ
$$

$$
\Delta\theta = 20^\circ - 18.43^\circ = 1.57^\circ
$$

**Step 4 — Final Safety Verdict:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Operating tilt } \theta = 20^\circ > \theta_{max} = 18.43^\circ \implies \text{UNSAFE without continuous operator input.}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Students frequently forget to perform this **two-way safety check**. Even if the handle force (Derivation #1) is *physically* achievable, the truck may **self-tip backward** if the operator momentarily loses grip. Always check both **operator ergonomics** AND **geometric self-stability**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Safety Failure Modes (FMEA-Style)

| Failure Mode | Cause | Effect | Severity (1-10) | Probability (1-10) | RPN | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Backward tipping** | Excessive tilt angle | Operator injury, load damage | 9 | 5 | 45 | Operator training, tilt limiters |
| **Overloading** | $W > W_{rated}$ | Wheel/frame fatigue fracture | 8 | 6 | 48 | Posted load limits, color coding |
| **Wheel failure** | Worn bearings, flat tyre | Sudden load drop | 7 | 4 | 28 | Pre-use inspection checklist |
| **Pinch points** | Hands on handle during load placement | Crush injury | 8 | 7 | 56 | PPE gloves, awareness training |
| **Musculoskeletal injury** | Repetitive push/pull, posture | Chronic back pain, MSDs | 9 | 8 | 72 | Job rotation, ergonomics audit |
| **Slipping on incline** | Wet/oily floor, slope | Loss of control | 8 | 5 | 40 | Anti-slip wheels, slope limits |
| **Load shift during transit** | Unbalanced stacking, loose straps | Drop, strike injury | 9 | 6 | 54 | Strap and secure, balanced CG |

> *RPN = Risk Priority Number = Severity × Probability. A value > 50 indicates a **HIGH-priority hazard** requiring immediate control.*

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hand Truck Safety Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator PPE Check] --> B[Pre-Use Inspection]
    B --> C[Load Capacity Verification]
    C --> D[Load Placement on Toe Plate]
    D --> E[Tilting & Moment Balance Check]
    E --> F{Tilt Angle Safe?}
    F -- No --> G[Re-adjust Load CG]
    G --> E
    F -- Yes --> H[Initiate Rolling Motion]
    H --> I[Continuous Ergonomic Monitoring]
    I --> J{Operator Fatigue Detected?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Stop & Rotate Operator]
    J -- No --> L[Deliver to Destination]
    L --> M[Post-Trip Inspection & Storage]
    M --> N[Log Maintenance Entry]
```

## 4.2 Force Vector & Moment-Balance Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Load_Block[Load W = mg]
        L1[Weight W acting downward]
    end
    subgraph Truck_Frame[Hand Truck Frame]
        T1[Toe Plate]
        T2[Wheel Axle - Pivot Point]
        T3[Handle Height h]
    end
    subgraph Force_Block[Operator Applied Force]
        F1[Handle Force Fh horizontal]
    end
    L1 -- distance d from axle --> T2
    T2 --- T1
    T3 --- T2
    F1 -- applied at height h --> T3
    T2 -- Tilt angle theta --> L1
```

## 4.3 Safety Decision Tree for Hand Truck Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Pre-Use Hand Truck] --> Q1{Wheels intact?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[REJECT - Maintenance Required]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Load less than rated capacity?}
    Q2 -- No --> R2[REJECT - Reduce Load]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Floor surface clean & dry?}
    Q3 -- No --> R3[REJECT - Clear Debris / Dry Surface]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Operator PPE compliant?}
    Q4 -- No --> R4[REJECT - Wear PPE]
    Q4 -- Yes --> APPROVED[APPROVED FOR OPERATION]
    APPROVED --> Operate[Operate within NIOSH limits]
    Operate --> End[Complete task & log activity]
```

## 4.4 Hand Truck Component Identification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HT[Hand Truck Assembly]
    HT --> C1[Handle Assembly with Grips]
    HT --> C2[Vertical Backrest Frame]
    HT --> C3[Toe Plate - Load Contact]
    HT --> C4[Wheel Set with Axle]
    HT --> C5[Cross Bracing for Rigidity]
    HT --> C6[Optional Nose Plate Extension]
    C1 --> S1[Component: Steel Tube with Rubber Grip]
    C2 --> S2[Component: Channel Section Mild Steel]
    C3 --> S3[Component: 5 mm MS Plate, Ribbed]
    C4 --> S4[Component: Rubber Tyre on Nylon Hub]
    C5 --> S5[Component: 12 mm MS Round Bar]
    C6 --> S6[Component: Folding Hinge Mechanism]
```
