# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Q: Define a hand truck. List any four types of hand trucks commonly used in industrial material handling.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hand truck** is a manually operated, wheeled material handling device used to transport loads over short distances by a single operator without external power.

**Four common types:**

1. **Two-wheeled sack truck** – for vertical stacking and boxed goods.
2. **Four-wheeled platform truck** – for flat-surface, long-distance loads.
3. **Drum truck** – specialized for cylindrical containers (drums/barrels).
4. **Pallet truck (low-lift)** – for horizontal movement of palletized loads.

*(Word count: ~ 45; written concisely as expected in Part A.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**
**Q: State the NIOSH recommended maximum push force for continuous manual material handling operations. Why is it important to comply with this limit?**

**Model Answer:**

The **NIOSH recommended maximum push force** for continuous, repetitive manual material handling is approximately **230 N (≈ 50 lbf)** for a single operator on a smooth, level surface.

**Importance of compliance:**

- Prevents cumulative trauma disorders (CTDs) and **musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)**.
- Reduces occupational back injuries, which account for **~ 25%** of all workplace injuries.
- Ensures compliance with **OSHA ergonomic standards** and the **Factories Act 1948** (India).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hand Truck Questions:**
> - Students often **omit the unit conversion** from kg to N (multiplying by $g = 9.81$). This loses **2 full marks**.
> - Many forget to include the **cos θ term** in the moment equation, leading to an incorrect handle force. **Loss: 3 marks**.
> - Failing to perform the **ergonomic safety check** against the NIOSH 230 N limit means missing the application part of the question. **Loss: 2 marks**.
> - Writing the formula without stating its **physical meaning** in words loses partial credit. Always **define every variable in the equation**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, Module 1, Part B Choice 1]**

**(a)** Derive the equation for the **required handle force $F_h$** to balance a load of weight **W** on a two-wheeled hand truck tilted at angle $\theta$ from vertical. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker is transporting **W = 250 kg** (weight = 2452.5 N) on a hand truck. The CG of the load is **d = 0.45 m** from the axle, handle height **h = 1.1 m**, tilt angle **θ = 18°**, and rolling friction coefficient **μr = 0.012**. Calculate: (i) the handle force for static balance, (ii) the additional force to overcome rolling friction, and (iii) verify if the operation is within the **NIOSH 230 N safe push limit**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation of $F_h$ (7 marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. The hand truck is rigid (no frame deformation).
2. The load is a point mass acting at its CG.
3. The floor is level; no slope correction.
4. Friction is purely rolling (negligible bearing friction).
5. The system is in **quasi-static** equilibrium.

**Free Body Diagram & Moment Equation:**

Take moments about the wheel axle (the pivot). The weight $W$ acts downward at horizontal distance $d$ from the axle. The handle force $F_h$ acts horizontally at height $h$ above the axle. The tilt angle $\theta$ is measured from the vertical.

**Vertical projection of handle height** (effective moment arm of $F_h$):

$$
\text{Arm}_{F_h} = h \cos\theta
$$

**Horizontal projection of CG distance** (effective moment arm of $W$):

$$
\text{Arm}_{W} = d
$$

For static balance, sum of moments about the axle = 0:

$$
F_h \cdot h \cos\theta = W \cdot d
$$

Solving for $F_h$:

$$
\boxed{F_h = \frac{W \cdot d}{h \cos\theta}}
$$

This is the **minimum force** the operator must apply horizontally to keep the hand truck in rotational equilibrium.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
$W = 250 \times 9.81 = 2452.5$ N, $d = 0.45$ m, $h = 1.1$ m, $\theta = 18°$, $\mu_r = 0.012$.

**Step 1 — Static handle force:**

$$
F_h = \frac{2452.5 \times 0.45}{1.1 \times \cos(18^\circ)}
$$

$$
\cos(18^\circ) \approx 0.9511
$$

$$
F_h = \frac{1103.625}{1.1 \times 0.9511} = \frac{1103.625}{1.0462}
$$

$$
F_h \approx 1054.86 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Rolling friction force:**

$$
F_r = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.012 \times 2452.5 = 29.43 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total force to initiate motion:**

$$
F_{total} = F_h + F_r = 1054.86 + 29.43 = 1084.29 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — NIOSH safety check:**

$$
\frac{F_{total}}{F_{push,max}} = \frac{1084.29}{230} \approx 4.71
$$

Since $F_{total} \gg 230$ N, the operation **violates the NIOSH ergonomic limit by a factor of ~4.7**.

**Conclusion:** A 250 kg load on a two-wheeled hand truck is **unsafe for a single operator**. Recommended alternatives: **(i)** use a four-wheeled platform truck, **(ii)** reduce the load to under ~50 kg, or **(iii)** upgrade to a **motorized hand truck**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern, Module 1, Part B Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a two-wheeled hand truck. With the help of a free-body diagram, describe the **moment balance about the wheel axle** that allows the operator to balance and move the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-wheeled hand truck has the following specifications: **maximum rated load = 200 kg**, handle height **h = 1.0 m**, load CG distance **d = 0.35 m**, and operating tilt angle **θ = 15°**. An operator can apply a maximum sustained horizontal force of **F_h = 300 N** at the handle. Calculate: (i) the **actual handle force** required for the rated load, (ii) the **safety margin** in percentage terms, and (iii) the **maximum safe tilt angle** $\theta_{max}$ beyond which the truck would self-tip backward without operator input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Principle of Operation (7 marks)

The two-wheeled hand truck operates on the **principle of moment (torque) balance** about the wheel axle. When the operator tilts the truck backward, the load weight $W$ — which originally exerts a moment trying to rotate the truck forward — is now counteracted by the **horizontal handle force $F_h$** acting at the elevated handle position.

The free-body diagram shows three forces: (1) the load weight $W$ acting at the CG, (2) the operator's force $F_h$ at the handle, and (3) the **normal reaction** at the wheels. The system is in **rotational equilibrium** when the clockwise moment of $W$ equals the counter-clockwise moment of $F_h$. The hand truck does not lift the load — it **redistributes the load weight to the wheels**, allowing the operator to move it via rolling rather than lifting.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
$W = 200 \times 9.81 = 1962$ N, $h = 1.0$ m, $d = 0.35$ m, $\theta = 15°$, $F_{h,available} = 300$ N.

**Step 1 — Actual handle force required:**

$$
F_{h,req} = \frac{W \cdot d}{h \cos\theta} = \frac{1962 \times 0.35}{1.0 \times \cos(15^\circ)}
$$

$$
\cos(15^\circ) \approx 0.9659
$$

$$
F_{h,req} = \frac{686.7}{0.9659} \approx 710.95 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Safety margin calculation:**

Since $F_{h,req} = 710.95$ N > $F_{h,available} = 300$ N, the operator **cannot** safely handle this load.

$$
\text{Safety Margin} = \frac{F_{h,available} - F_{h,req}}{F_{h,req}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Safety Margin} = \frac{300 - 710.95}{710.95} \times 100\% \approx -57.8\%
$$

A **negative safety margin** indicates the system is **unsafe by 57.8%** — the operator lacks the strength to perform the task.

**Step 3 — Maximum self-stable tilt angle:**

$$
\theta_{max} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{h}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.35}{1.0}\right) = \arctan(0.35)
$$

$$
\theta_{max} \approx 19.29^\circ
$$

**Conclusion:** The operating tilt of 15° is **below** the self-tipping limit of 19.29°, so the truck is geometrically stable. However, the operator lacks the strength to balance the rated 200 kg load. **Recommended action:** Reduce the load to approximately:

$$
W_{safe} = \frac{F_{h,available} \cdot h \cos\theta}{d} = \frac{300 \times 1.0 \times 0.9659}{0.35} \approx 827.86 \text{ N} \approx 84.4 \text{ kg}
$$

The operator can safely handle up to **~ 84 kg** under these conditions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Hand Trucks (Module 1):**

- **Definition:** A hand truck is a **manually operated, wheeled material handling device** for short-distance transport.
- **Primary types:** Two-wheeled sack truck, four-wheeled platform truck, drum truck, pallet truck, appliance dolly.
- **Operating principle:** Tilt and moment balance about the wheel axle — **does not lift the load off the ground**.
- **Master Formula:** $F_h = \dfrac{W \cdot d}{h \cos\theta}$ — the **single most important equation** for this topic.
- **NIOSH safe push limit:** **230 N** (continuous, single operator).
- **Rolling friction coefficient (rubber on concrete):** $\mu_r \approx 0.005$ to $0.01$.
- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² (always include in unit conversions).
- **Geometric self-tipping angle:** $\theta_{max} = \arctan(d/h)$.
- **Pushing is preferred over pulling** for ergonomics (lower back strain).
- **Always perform a TWO-way safety check:** (1) Operator force < 230 N, AND (2) Operating tilt < $\theta_{max}$.
- **Top 3 failure modes:** Overloading, backward tipping, and wheel/frame fatigue.
- **OSHA Regulation:** **29 CFR 1910.176** governs hand truck safety in the U.S.; equivalent Indian standards include **IS 1744:1986** and the **Factories Act 1948**.
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number) for hand truck injuries** is highest in: (i) musculoskeletal disorders (RPN = 72), (ii) pinch points (RPN = 56), (iii) load shift (RPN = 54).
- **For Part B 14-mark questions:** Always show **(a) Free-body diagram, (b) Unit conversions, (c) Step-by-step substitution, (d) Safety interpretation, (e) Recommendation**. Missing any one of these loses 2-3 marks.
- **Numerical values to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $F_{push,max} = 230$ N, $\mu_r \approx 0.01$ for rubber-tyred wheels on dry concrete.
- **Ergonomic ratio:** Pushing force is approximately **85%** of pulling force for the same load (NIOSH data).
- **Real-world impact:** Hand truck-related injuries account for **~ 95,000 U.S. workplace injuries/year** — emphasizing the safety-critical nature of this topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dollies and Wheel Barrows - Storage of Specific Materials - Problems with Hazardous Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dollies and Wheel Barrows - Storage of Specific Materials - Problems with Hazardous Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Dollies — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **dolly** (also called a hand truck, truck, or trolley) is a low-profile wheeled platform or frame used to transport heavy, bulky, or awkward loads over short horizontal distances within an industrial plant, warehouse, or construction site. Dollies convert sliding friction into **rolling friction**, dramatically reducing the manual force required to move loads.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A dolly is a manually-propelled or powered material-handling device, consisting of a frame mounted on wheels, used to lift, support, and move individual units of load without lifting the load fully off the ground. It is classified under **unit-load handling equipment** in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Mobile Floor"
Imagine you are trying to drag a heavy refrigerator across your kitchen floor — it scratches the tiles and you struggle. Now, if you place that refrigerator on a **sliding sheet of casters** (small wheels), the same refrigerator glides with a tiny push. That "sheet of casters" is exactly what a dolly does: it gives a load a temporary set of legs so it can **walk** instead of being **dragged**.

## 1.2 Wheelbarrows — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

A **wheelbarrow** is a single-person, hand-propelled carrier consisting of a tray (or bucket) supported on one or two wheels at the front and two handles at the rear. It is used to transport small quantities of construction debris, garden refuse, concrete, or bulk material over uneven terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Distinction (High-Exam-Value):**
> - A **Dolly** has the load **above** the wheels (low center of gravity, pushed/pulled).
> - A **Wheelbarrow** has the load **between** the wheel and operator (load suspended over the wheel, lifted at the back handles).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscle Multiplier"
Think of a wheelbarrow as a **lever with a wheel** — the front wheel is the **fulcrum**, the load sits near the fulcrum, and the operator's hands at the long handle end supply a small downward force that lifts a much heavier load in between. This is essentially a **Class 2 lever** in disguise.

## 1.3 Storage of Specific Materials — Formal Definition

Storage of specific materials refers to the systematic placement, segregation, and containment of **raw materials, in-process goods, and finished products** in designated storage areas, following prescribed engineering, regulatory, and safety standards. Specific materials include **flammables, corrosives, oxidizers, toxics, reactives, compressed gases, and radioactive substances**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Storage planning integrates three engineering disciplines:
> 1. **Ergonomics** (operator access and reach)
> 2. **Fire engineering** (compartmentation and sprinkler zones)
> 3. **Chemical compatibility** (incompatibility matrices)

## 1.4 Problems with Hazardous Materials — Formal Definition

Hazardous materials (HAZMAT) pose **physical, health, or environmental hazards** due to their inherent chemical, biological, or radiological properties. Problems include **incompatible storage, leakage, ignition, explosion, toxic exposure, corrosion damage, and regulatory non-compliance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Standards Referenced in KTU 2024:**
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106** — Flammable liquids
> - **NFPA 30** — Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code
> - **NFPA 704** — Hazard Identification Rating (the "fire diamond")
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.120** — HAZWOPER (Hazardous Waste Operations)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Pantry Rules"
In your home kitchen, you do **not** store bleach next to ammonia (toxic gas on mixing) and you do not store cooking oil next to the gas cylinder (fire risk). Industrial hazardous storage follows the **same logic — scaled up by a factor of 10,000**, with the added risk that any leak can injure dozens of workers, ignite a building, or contaminate groundwater. **Incompatibility, not quantity, is what kills.**

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Coefficient of rolling friction (μᵣ) for steel wheel on steel rail:** **0.001 to 0.002**
- **Coefficient of kinetic friction for a wooden skid on concrete:** **0.30 to 0.50**
- **Rolling resistance reduction factor:** up to **99 %** vs. sliding friction
- **Standard manual push force limit (NIOSH):** **≤ 230 N (≈ 23 kgf)** for continuous work
- **Maximum recommended manual push speed:** **≤ 1.0 m/s (≈ 3.6 km/h)**
- **NFPA 704 rating scale:** **0 (no hazard) to 4 (severe hazard)**
- **Pallet standard dimensions:** **1200 mm × 1000 mm (Euro)** or **1219 mm × 1016 mm (ISO)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Dollies

Dollies are classified by **number of wheels**, **load orientation**, and **power source**.

### 2.1.1 By Load Orientation
- **Platform Dolly (Pallet Dolly):** Flat platform on 4 swivel casters; load rests on the platform. Capacity: **250 kg to 2000 kg**.
- **Hand Truck (Two-Wheel Vertical):** Two wheels at the base, a vertical back plate, and handles at the top. The load leans back against the plate. Capacity: **150 kg to 350 kg**.
- **Drum Dolly:** Circular cradle with casters, sized to hold a 55-gallon (≈ 200 L) steel drum. Capacity: **250 kg to 500 kg**.
- **Furniture Dolly:** Square or rectangular low platform with reinforced corners.
- **Piano Dolly:** Heavy-duty version with strap retainer.

### 2.1.2 By Power Source
- **Manual:** Operator force only.
- **Motorized (Powered Industrial Truck, PIT):** Battery or IC engine. Regulated under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178**.

## 2.2 Classification of Wheelbarrows

- **Single-Wheel Wheelbarrow:** One front wheel; high maneuverability, but unstable; load capacity **80–150 kg**.
- **Two-Wheel Wheelbarrow (Sack Barrow / Garden Cart):** Two front wheels; greater stability; capacity **150–250 kg**.
- **Motorized Wheelbarrow:** Engine-driven for construction sites; capacity up to **500 kg**.
- **Tipping Wheelbarrow:** Hinged tray that pivots on the frame for dump-discharge.

## 2.3 The Physics of Rolling vs. Sliding — Why Dollies Save Energy

When a load is dragged, the resisting force is governed by **kinetic friction**:

$$F_{sliding} = \mu_k \cdot W$$

where $W = m \cdot g$ is the weight of the load and $\mu_k$ is the coefficient of kinetic friction.

When the same load is placed on a dolly, the resisting force is governed by **rolling resistance**:

$$F_{rolling} = C_{rr} \cdot W$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** (dimensionless, but typically 0.001 to 0.01 for hard wheels on hard floors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **force ratio** is $\frac{F_{sliding}}{F_{rolling}} = \frac{\mu_k}{C_{rr}}$. For a wooden skid on concrete ($\mu_k = 0.4$) and a steel wheel on steel ($C_{rr} = 0.0015$), the ratio is **≈ 267**. In other words, a dolly requires **267 times less force** than dragging.

## 2.4 Stability of Dollies and Wheelbarrows — The Tipping Moment

A critical safety problem in dolly/wheelbarrow operation is **tipping** during transit, especially over uneven surfaces or when the load is not centered.

**Condition for stability against forward tipping:**

$$\frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{F_{push} \cdot h_{push}} < 1$$

where:
- $W$ = total weight (load + dolly), N
- $d_{cg}$ = horizontal distance from the front wheel axle to the combined center of gravity, m
- $F_{push}$ = horizontal push force applied at the handles, N
- $h_{push}$ = height at which the push is applied, m
- The ratio $\frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{F_{push} \cdot h_{push}}$ is the **tipping index**. A value **< 1** is safe; **≥ 1** means the load will tip forward.

**Condition for stability against rearward tipping (load sliding off the back):**

$$\mu_{load-dolly} \geq \frac{F_{push}}{W}$$

The load must have sufficient friction with the dolly platform (or be mechanically clamped) so that the push force does not slide the load off the rear of the dolly.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sliding friction force | $F_{sliding} = \mu_k W$ | N | Force to drag a load |
| 2 | Rolling resistance force | $F_{rolling} = C_{rr} W$ | N | Force to push a dolly |
| 3 | Tipping index | $T_i = \frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{F \cdot h}$ | dimensionless | Stability check |
| 4 | Lever advantage of wheelbarrow | $M_A = \frac{L_{handle}}{L_{load}}$ | ratio | Lifting force reduction |
| 5 | Center of gravity (combined system) | $x_{cg} = \frac{m_1 x_1 + m_2 x_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | m | Combined load location |
| 6 | Maximum push force (NIOSH) | $F_{push,max}$ | **≤ 230 N** | Ergonomic limit |
| 7 | NFPA 704 rating | 0–4 scale | unitless | Hazard severity |
| 8 | Stacking pressure (cylindrical drum) | $p = \frac{W}{A_{contact}}$ | Pa | Floor load check |
| 9 | Safe storage height (liquids) | $h_{max}$ | **≤ 1.5 m** for 200 L drums | OSHA 1910.106 |
| 10 | Cabinet vent ratio (flammables) | $\frac{A_{vent}}{V_{cab}}$ | **≥ 0.0025 m²/m³** | NFPA 30 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** Memorize the **Tipping Index** formula and the **NFPA 704 scale**. These appear in nearly every end-semester paper for this module.

## 2.6 Storage of Specific Materials — Engineering Logic

The storage plan must satisfy four engineering rules:

1. **Segregation by chemical class** — acids away from bases, oxidizers away from organics.
2. **Segregation by fire class** — Class A (ordinary combustibles) separate from Class B (flammable liquids).
3. **Containment** — secondary containment equal to **110 % of the largest container** (EPA requirement).
4. **Access and egress** — aisles at least **1.0 m wide** for manual handling; **2.5 m** for forklift access.

## 2.7 Problems with Hazardous Materials — Engineering Classification

| Hazard Class | Example | Storage Rule | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flammable liquids | Petrol, acetone | NFPA 30, flammable cabinet | Pool fire, VCE |
| Oxidizers | Hydrogen peroxide, nitrates | Separate by **≥ 6 m** from flammables | Intensified fire |
| Corrosives (acids/bases) | H₂SO₄, NaOH | Acid cabinet **separate** from base cabinet | Skin burn, tank rupture |
| Toxics | Cyanides, pesticides | Locked, ventilated, signed | Inhalation, ingestion |
| Reactives (water-reactive) | Sodium, lithium | Dry storage, sealed containers | Fire / H₂ release |
| Compressed gases | O₂, C₂H₂ | Upright, chained, capped | Cylinder rocket, BLEVE |
| Radioactives | Co-60 sources | Lead-shielded, signed | Radiation exposure |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Tipping Index for a Loaded Hand Truck (Dolly)

**Problem Setup (as posed in a typical KTU numerical):**
A worker pushes a two-wheel hand truck loaded with a 100 kg crate. The combined center of gravity is located 0.30 m in front of the wheel axle. The worker applies a horizontal push force at a height of 1.10 m. The dolly itself weighs 20 kg. Determine the tipping index and the maximum allowable push force.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data and convert to SI units.**

Total weight $W = (m_{load} + m_{dolly}) \cdot g = (100 + 20) \cdot 9.81 = 1177.2 \text{ N}$

Horizontal distance from axle to CG: $d_{cg} = 0.30 \text{ m}$

Height of push application: $h_{push} = 1.10 \text{ m}$

**Step 2 — State the tipping moment equilibrium.**

The hand truck will tip forward about the front wheel axle when the **overturning moment** (from $F_{push}$ acting at height $h_{push}$) equals the **restoring moment** (from $W$ acting through $d_{cg}$).

At the tipping threshold (in equilibrium, just about to tip):

$$F_{push} \cdot h_{push} = W \cdot d_{cg}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical push force.**

$$F_{push,crit} = \frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{h_{push}}$$

Substituting values:

$$F_{push,crit} = \frac{1177.2 \cdot 0.30}{1.10} = 321.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the ergonomic limit.**

The NIOSH recommended maximum push force for continuous work is **230 N**. Since $F_{push,crit} = 321.05 \text{ N}$ exceeds 230 N, the operator is **at risk of tipping** if they apply more than 230 N, which is allowed ergonomically.

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** A **lighter load or a lower center of gravity** is required. For example, if the load is reduced so that $W_{new} = 85$ kg total, then $F_{push,crit} = \frac{85 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.30}{1.10} = 227.4$ N — just under the limit. This is the standard KTU "design for safety" numerical.

**Step 5 — State the Tipping Index explicitly.**

$$T_i = \frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{F_{push} \cdot h_{push}}$$

For $F_{push} = 230 \text{ N}$:

$$T_i = \frac{1177.2 \cdot 0.30}{230 \cdot 1.10} = 1.396$$

Since $T_i > 1$, **the load will tip** even at the ergonomic push-force limit. The design is unsafe.

For $W_{new} = 85 \text{ kg}$:

$$T_i = \frac{(85 \cdot 9.81) \cdot 0.30}{230 \cdot 1.10} = 0.988 \approx 0.99 \;\; (\text{safe, marginally})$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Force to Push a Loaded Dolly on a Concrete Floor

**Problem Setup:**
A pallet dolly carries a 750 kg load. The dolly has four polyurethane casters, each of diameter 100 mm. The coefficient of rolling resistance for polyurethane on concrete is $C_{rr} = 0.012$. The floor has a 2° slope upward. Find the total horizontal force required to push the dolly at constant velocity.

**Step 1 — Compute the rolling resistance force.**

$$F_{roll} = C_{rr} \cdot W = 0.012 \cdot 750 \cdot 9.81 = 88.29 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gravitational component along the slope.**

The slope angle $\theta = 2°$. The component of weight acting against the motion along the slope is:

$$F_{slope} = W \cdot \sin\theta = 750 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(2°) = 750 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0349 = 256.78 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the resisting forces (constant velocity ⇒ sum = applied force).**

$$F_{push,total} = F_{roll} + F_{slope} = 88.29 + 256.78 = 345.07 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the ergonomic limit.**

Since $345.07 \text{ N} > 230 \text{ N}$, **a single worker cannot safely push this load up this slope**. The KTU-correct recommendation is to (a) reduce the slope, (b) use a motorized dolly, or (c) add a second worker.

## 3.3 Derivation: Secondary Containment Volume (Hazardous Liquid Storage)

**Problem Setup:**
A chemical store contains three 200 L drums of sulfuric acid. The regulatory minimum secondary containment volume is **110 % of the largest single container**. Find the required sump volume and the freeboard height if the sump is 2.0 m × 2.0 m.

**Step 1 — Compute the largest container volume.**

$$V_{largest} = 200 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 110 % rule.**

$$V_{sump} = 1.10 \cdot 200 = 220 \text{ L} = 0.220 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the freeboard height.**

$$h_{sump} = \frac{V_{sump}}{A_{sump}} = \frac{0.220}{2.0 \cdot 2.0} = 0.055 \text{ m} = 55 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Add a 25 mm safety margin (engineering best practice).**

$$h_{design} = 55 + 25 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting a Dolly for a Specific Material

| Material | Best Dolly Type | Key Reason | Critical Safety Check |
|---|---|---|---|
| 200 L steel drum (full, ≈ 350 kg) | **Drum dolly with strap retainer** | Cradle geometry matches drum | Strap tension ≥ 1.5 × drum weight |
| 25 kg bags of cement | **Platform dolly (4 swivel casters)** | Bags stack stably, low CG | Stack height ≤ 1.5 m, no overhanging bags |
| Furniture (office, 80 kg) | **Furniture dolly with corner pads** | Surface area distributes load | Corners padded; load strapped to dolly |
| Gas cylinders (50 kg each) | **Cylinder dolly with chain** | Cylinder secured vertically | Chain closed; valve cap fitted |
| Roll of carpet (50 kg) | **Tilt truck or carpet dolly** | Cylindrical shape needs cradle | Eye-bolt pull point or strap |
| Glass panes | **A-frame glass dolly** | Vertical storage; cushioned back | Pane clamps engaged; back cushion intact |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Incompatibility for Hazardous Material Storage

| Stored Material | **Do NOT Store With** | Reaction / Hazard |
|---|---|---|
| **Flammable liquids** (acetone) | Oxidizers (H₂O₂, KMnO₄) | Fire / explosion |
| **Acids** (HCl, H₂SO₄) | Bases (NaOH, NH₄OH) | Exothermic neutralization, splash |
| **Acids** | Cyanides | HCN gas release |
| **Acids** | Sulfides | H₂S gas release |
| **Bases** | Metals (Al, Zn) | H₂ gas → fire/explosion |
| **Oxidizers** | Organics (oil, grease, sawdust) | Spontaneous combustion |
| **Water-reactives** (Na, Li) | Aqueous solutions, wet floors | H₂ fire / explosion |
| **Compressed O₂** | Flammable gases (C₂H₂, LPG) | Detonation if mixed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Classification of Material-Handling Aids

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Material Handling Aids] --> B[Powered]
    A --> C[Manual]
    A --> D[Semi-Powered]
    B --> B1[Forklift]
    B --> B2[Motorized Pallet Jack]
    B --> B3[Motorized Wheelbarrow]
    C --> C1[Dollies]
    C --> C2[Wheelbarrows]
    C --> C3[Hand Trucks]
    C --> C4[Sack Trucks]
    D --> D1[Electric Hand Trucks]
    D --> D2[Power-Assisted Trolleys]
    C1 --> C1a[Platform Dolly]
    C1 --> C1b[Drum Dolly]
    C1 --> C1c[Furniture Dolly]
    C1 --> C1d[Pallet Dolly]
    C2 --> C2a[Single-Wheel Wheelbarrow]
    C2 --> C2b[Two-Wheel Wheelbarrow]
    C2 --> C2c[Tipping Wheelbarrow]
    C3 --> C3a[Two-Wheel Vertical Hand Truck]
    C3 --> C3b[Convertible Hand Truck]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Wheelbarrow Lever Mechanics

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LeverSystem[Wheelbarrow as Class 2 Lever]
        F1[Operator Force F at Handle] -->|Long Arm L1| P[Fulcrum: Front Wheel Axle]
        P -->|Short Arm L2| L[Load W between Fulcrum and Handle]
    end
    Note[Mechanical Advantage MA = L1 / L2] --- LeverSystem
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Storage Decision Tree for Incoming Material

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Incoming Material] --> Q1{Is it hazardous?}
    Q1 -->|No| GenStore[General Warehouse Storage]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{What hazard class?}
    Q2 --> Q2a[Flammable Liquid]
    Q2 --> Q2b[Oxidizer]
    Q2 --> Q2c[Corrosive]
    Q2 --> Q2d[Toxic]
    Q2 --> Q2e[Reactive]
    Q2 --> Q2f[Compressed Gas]
    Q2a --> FC1[Approved Flammable Cabinet per NFPA 30]
    Q2b --> FC2[Dedicated Oxidizer Store, 6 m from flammables]
    Q2c --> FC3[Acid Cabinet SEPARATE from Base Cabinet]
    Q2d --> FC4[Locked, Ventilated, Signed Toxics Store]
    Q2e --> FC5[Dry Store, Sealed Containers, No Water Sources]
    Q2f --> FC6[Upright, Chained, Capped, Separate Oxidizer vs Fuel Gases]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: NFPA 704 Diamond (Hazard Rating Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    NFPA[NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond] --> Top[Blue: Health Hazard 0-4]
    NFPA --> Left[Red: Flammability Hazard 0-4]
    NFPA --> Right[Yellow: Reactivity Instability 0-4]
    NFPA --> Bottom[White: Special Hazard Symbol]
    Top --> Top0[0: No Hazard]
    Top --> Top1[1: Slight]
    Top --> Top2[2: Moderate]
    Top --> Top3[3: Serious]
    Top --> Top4[4: Deadly]
    Left --> L0[0: Will not burn]
    Left --> L4[4: Flash point below 22.8 C]
    Right --> R4[4: May detonate]
    Bottom --> B1[OX: Oxidizer]
    Bottom --> B2[W: Water Reactive]
    Bottom --> B3[SA: Simple Asphyxiant]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hazardous Storage Cell

```mermaid
graph TD
    HSC[Hazardous Material Storage Cell] --> S1[1 Structural Shell: Fire-Rated Walls 2 hr]
    HSC --> S2[2 Ventilation: Mechanical 6 Air Changes per Hour]
    HSC --> S3[3 Containment: 110 percent Sump]
    HSC --> S4[4 Segregation: Class-Based Shelving]
    HSC --> S5[5 Detection: Gas and Fire Sensors]
    HSC --> S6[6 Suppression: Class B Extinguisher within 10 m]
    HSC --> S7[7 Signage: NFPA 704 Diamond on Door]
    HSC --> S8[8 Access Control: Locked, Key-Coded Entry]
    HSC --> S9[9 PPE Store: Spill Kit and Respirator Outside Door]
    HSC --> S10[10 Documentation: SDS Folder, Updated Within 3 Months]
```

## 4.6 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Safe Dolly Operation Workflow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant Sup as Supervisor
    participant Dl as Dolly
    participant Ld as Load
    participant Fl as Floor / Path

    Op->>Sup: Request dolly and load dimensions
    Sup-->>Op: Confirm load weight within dolly capacity
    Op->>Ld: Inspect load for sharp edges / leakage
    Op->>Dl: Inspect wheels, brakes, handle
    Op->>Ld: Position load with CG centered
    Op->>Dl: Strap or clamp load securely
    Op->>Fl: Inspect path for obstacles and slope
    Op->>Dl: Push at controlled speed ≤ 1.0 m/s
    Op->>Ld: Unstrap and unload at destination
    Op->>Dl: Return dolly to storage and report
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define a dolly. Differentiate between a platform dolly and a drum dolly in two points.*

**Model Answer:**
A **dolly** is a manually-propelled or powered wheeled device used to transport individual unit loads over short horizontal distances in a plant or warehouse, by converting sliding friction into rolling friction.

| Feature | Platform Dolly | Drum Dolly |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Flat horizontal platform | Circular cradle |
| **Best For** | Boxes, bags, crates, pallets | 200 L cylindrical drums |
| **Wheels** | Usually 4 swivel casters | 3 or 4 casters on ring frame |
| **Load Retention** | None (load sits freely) | Strap or chain retainer required |
| **Capacity Range** | 250–2000 kg | 250–500 kg |

**(3 marks awarded for: definition 1 mk + any two valid differences 1 mk each)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
*State the NFPA 704 hazard rating scale and identify what the four colored quadrants represent.*

**Model Answer:**
The **NFPA 704** "fire diamond" uses a **0 to 4 rating scale**, where **0 = no hazard** and **4 = severe hazard**.

| Quadrant | Color | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Top** | **Blue** | **Health hazard** (toxicity on exposure) |
| **Left** | **Red** | **Flammability** (ease of ignition) |
| **Right** | **Yellow** | **Reactivity / Instability** (ease of decomposition) |
| **Bottom** | **White** | **Special hazards** (OX, W, SA symbols) |

**(3 marks: 1 for scale, 2 for the four quadrants)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, ESE]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the engineering classification of dollies and wheelbarrows. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a single-wheel wheelbarrow as a Class 2 lever, stating the location of the fulcrum, effort, and load.

**(b) [7 marks]** A two-wheel hand truck has a combined mass of 130 kg (load + truck). The combined center of gravity is located 0.40 m horizontally in front of the wheel axle. The operator pushes horizontally at a height of 1.20 m from the ground. Determine:
   (i) the **critical push force** at which the truck will tip forward,
   (ii) whether the design is **ergonomically safe** as per NIOSH (F_max = 230 N),
   (iii) the **maximum permissible combined mass** to bring the critical push force down to 230 N.

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Classification (4 marks):**

**Dollies** are classified as:
1. **By load orientation** — platform dollies, drum dollies, furniture dollies, pallet dollies, cylinder dollies.
2. **By power source** — manual dollies, powered (motorized) dollies, semi-powered (electric-assist) dollies.

**Wheelbarrows** are classified as:
1. **Single-wheel** (high maneuverability, low stability)
2. **Two-wheel** (high stability, lower maneuverability)
3. **Tipping** (hinged tray for dump discharge)
4. **Motorized** (engine-driven for construction sites)

**Working as a Class 2 Lever (3 marks):**
A single-wheel wheelbarrow operates as a **Class 2 lever**:
- **Fulcrum (F):** the front wheel axle
- **Load (W):** the material in the tray, located between the fulcrum and the operator
- **Effort (E):** the upward force applied by the operator's hands at the rear handles

**Mechanical Advantage:**

$$M_A = \frac{\text{Effort Arm } (L_1)}{\text{Load Arm } (L_2)}$$

Typical $L_1$ ≈ 1.0–1.4 m, $L_2$ ≈ 0.4–0.6 m, giving $M_A \approx 2$ to $3.5$. This means a 100 kg load feels like 30–50 kg to the operator.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

**Given:**
- $W = 130 \cdot 9.81 = 1275.3 \text{ N}$
- $d_{cg} = 0.40 \text{ m}$
- $h_{push} = 1.20 \text{ m}$

**(i) Critical push force** (1 mark derivation + 1 mark substitution):

At the tipping threshold, the overturning moment equals the restoring moment:

$$F_{push,crit} \cdot h_{push} = W \cdot d_{cg}$$

$$F_{push,crit} = \frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{h_{push}} = \frac{1275.3 \cdot 0.40}{1.20} = 425.10 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Ergonomic check** (1 mark reasoning):

Since $F_{push,crit} = 425.10 \text{ N} > 230 \text{ N}$ (NIOSH limit), the design is **NOT ergonomically safe**. The operator is capable of applying a force that tips the load.

**(iii) Maximum permissible mass** (2 marks):

Set $F_{push,crit} = 230 \text{ N}$ and solve for $W_{max}$:

$$W_{max} = \frac{F_{push,crit} \cdot h_{push}}{d_{cg}} = \frac{230 \cdot 1.20}{0.40} = 690 \text{ N}$$

$$m_{max} = \frac{W_{max}}{g} = \frac{690}{9.81} = 70.34 \text{ kg}$$

**Conclusion:** The combined mass of load + truck must not exceed **70.3 kg** to remain ergonomically safe under the stated CG location. **The current 130 kg load is unsafe.** Recommended actions: reduce load, shift load backward to reduce $d_{cg}$, or add a wheel brake.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 1, ESE]**
**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the engineering criteria for the **storage of hazardous materials**. Discuss the **segregation matrix** for the following pairs, stating the hazard and the storage rule for each:
   (i) Flammable liquids and oxidizers
   (ii) Acids and cyanides
   (iii) Acids and bases
   (iv) Compressed oxygen and acetylene

**(b) [7 marks]** A chemical warehouse stores four 200 L drums of sulfuric acid (density 1840 kg/m³) on a flat floor. Compute:
   (i) the **mass** of the acid in one drum,
   (ii) the **floor contact pressure** under one drum if the drum base diameter is 580 mm,
   (iii) the **required secondary containment volume** per the 110 % rule,
   (iv) the **sump depth** if the sump is 1.5 m × 2.0 m.

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Engineering Criteria for Hazardous Material Storage (3 marks):**
1. **Segregation by chemical class** (acids, bases, oxidizers, flammables, reactives, toxics).
2. **Containment** — secondary containment equal to 110 % of the largest container.
3. **Ventilation** — minimum 6 air changes per hour for flammable stores.
4. **Fire rating** — walls and doors fire-rated for at least 2 hours.
5. **Access and egress** — 1 m aisle minimum, emergency exits unobstructed.
6. **Signage** — NFPA 704 diamond, hazard class labels, "No Smoking" signs.
7. **SDS availability** — within 3 months of issue date.
8. **PPE** — spill kit, respirator, eye-wash within 10 m of the store.

**Segregation Matrix (4 marks):**

| Pair | Hazard | Storage Rule |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Flammables + Oxidizers | Oxidizer intensifies fire; can cause spontaneous ignition | Store in **separate fire compartments**; minimum **6 m separation** or **1-hour fire wall** |
| (ii) Acids + Cyanides | Mixing releases **HCN gas** (lethal) | Store in **physically separate cabinets** with separate ventilation; never on the same shelf |
| (iii) Acids + Bases | Exothermic neutralization; spattering; vapor release | Store in **separate cabinets**; acids on lower shelves; bases above |
| (iv) O₂ + C₂H₂ (Acetylene) | If mixed, detonable mixture; backfire risk | Store in **separate gas rooms**; minimum **6 m separation** or **fire-rated partition**; chained upright |

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

**Given:**
- $V_{drum} = 200 \text{ L} = 0.200 \text{ m}^3$
- $\rho_{acid} = 1840 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D_{drum} = 580 \text{ mm} = 0.580 \text{ m}$
- Sump dimensions: $1.5 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m}$

**(i) Mass of acid in one drum** (1 mark):

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 1840 \cdot 0.200 = 368 \text{ kg}$$

**(ii) Floor contact pressure** (2 marks):

$$A_{drum} = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.580^2}{4} = 0.2642 \text{ m}^2$$

$$W = m \cdot g = 368 \cdot 9.81 = 3610 \text{ N}$$

$$p = \frac{W}{A_{drum}} = \frac{3610}{0.2642} = 13664 \text{ Pa} \approx 13.66 \text{ kPa}$$

**Note:** This is well below the typical concrete floor allowable of **≥ 50 kPa**, so the floor is adequate.

**(iii) Required secondary containment** (2 marks):

$$V_{sump} = 1.10 \cdot V_{largest} = 1.10 \cdot 200 = 220 \text{ L} = 0.220 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iv) Sump depth** (2 marks):

$$A_{sump} = 1.5 \cdot 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ m}^2$$

$$h_{sump} = \frac{V_{sump}}{A_{sump}} = \frac{0.220}{3.0} = 0.0733 \text{ m} \approx 74 \text{ mm}$$

Add a 25 mm safety margin: $h_{design} = 74 + 25 = 99 \text{ mm} \approx 100 \text{ mm}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (Costing Students 2–4 Marks Each Year):**
> 1. **Forgetting the dolly's own mass** in tipping calculations. The combined $W$ is **load + dolly**, not just the load.
> 2. **Confusing $F_{push}$ with $F_{lift}$**. In tipping, $F_{push}$ is horizontal; in wheelbarrow lever analysis, the handle force is **vertical**. Mixing them is a direct zero.
> 3. **Using $\mu$ (sliding) instead of $C_{rr}$ (rolling)** — these differ by a factor of 100–300. Wrong coefficient = wrong force by two orders of magnitude.
> 4. **Ignoring the 110 % secondary containment rule** and using 100 % exactly. The regulatory minimum is 110 %, with a 25 mm freeboard engineering margin recommended.
> 5. **Not stating units** in intermediate steps. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit on a numerical.
> 6. **Storing oxidizers next to flammables** in diagrams — this is an automatic zero in any segregation question.
> 7. **Neglecting the NFPA 704 quadrant colors** — writing only the rating without the color (Blue, Red, Yellow, White) costs 0.5 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Dolly:** low-profile wheeled platform; load above wheels; pushed/pulled horizontally.
- **Wheelbarrow:** single/double front wheel; load suspended over the wheel; lifted at the rear handle.
- **Wheelbarrow is a Class 2 lever:** fulcrum = front wheel axle, effort = handle, load = tray contents.
- **Rolling resistance reduces push force by up to ~99 %** vs. sliding friction ($C_{rr} \ll \mu_k$).
- **Tipping Index:** $T_i = \frac{W \cdot d_{cg}}{F \cdot h_{push}}$; safe if **< 1**.
- **NIOSH continuous push-force limit:** **≤ 230 N**.
- **NFPA 704 scale:** **0 (none) to 4 (severe)**; colors **Blue (Health), Red (Flammability), Yellow (Reactivity), White (Special)**.
- **Secondary containment rule:** **110 % of the largest container volume** + 25 mm freeboard.
- **Storage separation rule for oxidizers vs. flammables:** minimum **6 m** OR **1-hour fire-rated wall**.
- **Acids must never be stored with cyanides** (HCN gas) or with **bases** (exothermic reaction).
- **Compressed O₂ must never be stored with C₂H₂** (acetylene) — detonable mixture.
- **Water-reactive metals (Na, Li)** must be stored in **dry, sealed** containers, away from any water source.
- **Standard pallet sizes:** 1200 × 1000 mm (Euro) / 1219 × 1016 mm (ISO).
- **Maximum manual push speed:** **≤ 1.0 m/s** (≈ 3.6 km/h).
- **Always use the combined mass (load + dolly)** in tipping and pressure calculations — never the load alone.
- **Always include a strap, chain, or clamp** when transporting cylindrical loads (drums, gas cylinders).
- **Governing standards to cite in answers:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106, 1910.120, 1910.178; NFPA 30, NFPA 704.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Safety in Material Handling**, *Liquids* refer to a broad classification of bulk and packaged fluid materials whose safe storage, transfer, and processing require specialized engineering controls. These include **flammable liquids** (flash point below 37.8 °C), **combustible liquids** (flash point at or above 37.8 °C), **corrosive liquids** (acids and alkalis), **toxic liquids**, and **reactive liquids** (oxidizers, water-reactives). The discipline covers the entire operational chain — receipt, storage, dispensing, transfer, and disposal — governed by standards such as **NFPA 30** (Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code), **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106**, and **IS 1446** (Indian Standard for Classification of Dangerous Goods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 1 — Liquids):**
> The study of liquids under *Safety in Material Handling* emphasizes the **identification of hazards**, **selection of storage systems**, **understanding of fluid dynamics during transfer**, and **mitigation of physical and chemical risks** such as fire, explosion, toxic exposure, and environmental contamination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Think of liquids like **water balloons in a delivery truck** — except instead of just water, they might contain petrol, sulfuric acid, or acetone. Each one behaves differently: some evaporate into explosive vapours the moment they touch air (like petrol), some eat through metal (like acid), and some sit quietly unless disturbed. The "safety engineer" is the person who decides *how* the truck is built, *where* the balloons are stored, *how fast* they can be moved, and *what protective gear* the loader must wear. Liquids have no fixed shape — they always go where gravity tells them to — so we have to think in terms of **containment, pressure, vapour, and flow**, not just weight and stacking.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

The following constants and reference values are mandatory recall items for KTU examinations:

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of water at 4 °C:** $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific gravity (SG):** dimensionless ratio $\rho_{\text{liquid}} / \rho_{\text{water}}$
- **1 atm** = $101.325 \text{ kPa}$ = $1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- **Flash Point classifications (NFPA 30):**
  * **Class IA** — Flash point below 22.8 °C **and** boiling point below 37.8 °C
  * **Class IB** — Flash point below 22.8 °C **and** boiling point at or above 37.8 °C
  * **Class IC** — Flash point 22.8 °C – 37.8 °C
  * **Class II** — Flash point 37.8 °C – 60 °C (Combustible)
  * **Class IIIA** — Flash point 60 °C – 93.3 °C
  * **Class IIIB** — Flash point above 93.3 °C

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Liquids" Deserve a Dedicated Module:**
> Liquids are the **most frequently transferred hazardous material** in any process industry (oil & gas, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, paints, food). Their unique combination of **fluidity, vapour generation, and pressurization** makes them the single largest contributor to industrial fire incidents. Globally, approximately **33% of major process-industry fires** involve flammable liquids (per API and CSB statistics).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Liquid Safety Engineering

The safe handling of liquids rests on **four interconnected engineering pillars**, which examiners regularly test through short conceptual questions:

### Pillar A — Hydrostatic Pressure Behaviour
Liquids generate pressure based on **depth**, not on the volume or shape of the container. This is the *Pascal* principle applied to a static column.

- The hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ is given by:

$$P = \rho g h$$

- **Why this matters for safety:** A 10 m tall fuel-storage tank exerts the same bottom pressure as any other 10 m tank, regardless of whether it holds 100 L or 100,000 L. **Wall thickness, weld integrity, and foundation design must therefore be calculated for maximum credible depth**, not maximum volume.

### Pillar B — Vapour Pressure and Flash Point
At any temperature, every volatile liquid emits vapour above its surface. The equilibrium pressure of this vapour is the **vapour pressure**. When the local concentration of vapour equals its **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)**, the mixture becomes ignitable.

- A liquid's **flash point** is the lowest temperature at which it gives off enough vapour to form an ignitable mixture near its surface.
- A liquid's **fire point** is the temperature at which sustained combustion occurs.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** is the temperature at which the liquid ignites spontaneously **without an external spark**.

### Pillar C — Static Electricity Generation
Whenever a liquid moves through a pipe, pours through air, or is filtered, it picks up **electrostatic charge**. If this charge accumulates faster than it dissipates, the resulting discharge can ignite flammable vapours. **Bonding and grounding** of all transfer equipment is therefore non-negotiable.

### Pillar D — Compatibility and Segregation
Different liquids can react violently when mixed — for example, oxidizing acids contacting organic solvents, or water contacting **sodium metal**. Liquid storage areas must implement **segregation matrices** based on chemical compatibility, as codified in the **Hazardous Materials Identification System (HMIS)** and **GHS classification**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the units of every variable. KTU examiners routinely award only partial credit when units are missing or mismatched.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Standard SI Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure | $P = \rho g h$ | $\rho$ = density, $g$ = gravity, $h$ = depth | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | Gauge pressure | $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$ | absolute and atmospheric pressures | Pa |
| 3 | Hydrostatic force on a vertical wall | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g h^{2} w$ | $w$ = width of wetted wall | N |
| 4 | Centroid of pressure depth | $h_{cp} = \tfrac{2}{3} h$ | for a rectangular wall | m |
| 5 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $A$ = cross-section, $v$ = velocity | m³/s |
| 6 | Tank filling time | $t = V / Q$ | $V$ = tank volume, $Q$ = flow rate | s |
| 7 | Reynolds number | $Re = \tfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $D$ = pipe diameter, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 8 | Viscosity (kinematic) | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | ratio of dynamic to density | m²/s |
| 9 | Static charge relaxation time | $\tau = \varepsilon \varepsilon_{0} \rho$ | $\varepsilon$ = permittivity, $\rho$ = resistivity | s |
| 10 | Clausius–Clapeyron (vapour pressure vs. T) | $\ln P = -\tfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T} + C$ | $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ = enthalpy of vaporization | Pa |
| 11 | Raoult's Law | $P_{i} = x_{i} P_{i}^{\star}$ | $x_{i}$ = mole fraction, $P_{i}^{\star}$ = pure vapour pressure | Pa |
| 12 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho Q = \rho A v$ | — | kg/s |
| 13 | Buoyancy (Archimedes) | $F_b = \rho_{\text{fluid}} g V_{\text{displaced}}$ | — | N |
| 14 | Vent sizing for tank | $A_{\text{vent}} = \tfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{K \cdot v_{\text{out}}}$ | $K$ = discharge coefficient | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions and Validity:**
> - $P = \rho g h$ is valid **only for static, incompressible fluids**.
> - The vertical-wall force formula assumes the wall is **fully wetted** from free surface to depth $h$.
> - The Reynolds number transitions: laminar $Re < 2300$, transitional $2300 \le Re \le 4000$, turbulent $Re > 4000$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not just academic — they directly determine:

1. **Tank wall thickness** during design (per ASME BPVC Section VIII).
2. **Sizing of pressure-relief devices** (per API 2000).
3. **Sizing of dikes and bund walls** for spill containment (typically 110% of the largest tank's volume).
4. **Grounding conductor sizing** for static-electricity dissipation.
5. **Determination of fire-protection foam quantity** based on tank surface area $A = \pi r^{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Force on a Vertical Rectangular Tank Wall

We will derive the **total hydrostatic force** and the **depth of the centre of pressure** for a rectangular tank wall of width $w$ submerged to depth $h$, measured from the free liquid surface.

### Step 1 — Pressure at an elemental strip
Consider a thin horizontal strip on the wall, located at depth $y$ below the free surface, of thickness $dy$ and width $w$.

The pressure at depth $y$ is:
$$P(y) = \rho g y$$

### Step 2 — Force on the elemental strip
The differential force on this strip is:
$$dF = P(y) \cdot dA = \rho g y \cdot (w \, dy)$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from surface ($y = 0$) to bottom ($y = h$)
$$F = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g w y \, dy = \rho g w \left[ \tfrac{y^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{h}$$

$$F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g w h^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Locate the centre of pressure (depth where resultant acts)
Moment of the differential force about the free surface:
$$dM = y \, dF = \rho g w y^{2} \, dy$$

Total moment:
$$M = \int_{0}^{h} \rho g w y^{2} \, dy = \rho g w \left[ \tfrac{y^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{h} = \tfrac{1}{3} \rho g w h^{3}$$

Centroid depth of the pressure distribution (by definition):
$$h_{cp} = \frac{M}{F} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{3} \rho g w h^{3}}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho g w h^{2}} = \tfrac{2 h}{3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The centre of pressure lies at **two-thirds** of the depth, *not* at the geometric centre of the wall. This is because pressure is *not uniform* — it grows linearly with depth, so the "average" load sits lower than the geometric middle. This fact is critical when designing **bolted connections and stiffener rings** on tank walls.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number and Flow Regime Determination

### Step 1 — Definition
The Reynolds number is the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in a flowing fluid:

$$Re = \frac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}} = \frac{\rho v^{2} D^{2}}{\mu v D} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

### Step 2 — Worked numerical example
**Given:** Diesel oil ($\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 0.0035 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) flows through a pipe of diameter $D = 0.05 \text{ m}$ at velocity $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$. Determine the flow regime.

$$Re = \frac{(850)(2.5)(0.05)}{0.0035}$$

$$Re = \frac{106.25}{0.0035} = 30357$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**. This matters because turbulent flow produces **higher pressure drops** (and thus requires thicker pipe walls and stronger pumps) and causes **greater electrostatic charge generation**.

### Step 3 — Why turbulent flow matters for safety
- Static charge generation rate $\propto v^{1.75}$ for laminar, $\propto v^{2}$ for turbulent.
- Recommended **transfer velocity** for flammable liquids is **less than 7 m/s** to limit static accumulation (API RP 2003).
- Lower the velocity: $v_{\max} = \sqrt{0.5 \cdot \text{Limit}}$ where limit is typically $25 \text{ (m/s)}^{2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Charge Relaxation and Safe Wait Time

### Step 1 — Governing differential equation
When liquid moves through a pipe, charge accumulates at a rate $J$. It dissipates through the bulk resistivity $\rho_e$ of the liquid. The decay follows:

$$Q(t) = Q_{0} \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau = \varepsilon \varepsilon_{0} \rho_{e}$ is the **relaxation time constant**.

### Step 2 — Numerical example
**Given:** Toluene ($\varepsilon = 2.4$, $\rho_{e} = 10^{10} \, \Omega \cdot \text{m}$).

$$\tau = (2.4)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(10^{10}) = 0.21 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Safe wait time
After stopping flow, one waits **5 time constants** to allow charge to decay below 1% of original:

$$t_{\text{safe}} = 5\tau = 5(0.21) = 1.07 \text{ s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> For low-conductivity liquids (e.g., hexane, $\rho_e \approx 10^{14} \, \Omega\cdot\text{m}$), $\tau$ can exceed **100 seconds**, meaning operators must wait over **8 minutes** after shutdown before opening inspection ports or sampling. **This is a frequent source of KTU exam questions.**

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Liquid Hazard Classification

| Hazard Indicator | Low Risk | Medium Risk | High Risk | Extreme Risk |
|------------------|----------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| Flash Point (°C) | $>93.3$ | $60 - 93.3$ | $22.8 - 60$ | $<22.8$ |
| Vapour Pressure (kPa @ 38 °C) | $<1$ | $1 - 15$ | $15 - 70$ | $>70$ |
| Reactivity with Water | None | Mild | Vigorous | Explosive |
| Toxicity (LD₅₀ mg/kg) | $>5000$ | $500 - 5000$ | $50 - 500$ | $<50$ |
| Storage Mandate | Open drums | Closed drum | Bunded tank | Nitrogen-blanketed sealed tank |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flammable Liquid Storage Facility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INCOMING TANKER TRUCK"] --> nodeB["GROUNDING AND BONDING STATION"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TRANSFER PUMP WITH VAPOUR RECOVERY"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PIPELINE MANIFOLD WITH BLOCK VALVES"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["FIRE PROOF BULK STORAGE TANK WITH DIKE BUND"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["DAY TANK FOR DAILY DISPENSING"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["DISPENSING PUMP WITH FLOW METER"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["SAFETY DISPENSING CABINET WITH EXPLOSION PROOF WIRING"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["WASTE COLLECTION DRUM WITH LABELING"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["NITROGEN BLANKETING SYSTEM FOR VAPOUR SPACE"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["PRESSURE VACUUM RELIEF VALVE"]
    nodeE --> nodeL["FOAM FIRE SUPPRESSION CHAMBER"]
    nodeE --> nodeM["EMERGENCY SHUTOFF AND SPILL CONTAINMENT SUMP"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> Each block represents a *safety barrier* in the liquid-handling chain. Notice the redundant safeguards: the dike bund around the bulk tank provides tertiary containment; the nitrogen blanketing prevents vapour ignition; the PV valve prevents tank rupture from thermal expansion or pumping over-pressure. This is the **layered protection model (LOPA)** that KTU examiners expect students to describe in 14-mark answers.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Selecting Storage Method by Hazard Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["START: IDENTIFY LIQUID HAZARD CLASS"] --> nodeQ1{"IS FLASH POINT BELOW 37.8 C"}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeQ2{"IS VAPOUR DENSITY GREATER THAN 1"}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeA1["GROUND LEVEL VENTED DRUM CABINET WITH MECHANICAL VENTILATION"]
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeA2["NITROGEN BLANKETED ABOVE GROUND TANK WITH FLAME ARRESTORS"]
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ3{"IS IT CORROSIVE OR REACTIVE"}
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeA3["DOUBLE WALLED TANK WITH LEAK DETECTION IN CHEMICALLY RESISTANT BUND"]
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeA4["STANDARD BUNDED ABOVE GROUND TANK OR FIRE RATED DRUM CABINET"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeEnd["DOCUMENT INVENTORY AND TRAIN OPERATORS"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeEnd
    nodeA3 --> nodeEnd
    nodeA4 --> nodeEnd
```

## 4.3 Cause–Effect Chain for Static Electricity Ignition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["LIQUID FLOW THROUGH PIPE OR FILTER"] --> node2["CHARGE SEPARATION AT INTERFACE"]
    node2 --> node3["CHARGE ACCUMULATION IN BULK LIQUID"]
    node3 --> node4{"GROUNDING INTACT"}
    node4 -- NO --> node5["POTENTIAL RISES ABOVE 30 KV"]
    node4 -- YES --> node6["CHARGE DISSIPATES SAFELY TO EARTH"]
    node5 --> node7["BRUSH DISCHARGE NEAR LIQUID SURFACE"]
    node7 --> node8["VAPOUR IGNITION AND TANK FIRE"]
    node6 --> node9["SAFE TRANSFER COMPLETED"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Lesson from the Diagram:** A missing ground wire is the **single most common root cause** of tank-truck fires during flammable-liquid transfer. The diagram makes it clear that **the only path to safety** is uninterrupted electrical continuity from the truck to the receiving tank to the earth pit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **Define "Flash Point" of a flammable liquid. Why is it the single most important property for classifying liquid storage hazards?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Flash point is the *lowest temperature* at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an **ignitable mixture** with air near its surface, evidenced by a flash when an ignition source is applied.
- **Class IA example (1 mark):** For example, **n-hexane** has a flash point of approximately **−22 °C**, meaning it is ignitable even in cold weather storage.
- **Why critical for classification (1 mark):** Flash point determines the **NFPA 30 hazard class**, which in turn dictates storage type, container size limits, electrical zoning, and ventilation requirements. A lower flash point means greater ignition probability, requiring more stringent engineered safeguards.

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Explain the difference between "Bonding" and "Grounding" in static-electricity control during liquid transfer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Bonding (1 mark):** Connecting two conductive objects (e.g., the truck and the receiving tank) with a wire to ensure they are at the *same electrical potential*, preventing sparks *between them*.
- **Grounding (1 mark):** Connecting one of those objects to the *earth* through a low-resistance path (typically less than **10 Ω**) so accumulated static charge leaks away safely.
- **Distinction (1 mark):** Bonding equalises potential *between* two objects; grounding equalises potential *between* an object and the earth. For liquid transfer, **both are mandatory** — bond first, then ground.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students frequently *describe* hazards but fail to *quantify* them. **Always include numerical estimates** (pressure, flow rate, charge relaxation time, etc.) wherever the data permits.
> - Avoid generic statements like "use proper PPE." Examiners want **specific PPE** (e.g., "nitrile gloves for hydrocarbon splash, splash goggles, and self-contained breathing apparatus for vapour concentrations above 10% LFL").
> - For 7-mark sub-questions, aim for **at least 4 distinct points** plus a labelled diagram or formula.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
> **(a)** Derive an expression for the total hydrostatic force acting on a vertical rectangular tank wall of width $w$ submerged to a depth $h$, and locate the centre of pressure. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A cylindrical fuel-storage tank of diameter **6 m** and height **10 m** is filled with petrol (density **740 kg/m³**, flash point **−43 °C**). Compute (i) the hydrostatic force on the bottom plate, and (ii) the maximum permissible fill height if the tank wall is rated for **180 kPa** hydrostatic pressure. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
The full derivation as shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes:
1. Pressure on elemental strip: $P(y) = \rho g y$ — 1 mark.
2. Force on elemental strip: $dF = \rho g w y \, dy$ — 1 mark.
3. Total force integral and evaluation: $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g w h^{2}$ — 2 marks.
4. Moment about free surface: $M = \tfrac{1}{3} \rho g w h^{3}$ — 1 mark.
5. Centre of pressure: $h_{cp} = \tfrac{2h}{3}$ — 2 marks.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Sub-part (i) — Hydrostatic force on bottom plate (3 marks):**
The pressure at the bottom of a 10 m column:

$$P = \rho g h = (740)(9.81)(10) = 72594 \text{ Pa} \approx 72.6 \text{ kPa}$$

Bottom area of cylindrical tank:

$$A = \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \tfrac{\pi (6)^{2}}{4} = 28.27 \text{ m}^{2}$$

Total force on bottom plate:

$$F = P \cdot A = 72594 \times 28.27 = 2.052 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \approx 2052 \text{ kN}$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Maximum permissible fill height (4 marks):**
Given $P_{\max} = 180 \text{ kPa} = 180000 \text{ Pa}$:

$$h_{\max} = \frac{P_{\max}}{\rho g} = \frac{180000}{(740)(9.81)} = 24.8 \text{ m}$$

Since the tank is only **10 m tall**, the entire tank can be safely filled. **Note:** This is why real tanks are rated by *over-pressure scenarios* (e.g., blocked vent, thermal expansion) rather than by hydrostatic pressure alone.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain with a flowchart how static electricity is generated during the transfer of a low-conductivity flammable liquid, and list four preventive measures. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A tank containing toluene (density **867 kg/m³**, viscosity **0.0006 Pa·s**) is emptied through a **50 mm** diameter pipe at a velocity of **3.5 m/s**. Determine (i) the flow regime (Reynolds number), and (ii) whether the velocity exceeds the API RP 2003 recommended limit for static control. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Static Electricity Generation (7 Marks):**
Refer to the **cause–effect diagram** in Section 4.3 of these notes.

1. **Generation mechanism (2 marks):** As liquid flows through the pipe, charge separation occurs at the liquid–pipe interface. The flowing liquid carries away negative charges, leaving the pipe positively charged. Charge accumulates in the receiving tank.
2. **Flowchart logic (2 marks):** Liquid flow → charge separation → bulk accumulation → ground integrity check → either safe dissipation OR potential rise to ≥ 30 kV → brush discharge → vapour ignition → fire.
3. **Four preventive measures (3 marks — 1 mark each minus 1 for missing any):**
   - **Bonding** of truck to receiving tank with a copper braid.
   - **Grounding** to an earth pit of resistance less than **10 Ω**.
   - **Velocity limitation** to **less than 7 m/s** for the initial fill (to avoid charge peak).
   - **Relaxation wait time** of at least **5τ** (typically 30 seconds for toluene, 8 minutes for hexane) after flow stops before sampling or opening manholes.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Sub-part (i) — Reynolds number (3 marks):**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{(867)(3.5)(0.05)}{0.0006}$$

$$Re = \frac{151.725}{0.0006} = 252875$$

**Sub-part (ii) — Velocity check (4 marks):**
Since $Re = 252875 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**. API RP 2003 recommends an initial filling velocity **less than 1 m/s** until the submerged fill pipe is covered, and a maximum sustained velocity of **7 m/s** thereafter.

Since $v = 3.5 \text{ m/s} < 7 \text{ m/s}$, the sustained transfer is **within** the recommended limit. **However**, during initial fill, the operator must throttle the pump to less than 1 m/s until the fill pipe outlet is **at least 150 mm below the liquid surface**, otherwise a charge-generating splash occurs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students will simply write "velocity is OK" without examining the *initial fill condition*. KTU examiners expect the **two-stage analysis**: initial fill ≤ 1 m/s, sustained flow ≤ 7 m/s.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-day KTU preparation):**

- [ ] **Flash point < 37.8 °C** = Flammable; **≥ 37.8 °C** = Combustible (NFPA 30 rule).
- [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure** depends on depth, not on volume: $P = \rho g h$.
- [ ] **Total force on a vertical wall:** $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g w h^{2}$; **centroid of pressure** at $\tfrac{2h}{3}$ from the surface.
- [ ] **Reynolds number thresholds:** Laminar $Re < 2300$, Turbulent $Re > 4000$.
- [ ] **Static-charge relaxation time** $\tau = \varepsilon \varepsilon_{0} \rho_{e}$; safe wait = **5τ**.
- [ ] **Bonding** equalises potential *between* objects; **grounding** equalises *with earth*.
- [ ] **Maximum transfer velocity** for flammable liquids: **7 m/s** sustained, **1 m/s** during initial fill (API RP 2003).
- [ ] **Spill containment dike** capacity must be **110%** of the largest single tank.
- [ ] **Pressure-vacuum relief valves** required on all atmospheric and low-pressure storage tanks.
- [ ] **Nitrogen blanketing** is mandatory for Class IA liquids to suppress vapour space ignition.
- [ ] **Foam suppression** chambers are sized based on tank surface area: $A = \pi r^{2}$.
- [ ] **Reactivity matrix** must be consulted before co-storage — never store oxidizers with organics.
- [ ] **Respirator selection** for vapour concentrations above **10% LFL** requires SCBA, not air-purifying respirators.
- [ ] **Earth-pit resistance** for static grounding should be **less than 10 Ω**, tested annually.
- [ ] **Vapour-recovery systems** reduce emissions by 95–99% during truck loading and are required under **IS 1446** and **EPA norms**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solids - Storage and Handling of Cryogenic Liquids - Shipping and Receiving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solids - Storage and Handling of Cryogenic Liquids - Shipping and Receiving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cryogenic Liquids** are liquefied gases held at extremely low temperatures, defined universally (per **CGA P-12** and **NFPA 55**) as fluids with **normal boiling points below −150 °C (123 K; −238 °F)** at standard atmospheric pressure of **101.325 kPa (1 atm)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (CO1 – K1 Level):**
> *"A cryogenic liquid is any refrigerant or industrial gas in liquid phase whose boiling temperature is below −150 °C under standard atmospheric conditions, requiring specialised vacuum-insulated containment and strict thermal management."*

### 1.2 Common Cryogenic Liquids — Master Reference Table

| Cryogen | Chemical Symbol | Normal Boiling Point (K) | Normal Boiling Point (°C) | Critical Temperature (K) | Latent Heat of Vaporisation, Lᵥₐₚ (kJ/kg) | Primary Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid Helium | LHe | **4.2** | −268.9 | 5.2 | 20.4 | MRI machines, superconductors |
| Liquid Hydrogen | LH₂ | **20.3** | −252.9 | 33.2 | 445.6 | Rocket fuel, fuel cells |
| Liquid Neon | LNe | **27.1** | −246.0 | 44.4 | 85.7 | High-voltage indicators, lasers |
| Liquid Nitrogen | LN₂ | **77.4** | −195.8 | 126.2 | 199.3 | Cryopreservation, food freezing, inerting |
| Liquid Air | LAIR | ~78–84 | −189 to −195 | 132.6 | ~209 | Industrial gas feed |
| Liquid Fluorine | LF₂ | 85.2 | −188.0 | 144.3 | 172.1 | Rocket oxidiser |
| Liquid Argon | LAr | **87.3** | −185.9 | 150.9 | 161.1 | Welding, metallurgy, lighting |
| Liquid Oxygen | LOX | **90.2** | −183.0 | 154.6 | 213.1 | Medical, steel-making, rockets |
| Liquid Methane | LCH₄ | 111.7 | −161.5 | 190.6 | 510.8 | LNG component |
| LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) | LNG | ~111 | −162.0 | ~190 | ~510 | Industrial/domestic fuel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for Safety Engineers?** Many "gases" used in industry are stored as **cryogenic liquids** because the liquid state is **600–800 times denser** than the gaseous state. This makes storage efficient but introduces catastrophic hazards: rapid pressure rise on heat ingress, embrittlement of structural metals, and asphyxiation.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Thermos Flask Concept

Imagine pouring cold water into an ordinary steel bucket: within hours the water warms to room temperature because steel readily conducts heat. Now pour liquid nitrogen (LN₂) at −196 °C into the same bucket — it would violently boil, splatter, and the steel would become **brittle like glass** within seconds.

The cryogenic industry solved this with the **DEWAR principle** (invented by Sir James Dewar, 1892):

> 🥤 *Picture a sealed double-walled thermos flask: the inner bottle holds your cold drink; the space between the inner and outer walls is **evacuated** (vacuum) so neither conduction nor convection can carry heat inward; the inner wall is **shiny silvered** to bounce back radiant heat.*

**A cryogenic storage vessel IS a giant, code-stamped, ASME-certified thermos flask.** This single mental model explains 90 % of cryogenic storage design.

### 1.4 Why a Separate Module on "Solids & Cryogenics"?

In **Safety in Material Handling**, the three classical states of matter present unique hazards:

- **Solids** — dust explosions, structural collapse of silos, bridging/arching in hoppers.
- **Cryogenic Liquids** — extreme cold, pressure, oxygen enrichment, asphyxiation.
- **Shipping/Receiving** — the *interface* where materials leave the regulated custody of one entity and enter another; the highest-risk node of any supply chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Learning Outcome:** After studying this topic, students must be able to *(a)* classify cryogenic liquids by physical properties, *(b)* identify correct storage vessel types, *(c)* describe handling PPE and engineering controls, and *(d)* execute safe shipping and receiving protocols per Indian and international standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Storage of Cryogenic Liquids — Engineering Hierarchy

Storage vessels are classified by **physical state inside the vessel**:

#### 2.1.1 Atmospheric Dewar (Non-Pressurised Storage)

- Operates at **~101 kPa (slightly above atmospheric)**.
- Vapour space is vented; liquid temperature is essentially the normal boiling point.
- Boil-Off Gas (BOG) is continuously released or captured.
- **Typical use:** LN₂, LAr laboratory dewars (5–500 L).
- **Pressure class:** **0.05–0.5 barg**.

#### 2.1.2 Low-Pressure Bulk Storage Tank

- Operates at **0.5–10 barg**.
- Cylindrical, horizontal or vertical; **vacuum + perlite + MLI** insulation.
- Equipped with **dual Pressure Relief Valves (PRV)** + bursting disc.
- **Typical capacity:** 1,000–100,000 L for LN₂/LOX/LAr.
- **Construction standard:** **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1**.

#### 2.1.3 High-Pressure Cylinder/Cryocylinder

- Operates at **15–35 barg** (e.g., LN₂ for medical/aviation use).
- Filled with porous **zeolite/silica gel getter** to absorb vapour.
- **Standard capacity:** 240–450 L water-volume (liquid).

#### 2.1.4 Cryogenic ISO Tank Container (Intermodal)

- **20 ft or 40 ft ISO frame**, **21,000–30,000 L capacity**.
- Working pressure **≈ 2.2 barg**, test pressure **≈ 4.4 barg**.
- Suitable for road, rail, and sea (with **IMDG Code** compliance).

### 2.2 Cryogenic Liquid Handling — The "8-Layer Defence" Framework

| Layer | Control Type | Examples | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Engineering Design | Passive | Vacuum jacket, MLI, rupture discs | High |
| 2. Process Controls | Active | PRV set-points, alarms, PLC interlocks | High |
| 3. PPE | Behavioural | Cryogenic gloves, face shield, apron | High |
| 4. Ventilation | Environmental | O₂ monitors, mechanical exhaust | Critical |
| 5. Procedural | Administrative | SOPs, permits-to-work, lock-out/tag-out | Medium |
| 6. Training | Competence | Initial + refresher cryogenics certification | Medium |
| 7. Emergency Response | Mitigation | Spill kits, eyewash, O₂ resuscitation | High |
| 8. Medical Surveillance | Health | Pre-employment + annual lung/skin tests | Low |

### 2.3 Hazards of Cryogenic Liquids — The "Seven Deadly Hazards"

1. **Extreme Cold** — Frostbite (skin freezes in <2 s at LN₂ splash).
2. **Asphyxiation** — A 1 L spill of LN₂ vaporises to ≈ **700 L** of N₂ gas, displacing O₂ below the **19.5 %** OSHA IDLH threshold.
3. **Pressure Rise on Trapped Liquid** — Liquid-to-vapour expansion ratio of LN₂ = **1 : 694**.
4. **Oxygen Enrichment** — LOX spills condense atmospheric O₂ on cold surfaces; **any organic + LOX = explosion**.
5. **Materials Embrittlement** — Carbon steel loses ductility below −29 °C; austenitic stainless steel (304L) is mandatory.
6. **Hydrogen Embrittlement** — LH₂ diffuses into metals causing delayed fracture.
7. **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)** — If an external fire weakens a vessel, superheated liquid flashes catastrophically.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Master Reference

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Convention:** All delimiter symbols (for *absolute* or *magnitude*) are written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | Stefan–Boltzmann Radiation Heat Leak | $Q_{rad} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon_{eff} \cdot A \cdot \left( T_{w}^{4} - T_{c}^{4} \right)$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{K}^{-4}$; $\varepsilon_{eff}$ = effective emissivity; $A$ = m²; $T$ = K | $\varepsilon_{eff}$ with 50-layer MLI ≈ **0.01–0.03** |
| F2 | Effective Emissivity with MLI | $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx \dfrac{1}{N+1}$ | $N$ = number of reflective layers | $N = 50 \Rightarrow \varepsilon_{eff} \approx 0.019$ |
| F3 | Boil-Off Mass Rate (BOMR) | $\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{L_{vap}}$ | $L_{vap}$ = latent heat of vaporisation (J/kg) | LN₂ BOMR ≈ **0.1–1.0 %/day** |
| F4 | Theoretical Hold-Up Time | $t_{hold} = \dfrac{m_{liq} \cdot L_{vap}}{Q_{total}}$ | $m_{liq}$ = initial liquid mass (kg); $t$ = seconds | LN₂ ≈ **25–90 days** for bulk |
| F5 | Liquid-to-Vapour Expansion | $V_{gas} = V_{liq} \cdot \rho_{liq} \cdot \dfrac{T_{STP}}{M \cdot P_{STP}} \cdot R$ | M = molar mass; R = 8.314 J/mol·K | LN₂ ratio ≈ **1 : 694** |
| F6 | Critical Pressure (Spherical) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{ult} \cdot t}{D}$ | $\sigma_{ult}$ = ultimate tensile (Pa); $t$ = wall thickness; $D$ = diameter (m) | LOX spheres designed for **1.67 × MAWP** |
| F7 | Critical Pressure (Cylindrical) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult} \cdot t}{r}$ | $r$ = inner radius (m) | Hoop stress governs |
| F8 | Thermal Conductance (Vacuum + MLI) | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{N \cdot k_{s}}{\Delta x} + k_{rad}$ | $k_s$ = spacer conductance; $\Delta x$ = layer thickness | $k_{eff} \approx 10^{-4}\ \text{W/m·K}$ |
| F9 | Reynolds Number (Cryogenic Transfer) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity; $v$ = velocity (m/s) | Laminar if $Re < 2300$ |
| F10 | Cryogenic Storage Pressure (N₂ example) | $P_{tank} = P_{atm} + \rho_{LN2} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $\rho_{LN2} \approx 808\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$; $h$ = liquid depth | ~ **0.5–4 barg** typical |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LNG terminal design** (e.g., Kochi, Dahej) uses F1–F4 to size **BOG re-liquefaction plants**.
- **Medical MRI facilities** use F3–F4 to plan LHe top-up cycles for **1.5-T superconducting magnets**.
- **Rocket launch sites (ISRO, Sriharikota)** use F6–F7 to certify LOX/LH₂ run tanks.
- **Pharmaceutical cold chains** rely on F4 to validate 96-hour LN₂ shipping dewars (MVE, Taylor Wharton).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Boil-Off Rate from a Vacuum-Insulated LN₂ Tank

**Problem Statement:** A vertical LN₂ bulk storage tank has inner-vessel surface area $A = 12\ \text{m}^{2}$, 60 layers of MLI yielding $\varepsilon_{eff} = 0.016$, outer-wall temperature $T_w = 293\ \text{K}$, and cryogen temperature $T_c = 77.4\ \text{K}$. If the latent heat of vaporisation of nitrogen is $L_{vap} = 199.3\ \text{kJ/kg}$, determine the boil-off rate in **kg/day** and the **hold time** if the tank holds $1{,}500\ \text{kg}$ of LN₂.

#### Step 1 — Apply the Stefan–Boltzmann Law for Radiation Heat Influx

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{rad} &= \sigma \cdot \varepsilon_{eff} \cdot A \cdot \left( T_{w}^{4} - T_{c}^{4} \right) \\
        &= (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot (0.016) \cdot (12) \cdot \left( 293^{4} - 77.4^{4} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Evaluate the Fourth-Power Temperature Terms

$$
\begin{aligned}
293^{4} &= (293)^{2} \cdot (293)^{2} \\
       &= 85{,}849 \cdot 85{,}849 \\
       &= 7.37 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
