2. **Web:** Flat or dished plate connecting rim to hub. For very large sheaves, spoked or cut-out web designs reduce weight without sacrificing strength.
3. **Hub:** Central boss containing the bearing bore. Sized to accommodate the bearing with press-fit tolerance.
4. **Bearings:** Anti-friction deep-groove ball bearings (e.g., **6205 ZZ**) or tapered roller bearings for heavy radial loads.
5. **Shaft/Dead Axle:** Either rotating with the sheave (live shaft) or fixed (dead shaft) with sheave rotating around it.

**Functions:** (i) Change direction of rope without slip. (ii) Multiply force via block and tackle arrangements. (iii) Reduce rope wear by providing a smooth contact surface. (iv) Support the suspended load via integrated bearings.

**Part (b) – Sheave Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Minimum sheave diameter (using IS 3177, K = 20):

$$
D_{min} = K \cdot d = 20 \cdot 20 = 400 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply safety margin factor of 1.25:

$$
D_{design} = 1.25 \cdot 400 = 500 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Groove radius (standard empirical formula):

$$
r = 0.55 \cdot d = 0.55 \cdot 20 = 11 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Groove angle selection:

$$
\alpha = 45° \quad (\text{standard for general purpose})
$$

**Step 5:** Rope breaking load check:

$$
\text{Breaking Load} = \sigma_{uts} \cdot A_{rope} = 1800 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 20^2 = 565{,}487 \text{ N} \approx 565.5 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6:** Actual factor of safety:

$$
SF = \dfrac{565.5}{50} = 11.31
$$

Since **11.31 > 5 (required SF)**, the rope is safe. **Final design: Sheave diameter = 500 mm, groove radius = 11 mm, groove angle = 45°.**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction of a hoisting drum. Differentiate between smooth and grooved drums. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric hoist lifts a load of **30 kN** through a height of **8 m**. The wire rope diameter is **18 mm** and the drum diameter is **360 mm**. Determine: (i) Total number of turns on the drum, (ii) Length of the drum barrel, (iii) Torque required on the drum shaft. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) – Hoisting Drum Construction (7 Marks):**

A hoisting drum consists of:

1. **Barrel (Shell):** Central cylinder of forged steel or welded plate construction where the rope is wound. Length is calculated based on lifting height and rope diameter.
2. **Flanges:** Two raised circular plates welded or bolted at each end of the barrel to retain the rope.
3. **Shaft:** Heavy forged steel shaft (typically **EN8 / 45C8 grade**) that transfers torque from the gearbox to the drum.
4. **Coupling:** Rigid or flexible coupling connecting the drum shaft to the gearbox.
5. **Bearings:** Heavy-duty plummer blocks or pillow blocks (e.g., **SN 518 series**) supporting the shaft.

**Smooth vs Grooved Drums:**

| Feature | Smooth Drum | Grooved Drum |
|---|---|---|
| Surface | Plain cylindrical barrel | Helical grooves machined |
| Rope Cross-over | Frequent (causes damage) | Prevented by grooves |
| Rope Life | Lower | Higher (40% more) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (machining cost) |
| Application | Light duty, infrequent use | Heavy duty, continuous service |

**Part (b) – Numerical Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Total Number of Drum Turns**

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{\pi \cdot D} + n = \dfrac{8000}{\pi \cdot 360} + 2
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{8000}{1130.97} + 2 = 7.07 + 2 \approx 9.07
$$

Rounding up: **N = 10 turns**

**Step 2: Length of Drum Barrel**

Including dead turns (n = 2) and flange clearances (4d):

$$
L = (N + n + 4) \cdot d = (10 + 2 + 4) \cdot 18
$$

$$
L = 16 \cdot 18 = 288 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Torque on Drum Shaft**

$$
T = W \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = 30{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{360}{2}
$$

$$
T = 30{,}000 \cdot 180 = 5{,}400{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 5.4 \text{ kN·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Using the rope diameter instead of the drum diameter to calculate torque. Always use **D/2** as the moment arm.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the dead turns in the barrel length calculation. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Not adding the flange clearance margin ($+4d$). Examiners specifically look for this.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing sheave ratio (D/d = 20) with drum ratio (D/d = 15–20). They are similar but different in application.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Points:**

- **Sheave Function:** Redirects rope direction; passive component.
- **Drum Function:** Stores and pays-out rope; active motor-driven component.
- **Minimum Sheave Ratio:** **D/d ≥ 20** (IS 3177 / ASME B30.5).
- **Minimum Drum Ratio:** **D/d ≥ 15** (lower bound for general use).
- **Standard Groove Radius:** **r = 0.55 d** (sheaves only).
- **Standard Groove Angle:** **35° to 45°** (45° most common).
- **Dead Turns:** **1.5 to 2 turns** minimum, mandatory for all hoisting drums.
- **Barrel Length Formula:** $L = (N + n + 4) \cdot d$.
- **Number of Turns:** $N = H / (\pi D) + n$.
- **Torque on Shaft:** $T = W \cdot D/2$.
- **Combined Stress:** Always use the von Mises equivalent for drum shafts.
- **Material Selection:** Cast steel for sheaves, forged steel for drum shafts.
- **Inspection:** Monthly for sheaves, weekly for drums under heavy duty.
- **Safety Factor on Rope:** **5 to 6** for cranes and hoists.
- **Grooved drums > Smooth drums** for rope life and safety.
- **Flange clearance:** Always provide **2d** clearance on each flange.

**Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"Sheaves STEER, Drums STORE"* — Sheaves redirect (steer) rope, Drums store (wound) rope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overloading** is a hazardous condition in material handling operations where the magnitude of the applied load (static, dynamic, or shock) on a machine, structure, lifting gear, or transport vehicle **exceeds its rated Safe Working Load (SWL)** or **Working Load Limit (WLL)**, thereby compromising structural integrity, operational stability, and the safety of personnel and property.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **IS 807**, **IS 3177**, **IS 3938**, and **Factories Act 1948 (Section 28 & 32)**:
- **SWL (Safe Working Load)** = Maximum load that a piece of equipment may **safely lift, suspend, or carry** under specified service conditions, as certified by the manufacturer or a competent authority.
- **WLL (Working Load Limit)** = The maximum mass or force the equipment is **rated to handle** under normal use.
- **Overloading Factor** = Ratio of Actual Load to Rated SWL. Any value **> 1.0** constitutes overloading.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The School Bag on a Child's Back**
> Imagine a school child carrying a backpack. The backpack is designed to carry **8 kg** safely. If the child stuffs in **15 kg** of books, the straps begin to tear, the stitching gives way, the child leans forward, and may eventually fall — that is **overloading**. In a factory, the "child" is the crane, forklift, hoist, or conveyor, and the "bag" is its rated lifting/moving capacity. Ignoring the rated limit in industrial material handling is catastrophic — it results in dropped loads, snapped cables, tipped forklifts, and fatalities.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Factor of Safety (Crane Hooks) | $FoS$ | **3.5 to 5.0** |
| Factor of Safety (Wire Ropes) | $FoS$ | **3.0 to 6.0** |
| Standard design overload test | — | **1.25 × to 1.5 × SWL** |
| Wind pressure factor (cranes) | — | As per **IS 875** |
| Shock factor (sudden lifting) | $K$ | **1.0 (slow) to 2.0 (sudden jerky lift)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Overloading

Overloading in material handling is classified into three fundamental types based on the **time-history of the load application**:

1. **Static Overloading**
   - The applied load exceeds SWL but is applied gradually and remains constant.
   - Example: Placing a 3-tonne load on a 2-tonne rated platform.

2. **Dynamic Overloading**
   - The load is applied with motion-induced inertial forces.
   - Caused by acceleration, deceleration, or swinging of suspended loads.
   - Effective load = Static load + Dynamic amplification.

3. **Shock Overloading (Impact Loading)**
   - Sudden application or release of load causing stress waves.
   - Example: A load dropped on a conveyor, or a crane brake applied abruptly.
   - Most destructive type — multiplies stresses by shock factor $K$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Overloading Failure

- **Step 1:** Load applied exceeds the equipment's **rated SWL/WLL**.
- **Step 2:** Stress in critical members (shafts, ropes, beams, axles) **surpasses elastic limit**.
- **Step 3:** **Plastic deformation** begins in ductile members; micro-cracks propagate in brittle members.
- **Step 4:** **Stability margin** reduces — center of gravity shifts, tipping moment increases.
- **Step 5:** **Catastrophic failure** — rope snap, hook opening, beam buckling, vehicle rollover.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE and Module Tests.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{eff} = L_{static} + L_{dynamic}$ | Effective load = static + dynamic component |
| 2 | $L_{eff} = L \cdot (1 + a/g)$ | Effective load considering upward acceleration $a$ |
| 3 | $L_{impact} = L \cdot K$ | Impact load using shock factor $K$ |
| 4 | $L_{design} = SWL \times n$ | Design load where $n$ = test overload factor (1.25–1.5) |
| 5 | $\sigma_{actual} = \dfrac{W_{applied}}{A_{cross\text{-}section}}$ | Actual working stress in member |
| 6 | $\sigma_{allowable} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{FoS}$ | Allowable design stress |
| 7 | $FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Factor of Safety (always **> 1**) |
| 8 | $M_{tipping} = W \cdot d$ | Tipping moment (load × horizontal offset) |
| 9 | $Overload\ Ratio = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{SWL}$ | Must be **≤ 1.0** for safe operation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL RULE:** Overloading Ratio $\dfrac{W_{actual}}{SWL} > 1.0$ **= UNSAFE / ILLEGAL**

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Overload analysis is foundational in:
- **Crane and hoist design** (IS 807, IS 3177)
- **Forklift stability analysis** (OSHA 1910.178, IS 10981)
- **Conveyor belt capacity selection** (IS 11592)
- **Industrial truck and trolley design**
- **Warehouse racking systems** (IS 1883)
- **Ship and offshore crane operations**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Load Under Dynamic Lifting

Consider a load $L$ being lifted vertically upward by a crane with acceleration $a$. Two forces act on the suspended load:

- **Weight acting downward:** $W = m \cdot g$
- **Tension in rope acting upward:** $T$

Applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** in the vertical direction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_{vertical} &= m \cdot a \\
T - m \cdot g &= m \cdot a \\
T &= m \cdot g + m \cdot a \\
T &= m \cdot (g + a) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since effective load on the rope equals the tension $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eff} &= T = m \cdot g \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right) \\
L_{eff} &= L_{static} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** When lifting with upward acceleration $a$, the effective load is amplified by the factor $\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right)$. If $a = g$, the load becomes **2× its static weight**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Crane Dynamic Overload Calculation

**Problem:** A crane rated **SWL = 5 tonnes** lifts a static load of **4.5 tonnes** with an upward acceleration of **2.5 m/s²**. Determine whether the operation is safe. Use $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$.

**Step 1 — Identify static load and acceleration:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{static} &= 4.5\ tonnes \\
a &= 2.5\ m/s^2 \\
g &= 9.81\ m/s^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute amplification factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor} &= 1 + \dfrac{a}{g} \\
&= 1 + \dfrac{2.5}{9.81} \\
&= 1 + 0.2548 \\
&= 1.2548 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective dynamic load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eff} &= L_{static} \times 1.2548 \\
&= 4.5 \times 1.2548 \\
&= 5.6466\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with SWL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eff} &= 5.6466\ tonnes \\
SWL &= 5.000\ tonnes \\
Overload\ Ratio &= \dfrac{5.6466}{5.0} = 1.129 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the ratio **1.129 > 1.0**, the operation constitutes **overloading by 12.9%** and is **UNSAFE**. Action: reduce static load to maintain $L_{eff} \leq 5.0$ tonnes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{safe,static} &= \dfrac{SWL}{1 + a/g} = \dfrac{5.0}{1.2548} \\
&= 3.984\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Forklift Tipping Moment Analysis

**Problem:** A forklift has a rated capacity of **2 tonnes** at a load center of **500 mm** from the fork face. The load is placed at **700 mm** from the fork face. Determine the effective load and check for stability.

**Step 1 — Compute load at standard vs. actual load center:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Capacity\ at\ actual\ LC &= \dfrac{SWL \times Standard\ LC}{Actual\ LC} \\
&= \dfrac{2.0 \times 500}{700} \\
&= 1.428\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Tip line check:**

A forklift tips forward when the load moment exceeds the counterweight moment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{load} &= W_{load} \times d_{load} = 1.428 \times 0.700 = 1.0\ t\cdot m \\
M_{counter} &= W_{truck} \times d_{CG}\ (\text{given by manufacturer})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Overload condition:**

Since the truck was rated for 2 t @ 500 mm, the actual lift capacity at 700 mm is **1.428 t**. If the operator attempts to lift **2.0 tonnes** at 700 mm, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Overload\ Ratio &= \dfrac{2.0}{1.428} = 1.40 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is 40% overloading — leading to forward tip-over hazard.**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Overloading Causes vs. Consequences vs. Controls

| Cause of Overloading | Consequence | Engineering Control |
|---|---|---|
| Exceeding SWL on crane | Rope snap, structural collapse | **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** |
| Improper load slinging | Load slip, swing, drop | **Tag-line, proper sling angles** |
| Sudden jerk during lifting | Shock loading | **Smooth acceleration controls** |
| Forklift load off-center | Forward tip-over | **Load center chart adherence** |
| Dynamic wind on crane | Side tipping | **Anemometers, wind limits** |
| Side pulling on load | Rope misalignment, breakage | **Use snatch block, vertical lift** |
| Combined load + personnel | Fatal falls | **No riding on loads rule** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overloading Failure Causal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Operator attempts lift]
    A1[Load applied to lifting equipment]
    A2{Load versus SWL comparison}
    A3[Effective load computed with dynamics]
    A4{Safe Operating Zone check}
    A5[Continue safe operation]
    A6[Issue warning to operator]
    A7[Equipment fails and load drops]
    A8[Fatal injury and property loss]
    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 -->|Greater than SWL| A3
    A2 -->|Within SWL| A5
    A3 --> A4
    A4 -->|Greater than 1.0| A6
    A4 -->|Within 1.0| A5
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Crane Anti-Overload System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSING
        B1[Load Cell Sensor]
        B2[Boom Angle Sensor]
        B3[Radius Encoder]
        B4[Wind Speed Anemometer]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTATION
        C1[Microprocessor Controller]
        C2[Load Moment Calculator]
        C3[Safety Logic Unit]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION
        D1[Horn Alarm]
        D2[Visual LED Indicator]
        D3[Hydraulic Cutoff Valve]
        D4[Motion Lock Relay]
    end
    B1 --> C2
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C2
    C2 --> C1
    C1 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    C3 --> D4
```

## 4.3 Forklift Stability Triangle Diagram (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Forklift at rest with load]
    F1[Three point stability triangle]
    F2[Front left wheel contact]
    F3[Front right wheel contact]
    F4[Rear axle pivot center]
    F5[Combined center of gravity CG]
    F6{CG inside triangle?}
    F7[Stable operation]
    F8[Tip-over hazard]
    F0 --> F1
    F1 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F1 --> F4
    F0 --> F5
    F5 --> F6
    F6 -->|Yes| F7
    F6 -->|No| F8
```

## 4.4 Stress-vs-Load Behavior Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Applied Load on Member]
    P1[Linear Elastic Region]
    P2[Yield Point]
    P3[Plastic Region]
    P4[Ultimate Failure Point]
    P5[Working Limit Marker at SWL]
    P6[Safety Margin Zone]
    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P5 --> P6
    P1 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Working Limit Marker (SWL)** must always lie **below the Yield Point** with a designed safety margin. Any load that pushes stress past yield constitutes **overloading**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define overloading. List any four causes of overloading in material handling equipment.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Overloading** is the condition in which the load applied to a material handling equipment (e.g., crane, hoist, forklift, conveyor) **exceeds its Safe Working Load (SWL)**, either statically or dynamically, resulting in unsafe stress levels and possible failure.

**Four causes:**
1. **Incorrect estimation** of load weight by the operator.
2. **Improper sling angle** causing load multiplication.
3. **Dynamic effects** — jerky lifts, sudden braking, swinging.
4. **Load center shift** in forklifts beyond rated distance.
5. **Side loading or dragging** loads with cranes.

*(Any four — ½ mark each, definition 1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between SWL and WLL.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | SWL (Safe Working Load) | WLL (Working Load Limit) |
|---|---|---|
| Meaning | Max load equipment can safely handle | Max load recommended for routine use |
| Basis | Determined by **manufacturer testing** under service conditions | Determined by **design calculation** with FoS |
| Usage | Stamped on the equipment | Used in manuals and operational guidelines |
| Term popularity | Common in **Indian & European standards** | Common in **US standards (ASME, OSHA)** |

*(2 marks for clear differentiation, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the concept of **overloading in cranes**, including static, dynamic, and shock loading. Discuss how each affects structural integrity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric overhead crane has SWL of **10 tonnes**. During operation it lifts a load of **9 tonnes** with upward acceleration of **1.5 m/s²**. Determine:
- (i) The effective load on the crane. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Whether the operation is safe. **(1 mark)**
- (iii) Maximum safe static load for safe operation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Concept of Overloading in Cranes (7 marks)**

Overloading in cranes occurs when the load suspended or lifted surpasses the **Safe Working Load** declared by the manufacturer. It is of three types:

**1. Static Overloading:**
The actual load exceeds SWL but is applied **gradually** without acceleration. The structural members experience stresses beyond their elastic limits. For example, lifting an 11-tonne load with a 10-tonne crane. Result — permanent bending, yield of hook, deformation of jib.

**2. Dynamic Overloading:**
The effective load includes **inertial forces** during acceleration or deceleration:

$$
L_{eff} = L \times \left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right)
$$

A crane lifting a 5-tonne load with $a = 0.2g$ experiences an effective load of **6 tonnes** — a 20% overload condition.

**3. Shock Overloading:**
Caused by **sudden jerks, abrupt braking, or dropping the load**. The shock factor $K$ multiplies the static load:

$$
L_{impact} = K \cdot L,\quad K = 1.0\text{ to }2.0
$$

A jerk-induced shock of $K=2.0$ doubles the load, often resulting in **instantaneous rope snap or hook failure**.

**Structural Effects:**
- **Hook** — opens up, neck tears.
- **Wire rope** — strands break, bird-caging.
- **Jib/Boom** — buckling, bending.
- **Brakes** — slippage, heat failure.

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 Causal Flow and Section 4.4 Stress Curve.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SWL &= 10\ tonnes \\
L_{static} &= 9\ tonnes \\
a &= 1.5\ m/s^2 \\
g &= 9.81\ m/s^2 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute amplification factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Factor} &= 1 + \dfrac{a}{g} = 1 + \dfrac{1.5}{9.81} \\
&= 1 + 0.1529 = 1.1529 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — (i) Effective Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eff} &= L_{static} \times 1.1529 \\
&= 9 \times 1.1529 \\
&= 10.376\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Safety Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Overload\ Ratio &= \dfrac{L_{eff}}{SWL} = \dfrac{10.376}{10.0} = 1.0376 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **1.0376 > 1.0**, the operation **is NOT safe** — overloading by 3.76%.

**Step 4 — (iii) Maximum Safe Static Load:**

For safe operation, $L_{eff} \leq SWL$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{static,max} \times 1.1529 &\leq 10.0 \\
L_{static,max} &= \dfrac{10.0}{1.1529} \\
&= 8.674\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The static load must be reduced from 9 tonnes to **8.674 tonnes** to ensure safe operation under the given acceleration.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **causes and consequences of overloading** in material handling with reference to cranes and forklifts. Include relevant Indian Standard codes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forklift is rated for **3 tonnes at 600 mm load center**. The operator lifts **3 tonnes at 900 mm load center**. Compute:
- (i) Effective safe capacity at 900 mm. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Percentage overloading. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Explain two safety controls to prevent this. **(2 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Causes and Consequences of Overloading (7 marks)**

**Causes:**
- **Human error:** Incorrect load weight estimation, failure to read load chart.
- **Improper rigging:** Wrong sling angle, asymmetric load distribution.
- **Dynamic effects:** Sudden acceleration/deceleration, swinging loads.
- **Environmental factors:** Wind on crane, uneven floor on forklift.
- **Equipment misuse:** Using equipment beyond design service.
- **Load shifting:** Load moves from center, increasing moment.

**Consequences in Cranes:**
- Wire rope failure → dropped load.
- Boom buckling → collapse.
- Crane tip-over → fatality.

**Consequences in Forklifts:**
- Forward tip-over.
- Mast failure.
- Loss of steering control.

**Relevant Indian Standards:**
- **IS 807** — Code of practice for design, manufacture, and testing of cranes.
- **IS 3177** — Code for overhead electric cranes.
- **IS 3938** — Code for wire rope slings.
- **IS 10981** — Forklift truck safety.
- **OSHA 1910.178** — Powered industrial trucks.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Rated\ Capacity &= 3\ tonnes \\
Standard\ LC &= 600\ mm = 0.6\ m \\
Actual\ LC &= 900\ mm = 0.9\ m \\
Actual\ Load &= 3\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — (i) Effective Capacity at 900 mm:**

Capacity at new load center reduces as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Capacity_{900} &= \dfrac{Rated\ Capacity \times Standard\ LC}{Actual\ LC} \\
&= \dfrac{3.0 \times 0.6}{0.9} \\
&= \dfrac{1.8}{0.9} \\
&= 2.0\ tonnes \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — (ii) Percentage Overloading:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Overload\ Ratio &= \dfrac{Actual\ Load}{Safe\ Capacity\ at\ 900mm} \\
&= \dfrac{3.0}{2.0} = 1.5 \\
Percentage\ Overload &= (1.5 - 1.0) \times 100 \\
&= 50\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — (iii) Safety Controls:**

1. **Adherence to Load Center Chart** — Operator must consult the manufacturer's chart before lifting, adjusting for actual load center.
2. **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** — Electronic system fitted on forklifts giving audio-visual alarms when load moment approaches the tipping threshold.
3. **Operator training & certification** as per IS 10981.
4. **Pre-use inspection** of forks, mast, hydraulics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert**
> - Students often **forget to apply the dynamic amplification factor** $\left(1 + \dfrac{a}{g}\right)$ when lifting with acceleration, losing up to **4 marks**.
> - Confusion between **SWL** and **WLL** is a common error. Memorize the distinction.
> - In forklift problems, students often **use the wrong load center**, applying actual load directly to rated capacity without correcting for the shift.
> - Always write the **final conclusion** ("SAFE" or "UNSAFE") explicitly — examiners allocate marks for this.
> - Show **units in every step** of numerical answers. Missing units = **½ mark deduction per step** in strict valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Overloading**

- **Definition:** Overloading = Applied Load > Safe Working Load (SWL).
- **Three Types:** Static, Dynamic, Shock (Impact) — most severe is shock.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $L_{eff} = L \times (1 + a/g)$
  - $L_{impact} = K \cdot L$
  - $FoS = \sigma_{ultimate} / \sigma_{working}$
  - Overload Ratio = Actual Load / SWL — must be **≤ 1.0**
- **Standards to Remember:**
  - IS 807 — Cranes
  - IS 3177 — Overhead cranes
  - IS 3938 — Wire rope slings
  - IS 10981 — Forklifts
  - OSHA 1910.178 — Powered industrial trucks
- **Design Test Load** = 1.25 × to 1.5 × SWL.
- **Shock Factor** $K$ = **1.0 to 2.0** depending on jerk severity.
- **Causes:** Wrong load estimate, bad rigging, jerky operation, off-center load, side-pull, wind.
- **Consequences:** Rope snap, hook failure, structural collapse, tip-over, fatalities.
- **Controls:** LMI systems, load charts, operator training, anti-two-block devices, pre-use inspection, smooth acceleration.
- **Always compute effective dynamic load** before deciding on safety.
- **Always state** your conclusion in numerical answers — examiners reward explicit statements.
- **Forklift load center** reduces capacity inversely with distance — capacity at greater LC < rated capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rope Fitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rope Fitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT833 – Safety in Material Handling, Module 2), **Rope Fitting** refers to the systematic engineering practice of selecting, terminating, splicing, attaching, and assembling ancillary hardware (fittings) onto natural fibre ropes, synthetic fibre ropes, or wire ropes to make them functionally safe for lifting, pulling, lashing, and load-securing operations in cranes, hoists, elevators, derricks, and rigging systems.

As per standard rigging nomenclature (aligned with IS 2266, IS 3658, IS 6938, ASME B30.9, and BS 302), a **rope fitting** is the complete assembly consisting of:

1. The **rope element** (the load-bearing tensile member).
2. The **end termination** (the mechanical interface that transfers rope load to the attachment hardware — e.g., spliced eye, ferrule-secure eye, wedge socket, poured socket, or mechanical grip socket).
3. The **hardware fitting** (shackles, hooks, eye-bolts, rings, master links, swivel pins, turnbuckles, thimbles, cable clamps, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *Rope Fitting* covers rope constructions (wire ropes — 6×19, 6×37, 8×19; fibre ropes — manila, sisal, polypropylene, polyester, nylon; chain ropes), rope terminations (eye splices, sockets, clips, knots), and end fittings such as **shackles, hooks, eye-bolts, master links, swivels, turnbuckles, thimbles, and wedge sockets** — together forming the *Fitting Assembly* used in material-handling safety.

### 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a heavy **construction crane** lifting a 5-tonne steel beam on a busy city site. The beam is not held directly by the crane's hook — instead, a **wire rope sling** cradles the beam, and at each end of this sling is a *fitting*: perhaps a **screw-pin shackle** that connects to the crane hook, or a **cable clip assembly** that forms a soft-eye termination. Every one of those tiny-looking shackles, clips, and thimbles must transmit tonnes of force without slipping, cutting, or failing.

Think of **rope fitting** as the *'joint surgery'* of material handling. The rope is the "muscle" and the fittings are the "tendons and bones" that allow the muscle to grip, lift, swivel, and attach. A powerful muscle attached to a weak bone is useless — and dangerous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Rope Fitting a Safety-Critical Topic?**
> According to industrial accident statistics (OSHA, HSE UK), approximately **25% of crane-related fatalities** are caused by rope/sling/fitting failures, not by the rope itself breaking in mid-span. The failures occur at the *terminations and fittings* where stress concentrations are highest.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Bolded for KTU Recall)

The following constants and standard metrics are recurring in KTU board questions:

- **Standard Wire Rope Construction Designations**: 6×19 (6 strands × 19 wires), 6×37, 6×61, 8×19 (rotation-resistant).
- **Nominal rope efficiency factor (η)**: typically **80% – 90%** for standard 6-strand ropes.
- **Factor of Safety (FoS) for lifting slings**: minimum **5:1** (per ASME B30.9 / IS 2266).
- **Factor of Safety for non-lifting (guy / stay ropes)**: typically **3:1** or **3.5:1**.
- **Modulus of Elasticity of Wire Rope**: **E ≈ 80 GPa to 110 GPa** (rope-specific).
- **Breaking strength of manila rope**: approximately **40 – 60 MPa** dry, **30 – 45 MPa** wet.
- **D/d ratio** (diameter of sheave / diameter of rope): minimum **20:1** for crane duty; minimum **30:1** for elevator duty.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Rope-Fitting Knowledge

To master this topic for KTU valuation, the subject must be understood in **five logical layers**:

#### Layer A — The Rope Itself (the parent component)
1. **Material Classification**
   - *Natural fibre ropes*: manila (abacá), sisal, hemp, coir.
   - *Synthetic fibre ropes*: polypropylene (PP), polyester, nylon, polyethylene, Kevlar/aramid.
   - *Wire ropes*: improved plow steel (IPS), extra improved plow steel (EIPS), stainless steel.
   - *Chain ropes*: calibrated load chain (Grade 80, Grade 100, alloy steel).

2. **Wire Rope Construction Parameters**
   - Number of strands × number of wires per strand (e.g., 6×19).
   - **Lay direction**: *regular lay (RL)* vs *Lang's lay (LL)*.
   - **Lay type**: *cross lay* vs *parallel lay (equal lay)*.
   - **Core**: fibre core (FC), independent wire rope core (IWRC), or wire strand core (WSC).

3. **Sling Geometry** (one of KTU's most-tested sub-topics)
   - Vertical hitch
   - Choker hitch
   - Basket hitch
   - Single-leg, two-leg (bridle), three-leg, four-leg slings.

#### Layer B — The Termination (how the rope ends)
1. **For wire ropes**: poured (spelter) socket, wedge socket, mechanical (swaged) socket, ferrule-secure eye, hand-tucked splice, cable clips (U-bolt clamps).
2. **For fibre ropes**: eye splice, back splice, short splice, end whipping, knots.

#### Layer C — The Hardware Fitting (the connected component)
1. **Shackles**: screw-pin, round-pin, bolt-type, anchor.
2. **Hooks**: sling hooks, eye hooks, sorting hooks, grab hooks.
3. **Eye-bolts**: plain pattern, shouldered (collared), swivel.
4. **Master link / oblong link**: the central load-bearing ring in multi-leg slings.
5. **Swivels**: to prevent rotation-induced load.
6. **Turnbuckles**: for tension adjustment.
7. **Thimbles**: inserted in rope eyes to preserve shape and prevent crushing.
8. **Cable clips / wire rope grips (U-bolt clamps)**: used to form eyes or join ropes.

#### Layer D — Strength Interaction (Why fitting governs capacity)
- A fitting assembly is only as strong as its **weakest element**, but in practice, the *termination* is the limiting zone due to:
  - Stress concentration at the eye.
  - Bending fatigue over the thimble.
  - Slippage at clip / wedge interfaces.

#### Layer E — Safety Verification (the regulatory layer)
- Proof Load Test, Factory Production Test (2× WLL), Periodic Inspection (every 6 / 12 months), Discard Criteria (per IS 3658 / ASME B30.9).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note**: In all tension / load tables, vertical bars `|x|` inside a markdown table will be rendered using `\vert` to prevent breaking the table parser.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / KTU Condition |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Breaking Load of Rope** | $B = \sigma_u \cdot A$ | $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength (MPa); $A$ = metallic cross-sectional area (mm²) | Used for fresh rope; efficiency factor applied later |
| 2 | **Working Load Limit (WLL)** | $WLL = \dfrac{U \cdot \eta}{FoS}$ | $U$ = minimum breaking load (N); $\eta$ = rope efficiency factor; $FoS$ = factor of safety | $\eta \approx 0.80$ for 6×19; $0.85$ for 6×37; $0.90$ for 8×19 |
| 3 | **Load on Each Leg of Sling (Symmetric)** | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$ | $T$ = tension per leg (N); $W$ = total load (N); $n$ = number of legs; $\theta$ = angle from horizontal | $\theta \geq 60°$ recommended; $\theta < 30°$ forbidden |
| 4 | **Choker Hitch Capacity** | $W_c = \dfrac{W_v \cdot C_c}{1}$ | $W_c$ = choker capacity; $W_v$ = vertical capacity; $C_c$ = choker factor | $C_c \approx 0.75$ for wire rope; $0.50$ for polypropylene |
| 5 | **Basket Hitch Capacity** | $W_b = 2 \cdot W_v \cdot C_b$ | $C_b \approx 0.50$ to $0.75$ | Hitches with D/d > 2 |
| 6 | **D/d Sheave Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{D_{sheave}}{D_{rope}}$ | Dimensionless | $R_{min} = 20$ for crane; $30$ for elevator; $34$ for personnel lifts |
| 7 | **Bending Stress at Sheave** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{E \cdot d}{D}$ | $E$ = rope modulus; $d$ = wire dia; $D$ = sheave dia | Fatigue life inversely proportional to $\sigma_b$ |
| 8 | **Efficiency of Socket Termination** | $\eta_s = \dfrac{T_{socket}}{U_{rope}}$ | Ratio form | $\eta_s \approx 0.90$ to $0.95$ for poured socket |
| 9 | **Efficiency of Clip Termination** | $\eta_c \approx 0.80$ to $0.85$ | — | Requires minimum 3 clips per termination (6×19 rope) |
| 10 | **Factor of Safety (Hoisting)** | $FoS = \dfrac{U_{rope}}{W}$ | — | $FoS_{min} = 5$ (personnel); $5$ (general crane duty) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above mathematical framework is not academic in nature — it directly governs:

- **Crane design**: The selection of boom pendants, hoist ropes, and equalizer sheaves.
- **Rigging plans** at construction sites: load charts depend on sling angle $\theta$.
- **Marine mooring**: applying correct FoS for synthetic ropes on offshore platforms.
- **Elevator suspension**: strict D/d ratios to ensure 25-year design life.
- **Stage-rigging (entertainment industry)**: where a single rigging failure can kill hundreds.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Recall — "R-FIT-S"**:
> **R**ope + **F**itting + **I**nspection + **T**ermination + **S**ling-angle.
> Always check these five in every numerical problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tension in a Multi-Leg Sling at Sling Angle θ

**Problem Statement (KTU Standard):**
A load of $W = 60 \text{ kN}$ is to be lifted using a four-leg wire-rope sling. The sling angle from horizontal is $\theta = 45°$. Each leg is constructed from 6×19 IPS wire rope of nominal diameter 16 mm (breaking load $U = 110 \text{ kN}$). Determine:
(a) Tension in each leg.
(b) Factor of Safety achieved.
(c) Is the rigging safe as per IS 2266 (FoS ≥ 5)?

**Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Decompose load geometry.**
Each sling leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The vertical component of tension $T$ in each leg must support a fraction of the total weight. By static equilibrium of the symmetric four-leg sling:

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = W
$$

where $n$ = number of legs.

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for leg tension.**

$$
T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
T = \frac{60 \text{ kN}}{4 \cdot \sin(45°)} = \frac{60}{4 \cdot 0.7071}
$$

$$
T = \frac{60}{2.8284} = 21.213 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the achieved Factor of Safety.**

$$
FoS = \frac{U_{rope}}{T_{actual}} = \frac{110 \text{ kN}}{21.213 \text{ kN}}
$$

$$
FoS = 5.185
$$

**Step 5 — Compare against KTU / IS 2266 requirement.**
The required minimum FoS for crane-duty slings is **5.0**. Since $5.185 \geq 5.0$, the rigging is **SAFE — by a thin margin of 3.7%**.

**Step 6 — Insight (KTU Examiner's View):**
If the rigger had instead used a sling angle of $\theta = 30°$, the new tension would be:

$$
T_{30°} = \frac{60}{4 \cdot \sin(30°)} = \frac{60}{4 \cdot 0.5} = 30 \text{ kN}
$$

which would push the FoS down to:

$$
FoS_{30°} = \frac{110}{30} = 3.667 < 5.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT SAFE}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Memorize):** A sling angle $\theta$ below $30°$ from horizontal is **dangerous** and should be rejected at site.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Number of Cable Clips for a Termination

**Problem Statement:**
A 6×19 wire rope of diameter $d = 20$ mm is terminated using standard U-bolt cable clips to form an eye. Determine the minimum number of clips required and the spacing of clips per IS 6938.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Recall IS 6938 empirical rule.**
For a 6×19 or 6×37 wire rope, the *number of clips* $N_c$ required for a turnback termination is:

$$
N_c = 3 \quad \text{(minimum)} \quad \text{with an additional clip per } 25 \text{ mm of rope diameter}
$$

Generalized form:

$$
N_c = 3 + \frac{d - 12}{25} \quad \text{(clips, rounded up)}
$$

where $d$ is rope diameter in mm.

**Step 2 — Substitute $d = 20$ mm.**

$$
N_c = 3 + \frac{20 - 12}{25} = 3 + \frac{8}{25} = 3 + 0.32
$$

$$
N_c = 3.32 \Rightarrow \text{round up to } 4 \text{ clips}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute clip spacing (pitch).**
The first clip is placed at a distance of **1 × rope width** ($1w$) from the dead-end of the loop, and successive clips are spaced at **6 × rope width** ($6w$) apart:

$$
w = d = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Spacing} = 6w = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Location of clip 1} = 1w = 20 \text{ mm from dead end}
$$

**Step 4 — Torque on U-bolt nuts.**

$$
T_{nut} = k \cdot d \quad \text{(empirical: } k \approx 30 \text{ N·m/mm for IPS rope)}
$$

$$
T_{nut} = 30 \times 20 = 600 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Rule**: "U-bolts must always be fitted so that the U-bolt saddle bears on the *dead end* (short end), and the nuts bear on the *live end* (load-bearing long end)." Reversing this halves the termination strength.

**Step 5 — Termination efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{clip} = \frac{T_{assembly}}{U_{rope}} \approx 0.80
$$

So effective breaking load of the terminated rope:

$$
U_{eff} = 0.80 \times 110 = 88 \text{ kN}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — D/d Ratio and Bending Fatigue Life

**Step 1 — Establish fatigue-life proportionality.**
The fatigue life $N_f$ (cycles to failure) of a wire rope over a sheave is empirically given by:

$$
N_f = K \cdot \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^m
$$

where $K$ is a constant (rope-specific), $D$ = sheave tread diameter, $d$ = rope diameter, $m \approx 3.0$ for 6×19 rope.

**Step 2 — Take ratios for two operating conditions.**

$$
\frac{N_{f,1}}{N_{f,2}} = \left(\frac{D_1 / d_1}{D_2 / d_2}\right)^m
$$

**Step 3 — Illustrative example.**
If condition 1 uses $D_1/d_1 = 20$ and condition 2 uses $D_2/d_2 = 30$:

$$
\frac{N_{f,1}}{N_{f,2}} = \left(\frac{20}{30}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^3 = \frac{8}{27} = 0.296
$$

This means using a larger sheave (D/d = 30) extends fatigue life by approximately **3.37×** compared to a smaller sheave.

---

### 3.4 KTU Examination Pattern — Analytical Matrix

The following matrix shows which equations get triggered by which problem types:

| Problem Type | Trigger Words | Primary Equations |
|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| "Sling carries load W at angle θ" | "four-leg sling", "tension", "angle" | $T = W / (n\sin\theta)$, $FoS$ |
| "Wire rope diameter to lift W" | "select diameter", "minimum" | $U = FoS \cdot W$, $d$ from table |
| "Cable clip termination" | "U-bolt", "number of clips" | IS 6938 rule |
| "Sheave diameter" | "D/d ratio", "fatigue" | $R_{min} = 20$ or $30$ |
| "Choker / basket factor" | "choker hitch", "basket" | $W_c = C_c \cdot W_v$ |
| "Proof load test" | "proof load", "2× WLL" | $P_L = 2 \cdot WLL$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Wire Rope Anatomy (Hierarchical Cross-Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ropeA[Rope Outer Surface]
    strand1A[Strand 1]
    strand2A[Strand 2]
    strand3A[Strand 3]
    strand4A[Strand 4]
    strand5A[Strand 5]
    strand6A[Strand 6]
    coreA[Core Fibre FC or IWRC]
    ropeA --> strand1A
    ropeA --> strand2A
    ropeA --> strand3A
    ropeA --> strand4A
    ropeA --> strand5A
    ropeA --> strand6A
    ropeA --> coreA
    strand1A --> wire1A[Outer King Wire]
    strand1A --> wire2A[Intermediate Wire Layer]
    strand1A --> wire3A[Inner Wire Core of Strand]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Sling Hitch Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loadB[Load W]
    vh[Vertical Hitch\nT = W]
    bh[Basket Hitch\nT = W/2]
    ch[Choker Hitch\nT = W * Cc]
    fourleg[Four Leg Sling\nT = W / 4 sin theta]
    loadB --> vh
    loadB --> bh
    loadB --> ch
    loadB --> fourleg
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Functional Architecture of a Complete Rope Fitting Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Load Path Architecture"
        masterLink[Master Link Top]
        sShackle1[Shackle Screw Pin A]
        sShackle2[Shackle Screw Pin B]
        slingLeg1[Sling Leg 1 Termination]
        slingLeg2[Sling Leg 2 Termination]
        thimble1[Thimble in Eye]
        clipAssy[Cable Clip Assembly 4 Clips]
        eyeBolt[Eye Bolt Attachment to Load]
    end
    masterLink --> sShackle1
    masterLink --> sShackle2
    sShackle1 --> slingLeg1
    sShackle2 --> slingLeg2
    slingLeg1 --> thimble1
    thimble1 --> clipAssy
    slingLeg2 --> eyeBolt
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Decision Tree for Rope Fitting Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Selection]
    q1{Load Type?}
    start --> q1
    q1 -- Personnel Lift --> P1[Wire Rope 6x37 FC, FoS 12]
    q1 -- Crane Hoist --> P2[Wire Rope 6x19 IWRC, FoS 5]
    q1 -- Marine Mooring --> P3[Polyester 3 strand, FoS 3]
    q2{Environment?}
    P1 --> q2
    P2 --> q2
    P3 --> q2
    q2 -- Corrosive --> R1[Galvanized or Stainless]
    q2 -- High Temp --> R2[EIPS or Aramid]
    q2 -- Normal --> R3[IPS Rope]
    r1{Termination?}
    R1 --> r1
    R2 --> r1
    R3 --> r1
    r1 -- Permanent --> T1[Poured Spelter Socket]
    r1 -- Reusable --> T2[Wedge Socket or Mechanical Grip]
    r1 -- Field --> T3[Cable Clips IS 6938]
    finish[Approved Fitting Assembly]
    T1 --> finish
    T2 --> finish
    T3 --> finish
```

### 4.5 Diagram E — Lay Direction Schematic (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Regular Lay RL]\nStrands twisted opposite to wires
    A2[Langs Lay LL]\nStrands twisted same as wires
    A3[Cross Lay]\nStrands cross wires at angle
    A4[Parallel Lay]\nWires parallel within strand
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic**: Regular Lay is "Right-hand" by default — strands twist LEFT while wires twist RIGHT. Lang's Lay has both twisting the same way. **Lang's lay lasts longer but is more prone to unlay; Regular lay is safer for general use.**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Rope Fitting". List any four types of rope end fittings used in material-handling safety.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 Definition + 2 List + 0 Relevance):**

*Definition (1 mark):*
A rope fitting is the engineered assembly of a rope (wire, fibre, or chain) together with its end termination and connecting hardware, used to transmit tensile load safely in lifting and material-handling operations.

*Four types of rope end fittings (2 marks — ½ mark each):*
1. **Thimble** — inserted in rope eye to preserve shape and prevent crushing.
2. **Shackle** — U-shaped pin-connected link for detachable connection.
3. **Eye-bolt** — threaded bolt with an eye for lifting attachment.
4. **Cable clip (U-bolt grip)** — used to form a wire-rope eye or join two ropes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the recommended minimum Factor of Safety (FoS) for:**
**(a) Wire-rope sling in crane duty, (b) Man-riding / personnel lift, (c) Non-lifting stay / guy rope.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per sub-part):**

| Application | Minimum FoS |
|---|---|
| Crane duty (hoisting slings) | **5 : 1** |
| Personnel / man-riding lifts | **12 : 1** (per ASME B30.9 / IS 7333) |
| Non-lifting stay / guy rope | **3 : 1** (or 3.5 : 1) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction of a 6×19 wire rope. State the function of the **core** and differentiate between **fibre core (FC)** and **independent wire rope core (IWRC)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-leg wire-rope sling is used to lift a steel plate of mass **8 tonnes**. The sling angle from horizontal is **60°**. Each leg is made of 6×37 IPS wire rope of **nominal diameter 18 mm**, having a breaking load of **120 kN**. Compute:
(i) Tension in each leg,
(ii) Factor of Safety achieved,
(iii) Comment on the safety of the operation as per IS 2266. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Construction of 6×19 Wire Rope (7 Marks)

**Sketch Description (2 Marks):**

A 6×19 wire rope consists of **6 strands laid helically around a central core**. Each strand contains **19 wires** arranged in two or three layers around a central king-wire (e.g., 1 + 6 + 12 configuration). Total wires = 6 × 19 = **114 wires** plus the core.

```
           Outer Surface (Crown Wires)
   ╱ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╲
  │  Strand 1   Strand 2        │
  │   ╱  ╲       ╱  ╲           │
  │  │ 19 │     │ 19 │  x6      │
  │   ╲  ╱       ╲  ╱           │
  │       ─── Core ───          │
   ╲ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ╱
```

**Function of Core (2 Marks):**
The core supports the strands and maintains the rope's circular cross-section under load. It also acts as a reservoir for lubrication, reducing internal friction and extending fatigue life.

**Difference between FC and IWRC (3 Marks):**

| Property | Fibre Core (FC) | Independent Wire Rope Core (IWRC) |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------------------------|
| Material | Hemp, polypropylene, or sisal | A separate small wire rope (e.g., 7×7) |
| Strength | Lower | ~7–10% higher breaking strength |
| Heat resistance | Poor (degrades >120 °C) | Excellent (suitable for hot ladle cranes) |
| Crush resistance | Low | High |
| Application | General-purpose, indoor | Heavy-duty, high-temperature, mining, marine |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Mass $m = 8 \text{ t} = 8000 \text{ kg}$
- Weight $W = m \cdot g = 8000 \times 9.81 = 78.48 \text{ kN}$
- Number of legs $n = 4$
- Sling angle $\theta = 60°$ from horizontal
- Breaking load per rope $U = 120 \text{ kN}$

**Step 1 — Tension per leg (3 Marks):**

$$
T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta} = \frac{78.48}{4 \cdot \sin(60°)}
$$

$$
T = \frac{78.48}{4 \cdot 0.8660} = \frac{78.48}{3.4641}
$$

$$
T = 22.655 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 2 — Factor of Safety (2 Marks):**

$$
FoS = \frac{U}{T} = \frac{120}{22.655} = 5.297
$$

**Step 3 — Safety Comment (2 Marks):**

As per IS 2266, the minimum FoS for general crane-duty hoisting is **5.0**. Since $5.297 \geq 5.0$, the rigging **is safe**. The margin is approximately **5.9%**, which is acceptable but indicates that any reduction in sling angle (e.g., $\theta$ dropping below $50°$) would make the operation unsafe. The rigger should maintain $\theta \geq 60°$ for a safer margin.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe with sketches **four common types of end fittings** used with wire ropes in material-handling operations. State the application of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 20 mm diameter 6×19 IPS wire rope (breaking load 175 kN) is terminated using **U-bolt cable clips** to form a soft eye. Determine:
(i) Minimum number of clips required as per IS 6938.
(ii) Spacing between successive clips.
(iii) Effective breaking load of the terminated assembly assuming clip efficiency $\eta_c = 0.80$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Four End Fittings (7 Marks — 1.75 Marks Each)

1. **Thimble** (1.75 Marks)
   - A grooved metallic (or polymer) ring inserted inside the rope eye.
   - Prevents the rope from being crushed against the parent fitting (e.g., shackle pin).
   - *Application*: Used in all soft-eye terminations, especially in crane slings.

2. **Shackle** (1.75 Marks)
   - U-shaped body with a removable pin (screw-pin, round-pin, or bolt-type).
   - *Application*: Connects sling eye to hook, master link, or lifting beam.

3. **Eye-bolt** (1.75 Marks)
   - A bolt with an integral eye for lifting; shoulder pattern preferred for in-line loads.
   - *Application*: Lifting of machinery, fabricated components, mould boxes.

4. **Wedge Socket** (1.75 Marks)
   - A tapered socket in which the rope is anchored by a wedge.
   - *Application*: Dead-end terminations in mine hoists, elevator ropes, cranes — where re-termination may be needed.

#### Part (b) — Cable Clip Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Rope diameter $d = 20$ mm
- Construction: 6×19 IPS
- Breaking load $U_{rope} = 175$ kN
- Clip efficiency $\eta_c = 0.80$

**Step 1 — Minimum number of clips (3 Marks):**

$$
N_c = 3 + \frac{d - 12}{25} = 3 + \frac{20 - 12}{25} = 3 + \frac{8}{25} = 3.32
$$

Rounding up:

$$
N_c = 4 \text{ clips}
$$

**Step 2 — Spacing between clips (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Pitch} = 6w = 6 \times d = 6 \times 20 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Location of first clip from dead end} = 1w = 1 \times 20 = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective breaking load of the assembly (2 Marks):**

$$
U_{eff} = \eta_c \cdot U_{rope} = 0.80 \times 175 = 140 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. Many students mistakenly place the U-bolt saddle on the *live end* (long end). The correct placement is **saddle on the dead (short) end, nuts on the live (load-bearing) end**.
> 2. Students frequently forget that the first clip must be positioned at exactly $1w$ from the dead end, *not at the thimble*.
> 3. Omitting the torque specification ($\sim 30$ N·m per mm of rope diameter) is a common reason for losing 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Rope Fitting** = Rope + Termination + Hardware.
- [ ] Three rope families: **Fibre (natural & synthetic), Wire, Chain**.
- [ ] Wire-rope designation: *strands × wires per strand* (e.g., 6×19 = 6 strands of 19 wires).
- [ ] **Core types**: FC (fibre), IWRC (independent wire rope core — stronger & heat-resistant).
- [ ] **Lay types**: Regular Lay (RL) vs Lang's Lay (LL); Cross Lay vs Parallel Lay.
- [ ] Sling geometry master equation: $T = W / (n \cdot \sin\theta)$.
- [ ] Sling angle rule: $\theta \geq 60°$ recommended; $\theta < 30°$ forbidden.
- [ ] **Choker factor** $C_c \approx 0.75$ for wire rope.
- [ ] **Basket factor** $C_b \approx 0.50$–$0.75$.
- [ ] **D/d ratio**: minimum 20 (crane), 30 (elevator), 34 (personnel).
- [ ] **Factor of Safety**: 5:1 (crane), 12:1 (personnel), 3:1 (stay ropes).
- [ ] **Cable clips**: $N_c = 3 + (d-12)/25$, spacing $= 6d$, first clip at $1d$.
- [ ] **U-bolt orientation**: saddle on the *dead* end; nuts on the *live* end.
- [ ] **Termination efficiency**: Poured socket $\sim 0.90$–$0.95$; Clips $\sim 0.80$–$0.85$.
- [ ] **Proof load** = $2 \times WLL$.
- [ ] **Discard criteria**: 10% broken wires in one rope lay, severe corrosion, kinks, bird-caging, reduction in diameter > 5%.
- [ ] Always lift with **shoulder-pattern (collared) eye-bolts** for in-line loading; plain-pattern eye-bolts only for angular loads ≤ 45°.
- [ ] **Master link** is the central load-bearing ring in multi-leg slings.
- [ ] **Shackle pin** type selection: screw-pin (frequent assembly), round-pin (frequent loading), bolt-type (permanent).
- [ ] Sling angle directly controls leg tension — a 30° drop from 60° to 30° doubles the tension in the sling leg.
- [ ] Thimbles preserve rope-eye geometry and prevent crushing against shackle pins.
- [ ] Material-handling regulations: **IS 2266** (wire rope), **IS 3658** (sling safety), **IS 6938** (wire-rope clips), **ASME B30.9** (slings), **ASME B30.10** (hooks).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Quick-Recall Table**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | Sling angle from horizontal | 60° ideal |
| $FoS$ | Factor of Safety | 5 / 12 / 3 |
| $\eta$ | Rope / termination efficiency | 0.80 – 0.95 |
| $D/d$ | Sheave / rope diameter ratio | ≥ 20 |
| $N_c$ | Number of cable clips | 3 + (d−12)/25 |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection and Replacement - Slings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inspection and Replacement - Slings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Slings

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **sling** is a flexible, non-rigid lifting accessory manufactured from chain, wire rope, metal mesh, or synthetic fibers (webbing or roundslings), used in conjunction with a lifting machine (crane, hoist, or powered industrial truck) to attach, support, or lift a load. Slings form the critical load-bearing interface between the lifting mechanism and the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sling (KTU 2024 Definition):** A lifting accessory, normally assembled from chain, wire rope, or textile webbing/round-sling fabric, used for attaching a load to a hoisting mechanism, where the safe working load (SWL) is governed by the angle of loading (sling angle) and the geometry of hitch.

**Classification of Slings by Construction Material:**

| Material Type | Common Use | Typical SWL Range |
|---|---|---|
| Chain Slings | Heavy loads, hot environments | 1 Tonne to 100+ Tonnes |
| Wire Rope Slings | Construction, steel industry | 0.5 Tonne to 100+ Tonnes |
| Synthetic Web Slings | Finished goods, fragile loads | 1 Tonne to 30 Tonnes |
| Synthetic Round Slings | Cylindrical loads, delicate surfaces | 1 Tonne to 85 Tonnes |
| Metal Mesh Slings | Hot metal, sharp-edged loads | 0.5 Tonne to 20 Tonnes |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tow-Rope of Industry"

Think of a sling exactly like the **tow-rope used by a farmer** to pull a heavy cart. The rope does not do the pulling itself — the tractor (or crane) does the work — but the rope *transmits* the force. Now, if the rope is frayed, knotted, or sun-damaged, it may snap mid-pull and cause disaster. **A sling is the industrial equivalent of that tow-rope**, except the stakes are far higher: a failed sling can drop a multi-tonne load onto workers, causing fatalities. This is why *inspection* and *replacement* are not optional maintenance tasks — they are **legal and moral safety imperatives**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sling Angle Principle:** The vertical angle a sling makes with the horizontal load dramatically affects the load on each sling leg. A 60° sling angle roughly **doubles** the tension in the sling compared to a 90° (vertical) hitch.

### 1.3 Types of Hitches

The *hitch* describes the way a sling is attached to the load, and it directly governs the inspection focus areas:

- **Vertical Hitch** — Single straight leg pull (1-leg sling).
- **Choker Hitch** — Sling passes *through* the load itself and cinches around it. Common cause of load-loss if the choke point shifts.
- **Basket Hitch** — Sling cradles the load; load is shared between two vertical legs.
- **Bridle Hitch** — Multi-leg sling (2, 3, or 4 legs) joined at a master link.

> [!WARNING]
> **Reduction in Capacity:** A choker hitch can reduce sling capacity by as much as **20-25%** compared to a vertical hitch due to bending stress at the choke point.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Inspection Regime

According to KTU 2024 / OSHA / ASME B30.9 aligned standards, sling inspection is a **three-tier obligation**, not a single event:

**Tier 1 — Initial Inspection (Pre-Use Check)**
- Performed **before every shift** by the rigger/slinger using the sling.
- Visual + tactile check: look for obvious damage, check tag, confirm color-code validity.
- Scope is limited to what is observable during normal handling.

**Tier 2 — Frequent Inspection**
- Performed at intervals of **daily to monthly** depending on service severity.
- Conducted by a *designated person* (need not be a "competent person" but trained).
- Covers wear patterns, surface contamination, partial deformation.

**Tier 3 — Periodic Inspection (Documented)**
- Conducted at intervals of **monthly to annually** based on service class.
- Performed by a *competent person* (formal qualification).
- Includes dimensional measurements, documentation, marking for identification, and **decision on RETIREMENT or REPAIR**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Class & Frequency Matrix:**

| Service Class | Operating Conditions | Frequent Inspection | Periodic Inspection |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal | Up to 5 lifts/day, smooth loads | Daily | Monthly |
| Heavy | 5-10 lifts/day, abrasive/sharp loads | Weekly | Quarterly |
| Severe | >10 lifts/day, hot/corrosive, multi-shift | Daily | Monthly |

### 2.2 Wire Rope Sling — Inspection Criteria & Retirement Thresholds

Wire rope slings fail progressively, and the inspection must catch failure *before* it propagates. The chief KTU-board-tested parameters are:

**A) Broken Wire Counting (Lay-Based):**
- **10 or more random broken wires in one lay**, OR
- **5 or more broken wires in one strand in one lay** → IMMEDIATE RETIREMENT.

**B) Diameter Reduction (Wear & Internal Damage):**
- A reduction in **rope diameter** of more than **5%** from the nominal (new) value → RETIRE.

$$ D_{\text{limit}} = 0.95 \times D_{\text{nominal}} $$

where $D_{\text{nominal}}$ is the original published rope diameter and $D_{\text{limit}}$ is the minimum allowable diameter after use.

**C) Severe Deformations — Immediate Removal:**
- **Kinking, crushing, birdcaging, popped core, or heat damage** (discoloration, fused wires) → RETIRE immediately. No "permitted percentage" applies; these are absolute failures.

### 2.3 Chain Sling — Inspection Criteria

Chain slings are graded (Grade 80, 100, 120) and have these retirement conditions:

- **Wear**: Any link showing **10% or more reduction in cross-section** (diameter) → RETIRE.
- **Elongation**: A link stretch of more than **5%** of original length → RETIRE.
- **Gouges, nicks, cracks**: Any visible crack or any single gouge/nick exceeding **10%** of link diameter → RETIRE.
- **Twisted or bent links** → RETIRE (no straightening allowed).

### 2.4 Synthetic (Web & Round) Sling — Inspection Criteria

- **Cuts, holes, snags, or tears** in the webbing → RETIRE.
- **Melting, charring, or hardening** due to heat or friction → RETIRE.
- **Chemical degradation** (acid/alkali attack) evidenced by brittleness or staining → RETIRE.
- **Broken or missing stitches** on cover, or visible core damage in roundslings → RETIRE.
- **Snag or melted area on a round sling** of any size that exposes the core yarn → RETIRE.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Sling Capacity Formulas (Mandatory for KTU Numerical Problems):**

| # | Parameter / Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Tension in each sling leg for vertical-lift multi-leg sling |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 n \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Tension per leg for multi-leg sling in basket configuration |
| 3 | $D_{\text{limit}} = 0.95 \cdot D_{\text{nominal}}$ | Minimum allowable wire-rope diameter (5% wear rule) |
| 4 | $L_{\text{limit}} = 1.05 \cdot L_{\text{nominal}}$ | Maximum allowable chain link length (5% stretch rule) |
| 5 | $W_{\text{limit}} = 0.90 \cdot W_{\text{nominal}}$ | Minimum allowable chain link diameter (10% wear rule) |
| 6 | $SWL_{\text{eff}} = SWL \cdot \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$ | Effective SWL reduction factor as sling angle flattens |

**Where:**
- $W$ = Total vertical load weight (N or kg-force)
- $n$ = Number of sling legs (2, 3, or 4)
- $\theta$ = Angle the sling makes with the **horizontal** (0° = horizontal sling, 90° = vertical sling)
- $T$ = Tension in **each** sling leg

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sling inspection is the **last line of defense** in lifting safety. In real industries (shipyards, steel plants, construction, ports), sling failures account for roughly **8-10%** of all crane-related fatalities globally. The KTU syllabus emphasizes sling inspection because every qualified engineer in a production or construction site must:

1. Read and act on the color-code / tag system.
2. Know the **numerical retirement thresholds** (10 wires, 5% wear, 10% reduction).
3. Make a **Go / No-Go decision** on each sling before a critical lift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sling Leg Tension for a Multi-Leg Bridle Hitch

This is the most-tested derivation in the KTU board paper for this module. Given a load of mass $M$ suspended by a $n$-leg sling, each leg making an angle $\theta$ with the **horizontal**, find the tension $T$ in each leg.

**Step 1 — Free Body Definition**

Let the load weight be $W = M \cdot g$ (vertically downward, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$).

**Step 2 — Resolve Tension Into Components**

Each sling leg carries a tension $T$ at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The **vertical component** of $T$ that supports the load is:

$$ T_{\text{vertical}} = T \cdot \sin\theta $$

(Recall: angle $\theta$ is measured from the horizontal, so vertical lift comes from the sine component.)

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of Vertical Forces**

For static equilibrium, the sum of all vertical components must equal the total load weight:

$$ \sum F_{\text{vertical}} = 0 $$

$$ n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = W $$

**Step 4 — Solve for T**

$$ T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta} $$

This is the **KTU standard formula for vertical-lift multi-leg slings**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Error:** Students often confuse $\sin\theta$ with $\cos\theta$ because they mentally picture $\theta$ from the vertical. **Remember: in this formula, $\theta$ is measured from the HORIZONTAL.**

**Step 5 — Special Case: Basket Hitch (Load on Two Sling Legs)**

In a basket hitch, the sling cradles the load; the load is supported by **two vertical reactions per leg** (one on each side of the load). The horizontal component $T \cdot \cos\theta$ in each leg adds to the vertical reaction. For a symmetric basket hitch with $n$ legs supporting load $W$:

$$ 2 \cdot n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = W $$

$$ T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot n \cdot \sin\theta} $$

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A steel plate of mass **2,000 kg** is to be lifted using a 2-leg wire rope sling. The sling makes an angle of **30°** with the horizontal. Find: (a) Tension in each leg. (b) Whether the sling (rated SWL 18 kN per leg at 90°) is safe.

**Given:**
- $M = 2000 \, \text{kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- $n = 2$
- $\theta = 30°$

**Solution Part (a) — Tension per Leg:**

Total weight:
$$ W = M \cdot g = 2000 \times 9.81 = 19{,}620 \, \text{N} = 19.62 \, \text{kN} $$

Using the formula:
$$ T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{19{,}620}{2 \cdot \sin 30°} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{19{,}620}{2 \cdot 0.5} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{19{,}620}{1.0} = 19{,}620 \, \text{N} = 19.62 \, \text{kN} $$

**Result:** Each leg carries **19.62 kN** of tension.

**Solution Part (b) — Sling Safety Check:**

Rated SWL per leg = 18 kN.
Actual load per leg = 19.62 kN.

Since $19.62 \, \text{kN} > 18 \, \text{kN}$:

$$ \text{Sling is UNSAFE. Do not use.} $$

**Action:** Either (i) use a sling with higher SWL, or (ii) increase the sling angle to 60° (which would reduce the per-leg tension).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** A 30° sling angle **DOUBLES** the tension compared to a 90° vertical lift. The minimum recommended sling angle is **60°** from horizontal (i.e., 30° from vertical) for safe lifting.

### 3.3 Derivation: Critical Sling Angle for Design

For a 2-leg sling with given $W$ and known single-leg SWL $SWL_{\text{leg}}$, find the minimum permissible angle $\theta_{\min}$ for safety.

**Condition:** $T \leq SWL_{\text{leg}}$

$$ \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot \sin\theta_{\min}} \leq SWL_{\text{leg}} $$

Rearranging:

$$ \sin\theta_{\min} \geq \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot SWL_{\text{leg}}} $$

$$ \theta_{\min} = \arcsin\left( \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot SWL_{\text{leg}}} \right) $$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $W = 20 \, \text{kN}$, $SWL_{\text{leg}} = 12 \, \text{kN}$, $n = 2$.

$$ \sin\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{20}{2 \times 12} = \dfrac{20}{24} = 0.833 $$

$$ \theta_{\min} = \arcsin(0.833) \approx 56.4° $$

**Conclusion:** The sling must be rigged at **at least 56.4°** from horizontal to operate within safe working load. Otherwise, the operation is non-compliant.

### 3.4 Derivation: Wire Rope Diameter Reduction Threshold

**Given:** Nominal rope diameter $D_{\text{nominal}} = 24 \, \text{mm}$ (from manufacturer tag).
**Measured** actual diameter under load $D_{\text{actual}} = 22.5 \, \text{mm}$.

**Percentage Reduction:**

$$ \%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{D_{\text{nominal}} - D_{\text{actual}}}{D_{\text{nominal}}} \times 100 $$

$$ \%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{24 - 22.5}{24} \times 100 $$

$$ \%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{1.5}{24} \times 100 = 6.25\% $$

**Decision Rule:**

$$ 6.25\% > 5\% \implies \text{RETIRE THE SLING.} $$

### 3.5 Inspection Decision Matrix (Master Table)

| Sling Type | Critical Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wire Rope | Random broken wires | $\geq 10$ in one lay | RETIRE |
| Wire Rope | Broken wires in one strand | $\geq 5$ in one lay | RETIRE |
| Wire Rope | Diameter reduction | $> 5\%$ | RETIRE |
| Wire Rope | Kink / birdcage / heat damage | Any | RETIRE IMMEDIATELY |
| Chain | Link wear | $\geq 10\%$ diameter loss | RETIRE |
| Chain | Link elongation | $\geq 5\%$ length increase | RETIRE |
| Chain | Crack or bent link | Any | RETIRE |
| Web Sling | Cut / hole / snag | Any visible | RETIRE |
| Web Sling | Melting / charring | Any | RETIRE |
| Round Sling | Exposed core / cover breach | Any | RETIRE |
| All Types | Missing / illegible tag | — | WITHDRAW & VERIFY |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sling Type Hierarchy (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sling Classification] --> B[Material Type]
    B --> C1[Chain Sling]
    B --> C2[Wire Rope Sling]
    B --> C3[Synthetic Web Sling]
    B --> C4[Synthetic Round Sling]
    B --> C5[Metal Mesh Sling]
    C1 --> D1[Grade 80 Alloy Steel]
    C1 --> D2[Grade 100 Alloy Steel]
    C1 --> D3[Grade 120 Alloy Steel]
    C2 --> E1[6x19 IWRC Construction]
    C2 --> E2[6x37 IWRC Construction]
    C2 --> E3[8x19 Seale Construction]
    C3 --> F1[Nylon Webbing]
    C3 --> F2[Polyester Webbing]
    C3 --> F3[Polypropylene Webbing]
    C4 --> G1[Polyester Cover and Core]
    C4 --> G2[Nylon Cover and Core]
    C5 --> H1[Stainless Steel Mesh]
    C5 --> H2[Carbon Steel Mesh]
```

### 4.2 Three-Tier Inspection Process Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Shift Start] --> P2[Initial Visual Inspection]
    P2 --> P3{Visible Damage?}
    P3 -- Yes --> P4[Tag and Withdraw]
    P3 -- No --> P5[Check Sling Tag and Color Code]
    P5 --> P6{Valid Tag?}
    P6 -- No --> P7[Remove from Service]
    P6 -- Yes --> P8[Check Hitch Configuration]
    P8 --> P9{Frequent Inspection Due?}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[Detailed Visual by Trained Person]
    P9 -- No --> P8
    P10 --> P11{Damage Found?}
    P11 -- Yes --> P12[Document and Repair Decision]
    P11 -- No --> P13[Periodic Inspection]
    P12 --> P14{Repairable?}
    P14 -- Yes --> P15[Authorized Repair]
    P14 -- No --> P16[Destroy and Dispose]
    P15 --> P13
    P13 --> P17{Competent Person Review}
    P17 --> P18[Record and Re-Mark]
    P18 --> P19[Return to Service]
    P19 --> P8
```

### 4.3 Sling Hitch Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Load W] --> H1[Vertical Hitch]
    L1 --> H2[Choker Hitch]
    L1 --> H3[Basket Hitch]
    L1 --> H4[Bridle Hitch 2 Leg]
    L1 --> H5[Bridle Hitch 4 Leg]
    H1 --> R1[T equals W]
    H2 --> R2[T equals 1.25 W approx]
    H3 --> R3[T equals 0.5 W per side]
    H4 --> R4[T equals W over 2 sin theta]
    H5 --> R5[T equals W over 4 sin theta]
```

### 4.4 Component Map of a Wire Rope Sling (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Wire Rope Sling] --> B[Master Link]
    A --> C[Sling Body Rope]
    A --> D[End Fittings]
    A --> E[Identification Tag]
    B --> B1[Cradle or Master Sub-Assembly]
    C --> C1[6 Strands around IWRC Core]
    D --> D1[Spliced Eye]
    D --> D2[Mechanical Swage Fitting]
    D --> D3[Open Spelter Socket]
    E --> E1[SWL Rating]
    E --> E2[Serial Number]
    E --> E3[Manufacturer Code]
    E --> E4[Date of Manufacture]
```

### 4.5 Decision Logic: Retire or Reuse? (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    STAGE1[Sling in Question] --> STAGE2{Type of Sling}
    STAGE2 -- Wire Rope --> STAGE3{10 or More Broken Wires}
    STAGE3 -- Yes --> STAGE4[RETIRE]
    STAGE3 -- No --> STAGE5{Diameter Loss Exceeds 5 Percent}
    STAGE5 -- Yes --> STAGE4
    STAGE5 -- No --> STAGE6{Visible Kink or Heat Damage}
    STAGE6 -- Yes --> STAGE4
    STAGE6 -- No --> STAGE7[Return to Service with Tag Update]
    STAGE2 -- Chain --> STAGE8{Link Wear Exceeds 10 Percent}
    STAGE8 -- Yes --> STAGE4
    STAGE8 -- No --> STAGE9{Link Elongation Exceeds 5 Percent}
    STAGE9 -- Yes --> STAGE4
    STAGE9 -- No --> STAGE10[Return to Service]
    STAGE2 -- Synthetic --> STAGE11{Cut or Melt or Chemical Damage}
    STAGE11 -- Yes --> STAGE4
    STAGE11 -- No --> STAGE10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any THREE conditions under which a wire rope sling must be immediately removed from service.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A wire rope sling must be **immediately retired** if any of the following conditions are observed (1 mark each, any three):

1. **Ten or more random broken wires** are found distributed in one lay of the rope, OR **five or more broken wires in a single strand** within one lay.
2. The **rope diameter has reduced by more than 5%** from the nominal (original) diameter as marked on the sling tag.
3. **Visible kinking, crushing, birdcaging, popped core, or heat damage** (discoloration, fused wires from exposure to high temperature) is present. Such damage is irreparable and warrants immediate removal.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between the three tiers of sling inspection: Initial, Frequent, and Periodic. Who performs each?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Tier | Performed By | Frequency | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Inspection** (1 mark) | The **rigger / slinger** (operator) | Before every shift / use | Quick visual + tag check to confirm fitness for that lift |
| **Frequent Inspection** (1 mark) | A **designated person** (trained but not necessarily certified) | Daily to monthly (by service class) | Detect wear, surface damage, partial deformation during active service |
| **Periodic Inspection** (1 mark) | A **competent person** (formally qualified) | Monthly to annually (by service class) | Full dimensional + documented audit; decides RETIRE / REPAIR / RECERTIFY |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the safe working load (SWL) reduction concept for multi-leg slings as the sling angle flattens. Derive the expression for the tension in each leg of a 2-leg vertical-lift sling, clearly stating the angle convention. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel beam of mass **1,500 kg** is to be lifted using a 2-leg wire rope sling. The sling is rigged at an angle of **45°** with the horizontal. The sling tag reads SWL = **12 kN per leg** (at 90°). **(i)** Calculate the actual tension in each leg. **(ii)** Determine if the sling is safe to use. **(iii)** If not safe, calculate the **minimum sling angle** from horizontal that would make the lift safe. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — SWL Reduction and Derivation (7 Marks)**

**SWL Reduction Concept (3 marks):**

The safe working load of a sling is rated for a **vertical (90°) hitch**. As the sling angle flattens (i.e., the angle from horizontal decreases), the tension in each leg increases **non-linearly** because the vertical component of tension ($T \sin\theta$) becomes smaller, requiring a larger $T$ to balance the load. This is why a sling rated for 10 tonnes vertically may only be safe for 5 tonnes when rigged at 30° from horizontal.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $W$ = total load weight, $n$ = number of sling legs, $\theta$ = angle between sling and horizontal.

For static equilibrium of the suspended load:

$$ \sum F_{\text{vertical}} = 0 $$

The vertical component of tension in one leg is $T \sin\theta$. With $n$ legs, the total vertical force is:

$$ n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = W $$

Solving for $T$:

$$ T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta} \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} $$

**Angle Convention:** $\theta$ is measured from the **horizontal** to the sling. When $\theta = 90°$, the sling is vertical, and $\sin\theta = 1$, giving $T = W/n$. When $\theta = 30°$, $\sin\theta = 0.5$, giving $T = 2W/n$ — **double the tension** of a vertical hitch.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $M = 1500 \, \text{kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$
- $n = 2$
- $\theta = 45°$
- $SWL_{\text{leg}} = 12 \, \text{kN} = 12{,}000 \, \text{N}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Total Weight W (1 mark):**

$$ W = M \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14{,}715 \, \text{N} = 14.715 \, \text{kN} $$

**Step 2 — Calculate Tension per Leg at 45° (2 marks):**

$$ T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{14{,}715}{2 \cdot \sin 45°} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{14{,}715}{2 \cdot 0.7071} $$

$$ T = \dfrac{14{,}715}{1.4142} = 10{,}405 \, \text{N} \approx 10.4 \, \text{kN} $$

**Step 3 — Safety Check (1 mark):**

$$ T_{\text{actual}} = 10.4 \, \text{kN} < SWL_{\text{leg}} = 12 \, \text{kN} $$

The sling **IS SAFE** for this lift (margin of $12 - 10.4 = 1.6 \, \text{kN}$, i.e., about 13%).

**Step 4 — Minimum Sling Angle for Safety (3 marks):**

For the lift to become unsafe, we need to find $\theta_{\min}$ such that $T = SWL_{\text{leg}}$:

$$ T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta_{\min}} = SWL_{\text{leg}} $$

$$ \sin\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot SWL_{\text{leg}}} = \dfrac{14{,}715}{2 \times 12{,}000} $$

$$ \sin\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{14{,}715}{24{,}000} = 0.6131 $$

$$ \theta_{\min} = \arcsin(0.6131) \approx 37.8° $$

**Conclusion:** The sling becomes unsafe if the rigging angle drops **below 37.8°** from horizontal. The current rigging of 45° is above this limit and therefore safe.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q. (a)** List and explain the **retirement criteria for wire rope slings** with reference to the 5% diameter reduction rule and the broken-wire counting rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6×19 IWRC wire rope sling has a nominal diameter of **28 mm**. During periodic inspection, the actual diameter is measured as **26.3 mm** and **7 broken wires** are found distributed randomly in one lay. **(i)** Determine whether the sling should be retired based on diameter. **(ii)** Determine whether it should be retired based on broken-wire count. **(iii)** If 4 of those 7 broken wires are concentrated in **a single strand**, does the decision change? Justify with reference to the relevant code rule. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Wire Rope Sling Retirement Criteria (7 Marks)**

Wire rope slings must be retired from service upon observation of any of the following conditions (per ASME B30.9 and KTU syllabus):

**1. Broken-Wire Counting Rule (3 marks):**

The number of broken wires allowed in a wire rope sling is **strictly limited** because each broken wire represents a localized stress concentration that propagates fatigue. The rules are:
- **10 or more randomly distributed broken wires in one lay** of the rope → RETIRE.
- **5 or more broken wires in any one strand in one lay** → RETIRE.
- The "lay" is the distance along the rope for one complete helical turn of a strand.

**2. Diameter Reduction Rule (3 marks):**

A reduction in rope diameter indicates internal wear, core failure, or external abrasion. The rule is:
- A reduction in **diameter of more than 5%** of the nominal value, measured perpendicular to the lay, → RETIRE.

$$ D_{\text{limit}} = 0.95 \times D_{\text{nominal}} $$

For a 28 mm rope, the minimum allowable diameter is $28 \times 0.95 = 26.6 \, \text{mm}$.

**3. Other Absolute Criteria (1 mark):**

- Kinking, crushing, birdcaging, popped core, severe corrosion, or heat damage → RETIRE IMMEDIATELY (no percentage tolerance).

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Inspection Problem (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
