- $D_{\text{nominal}} = 28 \, \text{mm}$
- $D_{\text{actual}} = 26.3 \, \text{mm}$
- Broken wires = 7 (random) in one lay, with 4 concentrated in one strand

**Step 1 — Diameter Reduction Check (2 marks):**

$$ \Delta D = D_{\text{nominal}} - D_{\text{actual}} = 28 - 26.3 = 1.7 \, \text{mm} $$

$$ \%\text{Reduction} = \dfrac{1.7}{28} \times 100 = 6.07\% $$

$$ 6.07\% > 5\% \implies \textbf{RETIRE based on diameter reduction.} $$

**Step 2 — Random Broken-Wire Count (2 marks):**

$$ 7 \text{ random wires} < 10 \text{ random wires} $$

$$ \therefore \text{Random count rule NOT violated. Sling COULD remain in service on this criterion alone.} $$

**Step 3 — Strand-Concentrated Broken-Wire Check (3 marks):**

Per ASME B30.9 / KTU code: **5 or more broken wires in a single strand in one lay** → RETIRE.

Broken wires in one strand = 4. Since $4 < 5$:

$$ \text{Strand concentration rule NOT violated on its own.} $$

**Final Decision:**

Even though the broken-wire criteria are not individually violated, the **diameter reduction criterion IS violated** (6.07% > 5%). The sling **MUST BE RETIRED** based on the diameter rule.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Angle Convention Confusion:** Students often write $T = \frac{W}{n \cos\theta}$ instead of $\frac{W}{n \sin\theta}$. This is the **#1 reason for losing marks** in sling-tension problems. **Always** confirm whether $\theta$ is measured from the **horizontal** (use sine) or from the **vertical** (use cosine).
> 2. **Forgetting the 2x factor in basket hitch:** A basket hitch shares the load differently from a vertical hitch, and students frequently miss the factor of 2 in the denominator.
> 3. **Mixing up percentage rules:** 5% applies to **diameter reduction** in wire rope; 10% applies to **chain link wear**; 5% applies to **chain link elongation**. Students confuse these regularly.
> 4. **Not stating "competent person" terminology:** Always use the exact phrase **"designated person"** for frequent inspection and **"competent person"** for periodic inspection — these are formally defined roles.
> 5. **Ignoring the broken-wire strand rule:** A sling can pass the *random* wire test (e.g., 8 wires broken) but still fail because 5 of them are in the *same strand*. Always check both rules.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert kg to N:** A $1500 \, \text{kg}$ load becomes $14{,}715 \, \text{N}$ — students sometimes substitute the mass directly into the tension formula, which is dimensionally wrong.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 12 Must-Remember Points for KTU 2024 Sling Inspection Exam:**

- [ ] **Sling** = flexible lifting accessory; NOT a standalone lifting device. Always used with a crane/hoist.
- [ ] **5 main types:** Chain, Wire Rope, Web (synthetic flat), Round (synthetic endless), Metal Mesh.
- [ ] **Three inspection tiers:** Initial (operator) → Frequent (designated person) → Periodic (competent person).
- [ ] **Wire rope broken-wire rule:** 10 random wires OR 5 in one strand in one lay → RETIRE.
- [ ] **Wire rope diameter rule:** > 5% reduction → RETIRE. Use $D_{\text{limit}} = 0.95 \cdot D_{\text{nominal}}$.
- [ ] **Chain wear rule:** > 10% diameter loss → RETIRE.
- [ ] **Chain stretch rule:** > 5% length increase → RETIRE.
- [ ] **Synthetic sling rule:** Any cut, melt, or chemical damage → RETIRE (no tolerance).
- [ ] **Sling angle formula:** $T = \frac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ where $\theta$ is from **horizontal**.
- [ ] **Basket hitch formula:** $T = \frac{W}{2 n \sin\theta}$ (extra factor of 2).
- [ ] **Minimum recommended sling angle:** **60° from horizontal** (30° from vertical).
- [ ] **Choker hitch capacity loss:** approximately **20-25%** compared to vertical hitch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Mnemonic — "5-10-10":**
> - **5%** wire rope diameter loss → Retire
> - **10** random broken wires in one lay → Retire
> - **10%** chain link wear → Retire
> (And remember the "5-5" twin: **5** broken wires in **one strand**, **5%** chain link elongation.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining the Topic: "Types" in Material Handling Safety

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course: OESFT833 – Safety in Material Handling)**, Module 2 deals with the **classification of material handling equipment** and the **types of hazards** associated with each. The word "Types" here refers to the taxonomic grouping of:

- **Material Handling Equipment (MHE)** — machines used to move, store, control, and protect materials throughout manufacturing, warehousing, distribution, and disposal.
- **Lifting Appliances & Lifting Gear** — devices used to raise, lower, or transport loads.
- **Associated Hazards** — mechanical, electrical, ergonomic, and environmental risks specific to each category.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Material Handling Equipment (MHE) is defined as any mechanical device or system used to transport, lift, position, or store goods within a workplace, encompassing both powered and non-powered machinery employed in industrial, construction, and logistics operations.*

### 1.2 The Master Classification Hierarchy

Material handling equipment is broadly classified into **five major families** based on the *principle of operation* and the *function* they perform:

| S.No. | Family | Primary Function | Typical Examples |
|-------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Industrial Trucks** | Short-distance horizontal transport | Forklifts, Pallet Jacks, Hand Trucks |
| 2 | **Conveyors** | Continuous bulk/unit load movement | Belt, Roller, Chain, Screw |
| 3 | **Cranes & Hoists** | Vertical & combined vertical–horizontal lifting | Overhead, Mobile, Tower, Jib |
| 4 | **Monorails & Automated Systems** | Fixed-path or guided movement | AGVs, Monorail Hoists, AS/RS |
| 5 | **Non-Powered / Manual Equipment** | Human-assisted handling | Dollies, Wheelbarrows, Hand Pallet Trucks |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Analogy" 🏥

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine a **hospital**. Doctors, nurses, stretchers, ambulances, IV stands, and elevators all *handle* patients (the "material"). Each tool exists for a specific task:
> - **Stretchers** = conveyors (continuous patient flow in corridors)
> - **Ambulances** = industrial trucks (transport between facilities)
> - **Cranes/elevators** = hoists (vertical movement between floors)
> - **Wheelchairs** = manual handling (human-powered)
> 
> Just as you would *never* move a critical patient in a wheelbarrow, you must **match the right equipment to the right load** — this matching is the heart of material handling safety.

---

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Safety Engineering

A KTU examiner expects students to know that **each equipment type has a unique hazard profile**. A forklift accident is fundamentally different from a conveyor entanglement, and the **PPE, guarding strategy, and inspection regime** differ accordingly.

### Key Standard Metric (Industry-Wide)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Factor (FoS)** is the universally applied metric for equipment selection:
>
> $$\text{Safety Factor} = \frac{\text{Ultimate Breaking Load}}{\text{Safe Working Load (SWL)}}$$
>
> - **Cranes & Hoists:** FoS = **2.0 minimum** (wheels/blocks); **1.5** for structural parts
> - **Wire Rope Slings:** FoS = **3.0 to 5.0**
> - **Chains:** FoS = **4.0 minimum**
> - **Synthetic Webbing Slings:** FoS = **5.0 to 7.0**

---

## 1.5 Five Foundational Categories — At a Glance

### Category 1: Industrial Trucks 🚜
Self-propelled vehicles used to push, pull, lift, stack, and transport loads. Powered by electric batteries, LPG, diesel, or CNG.

### Category 2: Conveyors 🏭
Mechanical systems that continuously transport materials over fixed paths using belts, rollers, chains, or vibration.

### Category 3: Cranes & Hoists 🏗️
Machines that lift and lower loads using ropes, chains, or hydraulic cylinders. Includes mobile, tower, overhead, and jib variants.

### Category 4: Automated & Guided Systems 🤖
Computer-controlled vehicles and storage systems (AGVs, AS/RS) that move materials without direct human operation.

### Category 5: Manual & Non-Powered Tools 🛒
Simple devices relying on human energy: hand trucks, dollies, manual pallet jacks, drum lifters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Equipment Type

### 2.1.1 Industrial Trucks (Forklift Family)

**Sub-Types:**

1. **Counterbalance Forklift** — most common; load counterbalanced by rear weight.
2. **Reach Truck** — mast extends forward; used in narrow aisles.
3. **Order Picker** — operator rises with the load to pick items.
4. **Pallet Jack (Electric & Manual)** — low-lift, walkie-type.
5. **Side Loader** — forks move laterally for long loads.
6. **Rough Terrain Forklift** — construction sites with uneven surfaces.

**Operational Hazards:**
- Tip-over (most fatal forklift accident mode)
- Pedestrian collision
- Falling load from mast
- Carbon monoxide (combustion engines indoors)

**Safety Systems (KTU Expected Knowledge):**
- Load backrest extension
- Seatbelt and operator restraint
- Horn, beacon, reverse alarm
- Mast tilt and lift cut-out
- Stability triangle (Tipping Center of Gravity → TCG)

---

### 2.1.2 Conveyors

**Sub-Types:**

| Conveyor Type | Load Type | Hazard Profile |
|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| Belt | Bulk, unit | Nip points, run-on points |
| Roller | Cartons, totes | Rolling nip |
| Chain | Heavy unit | Pull-in, shear |
| Screw | Powder, granules | Entanglement at trough |
| Pneumatic | Granules, chips | Pressure rupture, dust explosion |

**Critical Safety Components:**
- **Emergency Pull Cords** (along entire length)
- **Tongue/Finger Guards** at transfer points
- **Head/Tail Pulley Guards**
- **Troughing Idler Protection**
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) points** at drive

---

### 2.1.3 Cranes — The Detailed Hierarchy

```
Cranes
├── Static (Fixed Base)
│   ├── Overhead Bridge Crane
│   ├── Gantry Crane
│   ├── Jib Crane (Wall-mounted / Pillar)
│   └── Tower Crane (Construction)
└── Mobile
    ├── Crawler Crane
    ├── Truck-mounted Crane
    ├── Rough Terrain Crane
    └── All-Terrain Crane
```

**Key Safety Devices (KTU High-Yield):**
- **Anti-Two-Block (A2B) Device** — prevents hook block from contacting boom tip
- **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** — displays load vs. radius
- **Anti-collision system** — for overhead cranes sharing runway
- **Limit Switches** — upper/lower hoist, travel limits
- **Outrigger pads & level indicators**

---

### 2.1.4 Hoists (Sub-class of Cranes)

**Types:**
- **Manual Chain Hoist** — hand-chain operated
- **Electric Wire Rope Hoist**
- **Electric Chain Hoist**
- **Pneumatic Hoist**
- **Lever Hoist (Come-Along)**

**Hoist Safety Hierarchy:**

$$\text{Hoist Selection} = f(\text{Load}, \text{Lift Height}, \text{Duty Cycle}, \text{Environment})$$

---

### 2.1.5 Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems (AS/RS)

- Unit-load AS/RS
- Mini-load AS/RS (for bins/totes)
- Carousel systems (horizontal & vertical)
- AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)

**Hazard Class:** *Different* — these involve **software/control faults**, **collision avoidance failures**, and **battery hazards**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU ESE numerical questions (commonly 3–7 marks).**

### Formula Table

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\text{SWL} = \frac{\text{Breaking Load}}{\text{FoS}}$ | Safe Working Load | All lifting gear |
| 2 | $T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$ | Sling tension (vertical sling angle θ from horizontal) | Rigging calculations |
| 3 | $L_M = W \times R$ | Load Moment (W = load, R = radius) | Crane stability check |
| 4 | $\text{Max\%} = \frac{R_{\max} - R_{\min}}{R_{\max}} \times 100$ | Maximum slope for crane travel | Outrigger setup |
| 5 | $T_h = W \cdot \tan^{-1}(D)$ | Horizontal pull on rigging | Skidding loads |
| 6 | $W_c = \frac{W \cdot L_2}{L_1 + L_2}$ | Centre of gravity location | Multi-leg sling |
| 7 | $\text{Tip-Over Ratio} = \frac{W_{\text{front}}}{W_{\text{rear}}} \geq 1.0$ | Forklift stability | Truck operation |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{\text{Output Power}}{\text{Input Power}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | Hoist selection |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Impact:**
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.176** (USA) — governs forklift/mater handling in industry
> - **IS 3177 & IS 807** (India) — code for cranes and hoists
> - **Factories Act 1948 (Section 21, 28)** — Indian statutory requirement for safety in material handling
>
> Engineers in plant design, logistics, and manufacturing operations **must** select correct equipment type — a wrong choice can lead to fatal accidents and statutory non-compliance.

---

## 2.4 The "Selection Decision Tree" (KTU Thinking Framework)

```
START
  │
  ├── Is the load continuous? ──── YES ──► CONVEYOR
  │
  ├── Is the load > 25 kg? ──── YES ──► POWERED MHE
  │                                │
  │                                ├── Vertical lift needed? ── YES ──► CRANE / HOIST
  │                                │
  │                                └── Horizontal transport? ──► FORKLIFT / TRUCK
  │
  ├── Fixed path needed? ──── YES ──► AGV / MONORAIL
  │
  └── Load < 25 kg, infrequent? ──► MANUAL EQUIPMENT
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme places significant weight on **rigging calculations** and **sling tension derivations**. Below is the complete mathematical treatment, written explicitly step-by-step with no skipped algebra.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Sling Tension vs. Sling Angle

### Problem Setup
A load **W** is lifted using **n** identical slings, each making an angle **θ** with the **horizontal**. The slings are symmetrically arranged. Find the tension **T** in each sling.

### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Construction

The load **W** acts vertically downward. Each sling exerts a tension **T** at angle **θ** to the horizontal.

### Step 2 — Resolve the Tension into Components

Each sling's tension has:
- **Horizontal component:** $T \cos\theta$
- **Vertical component:** $T \sin\theta$

By symmetry, the horizontal components of all slings cancel out (equal and opposite).

### Step 3 — Apply Vertical Equilibrium

For static equilibrium, the sum of all vertical components equals the load:

$$\sum F_y = 0$$

$$n \cdot T \sin\theta - W = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve for T

$$n \cdot T \sin\theta = W$$

$$T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$$

> **Final Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
>
> $$\boxed{T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}}$$

### Step 5 — Sensitivity Analysis (Why This Matters for Safety)

As **θ → 0°** (sling nearly horizontal), **sin θ → 0**, and **T → ∞**.

| Sling Angle θ | sin θ | T/W (for 2-leg sling) | Risk Factor |
|---------------|-------|------------------------|-------------|
| 90° (vertical) | 1.000 | 0.500 W | Safest |
| 60° | 0.866 | 0.577 W | Standard |
| 45° | 0.707 | 0.707 W | Acceptable |
| 30° | 0.500 | 1.000 W | **DANGER** |
| 15° | 0.259 | 1.932 W | **CRITICAL** |
| 5°  | 0.087 | 5.74 W  | **FATAL** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common student error is using **θ measured from the vertical**. Always confirm — the standard convention is **θ from the horizontal**. Verify in the problem statement.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Safe Working Load (SWL) Verification

### Given Data
- Breaking Load of wire rope = **200 kN**
- Required Safety Factor = **5**
- Number of legs in sling = **2**
- Sling angle = **45°**

### Step 1 — Compute Single-Leg SWL

$$\text{SWL}_{\text{rope}} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Effective Lift Capacity of Sling Assembly

Using $T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$, solve for W:

$$W = T \cdot n \cdot \sin\theta$$

$$W = 40 \times 2 \times \sin(45°)$$

$$W = 40 \times 2 \times 0.7071$$

$$W = 56.57 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 3 — Verification Check

The maximum liftable load is **56.57 kN**, which is less than the rope's individual capacity (40 kN per leg) because of the angle-induced tension amplification.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Forklift Load Centre & Stability Triangle

### Problem
A forklift has the following specifications:
- Mast (front axle) load limit: **2500 kg**
- Rear axle counterweight: **1500 kg**
- Wheelbase: **L = 1.5 m**
- Load placed at distance **d = 0.6 m** from the front wheels

**Find:** Maximum safe load at distance **d**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Stability Triangle (Tipping Axis)

The tipping axis passes through the **front axle** of the forklift. The **Load Centre Distance (LCD)** is the horizontal distance from the front wheels to the centre of gravity of the load.

### Step 2 — Apply Moment Equilibrium about Tipping Axis

For the forklift to be stable, the **counter-moment** of the rear weight must exceed the **tipping moment** of the load:

$$W_c \cdot L \geq W \cdot d$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Maximum Load

$$W_{\max} = \frac{W_c \cdot L}{d}$$

$$W_{\max} = \frac{1500 \times 1.5}{0.6}$$

$$W_{\max} = \frac{2250}{0.6} = 3750 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Rated Capacity Limit

Since the mast limit is **2500 kg**, the controlling limit is:

$$W_{\text{safe}} = \min(2500, 3750) = \mathbf{2500 \text{ kg}}$$

### Step 5 — Reduced Capacity at Higher Load Centre

If the load centre increases to **0.8 m**:

$$W_{\text{stab}} = \frac{1500 \times 1.5}{0.8} = 2812.5 \text{ kg}$$

Mast still controls → 2500 kg.

If load centre = **1.0 m**:

$$W_{\text{stab}} = \frac{1500 \times 1.5}{1.0} = 2250 \text{ kg}$$

Now **stability controls** → forklift can lift only **2250 kg**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Takeaway:** As the load centre increases, the safe lifting capacity decreases **non-linearly**. This is why forklift nameplates always show a **load vs. centre chart**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Equipment Types

| Criterion | Forklift | Conveyor | Crane | AGV/AS/RS | Manual |
|-----------|----------|----------|-------|-----------|--------|
| **Initial Cost** | Medium | High | High | Very High | Low |
| **Operating Cost** | Medium | Low | High | Low | Low (but labour) |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium | Programmed | Low |
| **Throughput** | Medium | Very High | Low | High | Low |
| **Hazard Severity** | High (tip-over) | Medium (entanglement) | Very High (falling load) | Medium (collision) | Medium (musculoskeletal) |
| **Skill Required** | Licensed Operator | Maintenance Tech | Licensed Operator | Programming/Maintenance | Basic Training |
| **Statutory Inspection** | Daily | Weekly | Quarterly (IS 3177) | Daily | Visual |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Practice: Multi-Leg Sling with Unequal Loads

A load of **W = 12 kN** is lifted by a **3-legged sling**. Leg lengths are: $L_1 = 2$ m, $L_2 = 2$ m, $L_3 = 1$ m. All legs attach to a common ring.

### Step 1 — Sling Angle Calculation

Using small-angle approximation for vertical slings:

$$\theta_i = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{L_{\text{vertical}}}{L_i}\right)$$

Assuming vertical attachment height = 1.5 m:

$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.5}{2}\right) = 36.87°$$

$$\theta_3 = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.5}{1}\right) = 56.31°$$

### Step 2 — Horizontal Equilibrium

Let the ring shift by a small horizontal distance **x** due to uneven loading. By force balance:

$$T_1 \cos\theta_1 = T_2 \cos\theta_2 = T_3 \cos\theta_3$$

$$\Rightarrow T_1 = T_2 \text{ (by symmetry)}$$

$$T_3 = T_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\theta_1}{\cos\theta_3}$$

### Step 3 — Vertical Equilibrium

$$2T_1 \sin\theta_1 + T_3 \sin\theta_3 = W$$

$$2T_1 \sin(36.87°) + T_1 \cdot \frac{\cos(36.87°)}{\cos(56.31°)} \cdot \sin(56.31°) = 12$$

### Step 4 — Solve Numerically

$$2T_1 (0.6) + T_1 \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.555} \cdot 0.832 = 12$$

$$1.2 T_1 + 1.2 T_1 = 12$$

$$2.4 T_1 = 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_1 = 5 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 5 — Compute All Tensions

$$T_1 = T_2 = 5 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_3 = T_1 \cdot \frac{\cos(36.87°)}{\cos(56.31°)} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.8}{0.555} = 7.21 \text{ kN}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Insight:** The shortest sling carries the **highest load**. This is the principle behind the **80% load reduction** rule when using bridle slings with unequal legs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Material Handling Equipment] --> B[Industrial Trucks]
    A --> C[Conveyors]
    A --> D[Cranes and Hoists]
    A --> E[Automated Systems]
    A --> F[Manual Equipment]

    B --> B1[Counterbalance Forklift]
    B --> B2[Reach Truck]
    B --> B3[Pallet Jack]
    B --> B4[Order Picker]
    B --> B5[Rough Terrain Forklift]

    C --> C1[Belt Conveyor]
    C --> C2[Roller Conveyor]
    C --> C3[Chain Conveyor]
    C --> C4[Screw Conveyor]
    C --> C5[Pneumatic Conveyor]

    D --> D1[Overhead Bridge Crane]

```

    end

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are model questions matching **KTU 2024 Part A pattern** — short, definition-oriented, with concise model answers.

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Classify material handling equipment with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Material handling equipment is classified into **five major categories**:

1. **Industrial Trucks** (e.g., Forklift, Pallet Jack) — for short-distance horizontal transport.
2. **Conveyors** (e.g., Belt Conveyor, Roller Conveyor) — for continuous movement of bulk/unit loads.
3. **Cranes & Hoists** (e.g., Overhead Crane, Wire Rope Hoist) — for vertical lifting operations.
4. **Automated Systems** (e.g., AGVs, AS/RS) — for programmed, unmanned material flow.
5. **Manual Equipment** (e.g., Hand Truck, Drum Lifter) — for human-powered small-scale handling.

*(1 mark for naming categories + 2 marks for examples with correct pairing)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Safe Working Load (SWL) and explain its importance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Safe Working Load (SWL)** is the maximum load that a piece of lifting equipment is certified to lift under specified service conditions, calculated by dividing the breaking load by an appropriate **factor of safety**.

$$\text{SWL} = \frac{\text{Breaking Load}}{\text{Factor of Safety}}$$

**Importance:**
- Prevents overloading accidents (fatal in crane operations).
- Provides statutory compliance under **Factories Act 1948** and **IS 3177**.
- Forms the basis of daily pre-use inspection (operator must check SWL marking before lift).

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for importance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B offers **internal choice** between two full questions. Two complete alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of **cranes** used in industrial material handling with neat sketches and their typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Cranes are classified into **static (fixed-base)** and **mobile** types:

**1. Overhead Bridge Crane (Static)**
- Structure: Bridge girder spanning the bay, mounted on end-carriages running on elevated runway rails.
- Lifting mechanism: Trolley with hoist traverses along the bridge.
- Applications: Heavy machine shops, steel mills, warehouses.
- Safety devices: Anti-collision, travel limit switches, overload limiter.

**2. Gantry Crane (Static)**
- Similar to bridge crane but supported on legs running on ground rails.
- Applications: Outdoor storage yards, shipyards, container terminals.
- Safety: Wheel clamps, rail end buffers, anti-derailment.

**3. Jib Crane (Static)**
- A horizontal arm (jib) with hoist mounted on a wall bracket or pillar.
- Applications: Workstation-specific lifting, machine loading.
- Safety: Slew limit, hoist upper/lower limits.

**4. Mobile Cranes (Crawler, Truck-mounted, Rough Terrain, All-Terrain)**
- Mounted on crawlers or wheeled carriers.
- Applications: Construction sites, field erection, multi-location lifts.
- Critical safety: **Outrigger pads, level indicators, Load Moment Indicator (LMI), Anti-Two-Block (A2B) device.**

**5. Tower Crane (Static / Construction)**
- Vertical mast with horizontal jib; slewing at top.
- Applications: High-rise construction.
- Safety: Anemometer (wind speed), slewing limits, climbing/tie-in systems.

*(Award 1 mark per crane type with sketch mention + 2 marks for applications and safety devices)*

---

**(b)** A wire rope with an ultimate breaking load of **150 kN** is used in a 2-leg sling. The slings are inclined at **30° to the horizontal**. Calculate:
- (i) The tension in each sling leg. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The maximum safe load that can be lifted. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on the safety of the operation. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- $W = ?$ (unknown)
- $n = 2$ (number of legs)
- $\theta = 30°$
- Breaking Load $= 150$ kN
- Standard FoS for wire rope $= 5$

**Step 2 — Calculate SWL of single rope:**

$$\text{SWL} = \frac{150}{5} = 30 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Tension per leg formula:**

$$T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$$

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(30°)} = \frac{W}{2 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{W}{1} = W$$

So **T = W** (each sling carries the full load at 30° angle).

**Step 4 — (ii) Maximum safe load:**
Since each sling can carry $\text{SWL} = 30$ kN:

$$W_{\max} = T \cdot n \cdot \sin\theta = 30 \times 2 \times 0.5 = 30 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Safety comment:**

At $\theta = 30°$, the sling tension equals the full load, providing **zero margin** for dynamic effects. According to IS 3177 and standard rigging practice, the **minimum recommended sling angle is 45°** to maintain a safety margin. At 30°:
- A small swing or jerk could exceed SWL.
- The angle-induced amplification reduces effective capacity.
- **Recommendation:** Increase sling length to achieve $\theta \geq 45°$ or use a stronger rope.

*(Award 1 mark for SWL, 2 marks for tension equation, 1 mark for numerical result, 1 mark for safety commentary)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **forklift truck**. Discuss its stability triangle and the factors affecting safe load lifting capacity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A forklift truck is a powered industrial truck equipped with a vertical mast and two forks for lifting and transporting palletized loads. The load is counterbalanced by a rear-mounted counterweight, allowing lifting without outriggers.

**Components:**
- Mast (single/two/three-stage)
- Forks (standard length 1.07 m)
- Counterweight (rear)
- Operator cabin with overhead guard
- Hydraulic lift cylinder
- Tilt cylinder (forward/backward mast tilt)

**Stability Triangle:**
The forklift rests on a **three-point support** (two front wheels + single rear pivot axle). The imaginary triangle formed is called the **stability triangle**. The **front axle forms the tipping axis**. As long as the combined centre of gravity of the truck + load remains inside this triangle, the forklift is stable.

**Factors Affecting Safe Lifting Capacity:**

1. **Load Centre Distance (LCD):** As the load centre moves forward, capacity decreases.
2. **Lift Height:** Higher lifts shift CG upward, reducing stability.
3. **Mast Tilt:** Forward tilt increases tipping moment.
4. **Travel Speed:** Sudden stops/starts create dynamic loads.
5. **Surface Conditions:** Inclines, slippery floors reduce stability.
6. **Tyre Condition:** Worn tyres alter stability triangle.
7. **Load Weight vs. Rated Capacity:** The rated capacity is the ultimate governing parameter.

**Safety Systems:**
- Load backrest extension
- Seatbelt
- Operator presence sensor
- Mast tilt and lift cut-outs
- Stability control (advanced)

*(Award 2 marks for diagram + 2 marks for stability triangle + 3 marks for factors with explanation)*

---

**(b)** A load of **W = 20 kN** is to be lifted using a **3-legged sling**. The legs make angles of **60°, 45°, and 30°** with the horizontal. Determine the tension in each leg. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify unknowns:**
- $T_1$ (at $\theta_1 = 60°$)
- $T_2$ (at $\theta_2 = 45°$)
- $T_3$ (at $\theta_3 = 30°$)

**Step 2 — Assume symmetric load distribution assumption:**
For a *3-legged sling attached to a freely rotating ring*, the horizontal components are balanced by the geometry. Use the standard approximation:

$$\frac{T_i}{\sin\theta_i} = \text{constant (k)}$$

This means **steeper legs carry less tension, shallower legs carry more**.

**Step 3 — Sum of vertical components = W:**

$$\sum T_i \sin\theta_i = W$$

$$T_1 \sin 60° + T_2 \sin 45° + T_3 \sin 30° = 20$$

$$0.866\,T_1 + 0.707\,T_2 + 0.500\,T_3 = 20 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the proportionality relation:**

$$T_i = k \sin\theta_i$$

$$\Rightarrow T_1 = k \sin 60° = 0.866\,k$$

$$T_2 = k \sin 45° = 0.707\,k$$

$$T_3 = k \sin 30° = 0.500\,k$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into equation (i):**

$$(0.866)^2 k + (0.707)^2 k + (0.500)^2 k = 20$$

$$0.750\,k + 0.500\,k + 0.250\,k = 20$$

$$1.500\,k = 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = 13.333$$

**Step 6 — Compute individual tensions:**

$$T_1 = 0.866 \times 13.333 = 11.55 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_2 = 0.707 \times 13.333 = 9.43 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_3 = 0.500 \times 13.333 = 6.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Verification:**

$$11.55 \times 0.866 + 9.43 \times 0.707 + 6.67 \times 0.500$$

$$= 10.00 + 6.67 + 3.33 = 20.00 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly:
> 1. Use **θ from the vertical** instead of horizontal — wrong answer.
> 2. Forget to apply the proportionality rule for uneven sling angles.
> 3. Round off prematurely (use **4 decimal places** for trigonometric values).
> 4. Miss the **verification step** — always substitute back to confirm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Types):**

### Classification
- ☐ Five major families of MHE: **Trucks, Conveyors, Cranes, Automated, Manual**
- ☐ Sub-types of each family (at least 3 examples per family)

### Forklifts
- ☐ Stability triangle = front axle + rear pivot axle
- ☐ Capacity reduces with **increasing load centre distance**
- ☐ Most fatal mode = **tip-over**

### Conveyors
- ☐ Critical hazards = **nip points, run-on points, head/tail pulleys**
- ☐ Mandatory: emergency pull cords, pulley guards, LOTO points

### Cranes
- ☐ LMI = Load Moment Indicator
- ☐ A2B = Anti-Two-Block device
- ☐ Static vs Mobile classification
- ☐ Mandatory quarterly inspection (IS 3177)

### Sling Calculations
- ☐ Master formula: $T = \frac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ — θ measured from horizontal
- ☐ **Sling angle ≥ 45°** for safe practice
- ☐ Sling tension **increases** as angle decreases from vertical
- ☐ Shortest sling in multi-leg arrangement carries **highest tension**

### Safety Factors (Industry Standards)
- ☐ Wire rope slings: **FoS = 3 to 5**
- ☐ Chains: **FoS = 4**
- ☐ Cranes (structural): **FoS = 1.5**
- ☐ Synthetic webbing: **FoS = 5 to 7**

### Statutory Compliance (Indian Context)
- ☐ **Factories Act 1948** — Sections 21, 28
- ☐ **IS 3177** — Crane code
- ☐ **IS 807** — Crane design
- ☐ **OSHA 1910.176** — International reference

### Numerical Skills
- ☐ Always verify sling tension numerically
- ☐ State FoS assumption explicitly
- ☐ Show FBD before solving
- ☐ Carry forward at least 3 decimal places in intermediate steps

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Attachment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Method of Attachment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Method of Attachment** refers to the engineered and systematic procedures, hardware, and rigging configurations employed to securely connect a load to a lifting device (such as a crane, hoist, or winch) so that the load can be safely and stably hoisted, lowered, or transported without slipping, tipping, or structural failure of the connection. It encompasses sling selection, hitch configuration, hardware specification (shackles, eye-bolts, hooks, links), and the calculation of tensions acting on each leg of the rigging assembly. > [!IMPORTANT] > In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Method of Attachment* is the bridge between **the lifting machine** (crane/hoist) and **the load**. Failure at this interface is the single most common cause of material handling accidents worldwide. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine holding a heavy shopping bag with both hands. - If you hold the handles straight down (vertical), the weight is distributed evenly and your hands feel only the bag's weight (**vertical hitch**). - If you pull the handles outward at an angle to lift the bag, your hands feel **much more force** than the bag's actual weight, because the force is split into vertical (supporting weight) and horizontal (pulling outward) components (**basket hitch at an angle**). - If you loop one handle around the bag and pull (**choker hitch**), the force is even greater because of friction losses and geometry. > [!NOTE] > The *Method of Attachment* is precisely the engineering science of these everyday intuitions — calculating how sling angle, hitch type, and hardware change the actual tension in the rigging compared to the static weight of the load. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology - **Working Load Limit (WLL)** — bold — the maximum load a sling or attachment hardware is rated to lift under ideal service conditions. Unit: **kg** or **tonne (t)**. - **D/d Ratio** — bold — the ratio of the sling diameter (D) to the load diameter (d); affects sling body strength. - **Sling Angle (θ)** — bold — the angle between the sling leg and the horizontal plane. **θ = 90°** is a vertical lift. - **Safety Factor (SF)** — bold — typically **5:1** for wire rope slings, **4:1** for chain slings, and **5:1 to 7:1** for synthetic web slings. - **Vertical, Choker, and Basket Hitches** — bold — the three fundamental hitch configurations. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Methods of Attachment Methods of attachment are classified along three engineering axes: ### A. By Hardware (Sling Type) 1. **Wire Rope Slings** — Made of stranded steel wire; high strength, durable, resistant to abrasion. Common in heavy industry. 2. **Chain Slings** — Alloy steel links; resistant to heat and cutting, used in steel mills and foundries. 3. **Synthetic Web Slings (Nylon/Polyester)** — Lightweight, flexible, will not mar polished surfaces; used for finished goods. 4. **Metal Mesh Slings** — Used for hot or abrasive loads. 5. **Fiber Rope Slings** — Manila, sisal, polypropylene; limited to lighter duty and safer environments. ### B. By Hitch Configuration (the most exam-relevant classification) 1. **Vertical Hitch** — A single sling leg attached to a single hook directly above the load center of gravity. Tension = Load weight. 2. **Choker Hitch** — The sling passes around the load and one end passes through (or under) the other end, forming a choker point. The sling grips the load by friction. Reduces effective capacity by a factor (typically to **75% of vertical capacity**, called the *Choker Hitch Factor*). 3. **Basket Hitch** — The sling passes under the load with both eyes connected to the hook above. The load is supported at two points. Capacity depends directly on the sling angle. 4. **Bridle Hitch (Multi-Leg Sling)** — Two, three, or four sling legs joined by a master link or shackle to a single hook. Used for irregular or long loads. ### C. By Termination Hardware - Eye-and-eye slings, endless slings, shackles, eye-bolts (shoulder vs. regular), swivel hooks, master links, and connecting links. ## 2.2 Why Sling Angle Governs Capacity — The Core "Why" When a sling leg is pulled at an angle θ from the horizontal, the tension **T** in that leg has two components: - **Vertical component** (T·sin θ) — supports part of the load. - **Horizontal component** (T·cos θ) — squeezes the load inward. Because only the vertical component holds the weight up, the **tension must be larger than the weight it supports**. The smaller the angle θ, the larger the tension for the same vertical support — this is why shallow sling angles are dangerous. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Use `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` inside tables to avoid breaking markdown syntax. | Hitch Type | Tension Formula | Capacity Factor (vs Vertical) | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Vertical Hitch | $T = W$ | **1.00** | Direct lift, single leg | | Choker Hitch | $T = \dfrac{W}{C_h}$ | **$C_h = 0.75$** (typical) | Friction-dependent; *C_h* = choker factor | | Basket Hitch (1 sling) | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\sin\theta}$ | Angle measured from horizontal | | 2-Leg Bridle | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 n \sin\theta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2n \sin\theta}$ | *n* = number of sling legs bearing load | | 3-Leg Bridle | $T = \dfrac{W}{3 \sin\theta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3\sin\theta}$ | For symmetric loads | | 4-Leg Bridle | $T = \dfrac{W}{4 \sin\theta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{4\sin\theta}$ | For symmetric loads | | Sling Angle Factor (k) | $k = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$ | Multiplies WLL reduction | See angle table below | | D/d Ratio Factor | $f_{D/d}$ from table | Reduces sling body capacity | See D/d table below | ### Sling Angle vs. Reduction in Capacity (Vertical Capacity Multiplier) | Horizontal Sling Angle θ | Multiplier (1 / sin θ) | Effective Vertical Lift Capacity | |---|---|---| | **90°** (vertical) | **1.00** | **100%** | | **60°** | **1.155** | **~86.6%** | | **45°** | **1.414** | **~70.7%** | | **30°** | **2.000** | **50%** | | **15°** | **3.864** | **~25.9%** | > [!NOTE] > **The 30° Rule of Thumb**: A KTU-favored practical guideline — *never use a sling angle less than 30° from horizontal.* Below this, tensions climb dramatically and horizontal squeezing forces can damage the load or sling. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Shipyards and Steel Plants**: Multi-leg bridle hitches on plate and billet loads. - **Construction**: Basket hitches on concrete pipes; vertical hitches on rebar cages. - **Port Logistics**: Container spreaders use 4-leg bridles with precise angle calculations. - **Power Plants**: Turbine rotor lifts use matched-pair sling sets with verified WLL. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Tension in a Single Basket Hitch ### Setup Consider a load of weight **W** suspended by a single sling forming a basket. Both ends of the sling meet at the hook, and the sling makes an angle **θ** with the **horizontal** at the load contact points. ### Free Body Logic 1. The load is in static equilibrium. 2. Each sling leg carries equal tension **T** (by symmetry). 3. Each leg's vertical component = $T \sin\theta$. 4. Sum of vertical components = load weight: $$T \sin\theta + T \sin\theta = W$$ 5. Simplify: $$2 T \sin\theta = W$$ 6. Solve for T: $$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$ This is the **basket-hitch tension formula** appearing in every KTU question paper on this topic. ## 3.2 Derivation for an *n*-Leg Bridle Hitch ### Setup An *n*-leg bridle is attached to a load of weight **W** that is symmetric and balanced, so each leg takes an equal share. Each leg makes angle **θ** with the horizontal. ### Free Body Logic 1. Vertical equilibrium of load: $n \cdot (T \sin\theta) = W$ 2. Solve: $$T = \frac{W}{n \sin\theta}$$ 3. This is the **general bridle formula** — reduce to: - 2-leg: $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$ - 3-leg: $T = \dfrac{W}{3 \sin\theta}$ - 4-leg: $T = \dfrac{W}{4 \sin\theta}$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style 14-Mark Part A) > **Problem**: A steel plate weighing **W = 3000 kg** is lifted using a 2-leg wire-rope sling. The sling angle from the horizontal is **θ = 45°**. The sling's vertical Working Load Limit is **WLL_v = 2000 kg per leg**. Verify whether the lift is safe. ### Solution (Step-by-Step) **Step 1** — Compute tension in each leg: $$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta} = \frac{3000}{2 \sin 45°} = \frac{3000}{2 \times 0.7071} = \frac{3000}{1.4142}$$ $$T = 2121.3 \text{ kg per leg}$$ **Step 2** — Compare with WLL: $$T = 2121.3 \text{ kg} \quad \text{vs.} \quad WLL_v = 2000 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3** — Interpretation: $$T > WLL_v \implies \text{Lift is NOT safe}$$ **Step 4** — Recommendation: - Steepen the angle (use a longer sling), or - Use a sling with higher WLL, or - Reduce the load. **Step 5** — If the angle were **θ = 60°**: $$T = \frac{3000}{2 \sin 60°} = \frac{3000}{2 \times 0.8660} = \frac{3000}{1.732} = 1732.1 \text{ kg}$$ $$T = 1732.1 \text{ kg} < 2000 \text{ kg} \implies \text{Lift IS safe}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Hitch Type Alters Tension for the *Same* Load Assume **W = 1000 kg**, single sling rated **WLL_v = 1000 kg** vertical. | Hitch Type | Angle | Computed T per leg | Safety Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | Vertical (1 leg) | 90° | 1000 kg | At limit (Borderline) | | Basket (1 sling, both eyes up) | 60° | 577.4 kg | Safe | | Basket | 45° | 707.1 kg | Safe | | Basket | 30° | 1000 kg | At limit | | 2-leg Bridle | 30° | 500 kg | Safe | | 2-leg Bridle | 15° | 1932 kg | **UNSAFE** (exceeds WLL) | ## 3.5 D/d Ratio (Sling Diameter to Load Diameter) — Brief Treatment For wire rope slings in a basket hitch around a round object, the *D/d* ratio affects the body strength of the sling. If *D/d < 2*, the sling body can be damaged due to bending stress. - *D/d* ≥ **2** → 100% sling body capacity - *D/d* in [1, 2) → use a sling body efficiency factor (typically 0.86 to 1.00) from a standard table. - *D/d* < 1 → **NOT recommended**; use a spreader beam or larger sling. > [!NOTE] > This concept typically carries 2–3 marks in Part A of the KTU question paper. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Lifting System The figure below shows how the **Method of Attachment** sits as a critical sub-system within the overall material handling chain. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Crane / Hoist
Lifting Machine] --> B[Master Link
or Hook Block] B --> C[Connecting Hardware
Shackle / Sling Hook] C --> D[Method of Attachment
Sling + Hitch Configuration] D --> E[Load
The Object Being Lifted] F[Sling Type Selection
Wire Rope / Chain / Synthetic] --> D G[Hitch Geometry
Vertical / Choker / Basket / Bridle] --> D H[Hardware Capacity
WLL and Safety Factor] --> D I[Sling Angle theta
Sling to Horizontal] --> D J[Inspection
Pre-Use Checks] -.-> D K[Load Weight and CG
Center of Gravity] -.-> D ``` **Reading the diagram**: The crane generates lifting force, which travels through the master link → connecting hardware → **the Method of Attachment (the engineered decision block)** → and finally to the load. Four engineering inputs (F, G, H, I) determine the design of the Method of Attachment block. Two safety inputs (J, K) feed in as preconditions. ## 4.2 Hitch Configuration Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[Vertical Hitch
Single Sling Straight Down] H2[Choker Hitch
Sling Wraps Load] H3[Basket Hitch
Sling Under Load Both Ends Up] H4[Bridle Hitch
Multiple Legs Joined to Master Link] H1 --> R1[T equals W] H2 --> R2[T equals W over 0.75] H3 --> R3[T equals W over 2 sin theta] H4 --> R4[T equals W over n sin theta] ``` ## 4.3 Force Resolution Geometry (Basket Hitch) ```mermaid flowchart TD L[Load Weight W
Acting Vertically Downward] --> V[Vertical Component
T sin theta] L --> HZ[Horizontal Component
T cos theta] S1[Left Sling Leg
Tension T at angle theta] --> V S1 --> HZ S2[Right Sling Leg
Tension T at angle theta] --> V S2 --> HZ V --> EQ[2 T sin theta equals W] HZ --> EQ2[Horizontal forces cancel by symmetry] ``` > [!NOTE] > The horizontal components on the two legs are equal and opposite, hence they cancel and do not affect the load. Only the vertical components support the weight — but the *sling* itself feels the *full* tension **T**, not just the vertical component. This is the engineering reason why sling angle is critical. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define "Method of Attachment" in material handling. List the three principal hitch types. **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks' worth):** *Method of Attachment* is the engineered procedure and hardware used to connect a load to a lifting device, encompassing sling selection, hitch configuration, and connecting hardware to ensure safe, stable, and balanced lifting. The three principal hitch types are: 1. **Vertical Hitch** — direct single-leg connection. 2. **Choker Hitch** — sling wraps around the load; grips by friction. 3. **Basket Hitch** — sling passes under the load with both ends connected to the hook. *(Bonus 1-line for distinction: Bridle Hitch is a multi-leg variant used for long/irregular loads.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is the "Sling Angle Factor"? Why is a sling angle of 30° considered the practical minimum? **Model Answer (≈ 3 marks' worth):** The **Sling Angle Factor** is the multiplier $k = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta}$ that converts the *vertical* Working Load Limit of a sling to its effective capacity at an inclined configuration. For a basket hitch: $$T = k \cdot \frac{W}{2} = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$ At **θ = 30°**, the factor is **k = 2.00**, meaning each sling leg must support twice the vertical share of the load. Below 30°: - Tensions escalate sharply. - Horizontal squeezing forces grow, risking load damage. - Sling body bending stress becomes significant (poor D/d). Hence, **30° is the practical minimum** recommended by rigging safety standards. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) A load of 2.5 tonnes is to be lifted by a 4-leg wire-rope bridle sling. Each leg has a vertical WLL of 1.0 tonne. Find the minimum allowable sling angle from the horizontal for a safe lift. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1** — Equal-leg symmetric bridle tension formula: $$T = \frac{W}{4 \sin\theta}$$ **Step 2** — Safety condition: $T \le WLL$ $$\frac{W}{4 \sin\theta} \le 1.0 \text{ t}$$ **Step 3** — Solve for sin θ: $$\sin\theta \ge \frac{W}{4 \cdot WLL} = \frac{2.5}{4 \times 1.0} = 0.625$$ **Step 4** — Solve for θ: $$\theta \ge \arcsin(0.625) = 38.68°$$ **Step 5** — Practical minimum (round up to safe value): $$\boxed{\theta_{\min} \approx 39°}$$ **Interpretation (extra credit)**: Since this exceeds the 30° rule-of-thumb, the lift is feasible with standard rigging practice. Recommend using a sling angle of **at least 45°** for an additional safety margin. --- #### (b) With neat sketches, differentiate between a Vertical Hitch, a Choker Hitch, and a Basket Hitch. State one typical application of each. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** | Hitch Type | Sketch (described) | Tension Relation | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Vertical Hitch** | Single straight sling, eye to hook directly above load CG. | $T = W$ | Lifting a single eye-bolted machine part. | | **Choker Hitch** | Sling passes around load; one end is looped through the other end at the choker point. | $T = \dfrac{W}{C_h}$, with $C_h \approx 0.75$ | Bundling logs or pipe coils. | | **Basket Hitch** | Sling passes under load; both eyes lifted to the hook. | $T = \dfrac{W}{2\sin\theta}$ | Lifting concrete pipes, drums, or rolled coils. | (Sketch each as a simple labelled diagram showing load, sling, hook, and direction of force.) **Marks split (KTU pattern)**: - 3 marks: clear sketches of three hitches - 2 marks: correct tension expressions - 2 marks: correct real-world applications --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* #### (a) A 2-leg wire-rope sling with vertical WLL of 1500 kg per leg is used in a basket hitch. The sling angle is 60° from horizontal. Find the maximum safe load. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1** — Basket-hitch tension per leg (using 1 sling with 2 legs, i.e., *n* = 2): $$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$ **Step 2** — Safety condition $T \le WLL$: $$\frac{W}{2 \sin 60°} \le 1500 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3** — Solve for W: $$W \le 1500 \times 2 \times \sin 60° = 1500 \times 2 \times 0.8660 = 2598 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 4** — Apply a safety buffer (recommended 80% of calculated max): $$W_{\text{recommended}} = 0.8 \times 2598 \approx 2078 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answer**: $\boxed{W_{\max} \approx 2600 \text{ kg (theoretical); use 2000 kg in practice.}}$ --- #### (b) Explain the D/d ratio for wire-rope slings. What is its significance? (7 marks) **Model Solution:** The **D/d ratio** compares the *diameter of the sling* (D) to the *diameter of the load* (d) over which the sling is bent (in a basket hitch around a cylindrical load). **Significance**: 1. A small D/d ratio (sling too small relative to load) causes **high bending stress** in the sling wires, reducing sling body strength. 2. Standard rigging tables (e.g., ASME B30.9) prescribe efficiency multipliers based on D/d: - **D/d ≥ 2** → 100% body capacity. - **1 ≤ D/d < 2** → reduced capacity (e.g., 86%). - **D/d < 1** → not recommended; use a spreader beam. 3. Practical field test: if you can **fit the sling inside the load circumference with no gap**, D/d is adequate. **Engineering implication**: The D/d ratio is one of the leading causes of *hidden* sling degradation in field operations — slings pass visual inspection but fail at the body under load. **Marks split (KTU pattern)**: - 3 marks: definition and concept - 2 marks: table of D/d vs. efficiency - 2 marks: practical implication --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students lose marks in Method of Attachment problems:** > 1. **Confusing θ from horizontal vs. vertical.** KTU questions almost always state "from the horizontal." Using $\cos\theta$ instead of $\sin\theta$ in the basket-hitch formula is a **2-mark** deduction guaranteed. > 2. **Forgetting the choker factor $C_h = 0.75$** when a choker hitch is used. Students often write $T = W$ even for choker hitches, costing 1–2 marks. > 3. **Treating 4-leg bridles as if all 4 legs always share load equally.** In practice (and in KTU theory), only **3 legs** are considered to share load in a 4-leg bridle (the 4th may go slack due to load shift). Use $n = 3$ for safety-margin calculations unless symmetric lift is explicitly guaranteed. > 4. **Ignoring units** — write the answer in **kg** or **tonnes** as given, and include units throughout the derivation. > 5. **Skipping the angle-justification step** in minimum-angle problems — you must write "$\theta \ge \arcsin(\dots)$" and then box the numerical answer. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Method of Attachment** = sling + hitch + hardware, engineered for a specific load. - **Three primary hitches**: Vertical, Choker, Basket. **Bridle** = multi-leg basket. - **Tension in basket hitch** = $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$; in *n*-leg bridle = $T = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$. - **Choker Hitch Factor** $C_h \approx 0.75$ reduces vertical WLL. - **30° Rule of Thumb** — never sling below 30° from horizontal. - **D/d ratio ≥ 2** is required for full sling body capacity. - **Safety factors** (typical): wire rope = 5:1, chain = 4:1, synthetic = 5:1 to 7:1. - **For 4-leg bridles under asymmetric loading**, design as 3-leg (one slack) for safety. - **Horizontal sling components** cancel by symmetry but contribute to *sling tension* — they do not vanish from the equation. - **Always verify** WLL × Sling Angle Factor ≥ actual computed tension before lifting. - **Pre-lift inspection** is non-negotiable: slings with broken wires, cuts, burns, or kinks must be removed from service.

topics.title: Rated Capacities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rated Capacities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rated Capacity (RC)** is the **maximum load** that a piece of material-handling equipment, lifting accessory, or rigging component is **designed and certified** to lift, suspend, or move under **specific working conditions**, as established by the manufacturer and verified by recognized standards (e.g., **ASME B30**, **IS 4573**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.179**).

In KTU-speak, rated capacity is the **manufacturer's promise of the upper limit** — never to be exceeded in real-life operation. It is sometimes referred to as the **Working Load Limit (WLL)** or **Safe Working Load (SWL)**. The terminology used varies by region and standard:

| Term | Region / Standard | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Rated Capacity (RC)** | ASME (USA) | Maximum load a device is designed to handle |
| **Working Load Limit (WLL)** | Worldwide / OSHA | Maximum load permitted during routine service |
| **Safe Working Load (SWL)** | British / Indian Standards (IS) | Equivalent to WLL |
| **Maximum Allowable Working Load (MAWL)** | Marine / Offshore | Equivalent to WLL |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **hanging bridge** with a placard that reads: **"MAXIMUM 10 PERSONS AT A TIME."** That number is the bridge's **Rated Capacity**. Even if the bridge can physically hold 15 people for a second before snapping, the placard guarantees safe service for **only 10**. Anything beyond that — and the safety margin collapses.

In lifting, the rated capacity is **not** the load at which the chain breaks — that would be the **Ultimate Load**. The rated capacity is far below it, leaving a built-in **safety cushion** (called the *Factor of Safety*).

## 1.3 Why Rated Capacity Matters in Material Handling

> [!NOTE]
> - Every crane, hoist, sling, shackle, and lifting beam comes with a **plate** or **tag** stating its rated capacity.
> - Exceeding rated capacity is the **#1 cause of catastrophic lifting accidents** worldwide.
> - The KTU 2024 module specifically tests a student's ability to **select**, **verify**, and **derate** rated capacities based on **hitch type, sling angle, environmental factors, and shock loads**.

## 1.4 Key Distinctions Students Must Memorize

- **Ultimate Load (Breaking Strength)** = Load at which the component physically fails.
- **Design Factor (DF)** = Ultimate Load ÷ Rated Capacity (always greater than 1).
- **Rated Capacity** = Ultimate Load ÷ Design Factor.

The Design Factor is the **invisible shield** between the user and disaster.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of a Rated Capacity System

The rated capacity of any lifting system is governed by a **four-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Material Strength (Ultimate Tensile Strength of steel/wire/alloy).**
2. **Design Factor** applied by the manufacturer (typically 3:1 to 5:1 for slings; 5:1 for hoist chains).
3. **Hitch and Configuration Factor** (basket, vertical, choker — and sling angle).
4. **Service Derating Factor** (temperature, shock loading, abrasion, repeated cycles).

Each tier **multiplicatively reduces** the safe working limit, ensuring defense-in-depth.

## 2.2 Hitch Types and Their Capacity Multipliers

| Hitch Type | Description | Capacity vs. Single Vertical Leg |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Hitch** | Load supported by a single straight leg | $1.0 \times$ Vertical WLL |
| **Basket Hitch** | Load rests in the bight of the sling | $2.0 \times$ Vertical WLL |
| **Choker Hitch** | Sling passes around the load and through itself | $\approx 0.75 \times$ Basket Capacity |

## 2.3 Effect of Sling Angle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As the sling angle from horizontal **decreases** (sling becomes flatter), the **tension in each leg increases dramatically**, rapidly eroding the rated capacity.

This is the single most tested concept in the KTU module because it is also the most common cause of rigging errors on site.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $WLL = \dfrac{Ultimate\ Load}{Design\ Factor}$ | Working Load Limit | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$ | Tension in each sling leg (vertical) | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |
| 3 | $W_{max} = n \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Maximum load for $n$-leg sling at angle $\theta$ | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |
| 4 | $Effective\ WLL = WLL \times SHOCK\ FACTOR$ | Shock-adjusted WLL | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |
| 5 | $WLL_{basket} = 2 \times WLL_{vertical}$ | Basket hitch capacity | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |
| 6 | $WLL_{choker} = 0.75 \times WLL_{basket}$ | Choker hitch capacity | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |
| 7 | $WLL_{temp} = WLL \times K_{temp}$ | Temperature-derated WLL | $\text{kg}$ or $\text{t}$ |

Where:
- $W$ = Total load weight to be lifted.
- $n$ = Number of sling legs supporting the load.
- $\theta$ = Angle between sling leg and the horizontal.
- $SHOCK\ FACTOR$ = Multiplier based on lifting dynamics (1.0 calm, 1.25 normal, 1.5 jerky).
- $K_{temp}$ = Temperature derating coefficient (0 for above 400 °C, 1.0 below 100 °C).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crane Selection**: Engineers verify that the heaviest pick on site is below the crane's load chart at the given radius.
- **Sling Selection**: Riggers compute the correct sling diameter based on load and angle.
- **Shackle / Hook Verification**: Each fitting must be rated above the tension passing through it.
- **OSHA / Factory Inspector Audits**: Rated capacity tags are mandatory; missing tags halt operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Sling Tension vs. Angle

Let us derive the governing formula for a **two-leg sling** lifting a load of weight $W$.

**Free Body Diagram Setup:**
- Two symmetric sling legs, each making angle $\theta$ with the horizontal.
- Load $W$ hangs vertically, supported equally by both legs.

**Step 1 — Vertical Equilibrium:**
The vertical components of the tensions in both legs must balance $W$.

$$2 \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = W$$

**Step 2 — Solving for Tension $T$:**

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- When $\theta = 90^\circ$ (vertical sling): $T = W/2$ → standard basket hitch.
- When $\theta = 60^\circ$: $T = W / (2 \cdot 0.866) = 0.577 \cdot W$.
- When $\theta = 30^\circ$: $T = W / (2 \cdot 0.5) = W$ → tension equals full load — extremely dangerous.
- When $\theta \to 0^\circ$: $T \to \infty$ → theoretical infinite tension → sling snaps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why **OSHA mandates a minimum sling angle of 30°** from horizontal (i.e., a maximum sling-to-load angle of 60°). Anything below this is a prohibited configuration.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Liftable Load for Multi-Leg Sling

**Step 1 — Starting Point:**
We know the maximum permissible tension in a single leg is the leg's WLL.

$$T_{max} = WLL_{leg}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Vertical Equilibrium:**
For an $n$-leg sling at angle $\theta$:

$$n \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta = W_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Final Working Formula:**

$$W_{max} = n \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Caveat (Asymmetry Loss):**
In real practice, loads rarely balance perfectly across three legs. Standards (e.g., ASME B30.9) treat a **3-leg sling as having only the capacity of 2.5 legs**:

$$W_{max,\ 3-leg,\ practical} = 2.5 \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Capacity Derating for Shock Load

**Problem:**
A hoist is rated at **5 tonnes** WLL. The lifting operation involves a motorized crane **without controlled load acceleration**. The load is **4 tonnes**. Determine if the lift is safe.

**Step 1 — Identify the Shock Factor:**
From ASME B30.9:

$$\text{Motorized crane without controlled load} \rightarrow SHOCK\ FACTOR = 1.5$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Shock Factor:**

$$WLL_{effective} = 5 \text{ t} \div 1.5 = 3.33 \text{ t}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the Load:**

$$Load = 4\ \text{t} > WLL_{effective} = 3.33\ \text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
The lift is **NOT SAFE**. The operator must either reduce the load, increase the hoist capacity, or change to a controlled-acceleration system.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Two-Leg Sling Capacity Calculation

**Problem:**
A two-leg wire-rope sling has a single-leg WLL of **2 tonnes**. The sling is rigged at **60° from horizontal**. Calculate the maximum safe load.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Multi-Leg Formula:**

$$W_{max} = n \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 2 — Insert Values:**

$$W_{max} = 2 \times 2\ \text{t} \times \sin(60^\circ)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate Sine:**

$$\sin(60^\circ) = 0.866$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation:**

$$W_{max} = 4 \times 0.866 = 3.464\ \text{t}$$

**Step 5 — Result:**
The two-leg sling can safely lift a maximum load of **3.46 tonnes** at 60° from horizontal.

## 3.5 Worked Example: Temperature Derating

**Problem:**
A polyester sling is rated 4 t WLL at normal temperature. The load to be lifted is in an environment at **150 °C**. Polyester loses strength above 100 °C with $K_{temp} = 0.85$.

**Step 1 — Apply Temperature Coefficient:**

$$WLL_{derated} = 4\ \text{t} \times 0.85 = 3.4\ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with Load:**

If load $= 3\ \text{t}$ → Lift is safe.
If load $> 3.4\ \text{t}$ → Lift is unsafe; use a higher-rated sling or change material.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rated Capacity Determination Flow (Mermaid)

The following block diagram illustrates the logical flow a safety engineer follows to **arrive at the effective rated capacity** for any lifting configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Total Load W] --> B[Determine Hitch Type]
    B --> C[Select Sling Leg Count n]
    C --> D[Measure Sling Angle theta]
    D --> E[Look up WLL per Leg]
    E --> F[Apply Sling Angle Formula]
    F --> G[Wmax = n times WLLleg times sin theta]
    G --> H{Identify Lifting Dynamics}
    H -->|Calm| I[Shock Factor 1.0]
    H -->|Normal| J[Shock Factor 1.25]
    H -->|Jerky| K[Shock Factor 1.5]
    I --> L[Apply Temperature Check]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Temp within limit?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Effective WLL computed]
    M -->|No| O[Reject or change sling material]
    N --> P{W less than Effective WLL?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Lift APPROVED]
    P -->|No| R[Lift REJECTED]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Capacity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ULT[Ultimate Strength Layer]
        A1[Material Tensile Strength]
    end
    subgraph DF[Design Factor Layer]
        B1[Manufacturer Design Factor 3:1 to 5:1]
    end
    subgraph RC[Rated Capacity Layer]
        C1[Rated Capacity from Manufacturer]
    end
    subgraph HC[Hitch Configuration Layer]
        D1[Vertical]
        D2[Basket]
        D3[Choker]
    end
    subgraph SD[Service Derating Layer]
        E1[Shock Factor]
        E2[Temperature Derating]
        E3[Angle Derating]
    end
    subgraph FW[Final Working WLL]
        F1[Effective Safe Working Load]
    end
    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    D1 --> E1 --> F1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
```

## 4.3 Sling Angle vs. Effective Capacity Bar Chart (Conceptual)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Effective Capacity Multiplier vs. Sling Angle (2-Leg Sling)"
    x-axis "Sling Angle from Horizontal (degrees)"
    y-axis "Effective Capacity (x WLL of single leg)"
    bar [0.00, 0.87, 1.50, 1.73, 1.88, 2.00, 2.00]
```

**Reading the chart:** As the sling angle increases from 0° to 90°, the effective capacity of a two-leg sling rises from 0 to $2 \times WLL_{leg}$. Note how rapidly the capacity falls below 30° — this is why the **30° minimum sling angle rule** is non-negotiable in rigging practice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Rated Capacity" of a lifting device. How is it different from the "Ultimate Load"?**

**Model Answer:**
Rated capacity is the **maximum load** that a lifting device is designed to handle safely under specified service conditions, as determined by the manufacturer. Ultimate load, in contrast, is the load at which the device **physically fails** (breaks). Rated capacity is always **much lower** than the ultimate load because a **design factor** is applied to provide a safety margin:

$$Rated\ Capacity = \frac{Ultimate\ Load}{Design\ Factor}$$

For example, a hoist with 15 t ultimate load and a 5:1 design factor has a rated capacity of **3 tonnes**.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is meant by "Working Load Limit" (WLL)? Mention any two situations where the rated capacity must be derated.**

**Model Answer:**
WLL is the **maximum permissible load** that a sling or lifting accessory may be subjected to during routine service. It is the same as rated capacity.

The rated capacity must be derated when:
1. The **sling angle is below 60°** from horizontal — tension in each leg rises sharply.
2. **Shock loading** is present — sudden lifts, jerks, or motorized operations without controlled acceleration.
3. **High-temperature environments** — synthetic slings lose strength above 100 °C.
4. **Choker hitch** is used — capacity reduces to roughly 75% of basket hitch capacity.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A 3-leg wire-rope sling has a single-leg WLL of 1.5 tonnes. The slings are rigged at 45° from horizontal. The load to be lifted is 4.5 tonnes. A motorized crane without controlled acceleration is used.**

**(a)** Calculate the maximum safe load for this sling configuration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the appropriate shock factor and determine whether the lift is permitted. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Maximum Safe Load at 45°:**

Using the multi-leg sling formula:

$$W_{max} = n \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$$

Substituting $n = 3$, $WLL_{leg} = 1.5$ t, $\theta = 45^\circ$:

$$W_{max} = 3 \times 1.5 \times \sin(45^\circ)$$

$$\sin(45^\circ) = 0.7071$$

$$W_{max} = 4.5 \times 0.7071 = 3.182\ \text{tonnes}$$

However, applying the **ASME practical reduction** (3-leg treated as 2.5-leg):

$$W_{max,\ practical} = 2.5 \times 1.5 \times 0.7071 = 2.65\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Answer:** Maximum safe load $\approx$ **2.65 tonnes**.

**Part (b) — Shock Load Application:**

For a **motorized crane without controlled acceleration**, the shock factor from ASME B30.9 is **1.5**.

$$WLL_{effective} = \frac{2.65\ \text{t}}{1.5} = 1.767\ \text{tonnes}$$

Comparison:

$$Load = 4.5\ \text{t} \gg WLL_{effective} = 1.767\ \text{t}$$

**Conclusion:** The lift is **REJECTED**. The operator must:
- Use a sling with a higher single-leg WLL,
- Increase the sling angle above 60°, or
- Reduce the load.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A polyester round-sling is rated at 3 tonnes WLL in a vertical hitch at ambient temperature. A 2-leg basket hitch is to be used at 30° from horizontal in a workshop at 120 °C. The temperature derating coefficient for polyester at 120 °C is 0.85.**

**(a)** Compute the effective WLL considering basket hitch configuration and sling angle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Apply the temperature derating factor and state whether a 4-tonne load can be lifted. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Effective WLL with Hitch and Angle:**

Basket hitch capacity:

$$WLL_{basket} = 2 \times WLL_{vertical} = 2 \times 3 = 6\ \text{t}$$

Apply sling angle factor at 30°:

$$WLL_{angled} = 6 \times \sin(30^\circ) = 6 \times 0.5 = 3\ \text{t}$$

**Answer:** Configuration-adjusted WLL = **3 tonnes**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that a **30° sling angle** is the **OSHA minimum allowable** — not a recommended angle. At exactly 30°, the capacity drops to **only 50%** of the basket value. Always highlight this in your answer for full marks.

**Part (b) — Temperature Derating:**

Apply $K_{temp} = 0.85$:

$$WLL_{final} = 3 \times 0.85 = 2.55\ \text{tonnes}$$

Comparison with load:

$$Load = 4\ \text{t} > WLL_{final} = 2.55\ \text{t}$$

**Conclusion:** The lift is **REJECTED**. Recommendations:
- Use a higher-rated sling,
- Switch to a heat-resistant sling material (e.g., steel wire rope), or
- Cool the load below 100 °C before lifting.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Rated Capacities:**
>
> - **Rated Capacity (WLL)** = Ultimate Load ÷ Design Factor (typical DF = 3:1 to 5:1).
> - **Three primary hitch types**: Vertical (1×), Basket (2×), Choker (~0.75× Basket).
> - **Multi-leg sling formula**: $W_{max} = n \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$.
> - **3-leg sling practical capacity** = $2.5 \cdot WLL_{leg} \cdot \sin\theta$ (due to asymmetry).
> - **Minimum sling angle** = **30° from horizontal** (OSHA mandate).
> - **Shock Factors (ASME B30.9)**: 1.0 (calm), 1.25 (normal), 1.5 (jerky).
> - **Temperature derating** is mandatory for synthetic slings above 100 °C.
> - **Choker hitch** capacity ≈ 75% of basket hitch capacity.
> - **Effective WLL must always exceed the planned load** — otherwise lift is rejected.
> - Always **inspect** sling tags, shackle markings, and hoist nameplates before any lift.
> - **Common KTU mistake**: Forgetting to convert angle from horizontal; using 60° as if it were 60° from vertical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloy Chain Slings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alloy Chain Slings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alloy Chain Sling (KTU / ASME B30.9 Definition):** An *Alloy Chain Sling* is a lifting sling manufactured from heat-treated alloy steel chain links, designed for overhead lifting of heavy, hot, or abrasive loads where synthetic slings (nylon, polyester) would degrade. Each link is forged, quenched, and tempered to achieve a high strength-to-weight ratio, and the sling assembly consists of one or more chain legs terminated with master links, hooks, or rings on each end.

The **grade** of an alloy chain defines its ultimate tensile strength, working load limit, and permitted usage. The three common grades specified by the National Association of Chain Manufacturers (NACM) and accepted under **ASME B30.9** are:

| Grade | Material | Working Load Limit Factor | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grade 30 (Proof Coil)** | Carbon steel | Lowest | Light utility / non-overhead |
| **Grade 43 (High Test)** | Carbon steel | Medium | Light overhead / industrial |
| **Grade 63** | Carbon steel | Moderate | Overhead lifting |
| **Grade 80 (Transport)** | Alloy steel | High | Truck/rail tie-downs, NOT for overhead lifting |
| **Grade 100** | Alloy steel | Higher | Overhead lifting, mining, construction |
| **Grade 120** | Alloy steel | Highest | Premium overhead lifting |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** *Grade 80 chain* (often sold as "alloy" chain) is **NOT** rated for overhead lifting in most international standards. Always use **Grade 100 or Grade 120** for overhead material-handling slings. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a chain sling as a **team of load-bearing ropes working in unison**. If one rope is too weak, the load crushes the weakest link — *literally*. The "alloy" in the chain is like reinforcing a regular cotton rope with embedded steel fibres: the chain is heated and cooled in a controlled way so that its internal crystal structure becomes incredibly strong, allowing a thin metal ring to lift a load **4 to 5 times** its own weight safely.

Think of each chain **link** as a tiny, forged **miniature handshake** with its neighbour — every interface is symmetric, distributing load around its entire circular cross-section. Because of this geometry, the chain is rigid, doesn't stretch like a wire rope, and won't slip like a fibre sling when lifting a hot ingot from a furnace.

## 1.3 Key Standard Reference Metrics

- **Design (Safety) Factor for Overhead Lifting:** **3.0 to 5.0** (varies by standard — ASME sets **3.0** for Grade 100, EN 818 sets **4.0**).
- **Working Load Limit (WLL):** The maximum load the sling is **authorised** to lift under specific service conditions.
- **Proof Load:** Typically **2× WLL** for alloy chain — the load to which every new chain is tested before release.
- **Minimum Temperature:** −**40 °C** (below this, alloy steel becomes brittle).
- **Maximum Recommended Temperature:** **400 °C** (above this, the working load must be derated).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — How an Alloy Chain Sling Carries a Load

A chain sling is fundamentally a **flexible tension member**. When a load $W$ is suspended from a sling, the load is transferred through the following sequential path:

1. **Load attachment** → the **hook / master link / oblong link** on the load end.
2. **Tension transfer** through the **end link** into the **first full chain link**.
3. Each chain link is in **pure hoop tension** (ignoring its own weight) and **carries the same axial load** along the leg.
4. The **master link** (top ring) redistributes the load equally to all chain legs, provided the load is **symmetric and balanced**.
5. The **shackle / crane hook** bears the resultant vertical force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why alloy instead of mild steel?** Alloying elements such as *Chromium (Cr)*, *Nickel (Ni)*, and *Molybdenum (Mo)* produce fine-grain microstructures after quenching and tempering. This increases **ultimate tensile strength** by 60–100 % over carbon steel and dramatically improves **impact resistance** at low temperatures.

## 2.2 Tension in Multi-Leg Slings — The Angle Effect

This is the single **most-tested** concept in KTU examinations on chain slings. When a sling leg is **inclined at an angle $\theta$ from the horizontal**, only the **vertical component** of the leg's tension supports the load. The **horizontal component** squeezes the load inward and produces *compression* on the load-bearing surface (which the chain cannot see — only the rigging components feel it).

Let $n$ = number of sling legs and $\theta$ = angle each leg makes with the **horizontal**.

**Tension in each leg (multi-leg sling):**

$$T = \frac{W}{n \, \cos\theta}$$

The **effective vertical lifting component per leg** is $T \cos\theta = \dfrac{W}{n}$. The total *horizontal squeezing force* on the load is $n T \sin\theta$, which becomes significant at small angles.

The **maximum sling angle (best capacity)** occurs when $\theta = 90°$ (vertical leg), giving $T = W/n$. The **worst case** is when the sling is nearly horizontal, $\theta \to 0°$, and the tension $T \to \infty$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tension in each leg | $T = \dfrac{W}{n\cos\theta}$ | $W$ = total load (N or kg), $n$ = legs |
| 2 | Angle factor (multiplier on WLL) | $K_\theta = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$ | Pure multiplier; $K \ge 1$ always |
| 3 | Effective Working Load Limit (multi-leg) | $\text{WLL}_{\text{eff}} = \text{WLL}_{\text{single}} \times n \times \dfrac{1}{K_\theta}$ | Reduced by sling angle |
| 4 | Proof Load | $P_{\text{proof}} = 2 \times \text{WLL}$ | Manufacturer's factory test |
| 5 | Ultimate Load | $P_{\text{ult}} = \text{Design Factor} \times \text{WLL}$ | DF typically 3.0–5.0 |
| 6 | Single-leg WLL (Grade 100, nominal size $d$) | $\text{WLL} = 0.10 \times d^2$ (metric) or use ASME table | $d$ in mm → WLL in tonnes (approx.) |
| 7 | Loss of strength due to temperature | Derate per ASME B30.9 table | e.g., 400 °C → 75 % of WLL |
| 8 | D/d (sling-to-load corner) ratio effect | Min D/d ≥ 2 (chain vs load contact diameter) | Smaller D/d → bearing stress failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Recall Rule of Thumb for Sling Angles:**
> - $\theta = 90°$ → capacity factor = **1.00** (vertical, ideal)
> - $\theta = 60°$ → capacity factor = **1.16** (T = 1.16 W/n)
> - $\theta = 45°$ → capacity factor = **1.41** (T = 1.41 W/n)
> - $\theta = 30°$ → capacity factor = **2.00** (T = 2.00 W/n) — *The classic KTU 5-mark question!*

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel mills / Foundries:** Chain slings lift molten-metal ladles and hot ingots where temperatures up to **300 °C** would destroy fibre slings.
- **Marine / Shipyards:** Anchors, propeller shafts, and engine blocks are routinely rigged with Grade 100 four-leg chain slings.
- **Construction:** Prefabricated concrete and steel beams are lifted with chain slings because the **abrasion resistance** of forged links is unmatched.
- **Oil & Gas:** Drilling tools, BOPs, and tubing in derricks — chain slings resist oil, chemicals, and UV.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Leg Tension in a Multi-Leg Sling (Free-Body Diagram Approach)

Consider a load of weight $W$ suspended in equilibrium by a **2-leg chain sling**. Each leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the **horizontal**. Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be the tensions in the two legs.

**Step 1 — Vertical equilibrium of the load:**

The vertical component of the tension in each leg is $T \cos\theta$. Since the load is supported in the vertical direction:

$$\sum F_y = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; T_1\cos\theta_1 + T_2\cos\theta_2 = W$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal equilibrium of the load:**

The horizontal components of the two legs oppose each other (symmetric load). If the load is balanced, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta$, and:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; T_1\sin\theta - T_2\sin\theta = 0$$

Thus $T_1 = T_2 = T$.

**Step 3 — Substituting back into the vertical equation:**

$$T\cos\theta + T\cos\theta = W$$

$$2T\cos\theta = W$$

**Step 4 — Solving for tension in each leg:**

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{W}{2\cos\theta}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Generalisation to $n$ legs:**

By the same logic, if all $n$ legs are symmetrically inclined at the same angle $\theta$ to the horizontal:

$$nT\cos\theta = W$$

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{W}{n\cos\theta}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Expressing as a multiplier (KTU preferred form):**

Divide both sides of the equation by WLL of a single vertical leg:

$$\frac{T}{\text{WLL}} = \frac{W/n}{\cos\theta \cdot \text{WLL}} \;\Rightarrow\; T = \text{WLL} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\theta}$$

This is why KTU tables publish a **"Sling Factor"** $= 1/\cos\theta$.
