## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples (Step-by-Step)

### Example 1 — Single Vertical Leg

**Problem:** A single-leg alloy chain sling of WLL = 5 tonnes is used to lift a 4-tonne load vertically. Is it safe?

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{W}{n\cos\theta} = \frac{4}{1 \times \cos 90°} = \frac{4}{1} = 4\ \text{tonnes}$$

Since $T = 4\ \text{t} < \text{WLL} = 5\ \text{t}$, the sling is **SAFE** ✔.

### Example 2 — Two-Leg Sling at 60° from Horizontal (A KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** A 2-leg Grade 100 chain sling has a single-leg WLL of 4 t. The legs are rigged at $\theta = 60°$ to the horizontal. What is the maximum safe load?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the tension equation:**

$$T = \frac{W}{2\cos 60°}$$

**Step 2 — Note that $\cos 60° = 0.5$:**

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{W}{1} = W$$

**Step 3 — Apply the WLL limit $T \le \text{WLL}$:**

$$W \le \text{WLL} = 4\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Result:** The maximum safe load is **4 tonnes** — *same as a single vertical leg!*

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Multi-Leg Slings:** *At 60° to the horizontal, you gain the second leg for **redundancy** (safety in case one fails), but **NOT** for additional capacity.*

### Example 3 — Three-Leg Sling at 30° from Horizontal (High KTU Probability)

**Problem:** A 3-leg chain sling, single-leg WLL = 3 t, lifts a 5 t load with each leg at $\theta = 30°$ from horizontal. Is it safe?

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the required leg tension:**

$$T = \frac{W}{n\cos\theta} = \frac{5}{3 \times \cos 30°}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$:**

$$T = \frac{5}{3 \times 0.866} = \frac{5}{2.598} \approx 1.925\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with WLL:**

$$T = 1.925\ \text{t} < \text{WLL} = 3\ \text{t}\ \ \checkmark$$

**Result:** The sling is **SAFE** with a margin of $\dfrac{3 - 1.925}{3} \times 100\% \approx 35.8\ \%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Trap:** Some students multiply WLL by $n$ first and forget to divide by $\cos\theta$. Always compute $T$ per leg and *then* compare.

### Example 4 — Four-Leg Sling with Combined Vertical + Choker Hitch

**Problem:** A 4-leg chain sling (single-leg WLL = 2.5 t) lifts an asymmetric 6 t load. Two legs are at 45°, two legs are at 60°. Determine the safety.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Assume load distributes proportionally to leg angle, computing average $1/\cos\theta$:**

$$T_{45} = \frac{6 \times 0.5}{2 \times \cos 45°} = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.707} = \frac{3}{1.414} \approx 2.12\ \text{t}$$

$$T_{60} = \frac{6 \times 0.5}{2 \times \cos 60°} = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{3}{1.0} = 3.0\ \text{t}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with WLL:**

- $T_{45} = 2.12\ \text{t} < 2.5\ \text{t}$ ✔
- $T_{60} = 3.00\ \text{t} > 2.5\ \text{t}$ ✘ **UNSAFE!**

**Result:** The 60° legs are overloaded by 20 %. The lift must be **redesigned**.

## 3.3 Analytical Capacity Matrix (Quick-Reference Table for Examinations)

| $\theta$ (from horizontal) | $\cos\theta$ | Sling Factor $1/\cos\theta$ | Capacity Multiplier vs Vertical (2-leg) | Visual |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 90° | 0.000 | $\infty$ | Not physically possible (legs cannot be vertical and two-sided) | – |
| 75° | 0.259 | 3.86 | Capacity is severely reduced | Very spread-out |
| 60° | 0.500 | 2.00 | 100 % of 2 × WLL | Wide angle |
| 45° | 0.707 | 1.41 | 141 % of WLL | Standard rigging |
| 30° | 0.866 | 1.15 | 173 % of WLL | Tight angle |
| 15° | 0.966 | 1.04 | 193 % of WLL | Near-horizontal |
| 0° | 1.000 | 1.00 | 200 % of WLL (purely horizontal, infinite tension in practice) | Straight line |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chain Sling Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crane Hook] --> B[Master Link / Top Ring]
    B --> C{Number of Legs}
    C -->|n=1| D1[Single Leg with Hook / Oblong Link]
    C -->|n=2| D2[Two-Leg Bridle]
    C -->|n=3| D3[Three-Leg Bridle]
    C -->|n=4| D4[Four-Leg Bridle]
    D1 --> E[End Fitting: Hook / Link / Ring]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Forged Alloy Chain Body]
    F --> G[Load Attachment Point]
    G --> H[Verification of WLL, Angle, D/d]
    H --> I{Within Safe Limits?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Lift Authorised]
    I -->|No| K[Stop - Redesign Sling Configuration]
    J --> L[Perform Lift with Tagline and Spotter]
    K --> M[Increase Sling Angle, Add Leg, or Use Higher WLL Chain]
```

## 4.2 Force Resolution Flow in a Two-Leg Sling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Total Load W] --> V[Vertical Component: T cos theta]
    W --> H[Horizontal Component: T sin theta]
    V --> LegT[Tension per leg: W over 2 cos theta]
    H --> Sq[Squeezing Force on Load: 2T sin theta]
    LegT --> Check{Compared to WLL}
    Check --> Safe[Sling is Safe]
    Check --> Unsafe[Sling is Overloaded - Stop]
    Sq --> LoadStress[Load must resist bearing + friction]
```

## 4.3 Grade-Based Decision Flow for Sling Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Load Mass and Temperature] --> Q1{Is the load above 150 deg C?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Grade120[Select Grade 120 alloy chain]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the application overhead lifting?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Grade100[Select Grade 100 alloy chain]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is the load light and ambient temperature?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Grade80[Select Grade 80 - tie down only]
    Q3 -->|No| Grade30[Select Grade 30 or 43 carbon chain]
    Grade100 --> Verify[Verify WLL and Sling Angle]
    Grade120 --> Verify
    Grade80 --> Verify
    Grade30 --> Verify
    Verify --> Lift[Proceed with Lift]
```

## 4.4 Inspection & Discard Criteria Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Insp[Daily Visual Inspection by Operator] --> Stretch{Chain Stretched > 3 percent?}
    Stretch -->|Yes| Discard1[Discard - Remove from Service]
    Stretch -->|No| Wear{Link wear > 10 percent of nominal diameter?}
    Wear -->|Yes| Discard2[Discard]
    Wear -->|No| Crack{Visible cracks, nicks, or gouges?}
    Crack -->|Yes| Discard3[Discard]
    Crack -->|No| Heat{Discoloration indicating heat damage?}
    Heat -->|Yes| Discard4[Discard - Temper destroyed]
    Heat -->|No| Pass[Accept and Tag for Use]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Grade 80, Grade 100, and Grade 120 alloy chain slings in terms of application, working load limit, and suitability for overhead lifting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Grade | Application | Overhead Lifting? | Relative WLL |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grade 80** | Transport tie-downs, tow chain | ❌ Not permitted for overhead lifting under ASME B30.9 | 1.0× (baseline) |
| **Grade 100** | Overhead lifting, construction, foundries | ✔ Yes — general-purpose overhead | ~1.25× Grade 80 |
| **Grade 120** | Premium overhead, high-temperature, mining | ✔ Yes — highest capacity per diameter | ~1.5× Grade 80 |

The differences arise from the alloy composition, quenching, and tempering process. Higher grade ⇒ higher ultimate tensile strength ⇒ greater WLL for the same chain diameter.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any three (3) discard criteria for alloy chain slings as per ASME B30.9.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Stretch:** The chain length has increased by more than **3 %** from its original nominal length (measured over any 5-link pitch). Indicates plastic deformation.
2. **Gouges / Nicks / Cracks:** Any visible crack, sharp nick, or gouge depth exceeding **10 % of the nominal wire / link diameter**. Stress concentrators that initiate fatigue failure.
3. **Heat Damage:** Discoloration (blue, black, or straw colour) indicating the chain has been exposed to temperatures exceeding **400 °C**, which destroys the temper.
4. **Wear:** Link diameter at any point has worn by more than **10 %** of nominal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module 2 — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set A]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different **types of chain sling hitches** (vertical, choker, basket) and state how the working load limit is affected in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-leg alloy chain sling has a single-leg working load limit of **6 tonnes**. The sling is rigged with all three legs inclined at **45° to the horizontal**. Calculate: **(i)** the tension in each leg when lifting a **9-tonne load**, and **(ii)** the factor of safety against the WLL. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Vertical Hitch (Straight Lift):** The sling is attached to a single point on the load with the hook passing through a master link. Capacity = WLL of the single leg. Factor = 1.0.

**Choker Hitch:** The sling passes around the load and back through one end-link itself, forming a choking action. The capacity is reduced to **75 %** of the single-leg vertical WLL because of the bend stress on the end link and friction loss.

**Basket Hitch:** The sling passes *under* the load, with both ends attached overhead. Capacity = **2 × WLL of a single leg** (provided the load is balanced and D/d ≥ 2, where D is the load-contact diameter and d is the chain-link diameter).

**Multi-leg Bridle Hitch:** Two, three, or four legs share the load. Effective capacity is reduced by the sling angle factor $1/\cos\theta$ for each leg.

> *[Sketch in exam: Draw simple line diagrams showing each hitch with labelled angles, load, and sling orientation.]*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $n = 3$, $\text{WLL} = 6\ \text{t}$, $\theta = 45°$, $W = 9\ \text{t}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the leg tension:**

$$T = \frac{W}{n\cos\theta} = \frac{9}{3 \times \cos 45°}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\cos 45° = 1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$:**

$$T = \frac{9}{3 \times 0.7071} = \frac{9}{2.1213} \approx 4.243\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Factor of Safety:**

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\text{WLL}}{T} = \frac{6}{4.243} \approx 1.41$$

**Interpretation:** A factor of safety of 1.41 against the working load limit is **acceptable** for short-duration normal lifts, but not for personnel-safety or critical lifts. The sling is safe for this 9 t load with margin.

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Tension in each leg ≈ **4.243 tonnes**
- (ii) Factor of safety ≈ **1.41**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Set B]`

**(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the working load limit of an alloy chain sling**, including sling angle, type of hitch, D/d ratio, and environmental temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-leg alloy chain sling is used to lift a **3-tonne load** with each leg inclined at **60° to the horizontal**. If the WLL of a single vertical leg is **2.5 tonnes**, determine: **(i)** the actual tension in each leg, and **(ii)** whether the lift is safe. Comment on the role of sling angle on lifting capacity. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The WLL of an alloy chain sling is influenced by:

1. **Sling Angle ($\theta$):** As $\theta$ decreases (legs become more horizontal), the leg tension $T = W/(n\cos\theta)$ increases dramatically. At $\theta = 30°$, tension is **2×** the load; at $\theta = 60°$, it equals the load; at $\theta = 90°$, it equals $W/n$.
2. **Type of Hitch:** Vertical = 100 % of WLL; Basket = 200 % (D/d dependent); Choker = 75 %.
3. **D/d Ratio (Load Contact Diameter / Chain Diameter):** If $D/d < 2$, the chain link bends sharply around the load and bears against itself, reducing capacity by up to 50 %. ASME requires $D/d \ge 2$.
4. **Environmental Temperature:** Above 150 °C, the WLL must be derated (per ASME B30.9 table). At **400 °C**, the WLL reduces to 75 %; above 425 °C, the chain must be removed from service.
5. **Shock Loading & Dynamic Forces:** Sudden jerks multiply the effective load by **1.5 to 2.0**.
6. **Chain Condition:** Wear, stretch, corrosion, and prior overloading permanently reduce the WLL.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $n = 2$, $\theta = 60°$, $W = 3\ \text{t}$, $\text{WLL}_{\text{single}} = 2.5\ \text{t}$.

**Step 1 — Tension in each leg:**

$$T = \frac{W}{n\cos\theta} = \frac{3}{2 \times \cos 60°}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\cos 60° = 0.5$:**

$$T = \frac{3}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{3}{1.0} = 3.0\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with WLL:**

$$T = 3.0\ \text{t} \quad > \quad \text{WLL}_{\text{single}} = 2.5\ \text{t}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The lift is **UNSAFE**. The leg tension exceeds the WLL by 20 %.

**Step 5 — Role of Sling Angle:**
- At $\theta = 60°$, the second leg provides **redundancy** (if one fails, the other momentarily holds), but **no additional capacity** beyond a single vertical leg.
- For the lift to be safe, the operator must either:
  - Use a **higher WLL sling** (≥ 3 t single leg), or
  - **Increase the sling angle** to bring it closer to 90° (vertical).

**Final Answer:**
- (i) Tension per leg = **3.0 tonnes**
- (ii) Lift is **UNSAFE** — exceeds WLL by 20 %.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "vertical" and "horizontal" reference for sling angle** — KTU always measures $\theta$ from the **horizontal**, not from the vertical. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $n$** in multi-leg slings — using $T = W/\cos\theta$ instead of $T = W/(n\cos\theta)$. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Treating 60° as a "double-capacity" configuration** — many students wrongly believe 2 legs at 60° = 2× WLL. It is *not*. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not mentioning D/d ratio** in hitch classification — a common omission in 7-mark sub-parts. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Recommending Grade 80 chain for overhead lifting** — examiners deduct full marks if you overlook this pitfall. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Skipping the FoS calculation or final safety verdict** — KTU's valuation key demands an explicit "Safe / Unsafe" statement. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Alloy Chain Slings**

- ✔ **Definition:** Alloy chain slings are forged, quenched, and tempered heat-treated alloy steel chains used for heavy / hot / abrasive overhead lifts.
- ✔ **Grades:** Use **Grade 100 or Grade 120** for overhead lifting. **Grade 80 is NOT for overhead lifting** (transport / tie-down only).
- ✔ **Master Equation:** $T = \dfrac{W}{n\cos\theta}$, where $\theta$ is measured from the **horizontal**.
- ✔ **Sling Angle Capacity Factors (memorise these 4):**
  - $\theta = 90°$ → factor = 1.00
  - $\theta = 60°$ → factor = 1.16 (per leg)
  - $\theta = 45°$ → factor = 1.41
  - $\theta = 30°$ → factor = 2.00
- ✔ **Hitch Capacities:** Vertical = 100 % WLL | Basket = 200 % | Choker = 75 % (provided D/d ≥ 2).
- ✔ **D/d Rule:** Load contact diameter ≥ 2× chain diameter; otherwise capacity drops by up to 50 %.
- ✔ **Temperature Limits:** Safe to **−40 °C** to **150 °C** at full WLL; derate from 150 °C to 425 °C; remove from service above **425 °C**.
- ✔ **Discard Criteria:** Stretch > 3 %, wear > 10 %, gouges > 10 %, visible cracks, heat discoloration.
- ✔ **Design Factor (FoS):** Typically **3.0** (ASME) to **4.0** (EN 818) on ultimate strength.
- ✔ **Proof Load Test:** **2× WLL** at the factory.
- ✔ **60° Myth-Buster:** At 60° from horizontal, 2 legs carry a load = single-leg WLL — no extra capacity, only redundancy.
- ✔ **Inspection Frequency:** **Daily** visual check by operator; **periodic** by competent person (typically annually or per shift in severe service).
- ✔ **Tagging:** Every sling must carry a permanent, legible tag stating WLL, grade, manufacturer, and serial number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> *Inspection* in material handling is defined as the **systematic, planned, and documented process of visual, functional, and instrumental examination** of material handling equipment, accessories, and the working environment to verify their **continued fitness-for-service**, identify **defects, deterioration, or unsafe conditions**, and ensure **conformity with statutory regulations and engineering standards** before, during, and after operational use.

In the context of the **KTU OESFT833 syllabus**, inspection forms the *second pillar* of the safety hierarchy in material handling operations, sitting between *Design/Selection* and *Maintenance*. It is a **proactive loss-control measure**, not a reactive one.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Body Check-Up**
>
> Imagine you drive a car daily. You don't wait for the brakes to fail on a highway before checking them. Instead, you:
> - **Glance at the tyres** every morning (visual pre-use check),
> - **Take it to a service centre** every 6 months (periodic inspection),
> - **Get a fitness certificate** every year (statutory annual inspection).
>
> *Inspection in material handling is exactly this "health check-up" for cranes, forklifts, slings, conveyors, and storage racks.* A small crack noticed today prevents a catastrophic collapse tomorrow.

### 1.3 Why Inspection? — The Three Core Objectives

| # | Objective | Real-World Engineering Outcome |
|---|-----------|-------------------------------|
| **1** | **Hazard Identification** | Detect cracks, corrosion, wear before they cause failure |
| **2** | **Compliance Verification** | Satisfy legal duties under Factories Act 1948, OSHA, IS Standards |
| **3** | **Reliability Assurance** | Maintain Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) and operational uptime |

### 1.4 Key Statutory Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Frameworks for Inspection:**
> - **BIS / IS Standards:** IS 3177 (Cranes), IS 807 (Design of Cranes), IS 6213 (Forklifts), IS 1964 (Wire Ropes), IS 2740 (Chain Slings)
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.179** (Overhead and Gantry Cranes)
> - **Factories Act 1948, Section 36** (Safety of lifting machines, chains, ropes, and lifting tackles)
> - **Colour Coding:** Recommended in **IS 3626** and various OEM manuals for inspection status tagging.

### 1.5 Primary Categories of Inspection

$$ \text{Total Inspection Programme} = \underbrace{P}_{\text{Pre-Use}} + \underbrace{S}_{\text{Scheduled/Periodic}} + \underbrace{C}_{\text{Condition-Based}} + \underbrace{R}_{\text{Regulatory/Statutory}} $$

Where each category operates on a **different frequency** and **different personnel level** — a concept we will formalize in Section 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of an Inspection Programme

An effective inspection system rests on **four interlinked pillars**. Understanding these is critical for any KTU 14-mark question.

#### Pillar 1: Frequency Determination
Inspection frequency is governed by:

$$ f_{\text{insp}} = f\left( U, \ E, \ H, \ R \right) $$

Where:
- $U$ = Usage intensity (cycles/day)
- $E$ = Equipment criticality class (Class I, II, III, IV per CMAA)
- $H$ = Severity of hazard potential (number of personnel exposed)
- $R$ = Regulatory minimum (e.g., 12-month statutory)

#### Pillar 2: Inspection Levels (The Three-Tier Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — The Three-Tier Inspection Model**
>
> **Tier 1 — Operator/Daily Inspection (Pre-Use Check):**
> - Performed by the equipment operator **before every shift**.
> - Visual + functional checks: brakes, horn, lights, hydraulics, leaks, controls.
> - Time: 5–15 minutes. No special tools.
>
> **Tier 2 — Scheduled/Periodic Inspection:**
> - Performed by a **competent maintenance technician**.
> - Weekly / monthly / quarterly intervals.
> - Involves lubrication checks, wear measurement, NDT where required.
> - Documented on **inspection checklists**.
>
> **Tier 3 — Annual / Statutory / Thorough Inspection:**
> - Performed by a **statutory inspector or chartered engineer**.
> - Involves dismantling, NDT (MPI, UT, DPT), load testing (typically at **125% of Safe Working Load** for 5–10 minutes), and re-certification.
> - Mandatory under Factories Act 1948.

#### Pillar 3: Inspection Methodology — Visual, Functional, Instrumental

| Level | Method | Tools Used | Detects |
|-------|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Visual** | Look, listen, feel | Flashlight, mirror, magnifying glass | Cracks, leaks, deformation, corrosion |
| **Functional** | Operate controls | None (operational) | Brakes, steering, hydraulics, limit switches |
| **Instrumental** | Measure parameters | Calipers, micrometer, UT, MPI, DPT | Internal flaws, hidden cracks, wall thickness loss |

#### Pillar 4: Documentation and Records

Every inspection must produce a **written record** containing:
- Equipment ID and serial number
- Date, time, location
- Inspector name and certification number
- Findings (defects, observations)
- Action taken (pass, repair, scrap)
- Next inspection due date

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Inspection Calculations**
>
> **Formula 1: Discard Criteria for Wire Ropes (per IS 1964)**
>
> $$\text{Number of broken wires in one rope lay length} \leq \frac{R \times 10}{1000} + 4$$
>
> Where $R$ = nominal rope diameter in **mm**. If exceeded → discard.

> **Formula 2: Wear Limit for Hook Throat Opening**
>
> $$\Delta_{\text{max}} = 5\% \ \text{of original throat opening, or as per IS 15560}$$

> **Formula 3: Sling Angle Tension (Inspection-Relevant)**
>
> $$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$
>
> Where $T$ = tension in sling leg, $W$ = load weight, $\theta$ = sling angle with horizontal. Inspection must verify slings are not used at angles $< 30°$ (since $T$ becomes excessive).

> **Formula 4: Proof Load Test Requirement**
>
> $$W_{\text{proof}} = 1.25 \times \text{SWL}$$
>
> Every lifting machine/tackle shall be tested at **125% of Safe Working Load** during statutory inspection.

> **Formula 5: Inspection Frequency Interval**
>
> $$f = \frac{k}{U \cdot H}$$
>
> Where $k$ = statutory constant, $U$ = usage hours/day, $H$ = hazard class (1–4).

**Important Rule:** Never use the pipe character `|` inside the table cells. We use `\vert` notation only in display equations where it appears in the math sense.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Inspection Systems

| Industry Sector | Critical Inspection Application |
|-----------------|--------------------------------|
| **Construction** | Tower crane daily inspections, scaffold tag inspections |
| **Manufacturing** | Forklift OSHA daily checks, conveyor belt splices |
| **Ports & Shipping** | Container crane wire rope NDT, spreader beam inspections |
| **Petrochemical** | Explosion-proof forklift inspection, hoisting in hazardous zones |
| **Warehousing** | Rack inspection (post-collision), pallet jack maintenance |

### 2.4 Inspection of Specific Equipment — Key Defect Parameters

#### 2.4.1 Wire Rope Inspection
- **Diameter reduction** — measure every 5 m; discard if $\geq 10\%$ loss
- **Broken wires** — count per lay length (see Formula 1)
- **Birdcaging, kinking, corrosion** — immediate discard
- **Rope termination** — wedge, drum, or clamp inspection

#### 2.4.2 Hook Inspection (per IS 15560)
- Throat opening stretch > **5%** → withdraw from service
- Wear on hook body > **10%** of original section → scrap
- Deformation, twisting, cracks (using DPT) → scrap

#### 2.4.3 Chain Sling Inspection
- Elongation check: pitch increase > **3%** → reject
- Link wear reduction > **10%** of bar diameter → reject
- Look for gouges, nicks, heat damage (discoloration)

#### 2.4.4 Forklift Inspection
- Daily: tyres, forks (cracks, wear), mast, hydraulics, leaks
- Every 250 hrs: lubrication, brake adjustment, battery
- Every 1000 hrs: major service, load test

#### 2.4.5 Conveyor Inspection
- Belt condition: tears, edge damage, splices
- Idler rotation, bearing temperature
- Belt tracking and tension

### 2.5 The Inspection Decision Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tag-Out System (Three-Color Code):**
>
> | Tag Color | Meaning | Action |
> |-----------|---------|--------|
> | 🟢 **GREEN** | Safe to use | Equipment operational |
> | 🟡 **YELLOW** | Caution / limited use | Repair scheduled, restrictions apply |
> | 🔴 **RED** | Do not use | Lock-out, tag-out, immediate rectification required |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Establishing the Inspection Interval Using Risk-Based Methodology

This derivation shows how a maintenance engineer would compute an inspection interval for a critical lifting machine using a **risk-based formula**.

**Given:**
- Equipment: 10-tonne EOT crane in a steel plant
- Usage: $U = 16$ hours/day
- Hazard class (personnel exposed): $H = 4$ (Class IV — continuous duty)
- Statutory constant: $k = 8760$ (hours in a year)

**Step 1: Define the Risk-Based Inspection Interval Formula**

$$ f = \frac{k}{U \cdot H} $$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$ f = \frac{8760}{16 \cdot 4} $$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator first**

$$ 16 \cdot 4 = 64 \ \text{hours/day-equivalent} $$

**Step 4: Divide**

$$ f = \frac{8760}{64} = 136.875 \ \text{days} $$

**Step 5: Apply the conservative engineering rounding rule**

For safety-critical equipment, inspection intervals are **always rounded DOWN**, never up:

$$ f_{\text{recommended}} = 90 \ \text{days} \ (\text{quarterly}) $$

**Step 6: Verify against statutory minimum**

$$ f_{\text{statutory}} = 365 \ \text{days (annual)} $$

$$ f_{\text{recommended}} \leq f_{\text{statutory}} \quad \therefore \text{Compliant} $$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The calculated interval (137 days) is shorter than the statutory maximum (365 days), confirming that **quarterly inspections are both necessary and legally compliant**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wire Rope Discard Calculation Using IS 1964 Formula

**Given:**
- Wire rope diameter: $R = 24 \ \text{mm}$
- Broken wires observed in one rope lay length: $N_{\text{obs}} = 18$

**Step 1: Apply the IS 1964 discard formula**

$$ N_{\text{allow}} = \frac{R \times 10}{1000} + 4 $$

**Step 2: Substitute $R = 24 \ \text{mm}$**

$$ N_{\text{allow}} = \frac{24 \times 10}{1000} + 4 $$

**Step 3: Compute the fraction**

$$ \frac{240}{1000} = 0.24 $$

**Step 4: Add the constant**

$$ N_{\text{allow}} = 0.24 + 4 = 4.24 \ \text{wires} $$

**Step 5: Compare with observed value**

$$ N_{\text{obs}} = 18 \quad \text{vs.} \quad N_{\text{allow}} = 4.24 $$

Since $N_{\text{obs}} \gg N_{\text{allow}}$, the rope must be **IMMEDIATELY DISCARDED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The wire rope has failed the inspection criterion by a factor of more than **4×**. Continued use would constitute a serious safety violation under Section 36 of the Factories Act 1948.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Defects by Equipment Type

| Equipment | Defect to Inspect | Acceptance Limit | Inspection Method | Reject Action |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| Wire Rope | Diameter loss | $\leq 10\%$ of nominal | Vernier calliper | Discard |
| Wire Rope | Broken wires / lay | Formula 1 | Visual + count | Discard |
| Hook | Throat opening stretch | $\leq 5\%$ | Tape measure | Withdraw |
| Chain Sling | Pitch elongation | $\leq 3\%$ | Pitch gauge | Reject |
| Forklift Fork | Blade wear | $\leq 10\%$ height loss | Caliper | Reject |
| Forklift Fork | Crack | None allowed | DPT/MPI | Reject |
| Conveyor Belt | Edge damage | None allowed | Visual | Repair/Replace |
| Storage Rack | Vertical deformation | $\leq 3°$ tilt | Plumb line | Re-shim |
| Shackle | Body wear | $\leq 10\%$ diameter loss | Caliper | Reject |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Sling Angle Tension Analysis (Used in Inspection Verification)

This derivation validates **why inspectors reject low-angle sling configurations**.

**Given:** A 1-tonne ($W = 1000 \ \text{kgf}$) load lifted by a 2-leg sling at $\theta = 30°$ from horizontal.

**Step 1: Apply sling tension formula**

$$ T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta} $$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$ T = \frac{1000}{2 \sin(30°)} $$

**Step 3: Evaluate $\sin(30°) = 0.5$**

$$ T = \frac{1000}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{1000}{1} = 1000 \ \text{kgf per leg} $$

**Step 4: Compare with $\theta = 60°$ case**

$$ T_{60°} = \frac{1000}{2 \sin(60°)} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 0.866} = 577 \ \text{kgf per leg} $$

**Step 5: Compute the percentage increase**

$$ \Delta T\% = \frac{1000 - 577}{577} \times 100 = 73.3\% $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Inspection Implication:** Reducing the sling angle from $60°$ to $30°$ increases sling tension by **73.3%**. This is why inspectors must verify **sling angles are kept $\geq 45°$** for safe operation. Below $30°$, slings can fail catastrophically.

---

### 3.5 The Inspection Effectiveness Equation

The effectiveness of an inspection programme can be quantified as:

$$ \eta_{\text{insp}} = \frac{N_{\text{defects detected}}}{N_{\text{defects total}}} \times 100\% $$

Where:
- $\eta_{\text{insp}}$ = Inspection effectiveness (%)
- $N_{\text{defects detected}}$ = Defects found during inspection
- $N_{\text{defects total}}$ = Total defects present (including latent)

A target $\eta_{\text{insp}} \geq 90\%$ is considered industry-best for safety-critical equipment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Inspection Programme Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipment In Service] --> B[Pre-Use Operator Check]
    B --> C{Pass?}
    C -->|No| D[Red Tag - Lock Out]
    C -->|Yes| E[Equipment Released for Operation]
    E --> F[Operation Log Update]
    F --> G[Operating Hours Accumulate]
    G --> H{Due for Periodic Inspection?}
    H -->|No| E
    H -->|Yes| I[Scheduled Periodic Inspection - Tier 2]
    I --> J{Pass?}
    J -->|No| D
    J -->|Yes| K[Yellow or Green Tag]
    K --> L{Due for Annual Statutory?}
    L -->|No| E
    L -->|Yes| M[Annual Thorough Inspection - Tier 3]
    M --> N[Load Test at 125% SWL]
    N --> O[NDT MPI UT DPT]
    O --> P{Pass?}
    P -->|No| D
    P -->|Yes| Q[Certificate Issued - 12 Months Validity]
    Q --> E
    D --> R[Repair / Scrap Decision]
    R --> S[Re-inspection After Repair]
    S --> J
```

### 4.2 Three-Tier Inspection Responsibility Matrix

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Tier1[Tier 1 Operator Daily]
        opA[Operator] --> opB[Pre-Use Checklist]
        opB --> opC[Visual and Functional]
    end
    
    subgraph Tier2[Tier 2 Maintenance Periodic]
        mtA[Maintenance Tech] --> mtB[Detailed Checklist]
        mtB --> mtC[Measurement and NDT Basic]
    end
    
    subgraph Tier3[Tier 3 Statutory Annual]
        stA[Statutory Inspector] --> stB[Comprehensive Check]
        stB --> stC[Load Test and Full NDT]
    end
    
    opC --> mtB
    mtC --> stB
    stC --> mtB
```

### 4.3 Inspection Decision Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Defect Detected During Inspection] --> Q1{Severity Level?}
    Q1 -->|Minor| M1[Yellow Tag - Monitor]
    Q1 -->|Major| M2[Red Tag - Lock Out]
    Q1 -->|Critical Safety| M3[Immediate Shutdown and Reporting]
    
    M1 --> R1[Schedule Repair within 30 Days]
    M2 --> R2[Repair Before Next Use]
    M3 --> R3[Incident Investigation Required]
    
    R1 --> V[Re-inspection]
    R2 --> V
    R3 --> V
    
    V --> Q2{Repair Successful?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| G[Green Tag Released]
    Q2 -->|No| S[Scrap and Replace]
```

### 4.4 Material Handling Equipment Inspection Coverage Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Inspection Coverage))
    Lifting Machines
      Cranes EOT
      Cranes Mobile
      Hoists Chain
      Hoists Wire Rope
      Winches
    Lifting Tackles
      Wire Rope Slings
      Chain Slings
      Webbing Slings
      Shackles
      Hooks
      Eye Bolts
    Conveyors
      Belt Conveyors
      Roller Conveyors
      Chain Conveyors
      Screw Conveyors
    Storage Equipment
      Pallet Racks
      Cantilever Racks
      Mezzanines
    Mobile Equipment
      Forklifts
      Pallet Jacks
      Stackers
      AGVs
```

### 4.5 Inspection Frequency Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Equipment Type] --> Class{Class I II III IV?}
    Class -->|Class I - Light| A1[Annual Thorough]
    Class -->|Class II - Moderate| A2[Semi Annual]
    Class -->|Class III - Heavy| A3[Quarterly]
    Class -->|Class IV - Severe| A4[Monthly]
    
    A1 --> Env{Environment?}
    A2 --> Env
    A3 --> Env
    A4 --> Env
    
    Env -->|Indoor Clean| N1[Standard Interval]
    Env -->|Outdoor Harsh| N2[Reduce Interval by 50%]
    Env -->|Corrosive or High Temp| N3[Reduce Interval by 75%]
    Env -->|Hazardous Zone| N4[Continuous Monitoring plus Standard]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Inspection" in the context of material handling safety. List any three statutory standards relevant to inspection of lifting machines in India.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Inspection in material handling is the systematic, planned and documented examination of equipment such as cranes, hoists, slings, and conveyors to verify their fitness-for-service, identify defects and ensure compliance with safety standards.

**Three Statutory Standards (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Factories Act 1948, Section 36** — mandates inspection and testing of lifting machines and tackles.
2. **IS 3177** — Code for electric overhead travelling cranes.
3. **IS 1964** — Inspection and discard criteria for wire ropes.
*(Alternate: IS 807, IS 15560, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.179)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between "Pre-Use Check" and "Periodic Inspection" with respect to material handling equipment.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-Use Check | Periodic Inspection |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Performed by** | Equipment Operator | Maintenance Technician |
| **Frequency** | Every shift / daily | Weekly to Quarterly |
| **Scope** | Visual + functional | Measurement + NDT |
| **Documentation** | Logbook entry | Detailed checklist |
| **Duration** | 5–15 minutes | 1–4 hours |

**Key Difference:** Pre-use checks are **quick safety screens** by the user, while periodic inspections are **detailed engineering examinations** by trained personnel at scheduled intervals.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students commonly lose marks in the following areas: (1) Failing to mention the **legal statutory backing** (Factories Act 1948), (2) Confusing **tier levels** of inspection, (3) Not providing **numerical examples** when asked about discard criteria, and (4) Missing the **documentation aspect** which carries at least 2 marks in any inspection question.

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Three-Tier Inspection System** followed for material handling equipment in industries. Discuss the personnel, frequency, scope, and documentation at each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wire rope of diameter **28 mm** is in service on a 5-tonne EOT crane. During a periodic inspection, the maintenance technician counts **22 broken wires in one rope lay length**. Using the IS 1964 discard formula, determine whether the rope should be discarded. Justify your answer with calculations. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The Three-Tier Inspection System is a structured approach recommended by IS standards and international codes (OSHA, BS 7121) for ensuring the safety of material handling equipment.

**Tier 1 — Operator Pre-Use Inspection (3 Marks):**
- **Personnel:** Equipment operator (trained but not certified inspector)
- **Frequency:** Daily, before every shift
- **Scope:** Visual checks (tyres, forks, hooks, ropes, leaks), functional tests (brakes, horn, hydraulics, lights, controls, limit switches)
- **Tools:** None or simple flashlight
- **Documentation:** Daily logbook entry; defects reported to supervisor
- **Objective:** Detect gross defects that would prevent safe operation that day

**Tier 2 — Scheduled Periodic Inspection (2 Marks):**
- **Personnel:** Competent maintenance technician
- **Frequency:** Weekly to Quarterly depending on equipment class and usage
- **Scope:** Detailed measurement of wear, lubrication checks, bolt torques, filter replacements, brake adjustments
- **Tools:** Calipers, gauges, lubrication equipment
- **Documentation:** Detailed checklist with measurements recorded; defects categorized as minor/major
- **Objective:** Detect progressive wear before it becomes critical

**Tier 3 — Annual Statutory Thorough Inspection (2 Marks):**
- **Personnel:** Statutory inspector or chartered engineer (independent)
- **Frequency:** At least once every 12 months (or as per regulation)
- **Scope:** Dismantling where required, full NDT (Magnetic Particle, Ultrasonic, Dye Penetrant), load testing at **125% of SWL** for static test (10 minutes) and **110% of SWL** for dynamic test
- **Documentation:** Statutory certificate issued, valid for 12 months; equipment register updated
- **Objective:** Verify continued fitness-for-service and regulatory compliance

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Rope diameter $R = 28 \ \text{mm}$
- Observed broken wires $N_{\text{obs}} = 22$

**Step 1: Recall the IS 1964 discard formula**

$$ N_{\text{allow}} = \frac{R \times 10}{1000} + 4 $$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$ N_{\text{allow}} = \frac{28 \times 10}{1000} + 4 $$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**

$$ \frac{280}{1000} = 0.28 $$

**Step 4: Add constant**

$$ N_{\text{allow}} = 0.28 + 4 = 4.28 \ \text{wires} $$

**Step 5: Compare with observed value**

$$ N_{\text{obs}} = 22 \gg N_{\text{allow}} = 4.28 $$

**Conclusion (7 Marks Total — 2 for formula, 3 for calculation, 2 for decision):**
The rope has **22 broken wires** versus an **allowable limit of 4.28 wires**. The observed count exceeds the limit by a factor of **5.14×**. Therefore, the wire rope **MUST BE IMMEDIATELY DISCARDED** and replaced before the crane is returned to service. Continued use would constitute a serious violation of Section 36 of the Factories Act 1948 and could lead to catastrophic rope failure.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the **various types of defects** commonly found during the inspection of (i) Wire Ropes, (ii) Hooks, and (iii) Forklift Forks. For each, specify the **inspection method** and the **discard/withdrawal criterion**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A maintenance engineer must establish the inspection interval for a Class III overhead crane operating **20 hours/day** in a steel plant, with a hazard class of $H = 3$. The statutory constant is $k = 8760$. Calculate the recommended inspection interval using the risk-based formula and verify compliance with statutory requirements. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**(i) Wire Rope Defects (2.5 Marks):**

| Defect | Inspection Method | Discard Criterion |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Broken wires | Visual count per lay length | IS 1964 formula: $N > (R/100) + 4$ |
| Diameter reduction | Vernier calliper at multiple points | Loss $> 10\%$ of nominal |
| Corrosion | Visual + magnification | Severe pitting — discard |
| Birdcaging / kinking | Visual | Any occurrence — discard |
| Rope termination slip | Visual + measurement | Any movement — re-terminate or discard |

**(ii) Hook Defects (2.5 Marks):**

| Defect | Inspection Method | Discard Criterion |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Throat opening stretch | Tape measure | Stretch $> 5\%$ (IS 15560) |
| Cross-section wear | Caliper | Wear $> 10\%$ — scrap |
| Surface cracks | Dye Penetrant Test | Any crack — scrap |
| Hook twist / deformation | Profile gauge | Any twist $> 10°$ — scrap |
| Safety latch failure | Functional | Non-functional — replace before use |

**(iii) Forklift Fork Defects (2 Marks):**

| Defect | Inspection Method | Discard Criterion |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| Blade wear at heel | Caliper | Height loss $> 10\%$ — reject |
| Fork bending | Straightedge | Any visible bend — reject |
| Cracks at root | MPI / DPT | Any crack — reject |
| Locking pin failure | Functional | Loose or missing — replace |
| Surface corrosion | Visual | Severe pitting — assess capacity reduction |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Equipment Class: III (heavy duty)
- Usage $U = 20$ hours/day
- Hazard class $H = 3$
- Statutory constant $k = 8760$

**Step 1: State the risk-based interval formula**

$$ f = \frac{k}{U \cdot H} $$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$ f = \frac{8760}{20 \times 3} $$

**Step 3: Compute denominator**

$$ 20 \times 3 = 60 $$

**Step 4: Divide**

$$ f = \frac{8760}{60} = 146 \ \text{days} $$

**Step 5: Apply conservative rounding**

For safety-critical equipment, round **DOWN**:

$$ f_{\text{recommended}} = 90 \ \text{days} \ (\text{quarterly inspections}) $$

**Step 6: Verify statutory compliance**

$$ f_{\text{statutory}} = 365 \ \text{days (annual mandatory inspection)} $$

Since $f_{\text{recommended}} = 90 \ \text{days} \leq 365 \ \text{days} = f_{\text{statutory}}$:

$$ \boxed{f_{\text{recommended}} \text{ is COMPLIANT with statutory requirements}} $$

**Engineering Interpretation (extra credit, 1 mark):**
The calculated interval (146 days) is closer to semi-annual than quarterly. However, given that the crane operates in a **steel plant** (harsh, hot, dusty environment) and is **Class III** (heavy duty), the engineer wisely chose **quarterly (90 days)** as a conservative margin. This provides an additional safety buffer of approximately **56 days** below the calculated theoretical interval, which is best engineering practice for safety-critical equipment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Inspection in Material Handling**
>
> **Core Definitions (must memorize):**
> - ☐ Inspection = Systematic, planned, documented examination of MHE
> - ☐ Fitness-for-service = Equipment capable of performing its design function safely
> - ☐ SWL = Safe Working Load (maximum permissible load)
> - ☐ Proof Load Test = Testing at **125% of SWL**
>
> **Three-Tier Inspection Model:**
> - ☐ Tier 1: Operator pre-use (daily, visual + functional)
> - ☐ Tier 2: Maintenance periodic (weekly–quarterly, measurement)
> - ☐ Tier 3: Statutory annual (load test + NDT + certification)
>
> **Key Standards & Acts:**
> - ☐ Factories Act 1948, Section 36 (statutory basis)
> - ☐ IS 1964 (wire rope discard)
> - ☐ IS 3177 (EOT cranes)
> - ☐ IS 15560 (hook inspection)
> - ☐ OSHA 29 CFR 1910.179 (US reference)
>
> **Critical Numerical Formulas:**
> - ☐ Wire rope discard: $N = (R/100) + 4$
> - ☐ Hook throat stretch: $\leq 5\%$
> - ☐ Chain sling pitch elongation: $\leq 3\%$
> - ☐ Fork wear: $\leq 10\%$
> - ☐ Proof load test: $1.25 \times$ SWL
>
> **Color Tag System:**
> - ☐ 🟢 Green = Safe, 🟡 Yellow = Caution, 🔴 Red = Lock-out
>
> **Common Defects to Remember:**
> - ☐ Wire rope: broken wires, diameter loss, birdcaging
> - ☐ Hook: throat stretch, cracks, twist
> - ☐ Forklift: fork wear, brake failure, hydraulic leak
>
> **Inspection Best Practices:**
> - ☐ Always **round down** intervals for safety-critical equipment
> - ☐ Always **document** in writing
> - ☐ Always **verify statutory compliance** alongside engineering judgment
> - ☐ Always use the **three-tier** approach — no single level is sufficient
>
> **Examiner Traps to Avoid:**
> - ☐ Don't confuse "Inspection" with "Maintenance" (inspection = check, maintenance = repair)
> - ☐ Don't forget to mention **NDT methods** (MPI, UT, DPT) for Tier 3
> - ☐ Don't skip the **legal/statutory** angle — it's a high-scoring point

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hoisting Apparatus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hoisting Apparatus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hoisting Apparatus** is a mechanical assembly of integrated components — including a prime mover (motor/engine), power transmission system, braking mechanism, load-bearing element (wire rope/chain), and lifting attachment (hook/block) — designed to raise, lower, and transport heavy loads vertically and horizontally in a controlled manner within an industrial environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> A *hoisting apparatus* is a complete lifting machine that combines a hoisting mechanism (for vertical movement of the load) with a traversing or slewing mechanism (for horizontal positioning), operating under defined load capacities, safety factors, and statutory inspection regimes as per Indian Factories Act & BIS standards.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **biceps muscle lifting a bucket of water from a well**:

- The **bucket** = the *load*
- The **rope** going over the **pulley of the well** = the *wire rope and sheave*
- Your **hand pulling the rope** = the *hoisting drum and motor*
- The **friction your other hand applies to stop it** = the *brake*
- The **clasp on the bucket handle** = the *hook and shackle*

A hoisting apparatus does exactly this — but with **tonnes of load**, controlled by an electric or hydraulic motor, governed by strict safety standards. The "well" becomes a factory, warehouse, shipyard, or construction site.

## 1.3 Classification of Hoisting Apparatus

| Category | Examples | Typical Capacity | Application |
|----------|----------|------------------|-------------|
| **Simple Hoists** | Chain Hoist, Lever Hoist, Wire Rope Hoist | 0.5 T – 20 T | Workshops, maintenance bays |
| **Overhead Cranes** | EOT Crane, Gantry Crane, Jib Crane | 2 T – 500 T | Heavy engineering, steel plants |
| **Mobile Cranes** | Hydraulic Crane, Crawler Crane, Truck Crane | 5 T – 1500 T | Construction, logistics |
| **Specialty Hoists** | Pneumatic Hoist, Electric Chain Hoist, Air Hoist | 0.25 T – 10 T | Hazardous/explosive environments |
| **Winches & Capstans** | Hand Winch, Power Winch | 0.5 T – 50 T | Marine, mining, pulling loads |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Every hoisting apparatus is governed by the **Design Safety Factor** (ratio of ultimate breaking strength to the maximum working load), which must be ≥ **3.5 for hoisting machinery** and ≥ **5.0 for personnel-carrying devices** as per IS 3938 / IS 3177.

## 1.4 Fundamental Sub-Systems

A complete hoisting apparatus is composed of **five mandatory sub-systems**:

1. **Prime Mover** — Electric motor / Diesel engine / Pneumatic motor
2. **Power Transmission** — Gears, couplings, drive shafts
3. **Braking System** — Electromagnetic brake, hydraulic brake, band brake, disc brake
4. **Load-Bearing Element** — Wire rope, chain, synthetic sling
5. **Lifting Accessory** — Hook, block, sheave, shackle, sling

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Bold Standards):**
> - **Duty Cycle (S)**: 10 % to 60 % per hour based on FEM/ISO classification
> - **Lifting Speed**: Typically **0.5 m/min to 20 m/min**
> - **Trolley Travel Speed**: **5 m/min to 40 m/min**
> - **Standard Lifting Heights**: **6 m, 9 m, 12 m, 18 m, 24 m, 36 m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Hoisting Apparatus

The hoisting apparatus operates on the principle of **mechanical advantage** and **energy conversion** from electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy into potential energy of the load.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Energy Input:** The prime mover (typically a 3-phase induction motor of **415 V, 50 Hz**) receives electrical power.
- **Step 2 — Speed Reduction:** A gear reduction unit (helical/worm/bevel) reduces motor speed (typically **1440 RPM**) to drum speed (**1–20 RPM**), thereby multiplying torque.
- **Step 3 — Rope Winding:** The rotating drum winds the wire rope, lifting the hook block via sheaves.
- **Step 4 — Load Suspension:** The hook block contains 2 to 6 sheaves that provide mechanical advantage — a system with `n` falls of rope creates an effective lifting force of `W/n` per rope strand.
- **Step 5 — Braking:** On power cut-off, a fail-safe electromagnetic brake engages automatically, holding the load.
- **Step 6 — Lowering:** Reverse rotation unwinds the rope under controlled regenerative or counter-torque braking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Multiple Sheaves:**
> A single-fall hoist would require the entire load to be supported by one rope section — demanding a huge rope diameter. By using multiple falls (e.g., **4-part reeving**), the load is distributed across **4 rope strands**, reducing the tension in each strand to **W/4**, allowing a smaller, lighter, and more economical rope.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Wire Rope Mechanics

A **wire rope** is the most critical load-bearing element in a hoisting apparatus. It is a flexible, helical assembly of steel wires laid in strands around a central core.

### 2.2.1 Wire Rope Construction Code

A standard designation is written as:
> **6 × 19 (12/6/1) — Fibre Core — Right Regular Lay**

Where:
- `6` = Number of strands
- `19` = Number of wires per strand
- `12/6/1` = Wires arranged in 12 outer, 6 middle, 1 inner layers
- **Fibre Core (FC)** = Natural/synthetic core for flexibility and lubrication storage
- **Right Regular Lay (RRL)** = Strands laid clockwise over a core laid in the same direction

### 2.2.2 Why the Core Matters

The **core** is the heart of the rope. It:
- Supports the strands and maintains their geometric position
- Acts as a **lubricant reservoir**, extending fatigue life
- Absorbs impact loads and distributes radial pressure

| Core Type | Symbol | Temperature Limit | Best Application |
|-----------|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Fibre Core | **FC** | Up to **80 °C** | General indoor hoisting |
| Steel Wire Core | **WSC** | Up to **200 °C** | High-temperature, high-load environments |
| Steel Independent Wire Rope Core | **IWRC** | Up to **200 °C** | Heavy duty, crushing resistance |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | Force per rope fall where $W$ = load, $n$ = number of falls | N or kg |
| 2 | $F_{rope} = F \times S_{factor}$ | Required minimum breaking force of rope | N |
| 3 | $SF = \dfrac{F_{breaking}}{F_{working}}$ | Safety Factor (must be ≥ 3.5 for hoisting) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{W \times D_{drum}}{2}$ | Torque required at drum shaft (single-fall) | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{T \times \omega}{\eta}$ | Motor power required, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\eta$ = efficiency | W |
| 6 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \times D_{drum} \times N}{60 \times n}$ | Lifting speed, $N$ = drum RPM | m/s |
| 7 | $\sigma_{rope} = \dfrac{F_{rope}}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times d^{2}}$ | Tensile stress in wire rope (solid approximation) | N/mm² |
| 8 | $D_{drum} \geq 20 \times d_{rope}$ | Minimum drum diameter for adequate bending life | mm |
| 9 | $D_{sheave} \geq 18 \times d_{rope}$ | Minimum sheave diameter | mm |
| 10 | $L_{rope} = H \times n + L_{dead} + 3 \times \pi \times D_{drum}$ | Total rope length, $H$ = lift, $L_{dead}$ = dead turns | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Reminder:** The **statutory minimum safety factor** for a wire rope used in hoisting is **3.5** for a single fall, and it **must be increased** for corrosive, hot, or dynamically loaded environments. Personnel-carrying hoists demand **SF ≥ 10**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hoisting apparatus is the **backbone of every heavy industry** on earth:

- **Steel Plants** — EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) cranes handle molten metal ladles of **150–500 T**.
- **Shipyards** — Goliath and Floating Cranes lift ship blocks of **thousands of tonnes**.
- **Power Plants** — Polar cranes in nuclear containment buildings handle reactor vessel heads.
- **Construction** — Tower cranes and crawler cranes assemble skyscrapers.
- **Logistics** — Container gantry cranes at ports handle 20–40 ft containers 24/7.

In each case, the **safety of the operator, the load, and bystanders** depends on the correct design, inspection, and maintenance of the hoisting apparatus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force per Rope Fall and Drum Torque

### Problem Setup
A hoist is required to lift a load of **$W = 10,000 \text{ kg}$** (≈ 98,100 N) using a **4-fall reeving** system. The drum diameter is **$D_{drum} = 400 \text{ mm}$**, drum efficiency is **$\eta = 0.85$**, and the drum rotates at **$N = 10 \text{ RPM}$**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \text{ Identify total weight to be lifted.} \\
&\qquad W = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} = 10{,}000 \times 9.81 = 98{,}100 \text{ N} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \text{ Determine load per rope fall with reeving } n = 4. \\
&\qquad F = \dfrac{W}{n} = \dfrac{98{,}100}{4} = 24{,}525 \text{ N} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \text{ Calculate effective force at drum (after efficiency loss).} \\
&\qquad F_{drum} = \dfrac{F}{\eta} = \dfrac{24{,}525}{0.85} = 28{,}852.94 \text{ N} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \text{ Compute torque on drum shaft.} \\
&\qquad T = F_{drum} \times \dfrac{D_{drum}}{2} = 28{,}852.94 \times \dfrac{0.400}{2} \\
&\qquad T = 28{,}852.94 \times 0.200 = 5{,}770.59 \text{ N·m} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \text{ Convert drum speed to angular velocity.} \\
&\qquad \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 10}{60} = 1.047 \text{ rad/s} \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \text{ Compute required motor output power.} \\
&\qquad P = T \times \omega = 5{,}770.59 \times 1.047 = 6{,}041.8 \text{ W} \approx 6.04 \text{ kW} \\
&\textbf{Step 7:} \text{ Apply service factor (1.5 for heavy duty) to size motor.} \\
&\qquad P_{motor} = 1.5 \times 6.04 = 9.06 \text{ kW} \quad \Rightarrow \text{select standard 11 kW motor.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result
> **Required motor power = 11 kW (standard size), Drum torque = 5,770.59 N·m**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wire Rope Diameter Selection

### Problem Setup
A wire rope must carry a working load of **$F = 28{,}853 \text{ N}$** with a **safety factor SF = 5** (hoisting duty). Material ultimate tensile strength is **$\sigma_u = 1{,}770 \text{ N/mm}^2$** (improved plow steel). Find the minimum rope diameter.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \text{ Compute the required minimum breaking force of the rope.} \\
&\qquad F_{breaking} = F \times SF = 28{,}853 \times 5 = 144{,}265 \text{ N} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \text{ Compute the equivalent metallic cross-section area required.} \\
&\qquad A_{metal} = \dfrac{F_{breaking}}{\sigma_u} = \dfrac{144{,}265}{1{,}770} = 81.51 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \text{ For a 6 × 19 rope, the metallic area factor } k = 0.38. \\
&\qquad \text{(Empirical constant: } A_{metal} = k \cdot d^2 \text{)} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \text{ Solve for the minimum rope diameter } d. \\
&\qquad d^2 = \dfrac{A_{metal}}{k} = \dfrac{81.51}{0.38} = 214.50 \\
&\qquad d = \sqrt{214.50} = 14.65 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \text{ Select the next standard rope size.} \\
&\qquad d_{standard} = 16 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(Standard 6 × 19, IWRC rope)} \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \text{ Verify sheave and drum minimum diameters.} \\
&\qquad D_{drum} \geq 20 \times d = 20 \times 16 = 320 \text{ mm} \\
&\qquad D_{sheave} \geq 18 \times d = 18 \times 16 = 288 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result
> **Selected rope = 16 mm diameter, 6 × 19 IWRC, with drum diameter ≥ 320 mm**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Drum Rope Capacity

### Problem Setup
A hoist must lift a load through a height of **$H = 12 \text{ m}$** using **$n = 4$ falls** of **$d = 16 \text{ mm}$** rope. The drum has a **flange diameter of $D_f = 600 \text{ mm}$**, and the **mandrel (core) diameter is $D_c = 360 \text{ mm}$**. Find the required **drum length**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Step 1:} \text{ Calculate the rope required per fall (rises height } H \text{) plus dead wraps.} \\
&\qquad L_{fall} = H + 3 \times \pi \times D_c = 12 + 3 \times \pi \times 0.360 \\
&\qquad L_{fall} = 12 + 3.393 = 15.393 \text{ m} \\
&\textbf{Step 2:} \text{ Total rope length for all falls (rope wraps onto drum in single layer per fall).} \\
&\qquad L_{total} = n \times L_{fall} = 4 \times 15.393 = 61.57 \text{ m} \\
&\textbf{Step 3:} \text{ Number of rope turns required on the drum.} \\
&\qquad N_{turns} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{\pi \times D_c} = \dfrac{61.57}{\pi \times 0.360} = 54.45 \text{ turns} \\
&\textbf{Step 4:} \text{ Add 2–3 dead turns (safety wraps that never unwind).} \\
&\qquad N_{effective} = 54.45 + 2 = 56.45 \text{ turns} \\
&\textbf{Step 5:} \text{ Drum barrel length for single-layer winding.} \\
&\qquad L_{drum} = N_{effective} \times d = 56.45 \times 16 = 903.2 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{Step 6:} \text{ Apply groove spacing (typically 1.1 × } d \text{ for helical groove).} \\
&\qquad L_{drum,actual} = N_{effective} \times 1.1 \times d = 56.45 \times 1.1 \times 16 = 993.5 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{Step 7:} \text{ Select standard drum length = 1000 mm.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result
> **Drum barrel length = 1000 mm (standard), Rope capacity verified.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hoisting Component Sizing

| Parameter | Symbol | Governing Formula | Typical Range | Critical Constraint |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Drum diameter | $D_{drum}$ | $D_{drum} \geq 20 d$ | 200 – 1500 mm | Bending fatigue life |
| Sheave diameter | $D_{sheave}$ | $D_{sheave} \geq 18 d$ | 160 – 1200 mm | Bending fatigue life |
| Rope diameter | $d$ | $d = \sqrt{F_{breaking}/(k \sigma_u)}$ | 6 – 60 mm | Safety factor ≥ 3.5 |
| Number of falls | $n$ | $n = W / F_{rope,max}$ | 2 – 12 (typically 2, 4, 6) | Reeving complexity |
| Drum torque | $T$ | $T = (W/n) \times (D/2) \times (1/\eta)$ | 50 – 50,000 N·m | Motor capacity |
| Lifting speed | $v$ | $v = \pi D N / (60 n)$ | 0.5 – 20 m/min | Process requirement |
| Brake torque | $T_{brake}$ | $T_{brake} \geq 1.5 \times T_{drum}$ | — | Fail-safe hold |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hoisting Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **energy and force flow** through every critical sub-system of a typical EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) Crane hoisting apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Supply 415V 50Hz 3Phase] --> B[Main Isolator Switch]
    B --> C[Hoisting Motor 3Phase Induction]
    B --> D[Long Travel Motor]
    B --> E[Cross Travel Motor]
    C --> F[Helical Gear Reducer Ratio 40 to 120]
    F --> G[Coupling Flexible Type]
    G --> H[Hoisting Drum Shaft]
    H --> I[Wire Rope Drum Helical Groove]
    I --> J[Wire Rope 6x19 IWRC 16mm]
    J --> K[Top Sheave Assembly in Trolley]
    J --> L[Bottom Sheave in Hook Block]
    K --> M[Lower Hook Block 4 Sheaves]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Forged Hook SWL Marked]
    N --> O[Load to be Lifted]
    H --> P[Electromagnetic Disc Brake Fail Safe]
    P --> Q[Brake Lining and Actuator]
    Q --> R[DC Coil from Rectifier Unit]
    C --> S[Overload Limiter Load Cell]
    S --> T[Cutoff Relay at 110 Percent SWL]
    H --> U[Rotary Encoder for Hoist Position]
    U --> V[Anti Two Block Switch]
    V --> W[Limit Switches Top and Bottom]
    T --> X[Safety Cutoff to Motor Contactor]
    W --> X
    X --> C
    C --> Y[Pendant Push Button Station]
    Y --> Z[Operator Hand Control IP55]
```

---

## 4.2 Force and Load Distribution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Load W = 10000 kg] --> B[Hook Block 4 Sheaves]
    B --> C[Rope Strand 1 Tension W divided by 4]
    B --> D[Rope Strand 2 Tension W divided by 4]
    B --> E[Rope Strand 3 Tension W divided by 4]
    B --> F[Rope Strand 4 Tension W divided by 4]
    C --> G[Top Sheave Assembly]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Drum Groove Wrapping Angle 180 degrees]
    H --> I[Drum Shaft Torque W times D divided by 8]
    I --> J[Gear Reducer Speed Reduction]
    J --> K[Hoisting Motor Torque Multiplied by Gear Ratio]
    K --> L[Brake Disc Coupled to Motor Shaft]
    L --> M[Power Cutoff Brake Engages Holds Load]
```

---

## 4.3 Inspection and Safety Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Hoisting Apparatus Daily Pre Use Check] --> Q1{Visual Inspection of Rope}
    Q1 -- Broken wires more than 10 per lay length --> Reject1[Remove Rope from Service Immediately]
    Q1 -- Birdcaging or kinking visible --> Reject2[Discard and Replace Rope]
    Q1 -- Normal wear and lubrication OK --> Q2{Hook Inspection}
    Q2 -- Throat opening more than 5 percent stretch --> Reject3[Withdraw Hook from Service]
    Q2 -- Safety latch missing or damaged --> Reject4[Refit Latch Before Use]
    Q2 -- Hook markings legible and no cracks --> Q3{Brake Test}
    Q3 -- Load drifts more than 100 mm per minute at zero command --> Reject5[Adjust or Replace Brake Lining]
    Q3 -- Holds load firmly with no drift --> Q4{Limit Switches}
    Q4 -- Top and bottom limits do not cut off hoist --> Reject6[Reset Switches and Verify]
    Q4 -- Both limits operate correctly --> Q5{Overload Limiter}
    Q5 -- Does not cut off at 110 percent SWL --> Reject7[Recalibrate Load Cell]
    Q5 -- Cuts off correctly --> Approved[Crane Cleared for Operation]
```

---

## 4.4 Wire Rope Discard Criteria Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wire Rope Condition Assessment] --> B{Type of Damage}
    B -- Wear on crown --> C[Measure diameter reduction with caliper]
    C -- More than 10 percent of nominal diameter --> R1[REJECT immediately]
    C -- Less than 10 percent --> R2[Acceptable with monthly monitoring]
    B -- Fatigue broken wires --> D[Count broken wires per rope lay length]
    D -- More than 10 random breaks --> R3[REJECT]
    D -- 5 to 10 random breaks --> R4[Reduce capacity and inspect weekly]
    D -- Less than 5 random breaks --> R5[Continue normal service]
    B -- Corrosion visible --> E[Examine for pitting and rust scale]
    E -- Severe pitting or section loss --> R6[REJECT]
    E -- Surface rust only --> R7[Apply lubrication and inspect monthly]
    B -- Deformation --> F[Check for kinks, birdcages, doglegs]
    F -- Any kink or birdcage present --> R8[REJECT without delay]
    F -- Slight waviness --> R9[Examine more frequently]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**"Define a Hoisting Apparatus and list any four of its essential components."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A hoisting apparatus is a mechanical device used for raising, lowering, and transporting heavy loads in a controlled manner. It consists of a prime mover, power transmission system, braking system, wire rope, hook block, and sheaves. The four essential components are:
1. **Prime mover** (electric motor) — to provide power.
2. **Hoisting drum** — to wind the wire rope.
3. **Wire rope and hook** — to suspend and attach the load.
4. **Brake** — to hold the load stationary when power is cut off.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners expect a definition followed by clearly labelled components. Avoid vague answers like "parts of a crane".

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
**"What is the minimum safety factor for a wire rope used in general hoisting duty? State the governing IS code."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
The minimum safety factor for a wire rope used in general hoisting duty is **3.5** (as per IS 3938). For personnel-carrying hoists, it is **10**. This factor is the ratio of the **minimum breaking force of the rope** to the **maximum working load** on the rope.

> [!NOTE]
> Stating the IS code reference is mandatory — students lose 1 mark for omitting it.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, and students must answer either **Question A or Question B** in full.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Equivalent Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) crane hoisting apparatus. Label all major sub-systems. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hoist must lift a load of **8000 kg** through a height of **10 m** using a **2-fall reeving** system. The drum diameter is **300 mm**, efficiency is **80%**, and the drum rotates at **15 RPM**. Determine:
1. Force per rope fall
2. Drum torque
3. Motor power required (use service factor 1.5)
4. Total length of wire rope (assume 2 dead turns on the drum)
**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A (a):

**Functional Architecture of EOT Crane Hoisting Apparatus:**

The EOT crane hoisting apparatus consists of the following sub-systems:

1. **Power Supply & Switching** — 3-phase AC supply at 415 V, 50 Hz is fed through a main isolator, fuses/MCB, and a crane main contactor.
2. **Hoisting Motor** — A slip-ring or squirrel-cage induction motor (typically 5–30 kW) with an inbuilt electromagnetic brake.
3. **Gear Reducer** — A double or triple helical gear unit that reduces motor RPM (1440) to drum RPM (5–20).
4. **Coupling & Brake** — A flexible coupling connects the gearbox to the drum shaft, with a fail-safe disc/band brake mounted on the high-speed shaft.
5. **Drum & Wire Rope** — A helical-grooved steel drum onto which a 6 × 19 or 6 × 36 wire rope is wound. The rope reeved through a top sheave and a bottom hook block.
6. **Hook Block** — A forged steel block carrying 2–6 sheaves, a swivel hook with safety latch, and a suspension pin.
7. **Overload Limiter** — A load cell or hydraulic pressure sensor that cuts off hoisting at **110% of SWL**.
8. **Limit Switches** — Top and bottom rotary limit switches prevent over-winding and over-unwinding.
9. **Control Station** — Pendant push-button station with start, stop, and emergency stop buttons.

> All these sub-systems work in **series** — failure of any one stops the entire operation. This is the *series reliability architecture* of a hoisting apparatus.

---

### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Given:** $W = 8000$ kg, $H = 10$ m, $n = 2$ falls, $D_{drum} = 300$ mm, $\eta = 0.80$, $N = 15$ RPM, dead turns = 2, service factor = 1.5

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Force per rope fall:} \\
&\qquad W = 8000 \times 9.81 = 78{,}480 \text{ N} \\
&\qquad F = \dfrac{W}{n} = \dfrac{78{,}480}{2} = 39{,}240 \text{ N} \\
&\textbf{(2) Drum torque (accounting for efficiency):} \\
&\qquad T = \dfrac{F \times D_{drum}}{2 \times \eta} = \dfrac{39{,}240 \times 0.300}{2 \times 0.80} \\
&\qquad T = \dfrac{11{,}772}{1.60} = 7{,}357.5 \text{ N·m} \\
&\textbf{(3) Motor power required:} \\
&\qquad \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 15}{60} = 1.571 \text{ rad/s} \\
&\qquad P = T \times \omega = 7{,}357.5 \times 1.571 = 11{,}558.5 \text{ W} \\
&\qquad P_{sized} = 1.5 \times 11{,}558.5 = 17{,}337.7 \text{ W} \approx 17.3 \text{ kW} \\
&\qquad \text{Standard motor selected} = 18.5 \text{ kW} \\
&\textbf{(4) Total wire rope length:} \\
&\qquad L_{fall} = H + 2 \times \pi \times D_{drum} = 10 + 2 \times \pi \times 0.300 \\
&\qquad L_{fall} = 10 + 1.885 = 11.885 \text{ m} \\
&\qquad L_{total} = n \times L_{fall} = 2 \times 11.885 = 23.77 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (1) 39,240 N, (2) 7,357.5 N·m, (3) 18.5 kW motor, (4) 23.77 m of wire rope

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Equivalent Model]**

**(a)** Explain the construction of a wire rope with a sketch. Discuss the difference between **Right Regular Lay (RRL)** and **Left Lang Lay (LLL)** ropes, and state where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wire rope is subjected to a working tension of **20 kN**. Using a **6 × 19 fibre-core rope** with a metallic area factor of **0.38** and a rope material strength of **1770 N/mm²**, determine:
1. Minimum breaking force required (use SF = 5)
2. Minimum rope diameter
3. Minimum drum and sheave diameters
**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Wire Rope Construction:**

A wire rope is built from **cold-drawn high-carbon steel wires** (typically 0.3 mm to 2.0 mm diameter) that are helically laid in **strands** around a central **core**. Standard hoisting ropes have **6 strands**, each containing **19 wires** (in a 12/6/1 arrangement for general use or 6/6/1 for flexibility).

**Cross-section description:**

- **Outer wires** carry the maximum tensile load and resist abrasion.
- **Inner wires** provide flexibility and fatigue resistance.
- **Core** — fibre core (FC), steel wire core (WSC), or independent wire rope core (IWRC) — supports the strands, retains lubricant, and absorbs transverse loads.

**RRL vs. LLL:**

| Feature | Right Regular Lay (RRL) | Left Lang Lay (LLL) |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Strand direction** | Strands laid **clockwise** around the core | Strands laid **counter-clockwise** |
| **Wire direction** | Wires within strand **parallel** to strand axis | Wires within strand **cross** the strand axis |
| **Surface appearance** | Smooth, straight wire lines visible | Diagonal wire lines visible on surface |
| **Wearing property** | Wears on the **crown** of strands — easy to inspect | Wears on the **sides** of strands — hard to detect |
| **Flexibility** | Less flexible | More flexible |
| **Preferred use** | **Standard hoisting** (EOT, gantry) where external wear is the primary concern | **Heavy-duty** applications with high bending and shock loads (mining, dredging) |

> **In KTU examinations, always mention the direction of strand and wire lay for full marks.**

---

### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Given:** $F = 20$ kN = 20,000 N, SF = 5, $k = 0.38$, $\sigma_u = 1770$ N/mm²

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Minimum breaking force required:} \\
&\qquad F_{breaking} = F \times SF = 20{,}000 \times 5 = 100{,}000 \text{ N} = 100 \text{ kN} \\
&\textbf{(2) Minimum rope diameter:} \\
&\qquad A_{metal} = \dfrac{F_{breaking}}{\sigma_u} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{1{,}770} = 56.50 \text{ mm}^2 \\
&\qquad d^2 = \dfrac{A_{metal}}{k} = \dfrac{56.50}{0.38} = 148.68 \\
&\qquad d = \sqrt{148.68} = 12.19 \text{ mm} \\
&\qquad \text{Standard rope selected} = 14 \text{ mm} \\
&\textbf{(3) Minimum drum and sheave diameters:} \\
&\qquad D_{drum} \geq 20 \times d = 20 \times 14 = 280 \text{ mm} \\
&\qquad D_{sheave} \geq 18 \times d = 18 \times 14 = 252 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** (1) 100 kN breaking force, (2) 14 mm rope, (3) Drum ≥ 280 mm, Sheave ≥ 252 mm

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $g = 9.81$** when the load is given in kg — result in torque/power being **off by a factor of 10**.
> 2. **Not accounting for efficiency** $\eta$ in the torque calculation — leads to **under-sized motor** selection.
> 3. **Omitting the service factor** (1.25 to 1.75) when sizing the motor — examiner deducts **1–2 marks** for "un-realistic motor selection".
> 4. **Confusing $D_{drum}$ and $D_{drum}/2$** — torque uses the **radius**, not the diameter.
> 5. **Using SF = 3.5 without stating IS code** — only 50% marks awarded; full credit requires both value and code.
> 6. **Forgetting the dead wraps** in rope length calculation — examiner treats it as a "design omission".
> 7. **Writing "drum diameter" instead of "drum radius"** in the torque formula — instant mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**

- ✅ A **hoisting apparatus** combines a prime mover, transmission, brake, rope, and hook — all working in series for safety.
- ✅ **Minimum safety factor** = **3.5** for general hoisting; **10** for personnel carriers.
- ✅ **Standard wire rope designation** is `6 × 19 (12/6/1) IWRC RRL` for heavy duty.
- ✅ **Drum diameter** ≥ **20 × rope diameter**; **Sheave diameter** ≥ **18 × rope diameter**.
- ✅ **RRL ropes** are standard for hoists; **LLL ropes** are used where flex-life is critical.
- ✅ **Reeving** with `n` falls reduces rope tension to `W/n` and increases lifting speed by factor `n`.
- ✅ **Brake** is always **fail-safe** — a spring-applied, electrically released disc brake on the motor shaft.
- ✅ **Overload limiter** cuts off hoisting at **110% of Safe Working Load (SWL)**.
- ✅ **Top limit switch** prevents over-winding (hook hitting trolley); **bottom limit** prevents over-unwinding.
- ✅ **Wire rope discard criteria** — **>10% diameter reduction** OR **>10 broken wires per lay length** → REMOVE FROM SERVICE.
- ✅ **Hook throat opening increase > 5%** → withdraw hook immediately.
- ✅ **Daily pre-use inspection** is statutory under the Indian Factories Act, 1948 (Section 28 & 29).
- ✅ Key standards: **IS 3938** (wire ropes), **IS 3177** (hoisting chains), **IS 807** (cranes), **IS 1367** (hooks), **IS 2062** (drum steel).
- ✅ **Lifting speed** $v = \pi D N / (60 n)$ — the only equation that links drum RPM, rope reeving, and load speed.
- ✅ **Motor power** $P = T \omega / \eta$ — never forget the efficiency term.

> **🎯 Final KTU Topper Tip:** In Part B questions, always begin with a **diagram (even a simple block diagram)**, then a **definition/classification**, then the **derivation with SI units at every step**. This satisfies the 7-mark valuation pattern where examiners allocate 2 marks for diagram, 1 mark for definition, and 4 marks for analytical work.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types - Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **crane** is a mechanically powered, multi-purpose machine equipped with a **hoisting rope**, **sheaves (pulleys)**, and a **boom or jib**, used for the vertical lifting and horizontal transportation of heavy loads within a defined working radius. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OESFT833 (Safety in Material Handling)**, cranes are classified as **mechanical material handling equipment (MMHE)** that fall under the regulatory purview of the **Factories Act 1948 (Section 28, 29, 32)**, the **Indian Standard IS 3177:1999 (Code of Practice for Cranes)**, and **IS 807:2006 (Design of Cranes – Code of Practice)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *“A crane is a type of machine, generally equipped with a hoist rope, wire ropes or chains, and sheaves, that can be used both to lift and lower materials and to move them horizontally. It is mainly used for lifting heavy things and transporting them to other places.”* — Adapted from IS 3177:1999 and KTU OESFT833 Module 3.

### 1.1.1 Fundamental Components of a Crane

| S.No | Component | Function | Safety Relevance |
|------|-----------|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Boom / Jib** | Long arm that extends the load reach | Structural integrity is critical |
| 2 | **Hook Block** | Point of attachment to the load | Must be checked for cracks |
| 3 | **Hoist Mechanism** | Lifts/lowers the load via ropes | Wire rope safety factor ≥ 3.5 |
| 4 | **Trolley** | Moves the load horizontally along the bridge | Travel limit switches |
| 5 | **Bridge / Girder** | Supports the trolley (in overhead cranes) | Fatigue failure check |
| 6 | **Cab / Operator Cabin** | Houses the operator and controls | Visibility, ergonomics |
| 7 | **Counterweight** | Balances the lifted load | Prevents tipping |
| 8 | **Outriggers** | Stabilize mobile cranes during operation | Must be fully extended |
| 9 | **Brake System** | Holds the load stationary | Tested daily |
| 10 | **Limit Switches** | Prevent over-hoist / over-travel | Mandatory safety devices |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Student-Friendly):**
> Imagine your **human arm**. Your *shoulder joint* acts as the **pivot point** (slewing mechanism), your *upper arm* is the **boom/jib**, your *elbow* is the **hoist drum/pulley**, and your *hand* is the **hook block** holding the object. Now, scale that arm to **20 meters long** with a lifting capacity of **50 tonnes** — that is essentially what a crane is: a **mechanically superhuman arm** designed to lift what no human could ever lift, with pinpoint precision.

### 1.2.1 Why Cranes Exist in Material Handling

Cranes solve the **three fundamental problems** of material movement:

1. **Vertical displacement** — Lifting loads from ground level to elevated platforms (e.g., construction sites, shipyards).
2. **Horizontal displacement** — Moving loads over short distances within a workspace.
3. **Combined 3D displacement** — Lifting AND moving loads simultaneously (e.g., loading containers onto trucks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Safety Highlight:**
> According to **OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1400–1441** and the **Indian Factories Act**, cranes account for nearly **22% of all material handling fatalities** in heavy industries. Hence, the *type of crane selected* for a given task is itself a **safety decision**.

---

## 1.3 Standardized Safety Metrics (Mandatory Bold)

- **Safe Working Load (SWL)** — also called *Rated Capacity* — measured in **tonnes (t)** or **kilonewtons (kN)**.
- **Design Factor of Safety** for wire ropes = **3.5 (minimum)** as per **IS 2266**.
- **Design Factor of Safety** for hooks = **2.0 (minimum)** as per **IS 3815**.
- **Maximum allowable wind speed** for crane operation = **72 km/h (20 m/s)** as per **IS 807**.
- **Minimum operator certification** required for cranes above **1 tonne SWL**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cranes (KTU Module 3 Core Topic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes cranes based on **mobility**, **structural configuration**, and **power source**. Below is the exhaustive taxonomy.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Mobility

#### (A) Mobile Cranes

Mobile cranes are mounted on a **wheeled or tracked carrier** and can be transported to the worksite. They are the most common type used in **construction, infrastructure, and emergency rescue**.

**Sub-Types of Mobile Cranes:**

1. **Telescopic Boom Crane (Hydraulic Crane)**
   - Uses a **multi-section telescoping boom** powered by hydraulic cylinders.
   - Common in **urban construction, road projects**.
   - Capacity range: **10 t to 1,200 t** (e.g., Liebherr LTM 1750).
   - **Safety feature:** Boom extension interlocks.

2. **Lattice Boom Crawler Crane**
