   - Mounted on **caterpillar tracks**, offering excellent ground stability.
   - Used in **heavy industrial lifts, wind turbine installation**.
   - Capacity range: **50 t to 3,500 t** (e.g., Manitowoc 31000).
   - **Safety feature:** Track pressure distribution plates.

3. **Truck-Mounted Crane (Hydraulic Truck Crane)**
   - Mounted on a **commercial truck chassis**.
   - Fast mobilization, ideal for **short-duration lifts**.
   - **Safety feature:** Outrigger load sensors.

4. **Rough Terrain Crane**
   - 4-wheel drive, single cab, designed for **off-road sites**.
   - Used in **oil fields, mining, unpaved construction sites**.

5. **All-Terrain Crane**
   - Hybrid of truck-mounted and rough terrain cranes.
   - Can travel on **highways at 80 km/h** and operate in **rough sites**.

6. **Crawler Crane**
   - Tracked undercarriage, low ground pressure (~ **0.05–0.1 MPa**).
   - Cannot travel on public roads; must be dismantled and transported.

#### (B) Tower Cranes

Used in **high-rise building construction**. Fixed to the ground (or climbing the building itself).

**Sub-Types:**

1. **Hammerhead (Flat-Top) Tower Crane** — Horizontal jib, counter-jib.
2. **Luffing-Jib Tower Crane** — Jib can be raised/lowered to vary radius (used in congested sites).
3. **Self-Erecting Tower Crane** — Folds into a transportable unit; hydraulic erection.
4. **Climbing Tower Crane** — Climbs up the building as construction progresses.

**Safety Relevance:** Tower cranes are the **leading cause of crane-related fatalities** in urban construction (often due to wind, foundation failure, or slewing collisions).

#### (C) Overhead Cranes (Bridge / EOT Cranes)

Installed in **factories, warehouses, steel mills, shipyards**.

- **Single Girder EOT** — Light duty, up to **10 t**.
- **Double Girder EOT** — Heavy duty, up to **500 t**.
- **Gantry Crane** — Bridge supported on legs with wheels on ground rails.
- **Semi-Gantry Crane** — One end on a runway, other end on floor rails.
- **Jib Crane** — Wall-mounted or pillar-mounted with rotating jib.

#### (D) Specialized Cranes

- **Floating Crane** — Mounted on a barge for port operations.
- **Rail-Mounted Crane (RMG/RTG)** — Container terminal cranes.
- **Telescopic Handler (Telehandler)** — Hybrid of forklift and crane.
- **Level-Luffing Crane** — Used in shipyards for vertical load movement.

---

### 2.1.2 Classification by Power Source

| Power Source | Typical Application | Safety Note |
|--------------|--------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Electric** | Indoor EOT cranes, factory cranes | Earthing mandatory |
| **Diesel Engine** | Mobile cranes, rough terrain | Exhaust ventilation in tunnels |
| **Hydraulic** | Telescopic cranes, excavator cranes | Hydraulic hose burst risk |
| **Pneumatic** | Hazardous area (explosive) cranes | Air quality maintenance |
| **Hybrid (Diesel-Electric)** | Modern mobile cranes | Dual-system maintenance |

---

## 2.2 The Physics of Crane Operation (KTU High-Yield Theory)

### 2.2.1 The Four Operating Movements of a Crane

A crane can perform **four fundamental motions**, each governed by its own motor and safety system:

1. **Hoisting** — Vertical lifting/lowering (Hoist Motor + Brake).
2. **Luffing** — Raising/lowering the boom (Luffing Cylinder/Winch).
3. **Slewing** — Horizontal rotation of the crane (Slewing Motor + Gear).
4. **Traveling** — Moving the entire crane (Travel Motor + Wheels/Tracks).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick: "H-L-S-T"** → *Hoist, Luff, Swing, Travel* — the **four cardinal motions of a crane**. Examiners frequently ask students to list these.

### 2.2.2 The Lever Principle Behind Every Crane

Every crane operates on the **principle of moments (torque balance)**:

$$\sum M_{pivot} = 0$$

This means the **clockwise moment** (load × load radius) must be balanced by the **counter-clockwise moment** (counterweight × counterweight radius). When this balance is lost, the crane **tips forward** — the most catastrophic crane failure mode.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| S.No | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application / Limitation |
|------|-------------------|----------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Moment of Load (Tipping Moment)** | $M_L = W \times R$ | kN·m | Used to calculate stability |
| 2 | **Counterweight Moment** | $M_C = W_C \times R_C$ | kN·m | Must equal or exceed $M_L$ |
| 3 | **Maximum Safe Load (Theoretical)** | $W_{max} = \dfrac{M_C}{R}$ | kN | Assumes perfect balance |
| 4 | **Actual Safe Working Load (with Safety Factor)** | $SWL = \dfrac{W_{max}}{n}$ | kN | $n$ = factor of safety (typically 1.5 to 2.0 for mobile cranes) |
| 5 | **Wire Rope Factor of Safety** | $FoS = \dfrac{Breaking\ Strength}{Working\ Load}$ | dimensionless | Minimum 3.5 as per IS 2266 |
| 6 | **Hook Factor of Safety** | $FoS_{hook} = \dfrac{Proving\ Load}{SWL}$ | dimensionless | Minimum 2.0 as per IS 3815 |
| 7 | **Hoist Drum Line Pull** | $F = \dfrac{W}{n_{lines} \times \eta}$ | N | $n_{lines}$ = number of reeving parts, $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 8 | **Slewing Torque** | $T = F \times R$ | N·m | Resisting moment during rotation |
| 9 | **Wind Pressure on Crane** | $F_w = 0.5 \times \rho \times v^2 \times C_d \times A$ | N | Used for outdoor crane stability |
| 10 | **Ground Bearing Pressure (Outriggers)** | $p = \dfrac{W_{total}}{A_{outrigger}}$ | N/m² or kPa | Must be < allowable soil capacity |
| 11 | **Deflection of Crane Girder (EOT)** | $\delta = \dfrac{W \times L^3}{48 \times E \times I}$ | m | Limited to L/750 for EOT cranes |
| 12 | **Critical Buckling Load (Boom)** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times E \times I}{L_e^2}$ | N | Euler's formula for long booms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always carry these formulas in a single sheet during revision. The **Tipping Moment** ($M = W \times R$) and **Wire Rope FoS** questions appear in **almost every KTU ESE**.

### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Crane Type Used | Typical Capacity |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| **Shipbuilding** | Goliath / Gantry Crane | 1,000 t to 20,000 t |
| **Construction** | Tower Crane | 6 t to 80 t |
| **Power Plants** | Heavy Lift Crawler Crane | 600 t to 3,500 t |
| **Steel Mills** | Ladle Crane | 100 t to 550 t |
| **Container Ports** | Ship-to-Shore (STS) Crane | 40 t to 120 t (under spreader) |
| **Oil & Gas** | Offshore Pedestal Crane | 30 t to 1,500 t |
| **Warehouses** | EOT / Jib Crane | 1 t to 20 t |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Safe Load from Tipping Equilibrium

**Problem Statement:** A mobile crane has a **counterweight of 80 kN** located at a **radius of 3.5 m** from the slewing axis. Determine the **maximum load** it can lift at a radius of **10 m** without tipping. Assume a **factor of safety of 1.5** against tipping.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the Tipping Pivot:**
For a mobile crane, the **front outrigger** (or the front edge of the tracks) is the **tipping pivot** when lifting forward. The crane will tip about this pivot if the load moment exceeds the counterweight moment.

**Step 2 — Apply the Principle of Moments (Static Equilibrium):**

At the **incipient tipping condition**, the sum of moments about the tipping pivot equals zero:

$$\sum M_{pivot} = 0$$

The two competing moments are:
- **Restoring Moment** (provided by counterweight and crane's own weight on the rear side).
- **Overturning Moment** (provided by the lifted load on the front side).

**Step 3 — Write the Moment Equation:**

Let:
- $W_C$ = Counterweight = **80 kN**
- $R_C$ = Counterweight radius = **3.5 m**
- $W$ = Lifted load = **?** (unknown)
- $R$ = Load radius = **10 m**

Equating restoring and overturning moments:

$$W_C \times R_C = W \times R$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Theoretical Maximum Load ($W_{max}$):**

$$W_{max} = \dfrac{W_C \times R_C}{R}$$

Substituting values:

$$W_{max} = \dfrac{80 \times 3.5}{10} = \dfrac{280}{10} = 28\ \text{kN}$$

Converting to tonnes (1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN):

$$W_{max} = \dfrac{28}{9.81} \approx 2.854\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Factor of Safety (FoS):**

The **Safe Working Load (SWL)** is the theoretical maximum load reduced by the factor of safety:

$$SWL = \dfrac{W_{max}}{n}$$

With $n = 1.5$:

$$SWL = \dfrac{28}{1.5} \approx 18.67\ \text{kN}$$

$$SWL = \dfrac{18.67}{9.81} \approx 1.90\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer with Units:**

$$\boxed{SWL \approx 18.67\ \text{kN} \approx 1.90\ \text{tonnes}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the crane *theoretically* could lift 2.85 tonnes at 10 m radius, applying the safety factor of 1.5 brings the **safe** capacity down to **1.90 tonnes**. This is the value the operator uses on the load chart.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hoist Drum Line Pull

**Problem Statement:** A crane must lift a load of **50 kN** using a **4-part reeving system** (4 lines supporting the hook block). The **mechanical efficiency** of the hoist is **85%**. Calculate the **line pull** required on the hoist drum.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the Reeving Concept:**
In a multi-part reeving system, the **load is shared equally** among all the supporting lines. If there are $n_{lines}$ parts, each part carries $\dfrac{W}{n_{lines}}$ of the total load.

**Step 2 — Apply the Load Distribution:**

$$F_{line} = \dfrac{W}{n_{lines}}$$

With $W = 50$ kN and $n_{lines} = 4$:

$$F_{line} = \dfrac{50}{4} = 12.5\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Account for Mechanical Efficiency:**

The actual line pull must overcome not just the load per line, but also **friction losses** in the sheaves and drum. The efficiency factor $\eta$ reduces the effective force:

$$F_{drum} = \dfrac{F_{line}}{\eta}$$

With $\eta = 0.85$:

$$F_{drum} = \dfrac{12.5}{0.85} \approx 14.71\ \text{kN}$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_{drum} \approx 14.71\ \text{kN} \ (\approx\ 1,500\ \text{kgf})}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EOT Crane Girder Deflection Check

**Problem Statement:** A **simply supported EOT crane girder** of **span $L = 20$ m** carries a **hoist load of 50 kN** at the **midspan**. The girder is an **ISMB 600** with a **moment of inertia $I = 92,000 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^4$** and **Young's modulus $E = 2 \times 10^5\ \text{N/mm}^2$**. Check if the deflection is within the **permissible limit of L/750** as per **IS 3177**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the Midspan Deflection Formula for a Simply Supported Beam with a Central Point Load:**

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{W \times L^3}{48 \times E \times I}$$

**Step 2 — Convert All Units to a Consistent System (N and mm):**

- $W = 50\ \text{kN} = 50,000\ \text{N}$
- $L = 20\ \text{m} = 20,000\ \text{mm}$
- $E = 2 \times 10^5\ \text{N/mm}^2$
- $I = 92,000 \times 10^4\ \text{mm}^4 = 9.2 \times 10^8\ \text{mm}^4$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Formula:**

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{50,000 \times (20,000)^3}{48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 9.2 \times 10^8}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Step-by-Step:**

Numerator:
$$50,000 \times 8 \times 10^{12} = 4 \times 10^{17}$$

Denominator:
$$48 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 9.2 \times 10^8 = 48 \times 1.84 \times 10^{14} = 8.832 \times 10^{15}$$

Dividing:
$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{17}}{8.832 \times 10^{15}} = \dfrac{400}{8.832} \approx 45.29\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Permissible Deflection:**

$$\delta_{permissible} = \dfrac{L}{750} = \dfrac{20,000}{750} \approx 26.67\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Compare and Conclude:**

$$\delta_{max} = 45.29\ \text{mm} > \delta_{permissible} = 26.67\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Girder is UNSAFE — stiffener or higher section required.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Crane Selection Decision Table

The KTU Module 3 syllabus often asks: *"Which crane is suitable for a given application?"* Use the following decision matrix.

| Application Scenario | Best Crane Type | Why? |
|----------------------|-----------------|------|
| Lifting steel beams in a workshop | **EOT Crane (Single/Double Girder)** | Indoor use, fixed runway, repetitive lifts |
| High-rise building construction | **Tower Crane (Hammerhead/Luffing)** | Vertical reach > 100 m, fixed base |
| Unloading containers at a port | **Ship-to-Shore (STS) Gantry Crane** | High reach over ships, container spreader |
| Wind turbine installation in remote area | **Crawler Crane (Lattice Boom)** | No roads, soft ground, high capacity |
| Emergency rescue in collapsed building | **Telescopic Mobile Crane (All-Terrain)** | Fast mobilization, variable radius |
| Explosion-proof chemical plant | **Pneumatic Hoist + EOT** | No electrical spark risk |
| Moving molten metal in steel plant | **Ladle Crane (Heavy-duty EOT)** | Heat resistant, high capacity (100–500 t) |
| Small workshop, light loads (< 2 t) | **Wall-mounted Jib Crane** | Economical, 180° rotation, small footprint |
| Offshore oil platform | **Pedestal / API 2C Crane** | Marine environment, rated for vessel motion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Classification Tree of Cranes (Module 3 Master Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CRANES] --> B[Mobile Cranes]
    A --> C[Tower Cranes]
    A --> D[Overhead Cranes]
    A --> E[Specialized Cranes]

    B --> B1[Telescopic Boom Crane]
    B --> B2[Lattice Boom Crawler Crane]
    B --> B3[Truck Mounted Crane]
    B --> B4[Rough Terrain Crane]
    B --> B5[All Terrain Crane]
    B --> B6[Side Boom Crane]

    C --> C1[Hammerhead Tower Crane]
    C --> C2[Luffing Jib Tower Crane]
    C --> C3[Self Erecting Tower Crane]
    C --> C4[Climbing Tower Crane]

    D --> D1[Single Girder EOT]
    D --> D2[Double Girder EOT]

    D --> D5[Jib Crane]

    E --> E1[Floating Crane]
    E --> E2[Container Crane STS]
    E --> E3[RTG RMG Crane]

    E --> E5[Pedestal Crane]

    B1 -.Safety.-> S1[Outrigger Load Monitor]
    C1 -.Safety.-> S2[Anti Collision System]
    D1 -.Safety.-> S3[Over Travel Limit Switch]
    E2 -.Safety.-> S4[Trolley Anti Skew System]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Mobile Crane

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Power Source: Diesel Engine] --> T[Transmission: Hydraulic Pumps]
    T --> H[Hoist Motor]
    T --> L[Luffing Cylinder]
    T --> S[Slewing Motor]
    T --> Tr[Travel Motor]

    H --> D[Hoist Drum]
    D --> WR[Wire Rope]
    WR --> HK[Hook Block]
    HK --> LD[Lifted Load]

    L --> BM[Boom Assembly]
    BM --> WR

    S --> SW[Slewing Ring]
    SW --> UP[Upper Structure: Cab and Counterweight]

    Tr --> WH[Wheels or Tracks]
    WH --> GN[Ground]

    OP[Operator in Cabin] --> CTRL1[Control Lever Hoist]
    OP --> CTRL2[Control Lever Luff]
    OP --> CTRL3[Control Lever Slew]
    OP --> CTRL4[Control Lever Travel]

    CTRL1 --> H
    CTRL2 --> L
    CTRL3 --> S
    CTRL4 --> Tr

    SL[Safety Layer] --> LS1[Load Moment Indicator]
    SL --> LS2[Anti Two Block Device]
    SL --> LS3[Outrigger Load Sensor]
    SL --> LS4[Limit Switches]
    SL --> LS5[Anemometer for Wind Speed]
    SL --> LS6[Emergency Stop Button]

    LS1 -.feedback.-> OP
    LS5 -.alarm.-> OP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Forces Acting on a Crane (Free-Body Concept)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CraneSystem
        direction LR
        FW[Wind Force Fw acting on Boom] --> BOOM[Boom Jib]
        BOOM --> R1[Reaction at Pivot]
        BOOM --> LIFT[Load W acting downward]
        LIFT --> R1
        BOOM --> CW[Counterweight Wc on rear side]
        CW --> R1
    end

    R1 --> O1[Tipping Pivot Front Outrigger]
    O1 --> GR[Ground Reaction]

    M1[Moment Overturning: W x R] --> R1
    M2[Moment Restoring: Wc x Rc] --> R1
    M1 -.must be less than.-> M2
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: EOT Crane Working Cycle (Sequence Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ST1[Step 1: Operator Starts Crane] --> ST2[Step 2: Lower Hook to Load]
    ST2 --> ST3[Step 3: Attach Slings to Load]
    ST3 --> ST4[Step 4: Hoist Load Slightly to Test Lift]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5: Check for Tilt or Swing]
    ST5 --> ST6[Step 6: Hoist Load to Required Height]
    ST6 --> ST7[Step 7: Trolley Travel to Destination]
    ST7 --> ST8[Step 8: Lower Load to Resting Position]
    ST8 --> ST9[Step 9: Detach Slings and Return Hook]
    ST9 --> ST10[Step 10: Park Crane and Log Operation]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a crane. List any four fundamental motions a crane can perform.**

**Model Answer:**

A **crane** is a mechanical material handling device used for vertical lifting and horizontal movement of heavy loads, equipped with a hoisting mechanism, wire ropes, sheaves, and a boom or jib. (1 mark)

The **four fundamental motions** of a crane are: (2 marks)

1. **Hoisting** — Raising or lowering the load vertically.
2. **Luffing** — Raising or lowering the boom to change the load radius.
3. **Slewing (Swinging)** — Rotating the upper structure horizontally.
4. **Traveling** — Moving the entire crane from one location to another.

**Memory aid:** *H-L-S-T* (Hoist, Luff, Swing, Travel).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between a mobile crane and a tower crane. Mention two typical applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mobile Crane | Tower Crane |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Mobility** | Self-propelled on wheels or tracks | Fixed at one location (bolted or ballasted) |
| **Boom Type** | Telescopic or lattice boom | Horizontal jib with counter-jib |
| **Reach** | Typically 20–80 m radius | Up to 80–100 m radius, very high vertical reach |
| **Setup Time** | Quick (15–30 min) | Long (1–3 days for erection) |
| **Typical Applications** | (1) Loading/unloading trucks in construction, (2) Emergency rescue operations | (1) High-rise building construction, (2) Long-span industrial projects |

(3 marks for structured answer)

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Each Module typically offers one Part B question for 14 marks with **internal choice** between **Option A** and **Option B**. Sub-parts (a) and (b) usually carry **7 marks each**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) Classify cranes in detail. With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of an EOT (Electric Overhead Traveling) crane. Also list six safety devices used in EOT cranes. (7 marks)**

**(b) A mobile crane has a counterweight of 100 kN placed at a distance of 4 m from the slewing axis. The crane is lifting a load at a radius of 12 m. Calculate: (i) the theoretical maximum load that can be lifted, (ii) the Safe Working Load (SWL) using a factor of safety of 1.75, and (iii) whether the crane is safe if the actual load to be lifted is 2 tonnes. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Classification of Cranes:**

Cranes are classified into **four major categories**: (1 mark)

1. **Mobile Cranes** – Telescopic, Crawler, Truck-mounted, Rough Terrain, All-Terrain.
2. **Tower Cranes** – Hammerhead, Luffing-jib, Self-erecting, Climbing.
3. **Overhead (Bridge) Cranes** – Single Girder EOT, Double Girder EOT, Gantry, Semi-Gantry, Jib Crane.
4. **Specialized Cranes** – Floating, STS, RTG/RMG, Telehandler, Pedestal.

**Construction and Working of EOT Crane:**

An **EOT crane** consists of: (3 marks)

- **Bridge (Girder):** Horizontal beam spanning the entire bay, supported on end-carriages that travel on **runway rails** fixed on building columns.
- **Trolley:** Mounted on the bridge, houses the **hoist mechanism** (motor, gearbox, drum, wire rope, hook).
- **End Carriages:** Located at both ends of the bridge, each fitted with **travel wheels** driven by separate motors.
- **Cab / Pendant / Radio Control:** Used by the operator to control movements.
- **Power Supply:** Through **conductor bars (copper rails)** or **festoon cables** (cable reel).

**Working Principle:**
- The **hoist motor** rotates the drum, which winds/unwinds the wire rope, raising or lowering the **hook block**.
- The **trolley motor** moves the trolley horizontally along the bridge.
- The **bridge travel motors** move the entire crane along the runway rails.
- The combined motion enables the load to reach **any point** within the rectangular coverage area.

**Six Safety Devices in EOT Cranes:** (2 marks)

1. **Over-hoist limit switch** – Prevents hook from over-traveling upward.
2. **Over-travel limit switch** – Prevents trolley from hitting the bridge ends.
3. **End-stop buffers** – Absorb impact at the end of travel.
4. **Audible warning horn / hooter**.
5. **Load brake / Holding brake** – Holds the load stationary during power failure.
6. **Phase failure relay / Earth fault relay** – Protects motor from voltage issues.

**Sketch (Description in Text):** A **top view** showing a rectangular bay with two parallel runway rails on building columns, the bridge spanning across, a trolley on the bridge, and a hook hanging down. A **side view** showing the bridge height, trolley, hoist drum in the trolley housing, and the hook block suspended by wire rope.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**

- Counterweight: $W_C = 100$ kN
- Counterweight radius: $R_C = 4$ m
- Load radius: $R = 12$ m
- Factor of safety: $n = 1.75$
- Actual load to check: $W_{actual} = 2$ tonnes $= 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$ kN

**(i) Theoretical Maximum Load:**

$$W_{max} = \dfrac{W_C \times R_C}{R}$$

$$W_{max} = \dfrac{100 \times 4}{12} = \dfrac{400}{12} \approx 33.33\ \text{kN}$$

In tonnes: $W_{max} = \dfrac{33.33}{9.81} \approx 3.40$ tonnes **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Safe Working Load (SWL):**

$$SWL = \dfrac{W_{max}}{n} = \dfrac{33.33}{1.75} \approx 19.05\ \text{kN}$$

In tonnes: $SWL = \dfrac{19.05}{9.81} \approx 1.94$ tonnes **(3 marks)**

**(iii) Safety Check:**

Compare actual load with SWL:
$$W_{actual} = 19.62\ \text{kN} > SWL = 19.05\ \text{kN}$$

The actual load **exceeds** the SWL by a small margin. **(2 marks)**

$$\boxed{\text{Therefore, the crane is NOT safe to lift 2 tonnes at 12 m radius.}}$$

**Recommendation:** Either reduce the load to **1.94 tonnes** or increase the counterweight / move the load closer to the slewing axis.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) Explain the construction, working, and applications of a Tower Crane. List four safety precautions specific to tower crane operation. (7 marks)**

**(b) A crane is to lift a load of 80 kN using a 6-part reeving system. If the mechanical efficiency of the hoist is 80%, calculate the line pull required on the hoist drum. If the wire rope has a breaking strength of 200 kN, determine the factor of safety. Comment on whether the rope is safe. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Construction of a Tower Crane:**

A tower crane consists of: (3 marks)

- **Mast (Tower):** Vertical lattice steel tower, typically 50–80 m tall (can climb higher).
- **Jib (Working Arm):** Horizontal arm extending forward to carry the load and trolley.
- **Counter-Jib:** Rear arm carrying the **counterweight** to balance the load.
- **Trolley:** Moves along the jib to vary the working radius.
- **Operator's Cabin:** Located near the top of the mast for maximum visibility.
- **Slewing Unit:** Allows the entire upper structure to rotate 360°.
- **Base / Foundation:** Anchored to a concrete foundation or ballasted platform.
- **Climbing Frame (optional):** Allows the crane to "climb" up the building as construction progresses.

**Working Principle:**

- The **hoist winch** (mounted on the counter-jib or in the cabin) raises/lowers the load via a hook block suspended from the trolley.
- The **trolley winch** moves the trolley along the jib, varying the load radius.
- The **slewing motor** rotates the jib horizontally to position the load.
- The load can be **hoisted, lowered, slewed, and trolleed** — but the crane itself does not travel.

**Applications:** (2 marks)

1. Construction of high-rise buildings and skyscrapers.
2. Long-span industrial projects (power plants, refineries).
3. Assembly of large steel structures.
4. Lifting of precast concrete panels.

**Four Safety Precautions for Tower Cranes:** (2 marks)

1. **Foundation check** – The concrete base must withstand the overturning moment, and a torque test must be conducted before installation.
2. **Wind speed monitoring** – Operation must stop if wind speed exceeds 72 km/h (20 m/s); anemometer must be functional.
3. **Anti-collision systems** – When multiple tower cranes overlap, anti-collision devices must be installed.
4. **Regular inspection of load moment indicator (LMI)** – The LMI must be calibrated and operational to prevent overload.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given Data:**

- Load: $W = 80$ kN
- Number of reeving parts: $n_{lines} = 6$
- Mechanical efficiency: $\eta = 0.80$
- Wire rope breaking strength: $F_{break} = 200$ kN

**(i) Line Pull on the Hoist Drum:**

Step 1 — Load per line:

$$F_{line} = \dfrac{W}{n_{lines}} = \dfrac{80}{6} \approx 13.33\ \text{kN}$$

Step 2 — Account for mechanical efficiency:

$$F_{drum} = \dfrac{F_{line}}{\eta} = \dfrac{13.33}{0.80} \approx 16.67\ \text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{F_{drum} \approx 16.67\ \text{kN}} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**(ii) Factor of Safety of the Wire Rope:**

The actual load on the rope is the **line force** (since the rope supports the load through multiple parts, but the rope itself is rated to its breaking strength):

$$FoS = \dfrac{F_{break}}{F_{line}} = \dfrac{200}{13.33} \approx 15.0$$

$$\boxed{FoS \approx 15.0} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Comment on Safety:**

According to **IS 2266**, the minimum factor of safety for wire ropes in crane service is **3.5**.

Since $FoS = 15.0 > 3.5$, the wire rope is **VERY SAFE** for this application. **(2 marks)**

> **Note:** In practice, a more refined calculation considers the **drum diameter, rope construction, and load spectrum**. The above calculation assumes static loading.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of safety** — Always apply FoS to get the SWL; the theoretical maximum is not the safe load.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — Mixing kN, kgf, and tonnes leads to wrong answers. **Standardize: 1 tonne = 9.81 kN.**
> 3. **Wrong reeving assumption** — If the problem says "4-part reeving", the **load is divided by 4**, not multiplied.
> 4. **Efficiency confusion** — Efficiency is **always less than 1** and makes the **drum pull greater**, not less.
> 5. **Sketching the crane incorrectly** — Examiners award 1–2 marks for neat labeled sketches. Don't skip the sketch!
> 6. **Missing safety devices** — Always mention at least 2–3 mandatory safety devices (limit switch, brake, LMI) in any crane-related answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets for KTU ESE — Module 3, Cranes:**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [x] A **crane** is a mechanical lifting device with a **hoist, rope, sheaves, and boom/jib**.
- [x] Four fundamental motions: **Hoist, Luff, Swing (Slew), Travel** (Memory: *H-L-S-T*).
- [x] Four main categories: **Mobile, Tower, Overhead, Specialized**.

### 🔑 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [x] **Tipping Moment:** $M = W \times R$
- [x] **SWL:** $SWL = \dfrac{W_{max}}{n}$ where $n \geq 1.5$ for mobile cranes.
- [x] **Line Pull:** $F_{drum} = \dfrac{W}{n_{lines} \times \eta}$
- [x] **Wire Rope FoS (min):** **3.5** as per IS 2266.
- [x] **Hook FoS (min):** **2.0** as per IS 3815.
- [x] **EOT Deflection Limit:** $\delta \leq \dfrac{L}{750}$.

### 🔑 Crane Type Highlights
- [x] **EOT Crane** – Indoor factory use; **Singly/Doubly Girder**.
- [x] **Tower Crane** – High-rise; **Hammerhead or Luffing-Jib**.
- [x] **Mobile Crane** – Construction; uses **outriggers** for stability.
- [x] **Crawler Crane** – Heavy lift; **low ground pressure** (~0.05 MPa).
- [x] **Gantry Crane** – Outdoor; **legs with wheels** on rails.
- [x] **Jib Crane** – Small workshop; **wall or pillar mounted**.

### 🔑 Safety Devices (Mandatory to Remember)
- [x] **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)**
- [x] **Anti-Two-Block (A2B) Device**
- [x] **Over-Hoist Limit Switch**
- [x] **Outrigger Load Sensors**
- [x] **Anemometer** (for wind speed)
- [x] **Emergency Stop / Horn**
- [x] **Holding Brake / Fail-Safe Brake**

### 🔑 Indian Standards to Remember
- [x] **IS 807:2006** – Design of Cranes
- [x] **IS 3177:1999** – Code of Practice for Cranes
- [x] **IS 2266:2002** – Wire Ropes for Cranes
- [x] **IS 3815:1986** – Crane Hooks
- [x] **IS 4573:1982** – EOT Cranes
- [x] **Factories Act 1948, Sections 28, 29, 32** – Statutory requirements

### 🔑 Numerical Problem Strategy (5-Step Method)
1. **Read the problem** and list all **Given Data** with units.
2. **Identify the goal** (SWL, FoS, line pull, deflection).
3. **Choose the right formula** from the cheat sheet.
4. **Convert units** to a single system (SI: N, mm, MPa).
5. **Solve with FoS** — never present the theoretical max as the final answer.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Every crane lift is a stability problem. Master the moment, and you master the crane."* 🎯
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Safety in Material Handling (OESFT833)**, the topic **"Types"** under **Module 3** refers to the systematic classification of **material handling equipment, lifting devices, conveyors, cranes, hoists, industrial trucks, and storage/retrieval systems** used in industrial, manufacturing, warehousing, and construction environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Material handling equipment types* are categorized based on **function, mode of operation, power source, load capacity, mobility, and the type of material being transported.* The classification helps engineers select the appropriate equipment while ensuring compliance with safety standards such as **IS 3177, IS 4573, IS 807, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.179, and ASME B30 series**.

The major classification framework recognized by KTU follows the **"Unit Load Concept"** and the **MHMC (Material Handling Methods Classification)** developed by the College-Industry Committee on Material Handling Education.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Trolley System"**
>
> Imagine a large hospital where medicines, meals, laundry, and patients need to be moved between floors. The hospital uses:
> - **Wheeled trolleys** (manual trucks) for short distances within a ward,
> - **Elevators** (vertical conveyors) for moving between floors,
> - **Conveyor belts** (automated systems) for laundry,
> - **Cranes/hoists** (lifting devices) for heavy equipment.
>
> Just like the hospital doesn't use a forklift inside an ICU, an engineer must select the **right type** of material handling equipment based on **load, distance, frequency, and safety**. Mishandling this selection is what Module 3 aims to prevent.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used

The following standard metrics (highlighted in **bold**) are universally used when classifying and selecting material handling types:

- **SWL (Safe Working Load)**: Maximum load an equipment can safely handle — measured in **kg, tonnes, or Newtons (N)**.
- **Hook Height / Lift Height**: Vertical reach — measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Span (S)**: Horizontal distance between supports for overhead cranes — measured in **meters (m)**.
- **Travel Speed (v)**: Speed of horizontal/vertical movement — measured in **meters per minute (m/min)**.
- **Capacity (Q)**: Throughput of conveyors — measured in **tonnes per hour (t/h)**.
- **Hoisting Speed (v_h)**: Vertical lifting speed — measured in **m/min**.
- **Cross-Travel Speed (v_c)**: Lateral movement speed — measured in **m/min**.
- **Long-Travel Speed (v_l)**: Longitudinal movement speed — measured in **m/min**.

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Material Handling Types

Material handling types are broadly classified into **5 major categories** as per KTU/industry standards:

### A. By Type of Operation
1. **Transporting** — moving materials between locations (trucks, conveyors).
2. **Positioning** — orienting materials for processing (jigs, fixtures).
3. **Unit Load Formation** — grouping items into single loads (pallets, containers).
4. **Storage & Retrieval** — holding materials in warehouses (AS/RS).
5. **Order Picking** — selecting items from storage.

### B. By Equipment Type (KTU Module 3 Focus)
| Category | Examples |
|----------|----------|
| **Conveyors** | Belt, Roller, Chain, Screw, Pneumatic, Vibratory |
| **Cranes** | Overhead (EOT), Jib, Gantry, Tower, Mobile |
| **Hoists** | Manual, Electric, Pneumatic, Hydraulic |
| **Industrial Trucks** | Forklift, Pallet Jack, Hand Truck, Order Picker |
| **Lifting Tackles** | Slings, Shackles, Hooks, Eye Bolts |
| **Automated Systems** | AGVs, AS/RS, Robotic Arms |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification Logic

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes "Types" of material handling equipment using the **Type × Function × Hazard** matrix. Let us dissect each major type systematically.

---

### 2.2 Types of Conveyors

Conveyors are continuous material handling systems used for moving bulk or unit loads over fixed paths.

#### 2.2.1 Belt Conveyors
- **Principle**: Continuous loop of flexible belt driven by pulleys.
- **Use Case**: Bulk materials (coal, grain, sand), packaged goods.
- **Belt Speed (v)** typically: **0.5 to 5 m/s**.
- **Belt Width (B)**: determined by capacity and material size.

#### 2.2.2 Roller Conveyors
- **Principle**: Gravity or motor-driven rollers rotate to move loads.
- **Types**: Gravity roller, powered roller, accumulating roller.
- **Use Case**: Cartons, pallets in warehouses.
- **Load Capacity per Roller**: typically **50–200 kg per roller**.

#### 2.2.3 Chain Conveyors
- **Principle**: Chain transmits power to carry loads in slats or attachments.
- **Use Case**: Heavy unit loads, automotive assembly lines.

#### 2.2.4 Screw Conveyors
- **Principle**: Helical screw (auger) rotates inside a trough.
- **Use Case**: Bulk granular materials (cement, grain, flour).
- **Capacity formula** uses **pitch (s), diameter (D), rotational speed (N), and trough loading**.

#### 2.2.5 Pneumatic Conveyors
- **Principle**: Material suspended in air/gas stream through pipelines.
- **Use Case**: Lightweight bulk (plastics, powders).
- **Pressure**: **dilute phase (< 5 psig)** or **dense phase (> 5 psig)**.

#### 2.2.6 Vibratory Conveyors
- **Principle**: Eccentric drives impart oscillations to move material along trough.
- **Use Case**: Hot, abrasive, or fragile bulk materials.

---

### 2.3 Types of Cranes

Cranes are lifting machines that use a hoist, wire rope, and structural framework to lift and move loads.

#### 2.3.1 Overhead Electric Traveling Crane (EOT)
- **Components**: Bridge girder, end carriages, trolley, hoist.
- **Movements**: Long-travel (LT), cross-travel (CT), hoisting (H).
- **Use**: Heavy machine shops, steel plants.

#### 2.3.2 Jib Crane
- **Components**: Vertical mast, horizontal jib (boom), hoist.
- **Range**: **180° to 360°** rotation.
- **Capacity**: typically up to **10 tonnes**.

#### 2.3.3 Gantry Crane
- **Structure**: Bridge supported on legs moving on rails at ground level.
- **Use**: Shipyards, container terminals, outdoor storage.

#### 2.3.4 Tower Crane
- **Structure**: Tall vertical mast with horizontal jib.
- **Use**: High-rise construction.

#### 2.3.5 Mobile Crane
- **Mounted on**: Truck, crawler, or wheeled carrier.
- **Use**: Construction sites, equipment loading/unloading.

#### 2.3.6 Floating Crane
- Mounted on barges/pontoons for port operations.

---

### 2.4 Types of Hoists

A hoist is a device used for **lifting or lowering** a load using a drum or chain-wheel.

| Hoist Type | Power Source | Typical Capacity | Use Case |
|------------|--------------|------------------|----------|
| **Manual Chain Hoist** | Human | 0.5 – 10 tonnes | Workshops, low-frequency use |
| **Electric Wire Rope Hoist** | Electric motor | 1 – 100+ tonnes | Industrial cranes |
| **Electric Chain Hoist** | Electric motor | 0.125 – 5 tonnes | Assembly lines |
| **Pneumatic Hoist** | Compressed air | 0.25 – 10 tonnes | Explosive/hazardous areas |
| **Hydraulic Hoist** | Hydraulic fluid | 2 – 100+ tonnes | Heavy lifts, vehicle lifts |

---

### 2.5 Types of Industrial Trucks

| Truck Type | Power | Lifting Mechanism |
|------------|-------|-------------------|
| **Hand Truck (Sack Barrow)** | Manual | No lift |
| **Pallet Jack (Manual)** | Manual | Hydraulic lever |
| **Electric Pallet Jack** | Battery | Electric hydraulic |
| **Forklift Truck (Counterbalance)** | Diesel/LPG/Electric | Mast + forks |
| **Reach Truck** | Battery | Extending mast |
| **Order Picker** | Battery | Platform + mast |
| **Sideloader** | Battery/Diesel | Lateral fork positioning |
| **Rough Terrain Forklift** | Diesel | Heavy-duty mast |

---

### 2.6 Types of Lifting Tackles (Rigging Gear)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Tackle refers to the **accessories** used between the load and the lifting device. **75% of lifting accidents occur due to tackle failure**, making this a KTU high-priority topic.

- **Wire Rope Slings** — 6×19, 6×37, 6×61 constructions.
- **Chain Slings** — Grade 80, Grade 100, Grade 120.
- **Synthetic Web Slings** — Polyester, nylon.
- **Shackles** — Bow shackle, D-shackle (anchor shackle).
- **Hooks** — Eye hook, clevis hook, swivel hook, sorting hook.
- **Eye Bolts** — Plain, shoulder, swivel.
- **Turnbuckles**, **Lifting Beams**, **Spreader Bars**.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield formulas** for solving numerical problems in Module 3.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Q = 3.6 \times A \times v \times \rho$ | Belt conveyor capacity (t/h) | $A$ = cross-section area (m²), $v$ = belt speed (m/s), $\rho$ = bulk density (t/m³) |
| 2 | $B = 0.9 \times Q + 0.05$ | Belt width (m) for capacity Q (t/h) | Empirical from IS codes |
| 3 | $Q_{screw} = 60 \times \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times s \times N \times \phi \times \gamma$ | Screw conveyor capacity (t/h) | $D$ = screw diameter (m), $s$ = pitch (m), $N$ = rpm, $\phi$ = fill factor, $\gamma$ = bulk density (t/m³) |
| 4 | $P_{belt} = \frac{(C \times L \times v) + Q \times H}{367}$ | Belt conveyor power (kW) | $C$ = mass of moving parts per m (kg/m), $L$ = length (m), $H$ = lift height (m) |
| 5 | $SWL_{sling} = \frac{WLL}{n \times f}$ | Safe Working Load of sling | $WLL$ = Working Load Limit, $n$ = number of legs, $f$ = factor of safety |
| 6 | $S_{factor} = \frac{Ultimate\ Strength}{Working\ Load}$ | Factor of safety (dimensionless) | Typically 4 to 6 for hoists |
| 7 | $L_{eff} = L \times k$ | Effective sling length under load | $L$ = sling length, $k$ = angle factor |
| 8 | $L_{eff} = \frac{L_{cos\ 0°}}{cos\ \theta} = \frac{L}{cos\ \theta}$ | Sling length vs. angle $\theta$ from horizontal | For vertical: $L_{eff} = L$; at $60°$: $L_{eff} = 2L$ |
| 9 | $W = W_{0} \times k_{m}$ | Modified load on sling leg | $W_0$ = load, $k_m$ = mode factor (1.0 for vertical, 2.0 for choke, 0.5 for basket) |
| 10 | $P_{hoist} = \frac{W \times v_{h}}{6120 \times \eta}$ | Hoist motor power (kW) | $W$ = load (kg), $v_h$ = hoisting speed (m/min), $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 11 | $\sigma = \frac{W}{n \times A}$ | Stress in wire rope/chain | $W$ = load, $n$ = number of parts of line, $A$ = cross-sectional area |
| 12 | $T_{max} = \frac{W}{n} \times \frac{1}{\sin\alpha}$ | Tension in sling leg at angle $\alpha$ from horizontal | $W$ = load, $n$ = legs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Constants**:
> - **1 tonne = 1000 kg = 9.81 kN**
> - **1 hp = 746 W = 0.746 kW**
> - **Standard gravity g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Belt friction coefficient μ ≈ 0.3–0.4** (rubber on steel)

---

### 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Manufacturing Engineering**: Selection of conveyor systems for assembly lines (Toyota Production System, Ford assembly).
- **Construction Engineering**: Crane selection based on lifting height, radius, and load (e.g., Liebherr tower cranes).
- **Mining Engineering**: Heavy-duty conveyors for coal, iron ore handling.
- **Logistics & Warehousing**: AS/RS and AGV systems in Amazon fulfillment centers.
- **Port Engineering**: Gantry and ship-to-shore cranes for container handling.
- **Aerospace**: Specialized overhead cranes for engine assembly (up to **600 tonnes SWL**).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Belt Conveyor Capacity (Q)

The volumetric capacity of a belt conveyor depends on the **cross-sectional area** of the material on the belt, the **belt speed**, and the **bulk density**.

### Step 1: Define the Cross-Sectional Area

For a troughed belt with idler angle of **30°**, the loaded cross-section approximates a trapezoidal shape:

$$A = \frac{B^{2}}{4} \times \tan(15°) + \frac{B^{2}}{4} \times \tan(15°)$$

For a simplified engineering approximation:

$$A \approx 0.06 \times B^{2}$$

where **B** is the belt width in meters.

### Step 2: Convert Volumetric Flow to Mass Flow

The volumetric flow rate is the area times the belt speed:

$$\dot{V} = A \times v \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

### Step 3: Multiply by Bulk Density

$$\dot{m} = \dot{V} \times \rho = A \times v \times \rho \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Tonnes per Hour

Since **1 hour = 3600 s** and **1 tonne = 1000 kg**:

$$Q = \dot{m} \times \frac{3600}{1000} = 3.6 \times A \times v \times \rho$$

### Step 5: Final KTU Standard Form

$$\boxed{Q = 3.6 \times A \times v \times \rho \quad \text{(tonnes/hour)}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem**: A belt conveyor has belt width **B = 800 mm**, belt speed **v = 2 m/s**, and transports coal of bulk density **ρ = 0.85 t/m³**. Find the capacity Q.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Convert belt width to meters:

$$B = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

Step 2: Compute cross-sectional area:

$$A \approx 0.06 \times (0.8)^{2} = 0.06 \times 0.64 = 0.0384 \text{ m}^{2}$$

Step 3: Apply the formula:

$$Q = 3.6 \times A \times v \times \rho$$

$$Q = 3.6 \times 0.0384 \times 2 \times 0.85$$

$$Q = 3.6 \times 0.06528$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.235 \text{ tonnes/hour}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Tension and Power for Belt Conveyor

### Step 1: Define Effective Tension

The effective tension (T_e) is the difference between tight-side and slack-side tensions:

$$T_e = T_1 - T_2$$

### Step 2: Compute Power at Drive Pulley

Power transmitted at the drive pulley:

$$P_{drum} = T_e \times v \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

where **v** is belt speed in m/s.

### Step 3: Convert to kW

$$P_{kW} = \frac{T_e \times v}{1000}$$

### Step 4: Include Drive Efficiency

$$P_{motor} = \frac{T_e \times v}{1000 \times \eta_{motor}}$$

### Step 5: Full Empirical Formula (IS / CEMA Standard)

$$\boxed{P = \frac{(C \times L \times v) + (Q \times H)}{367}}$$

where:
- $C$ = mass per meter of belt + idlers + material (kg/m)
- $L$ = horizontal length (m)
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s)
- $Q$ = capacity (t/h)
- $H$ = lift height (m)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sling Tension Under Inclined Loading

This is **the most critical derivation for KTU exams** as it appears every year.

### Step 1: Define the Geometry

A sling leg makes angle **α** with the horizontal. The vertical leg carries load component **T_vertical**, while horizontal component **T_horizontal** squeezes the load.

$$T = \frac{W}{n \times \sin\alpha}$$

where:
- $W$ = total load (N)
- $n$ = number of sling legs
- $\alpha$ = angle between sling leg and horizontal

### Step 2: Analyze Vertical Component

For equilibrium in vertical direction:

$$n \times T \times \sin\alpha = W$$

Rearranging:

$$T = \frac{W}{n \times \sin\alpha}$$

### Step 3: Analyze Horizontal Component

$$T_{horizontal} = T \times \cos\alpha = \frac{W \times \cos\alpha}{n \times \sin\alpha} = \frac{W}{n \times \tan\alpha}$$

### Step 4: Mode Factors for Common Lifts

| Sling Configuration | Mode Factor (k_m) | Effective Load per Leg |
|---------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Vertical (single leg) | 1.0 | $W$ |
| Choke hitch | 0.75 (effective), but **load doubles** | $2W$ on the choke |
| Basket hitch (90°) | 0.5 | $W/2$ per leg |
| 60° from horizontal | $\approx 0.577$ | $W/(n \times 0.866)$ |

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem**: A 2-tonne load is lifted by a **2-leg sling** with each leg at **60°** from horizontal. Find the tension in each sling leg.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Convert load to Newtons:

$$W = 2000 \times 9.81 = 19620 \text{ N}$$

Step 2: Apply the formula with **n = 2**, **α = 60°**:

$$T = \frac{W}{n \times \sin\alpha}$$

$$T = \frac{19620}{2 \times \sin 60°}$$

$$T = \frac{19620}{2 \times 0.866}$$

$$T = \frac{19620}{1.732}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 11328 \text{ N} \approx 11.33 \text{ kN}}$$

Step 3: Verify against WLL:

If sling WLL is **15 kN** at 60°, then **Factor of Safety** = $15/11.33 \approx 1.32$ — **NOT SAFE**, indicating a heavier sling is required.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hoist Motor Power

### Step 1: Define Work Done per Minute

A hoist lifting load **W (kg)** at speed **v_h (m/min)** does work:

$$\text{Work per minute} = W \times v_h \quad \text{(kg·m/min)}$$

### Step 2: Convert to Watts

Since **1 kg·m/min = 9.81/60 W ≈ 0.1635 W**:

$$P_{out} = W \times v_h \times \frac{9.81}{60} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

### Step 3: Account for Mechanical Efficiency

$$P_{motor} = \frac{W \times v_h}{6120 \times \eta} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

The constant **6120** comes from: $60 \times 1000 / 9.81 \approx 6116$, rounded to **6120**.

### Step 4: Worked Example

> **Problem**: An electric hoist lifts a load of **5 tonnes** at **6 m/min** with mechanical efficiency **η = 0.85**. Find motor power.

**Solution**:

Step 1: $W = 5000$ kg, $v_h = 6$ m/min, $\eta = 0.85$.

Step 2: Apply formula:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{5000 \times 6}{6120 \times 0.85}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{30000}{5202}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 5.77 \text{ kW}}$$

Step 3: Select next standard motor size: **7.5 kW**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Types at a Glance

| Equipment Type | Best For | Hazards | Key Safety Standard |
|----------------|----------|---------|---------------------|
| **Belt Conveyor** | Bulk, continuous flow | Entrapment, nip points | IS 1891, OSHA 1910.219 |
| **Roller Conveyor** | Unit loads, cartons | Pinch, falling loads | IS 11592 |
| **Overhead Crane (EOT)** | Heavy loads, indoor | Swing, overload, drop | IS 807, ASME B30.2 |
| **Jib Crane** | Localized lifting | Tipping, swing | IS 4573, ASME B30.16 |
| **Tower Crane** | Construction lifting | Collapse, wind | IS 14218, FEM 1.001 |
| **Mobile Crane** | Outdoor lifting | Tip-over, swing | IS 4137, ASME B30.5 |
| **Forklift** | Pallet movement | Tip-over, struck-by | OSHA 1910.178, IS 7306 |
| **Pallet Jack** | Light loads | Foot injury | ANSI/ITSDF B56.1 |
| **Chain Hoist** | Workshop lifting | Drop, chain failure | IS 8999, ASME B30.16 |
| **Wire Rope Hoist** | Heavy industry | Rope failure, drop | IS 9964, ASME B30.17 |
| **Pneumatic Hoist** | Hazardous areas | Hose burst | ATEX, IS 1989 |
| **Wire Rope Sling** | General lifting | Abrasion, kinking | IS 2266, EN 13414 |
| **Chain Sling** | High-temp environments | Stretch failure | IS 6211, EN 818 |
| **AGV** | Automated warehouses | Collision, navigation | ISO 3691-4, ANSI B56.5 |
| **AS/RS** | High-density storage | Falling loads | EN 528, FEM 9.341 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Classify the different types of industrial cranes used in material handling with one specific application for each.**

**Model Answer**:

Industrial cranes are classified into the following types based on their **structural design and mobility**:

| # | Crane Type | Key Feature | Application |
|---|------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | **EOT Overhead Crane** | Bridge travels on elevated rails | Heavy machine shops, steel mills |
| 2 | **Jib Crane** | 180° to 360° rotating horizontal arm | Workstation loading, dock operations |
| 3 | **Gantry Crane** | Bridge supported on ground-level legs | Shipyards, container terminals |
| 4 | **Tower Crane** | Tall vertical mast, horizontal jib | High-rise construction |
| 5 | **Mobile Crane** | Mounted on truck/crawler chassis | Field erection, equipment loading |
| 6 | **Floating Crane** | Mounted on barge | Port handling, ship-to-shore |

Each crane is selected based on **lifting height, span, capacity, mobility, and indoor/outdoor use**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Wire Rope Slings and Chain Slings in terms of construction, applications, and safety.**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Wire Rope Slings | Chain Slings |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| **Construction** | Strands of steel wires helically wound (e.g., 6×19, 6×37) | Welded or forged steel links (Grade 80/100/120) |
| **Flexibility** | Highly flexible, conforms to load shape | Rigid, less conforming |
| **Weight** | Lightweight for given capacity | Heavier for same capacity |
| **Temperature Resistance** | Damaged above **400 °C** | Withstands up to **800 °C** |
| **Applications** | General lifting, marine, rigging | High-temperature zones (steel mills, foundries) |
| **Inspection** | Visual for broken wires, kinks | Visual + measurement for stretch and wear |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Higher |

**Safety Note**: Wire rope must be discarded if **more than 10 randomly distributed broken wires** or **5 broken wires in one strand** are observed in a length of 6 diameters.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question has TWO alternatives; answer ANY ONE. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (14 Marks)

**a)** Explain in detail the **different types of conveyors** used in material handling, with neat sketches and their typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A belt conveyor of length **120 m** carries coal at the rate of **400 t/h**. The belt speed is **2.5 m/s** and lift height is **8 m**. The mass of moving parts is **120 kg/m**. Calculate the **motor power required** if drive efficiency is **90%**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The major types of conveyors used in material handling are:

**1. Belt Conveyor** — A continuous loop of flexible belt runs over head and tail pulleys. Used for **bulk materials** (coal, ore, sand, grain). Capacity ranges from **100 t/h to 20,000 t/h**.

**2. Roller Conveyor** — Gravity or powered rollers transport unit loads (cartons, pallets). Common in **warehousing and packaging lines**.

**3. Chain Conveyor** — Chains with attachments (slats, flights) carry heavy loads. Used in **automotive assembly and pallet handling**.

**4. Screw Conveyor** — A rotating helical screw (auger) inside a trough moves granular materials. Used for **cement, grain, flour, ash**.

**5. Pneumatic Conveyor** — Material is suspended in a gas stream through pipelines. Used for **powders, plastics pellets, cement**.

**6. Vibratory Conveyor** — Eccentric drives create oscillations to transport material. Used for **hot, abrasive, or fragile bulk**.

**7. Bucket Elevator** — Buckets attached to a vertical belt/chain lift bulk material. Used for **vertical transport of grain, coal**.

**8. Cable Belt Conveyor** — Carrying belt rests on cables; used for **long-distance high-capacity transport** (up to 30 km).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data**:
- $L = 120$ m (length)
- $Q = 400$ t/h (capacity)
- $v = 2.5$ m/s (belt speed)
- $H = 8$ m (lift height)
- $C = 120$ kg/m (mass of moving parts)
- $\eta = 0.90$ (efficiency)

**Step 1: Apply the empirical belt conveyor power formula**:

$$P = \frac{(C \times L \times v) + (Q \times H)}{367}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**:

$$P = \frac{(120 \times 120 \times 2.5) + (400 \times 8)}{367}$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator**:

$$\text{Numerator} = (36000) + (3200) = 39200$$

**Step 4: Compute power at pulley**:

$$P = \frac{39200}{367} = 106.81 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Account for motor efficiency**:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{106.81}{0.90}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 118.68 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 6: Select standard motor size**: **125 kW** (next higher standard rating).

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (14 Marks)

**a)** Discuss the **different types of hoists** used in industries. Compare **electric wire rope hoists** and **pneumatic hoists** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A load of **3 tonnes** is to be lifted using a **4-leg wire rope sling**. Each sling leg is inclined at **45°** to the horizontal. The WLL of each sling leg is **5 kN**. Determine whether the sling is safe. Take **factor of safety = 5**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Hoists are lifting devices used to **raise or lower** loads vertically using a drum, chain, or wire rope mechanism. Major types are:

**1. Manual Chain Hoist** — Uses hand chain and gear train; capacities **0.5 to 10 tonnes**; used in workshops.

**2. Electric Wire Rope Hoist** — Motor-driven drum with wire rope; capacities **1 to 100+ tonnes**; used in cranes, manufacturing.

**3. Electric Chain Hoist** — Uses load chain instead of wire rope; capacities **0.125 to 5 tonnes**; cleaner operation.

**4. Pneumatic Hoist** — Driven by compressed air; used in **explosive/hazardous environments** (oil & gas, paint shops).

**5. Hydraulic Hoist** — Uses hydraulic cylinder; used for **heavy lifts and vehicle lifts**.

**6. Lever Hoist (Ratchet)** — Manual lever operation; portable; **0.25 to 9 tonnes**.

#### Comparison: Electric Wire Rope vs Pneumatic Hoist

| Feature | Electric Wire Rope Hoist | Pneumatic Hoist |
|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| **Power Source** | Electric motor | Compressed air |
| **Speed Control** | Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) | Throttle valve |
| **Capacity** | 1 – 100+ tonnes | 0.25 – 10 tonnes |
| **Speed** | Up to 20 m/min | Up to 10 m/min |
| **Hazardous Area Use** | Requires explosion-proof motor | Inherently safe (no sparks) |
| **Operating Cost** | Low (electricity) | Higher (compressed air generation) |
| **Maintenance** | Brushes, gears, motor | Simple, air motor, no electrical parts |
| **Noise** | Moderate | High (needs silencers) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data**:
- Load $W = 3$ tonnes $= 3000 \times 9.81 = 29430$ N
- Number of sling legs $n = 4$
- Angle $\alpha = 45°$ from horizontal
- WLL per leg $= 5$ kN $= 5000$ N
- Factor of safety $S = 5$

**Step 1: Calculate tension per leg using formula**:

$$T = \frac{W}{n \times \sin\alpha}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**:

$$T = \frac{29430}{4 \times \sin 45°}$$

$$T = \frac{29430}{4 \times 0.7071}$$

$$T = \frac{29430}{2.8284}$$

$$\boxed{T \approx 10405 \text{ N} \approx 10.4 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3: Compare with WLL**:

Tension per leg **10.4 kN** > WLL **5 kN**.

**Step 4: Check factor of safety**:

$$S_{actual} = \frac{WLL}{T} = \frac{5000}{10405} \approx 0.48$$

Since $S_{actual} < 5$, **the sling is NOT SAFE**.

**Step 5: Determine required WLL**:

$$WLL_{required} = T \times 5 = 10.4 \times 5 = 52 \text{ kN per leg}$$

**Conclusion**: The sling is unsafe. A sling with **WLL ≥ 52 kN per leg** must be selected.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Types" Module Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conveyor Power**: Students often forget to **divide by drive efficiency** (η). Marks lost: ~2.
> 2. **Sling Angle Confusion**: Some students use **cos α instead of sin α**. The formula uses **sin α** with α measured from the **horizontal**, or **cos α** if α is measured from the **vertical**. Marks lost: ~3.
> 3. **Belt Capacity**: Using volumetric flow without converting to **mass flow** (multiplying by density). Marks lost: ~2.
> 4. **Hoist Power Units**: Confusing **W, kW, and HP**. Always convert to **kW** for KTU numerical answers. Marks lost: ~1.
> 5. **Forgetting 3.6 Conversion**: The constant **3.6** converts (m³/s) × (t/m³) into t/h. Many students miss this. Marks lost: ~2.
> 6. **Skipping Unit Conversion**: Not converting **tonnes → Newtons** before plugging into formulas. Marks lost: ~1–2.
> 7. **Factor of Safety**: Forgetting to **check against code-specified minimum** (typically 4 for hoists, 5 for slings). Marks lost: ~2.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Types**

- **5 Major Categories** of material handling types: **Conveyors, Cranes, Hoists, Industrial Trucks, Lifting Tackles (plus Automated Systems)**.
- **Belt Conveyor Capacity Formula**: $Q = 3.6 \times A \times v \times \rho$ (t/h).
- **Belt Conveyor Power Formula**: $P = (C \cdot L \cdot v + Q \cdot H)/367$ (kW).
- **Sling Tension Formula**: $T = W / (n \cdot \sin\alpha)$.
- **Mode Factors**: Vertical = 1.0, Choke = 2.0, Basket = 0.5.
- **Standard Factor of Safety**: Hoists = **4**, Slings = **5**, Chains = **3** (minimum).
- **Belt Speed Range**: **0.5 to 5 m/s** typical.
- **6 Types of Cranes** (most asked): EOT, Jib, Gantry, Tower, Mobile, Floating.
- **5 Types of Hoists**: Manual, Electric Wire Rope, Electric Chain, Pneumatic, Hydraulic.
- **Wire Rope Discard Criteria**: **10 random broken wires** OR **5 broken wires in one strand** per 6 diameters.
- **Pneumatic Hoist**: Used in **explosive/hazardous areas** (no sparking risk).
- **Conveyor Safety**: **In-running nip points** are the #1 cause of conveyor accidents — guard all pulleys and head/tail drums.
- **Forklift Tip-Over Rule**: Never travel with load **higher than necessary**; tilt back when reversing.
- **Crane Wind Loading**: Operations must cease at wind speeds typically **> 50 km/h** for outdoor cranes.
- **Color Coding in Cranes**: Hook blocks often color-coded by **WLL** for quick identification.
- **Sling Angle Rule of Thumb**: **Avoid angles below 30°** from horizontal — sling tension skyrockets.
- **Inspection Frequency**: Cranes — **annual thorough inspection**; Slings — **before each shift**; Hoists — **monthly to quarterly**.
- **OSHA Standards**: 29 CFR 1910.179 (Overhead Cranes), 1910.178 (Forklifts), 1926.550 (Mobile Cranes in construction).
- **Indian Standards (IS)**: IS 807 (Cranes), IS 3177 (Chain Pulleys), IS 2266 (Wire Rope Slings), IS 7306 (Forklifts).
- **Belt Width Rule of Thumb**: $B = 0.9Q + 0.05$ m for lump size ≤ **0.1B**.
- **Screw Conveyor Pitch Rule**: For most materials, **pitch = diameter**; for sticky materials, **pitch = 0.7 × diameter**.
- **AGV/ASRS**: Future of material handling — fully **automated, sensor-driven, programmable**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Construction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design and Construction** in the context of material handling safety refers to the integrated engineering discipline that governs the conceptualization, calculation, drafting, fabrication, and assembly of material handling equipment and systems (such as cranes, hoists, conveyors, forklifts, slings, and storage racks) in strict conformity with established safety standards, statutory codes, and ergonomics principles. It encompasses **load path analysis**, **factor of safety selection**, **material specification**, **stress analysis**, **stability evaluation**, and the incorporation of **passive and active safety devices** into the built artifact.

The KTU 2024 syllabus positions this topic as the engineering bridge between theoretical mechanics and field-level occupational safety, ensuring that every manufactured lifting/handling device is *inherently safe by design* (a principle codified under the **Hierarchy of Controls**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** "Design and Construction" is the *third module* of OESFT833 and carries direct examination weightage in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Students must demonstrate the ability to compute **design loads, safety factors, stresses, and stability margins** for typical material handling equipment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a material handling device the way a civil engineer designs a bridge that must hold a heavy truck. You do not build it "just strong enough" — you build it with **redundancy, predictable failure modes, and inspection-friendly access points**. In the same way, a crane hook is not just a curved piece of steel; it is engineered with a known **Factor of Safety (FoS)**, a specific **grade of alloy steel**, a defined **fatigue life**, and features like a **safety latch** that prevent the load from slipping off accidentally.

> Imagine a **backpack strap**. A casual manufacturer might use cheap thread and a flimsy buckle. A safety-engineered manufacturer uses **reinforced stitching**, a **load-tested buckle**, **rated webbing**, and includes a **secondary retention loop**. Both carry books, but only the second is *designed for safe load handling*. This is the difference between ad-hoc fabrication and **Design and Construction under Safety Engineering**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are central to this module and are **bold-emphasized** for quick reference:

- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** (used in converting mass to weight loads)
- **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — typically between **1.5 and 6.0** depending on the application
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** — measured in **MPa** or **N/mm²**
- **Yield Strength (σ_y)** — the stress above which permanent deformation begins
- **Allowable Stress (σ_allow)** = σ_y / FoS
- **Dynamic Load Factor (DLF)** — multiplier applied for shock/impact loads
- **Standard ambient design temperature** for material handling equipment = **-20 °C to +40 °C** (per IS standards)
- **Wind load reference velocity** (for outdoor cranes) per IS 875 (Part 3)
- **Standard lifting speeds** for various crane classes per **FEM/IS classifications**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box:**
> - **Factor of Safety (FoS)** = $\frac{\text{Ultimate (or Yield) Load}}{\text{Working (or Design) Load}}$
> - It is a dimensionless number that quantifies the *margin* between the equipment's breaking threshold and its normal operational load.
> - Higher FoS values are mandated for **dynamic, lifting, and human-occupied** applications; lower FoS may be acceptable for static, non-lifting structures where failure is non-catastrophic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillars of Safe Design (Structured Logic Steps)

The design of any material handling equipment rests on five inter-locking pillars. Each must be explicitly justified in KTU exam answers.

1. **Define the Operational Envelope**
   - *Why:* To bound the design problem.
   - *How:* Specify **maximum load (SWL)**, lift height, travel distance, duty cycle (number of cycles per hour), and environmental conditions (temperature, dust, corrosive agents).

2. **Identify the Governing Load Cases**
   - *Why:* The worst credible load determines the strength requirement.
   - *How:* Combine the **static load**, the **dynamic load factor**, the **wind load (if outdoor)**, and the **seismic load (if applicable)** into a *design load envelope*.

3. **Select Material and Cross-Section**
   - *Why:* Material choice fixes σ_y and UTS.
   - *How:* Use **standard structural steel grades (e.g., IS 2062, ASTM A36)** for frames; **forged alloy steel (e.g., IS 1875)** for hooks, pins, and load-bearing shafts.

4. **Apply Stress and Deflection Checks**
   - *Why:* To ensure the equipment neither breaks nor deforms excessively.
   - *How:* Compute **direct stress, bending stress, shear stress, and combined stress (using principal-stress theory)**, then compare each to σ_allow.

5. **Integrate Safety Devices and Ergonomic Features**
   - *Why:* Mechanical strength alone is insufficient.
   - *How:* Embed **limit switches, overload relays, slack-rope devices, anti-two-block devices, emergency stops, and ergonomic operator cabins** directly into the construction drawing.

## 2.2 The Design Load Formula (Expanded)

The **Design Load (F_design)** is the working load amplified by all relevant dynamic and environmental multipliers:

$$F_{\text{design}} = F_{\text{SWL}} \times \phi_d \times \phi_w \times \phi_s$$

where the variables carry the meanings in the table below.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight (W) from mass (m) | $g = 9.81$ | N or kN |
| 2 | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{F_{\text{ultimate}}}{F_{\text{working}}}$ | Factor of Safety | $1.5 - 6.0$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}}$ | Allowable stress | derived | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 4 | $F_{\text{design}} = F_{\text{SWL}} \cdot \phi_d \cdot \phi_w \cdot \phi_s$ | Combined design load | derived | N or kN |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\text{direct}} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Direct / axial stress | derived | N/mm² |
| 6 | $\sigma_{\text{bending}} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress (flexure) | derived | N/mm² |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_{\text{shear}}}$ | Shear stress | derived | N/mm² |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{principal}} = \dfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Principal stress (max/min) | derived | N/mm² |
| 9 | $\delta_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{L}{n}$ | Deflection limit (n = 200-500) | e.g. $\dfrac{L}{300}$ | mm |
| 10 | $F_{\text{wind}} = q_z \cdot C_f \cdot A$ | Wind force on structure | $q_z$ = dynamic pressure | N |
| 11 | $\phi_d$ | Dynamic Load Factor | $1.1 - 2.0$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\phi_w$ | Wind load factor (outdoor crane) | $1.0 - 1.3$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\phi_s$ | Shock / seizure factor | $1.0 - 1.5$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-friendly tip:** In KTU 14-mark Part B questions on this topic, examiners expect you to (a) state the formula, (b) define each symbol, (c) justify the FoS value with reference to a code, and (d) substitute numerical values with units. Skipping the *justification* of FoS is a common mark-loss point.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Design and Construction discipline is the **first line of defense** in the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE). A properly designed crane with built-in overload protection, for example, prevents accidents *before* any human operator intervention is required. This principle underpins the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, the **OSH Code 2020**, the **BIS codes** (e.g., IS 807, IS 3177, IS 3938), and international standards like **ISO 12100** (Safety of Machinery — General Principles for Design) and **FEM 1.001** (European Federation of Materials Handling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Code references to memorize for KTU 2024:**
> - **IS 807** — Code of practice for design, manufacture, erection and testing (cranes)
> - **IS 3177** — Code of practice for overhead electric hoists
> - **IS 3938** — Code of practice for selection, installation and inspection of chain pulley blocks
> - **IS 2062** — Hot rolled medium and high tensile structural steel (material spec)
> - **IS 1875** — Carbon steel forgings (for hooks, shackles, etc.)
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.179** — Overhead and gantry cranes (USA benchmark)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Working Load to Allowable Stress

We will *rigorously* derive the relationship that lets us size a structural member from first principles.

**Given:**
- A lifting hook must carry a Safe Working Load (SWL) of $W = 20$ kN.
- The hook is forged from alloy steel with **Yield Strength** $\sigma_y = 600$ MPa.
- The applicable **Factor of Safety** is $\text{FoS} = 5$ (typical for lifting hooks under static load, per IS 1875).

**Step 1 — Convert the working load to design load.**

The design load is obtained by applying the FoS directly when only static conditions are considered:

$$F_{\text{design}} = \text{FoS} \times F_{\text{working}} = 5 \times 20 \text{ kN} = 100 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the allowable stress.**

By definition:

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{600 \text{ MPa}}{5} = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required cross-sectional area for the hook shank.**

Using the direct stress formula $\sigma = F / A$ and solving for A:

$$A_{\text{required}} = \frac{F_{\text{design}}}{\sigma_y} = \frac{100\,000 \text{ N}}{600 \text{ N/mm}^2} = 166.67 \text{ mm}^2$$

*Explanation:* We deliberately use $\sigma_y$ (not $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$) in the denominator when solving for *minimum* area, because we are sizing *to* the yield limit, and FoS has already been applied to the load.

**Step 4 — Convert the area to a circular bar diameter.**

For a solid circular cross-section, $A = \pi d^2 / 4$, so:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 166.67}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{212.21} = 14.57 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Select the next standard size.**

We round **up** to the nearest standard diameter, typically $d = 16$ mm. The actual area is then:

$$A_{\text{actual}} = \frac{\pi (16)^2}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Re-verify the working stress.**

$$\sigma_{\text{actual}} = \frac{F_{\text{working}}}{A_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{20\,000 \text{ N}}{201.06 \text{ mm}^2} = 99.47 \text{ MPa}$$

This is comfortably below $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 120$ MPa, and the *effective* FoS achieved is:

$$\text{FoS}_{\text{effective}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\text{actual}}} = \frac{600}{99.47} \approx 6.03$$

which is *higher* than the design minimum of 5 — a sign of a **safe and conservative** design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The conservative rounding-up in Step 5 is called **design oversizing** and is standard practice. It accounts for manufacturing tolerances, surface finish, and uncertainty in load estimation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Combined Loading on a Crane Boom (Bending + Axial)

Consider a **lattice boom jib crane** where the boom experiences both **axial compression (P)** from the lifted load and a **bending moment (M)** from the offset of the load from the boom's centroidal axis.

**Given:**
- Lifted load $P = 50$ kN (axial)
- Offset moment $M = 25$ kN·m (bending)
- Boom cross-section is a hollow circular tube: outer diameter $D = 200$ mm, inner diameter $d = 180$ mm
- Yield strength of steel $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa
- Adopted FoS = 3

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional geometric properties.**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(200^2 - 180^2\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 32400) = \frac{\pi}{4}(7600) = 5969 \text{ mm}^2$$

Moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D^4 - d^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(200^4 - 180^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.6\times10^9 - 1.04976\times10^9) = \frac{\pi}{64}(5.5024\times10^8) = 2.70\times10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

Section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{2.70\times10^7}{100} = 2.70\times10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual stresses.**

Axial (direct compressive) stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{axial}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50\,000}{5969} = 8.38 \text{ MPa}$$

Maximum bending stress (at extreme fibre):

$$\sigma_{\text{bending}} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{25\times10^6}{2.70\times10^5} = 92.59 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the maximum normal-stress (superposition) theory.**

For combined bending + axial on the same side of the cross-section:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_{\text{axial}} + \sigma_{\text{bending}} = 8.38 + 92.59 = 100.97 \text{ MPa}$$

The minimum (opposite) side stress:

$$\sigma_{\min} = -\sigma_{\text{axial}} + \sigma_{\text{bending}} = -8.38 + 92.59 = 84.21 \text{ MPa}$$

(If the bending direction is reversed, the side stresses swap sign.)

**Step 4 — Compare with allowable stress.**

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{250}{3} = 83.33 \text{ MPa}$$

We find that $\sigma_{\max} = 100.97$ MPa **exceeds** $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 83.33$ MPa by approximately 21.1%.

**Step 5 — Design correction.**

To restore safety, we can either:
- Increase the wall thickness (e.g., $d$ reduced from 180 to 160 mm), **or**
- Reduce the offset moment by relocating the load closer to the boom's centre line, **or**
- Use higher-grade steel (e.g., $\sigma_y = 350$ MPa), **or**
- Increase FoS to 4.

A quick FoS re-check on the *current* section gives:

$$\text{FoS}_{\text{achieved}} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{250}{100.97} \approx 2.48$$

This is below the design FoS of 3 — a *redesign* is mandated. The KTU exam typically expects the student to identify this deficiency and propose a remedy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Pitfall (KTU Examiner Alert):** Many students apply the **principal-stress theory** (with shear) to a pure-bending-plus-axial case, which unnecessarily complicates the calculation. For a boom under **tension/compression + bending with negligible torsion**, the **simple superposition** in Step 3 is correct and is *what the examiner is looking for*. Reserve the principal-stress formula for cases where **torsion** is also present.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of FoS for Common Material Handling Components

| Component | Loading Type | Recommended FoS | Governing Code / Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crane hook (static) | Static + shock | 4 - 6 | IS 1875 / IS 807 |
| Wire rope (lifting) | Cyclic fatigue | 3 - 5 (on UTS) | IS 2266 |
| Welded joint (fillet) | Static / fatigue | 3 - 4 | IS 816 / AWS D1.1 |
| Forged eyebolt | Static + shock | 5 | IS 1367 / DIN 580 |
| Conveyor belt | Cyclic + impact | 8 - 10 (on UTS) | IS 1891 |
| Storage rack upright | Buckling | 1.67 - 2.0 | AISI / EN 15635 |
| Wheel of overhead crane | Rolling contact fatigue | 2 - 3 | FEM 1.001 |
| Brake (holding) | Static friction | 2 - 2.5 | IS 3177 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Safe Design Lifecycle of Material Handling Equipment

The following diagram maps the *interactions* between design, material selection, fabrication, testing, and in-service inspection — the complete **Safe Design Lifecycle** that the KTU 2024 module expects students to describe.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Customer Need And Operational Envelope Definition"] --> nodeB["Load Analysis Static Dynamic Wind Seismic"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Selection Of Material Grade And Factor Of Safety"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Stress And Deflection Calculations"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Design Meets Allowable Limits And Codes"}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF["Preparation Of GA Drawing And Fabrication Drawings"]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG["Redesign Increase Section Or Material Grade"]
    nodeG --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeH["Procurement Of Certified Raw Material"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Fabrication Cutting Bending Welding Machining"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Non Destructive Testing NDT And Dimensional Inspection"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"All Tests Passed"}
    K -- Yes --> nodeL["Static Load Test At 125 Percent Of SWL"]
    K -- No --> nodeI
    nodeL --> nodeM["Integration Of Safety Devices Limit Switches Overload Relay E Stop"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Commissioning And Handover To User With Documentation"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Periodic Inspection Schedule As Per Statutory Norms"]
    nodeO --> nodeP{"Equipment In Service"}
    nodeP --> nodeQ["End Of Service Life Scrap Or Re Certification"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Load Path in a Simple Overhead Crane

The diagram below shows how a lifted load is transferred through the structural members of a single-girder overhead crane. The arrows indicate the *direction* of force flow — a key visualization for explaining safety-critical load paths in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLoad["Load Hook And Block 50 kN"] --> nodeRope["Wire Rope Tension 50 kN"]
    nodeRope --> nodeDrum["Hoist Drum And Shaft"]
    nodeDrum --> nodeGearbox["Gearbox Housing"]
    nodeGearbox --> nodeMotor["Motor And Brake Mount"]
    nodeMotor --> nodeTrolley["Trolley Frame"]
    nodeTrolley --> nodeEndcarriage["End Carriage Wheels"]
    nodeEndcarriage --> nodeRail["Gantry Rail Reaction 25 kN Per Side"]
    nodeRail --> nodeColumn["Building Column Or Gantry Leg"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of Safety Integration During Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1["Elimination Of Hazard At Concept Stage"] --> nodeP2["Substitution With Safer Alternative Design"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Engineering Controls Built Into Construction Limit Switches Guards Interlocks"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Administrative Controls SOPs Training Signage"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Personal Protective Equipment As Last Resort"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram for exam answer:** Each "node" maps to a *stage* in the **Hierarchy of Controls**. The left-most level is the most effective; the right-most (PPE) is the *least* effective. Always exhaust upstream controls before relying on PPE. This is a recurring 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (with Model Answers)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define "Factor of Safety" as applied to material handling equipment. Why is a higher Factor of Safety adopted for lifting hooks compared to storage rack columns?*

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

The **Factor of Safety (FoS)** is the numerical ratio of the *ultimate (or yield) load* a component can sustain to the *working (or design) load* it is expected to carry in service:

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{F_{\text{ultimate}}}{F_{\text{working}}}$$

A higher FoS (typically **4 to 6**) is mandated for **lifting hooks** because failure is **sudden, catastrophic, and endangers human life**, and because lifting involves **dynamic, shock, and fatigue** loads. In contrast, **storage rack columns** experience relatively **static, predictable, compressive** loads and failure is often *progressive buckling* — hence a lower FoS of **1.67 to 2.0** is acceptable per **EN 15635 / AISI standards**.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*List any four Indian Standard (IS) codes that govern the design and construction of material handling equipment.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **IS 807** – Design, manufacture, erection and testing of cranes (overhead, gantry, jib).
2. **IS 3177** – Code of practice for overhead electric hoists.
3. **IS 3938** – Selection, installation and inspection of chain pulley blocks.
4. **IS 2266** – Steel wire ropes for lifting purposes — selection and testing.
