# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Inspection Hierarchy Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **three-tier inspection flow** for any piece of material handling equipment, from operator pre-use check to annual statutory examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([Equipment Ready for Use]) --> A2{Operator Pre-Use Check}
    A2 -->|Defect Found| A3[Stop Work, Tag Out, Inform Supervisor]
    A2 -->|No Defect| A4[Issue Equipment for Operation]
    A3 --> A5[Corrective Action by Maintenance Team]
    A5 --> A6{Re-Test by Operator}
    A6 -->|Pass| A4
    A6 -->|Fail| A5
    A4 --> A7[Equipment in Service]
    A7 --> A8{Monthly / Scheduled PM Due}
    A8 -->|Yes| A9[Preventive Maintenance by Technician]
    A9 --> A10{PM Satisfactory}
    A10 -->|Yes| A7
    A10 -->|No| A11[Trigger Breakdown / Corrective Maintenance]
    A11 --> A5
    A8 -->|No| A7
    A7 --> A12{Annual 12-Month Period Elapsed}
    A12 -->|Yes| A13[Statutory Thorough Examination by Competent Person]
    A13 --> A14{Load Test 1.25x SWL and NDT Pass}
    A14 -->|Pass| A15[Issue Fitness Certificate Validity 12 Months]
    A15 --> A7
    A14 -->|Fail| A16[Equipment Shutdown and Major Repair]
    A16 --> A13
    A12 -->|No| A7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reading Tip:** The cycle `A7 → A8 → A7` represents the daily work loop. The cycle `A7 → A12 → A15 → A7` represents the annual statutory loop. Understand the *triggers* (elapsed time / hours) – this is what examiners test.

## 4.2 The 8 Pillars of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)

A KTU examiner favourite – draw a *radial/hub-and-spoke* diagram showing the 8 pillars with TPM as the central philosophy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T([TPM Zero Losses Zero Accidents]) --> P1[1 Autonomous Maintenance]
    T --> P2[2 Planned Maintenance]
    T --> P3[3 Quality Maintenance]
    T --> P4[4 Focused Improvement Kobetsu Kaizen]
    T --> P5[5 Early Management of New Equipment]
    T --> P6[6 Education and Training]
    T --> P7[7 Safety Health and Environment]
    T --> P8[8 TPM in Administration]
    P1 --> S1[Operator cleans lubricates inspects own machine]
    P2 --> S2[Time based PM schedule with zero breakdown target]
    P3 --> S3[Defect zero through Poka Yoke and root cause analysis]
    P4 --> S4[Cross functional teams solve chronic losses]
    P5 --> S5[Design equipment for easy maintenance from day 1]
    P6 --> S6[Multi skill training matrix for operators and technicians]
    P7 --> S7[Zero accident culture with JSA and near miss reporting]
    P8 --> S8[Apply TPM principles to office and support functions]
```

## 4.3 The Bathtub Curve – Failure Rate vs. Time

This curve justifies *why* inspection frequency changes over an equipment's life.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Time on X axis] --> Y[Failure Rate lambda on Y axis]
    Y --> Z1[Phase 1 Infant Mortality Burn In Region lambda decreasing]
    Y --> Z2[Phase 2 Useful Life Constant lambda low and stable]
    Y --> Z3[Phase 3 Wear Out Region lambda increasing rapidly]
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> Z3
    Z2 --> K[Strategy Constant PM or PdM enough]
    Z1 --> L[Strategy Burn in testing before commissioning]
    Z3 --> M[Strategy Replacement or overhaul]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Material handling equipment like wire ropes and brake linings are in **Phase 3** for most of their service life – hence the *visual + dimensional* inspection frequency increases as they age.

## 4.4 Maintenance Strategy Decision Flow (RCM Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Identify Equipment Function] --> Q2{Will failure have safety or environmental consequence}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3[Must do scheduled PM or redesign]
    Q2 -->|No| Q4{Is failure hidden until routine check}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q5[Schedule routine inspection PM]
    Q4 -->|No| Q6{Is failure cost significant}
    Q6 -->|No| Q7[Run to failure acceptable]
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q8{Failure pattern predictable}
    Q8 -->|Age related| Q9[Scheduled replacement or overhaul]
    Q8 -->|Random| Q10[Condition monitoring PdM]
```

## 4.5 Maintenance Cost vs. Equipment Age (Economic Life Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[New Equipment] --> A1[Low Maintenance Cost Phase]
    A1 --> A2[Rising Maintenance Cost Phase]
    A2 --> A3[Economic Life Limit where replacement cost is less than cumulative maintenance]
    A3 --> A4[Replace Equipment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Application:** The optimal *replacement age* is where the cumulative maintenance cost curve crosses the line representing the capital cost of a new equipment. Beyond that point, every additional rupee spent on maintenance could have bought a fraction of a new asset.

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define the following terms in the context of safety in material handling:**
**(a)** Competent Person  
**(b)** Thorough Examination  
**(c)** Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)

### **Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 Marks):**

**(a) Competent Person:** A person who possesses adequate technical qualifications, sufficient practical experience, and the requisite knowledge of the relevant statutory provisions, safety standards (IS, OISD, OSHA), and inspection procedures to correctly identify defects and assess the fitness of material handling equipment for continued safe use.

**(b) Thorough Examination:** A comprehensive, systematic, and detailed inspection of lifting machinery and tackle, carried out by a competent person, which may include partial dismantling, measurements, functional tests, and NDT, to determine whether the equipment is safe for continued operation. Under the Factories Act, this is mandated at intervals not exceeding **12 months**.

**(c) Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** A group of testing methods used to evaluate the integrity, properties, or composition of materials or components *without causing damage or altering their usability*. Common NDT methods applied in material handling are Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT), Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT), Ultrasonic Testing (UT), and Radiographic Testing (RT).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance. Give two examples of condition-monitoring techniques used in predictive maintenance.**

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Calendar time or running hours | Real-time condition of the equipment |
| **Decision basis** | Fixed schedule | Sensor data, threshold limits |
| **Cost profile** | Moderate, predictable | Higher initial (instruments) but lower long-term |
| **Risk of over-maintenance** | High | Low |

**Two examples of condition-monitoring techniques (1 mark each):**
1. **Vibration Analysis** – Accelerometers mounted on bearings, gearboxes, and motors detect imbalance, misalignment, looseness, and bearing defects via FFT spectra.
2. **Thermography (Infrared Imaging)** – Detects hot spots in electrical panels, overheated brakes, and failing bearings; used for EOT crane motors and slip rings.

*(Other acceptable answers: Oil/Ferrographic analysis, Acoustic Emission, Ultrasonic Thickness Gauging, Motor Current Signature Analysis.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice – 2 × 14 Marks)

---

### **🔷 Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern, OESFT833 Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **three-tier inspection system** for material handling equipment with reference to the **Factories Act, 1948**. Discuss the role and responsibilities of a *Competent Person* in statutory inspection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5-tonne EOT crane operates 10 hours per day, 6 days a week. Over 6 months (26 weeks), it had **6 breakdowns** with the following repair times (in hours): 8, 12, 6, 10, 14, 4. Calculate:
   (i) Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)
   (ii) Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)
   (iii) Steady-state Availability
   (iv) The reliability of the crane for a mission time of 50 hours. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) – Three-Tier Inspection System (7 Marks):**

The safe operation of any lifting appliance in Indian factories is governed by **Section 28 and Section 29 of the Factories Act, 1948**, which mandate a layered inspection approach:

**Tier 1: Pre-Use / Daily Operator Inspection (2 Marks)**
- Performed at the **start of every shift** by the equipment operator.
- Limited to sensory checks and simple functional tests:
  - Visual check of wire ropes, hooks (latch intact), chains.
  - Functional test of brakes, limit switches, horn, emergency stop.
  - Confirmation of oil levels, tyre pressure (forklifts), battery electrolyte.
- Defects recorded in the daily log; any unsafe condition results in **tag-out and withdrawal from service**.

**Tier 2: Periodic / Preventive Maintenance Inspection (2 Marks)**
- Carried out by **trained maintenance personnel** at frequencies of weekly/monthly or after a fixed number of operating hours.
- Activities include:
  - Lubrication of moving parts (greasing, oiling).
  - Dimensional checks (rope diameter, brake lining thickness, hook throat opening).
  - Bolt torque checks, electrical insulation tests, Megger tests.
  - Replacement of wear parts based on schedule.
- Documented in a **preventive maintenance schedule / logbook**.

**Tier 3: Statutory Thorough Examination (2 Marks)**
- Carried out by an **independent Competent Person** at least once in **12 months**.
- Includes:
  - **Load test at 1.25 × SWL** (1.5 × for new installations), with 5-minute hold.
  - **NDT** of critical welds and load-bearing members.
  - Verification of SWL marking, structural soundness, brake holding capacity, overload limiter, electrical earth continuity.
- A **Certificate of Fitness** is issued, valid for 12 months; a register is maintained in **Form 27 / Form 24**.

**Role of Competent Person (1 Mark):**
A Competent Person must hold a recognized qualification (e.g., degree/diploma in Mechanical/Electrical Engineering), possess adequate practical experience, and be registered with the Chief Inspector of Factories. Their statutory duty is to conduct a fair, unbiased, and technically rigorous examination and certify whether the equipment is fit for continued safe use.

#### **Part (b) – Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Total Operating Hours**

$$T_{\text{op}} = 10 \, \text{hr/day} \times 6 \, \text{days/week} \times 26 \, \text{weeks} = 1560 \, \text{hr}$$

**Step 2: Compute Total Repair Time**

$$T_{\text{rep}} = 8 + 12 + 6 + 10 + 14 + 4 = 54 \, \text{hr}$$

**Step 3: MTBF**

$$\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1560}{6} = 260 \, \text{hr/failure}$$

**Step 4: MTTR**

$$\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{54}{6} = 9 \, \text{hr/repair}$$

**Step 5: Steady-State Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{260}{260 + 9} = \dfrac{260}{269} = 0.9665 = 96.65\%$$

**Step 6: Failure Rate (assuming exponential reliability)**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \dfrac{1}{260} = 3.846 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{failures/hr}$$

**Step 7: Reliability at 50 hours**

$$R(50) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-3.846 \times 10^{-3} \times 50} = e^{-0.1923}$$

$$R(50) = 0.8251 = 82.51\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to convert *all* time units to a consistent base (hours). Days × hours/day × weeks must be computed carefully. Also, students often confuse MTBF and MTTF – remember MTBF is for *repairable* systems, MTTF for *non-repairable* ones.

---

### **🔷 Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Alternative Module 4 Choice]**

**(a)** What is **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**? Explain its **8 pillars** with a focus on how TPM can improve safety in material handling operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Breakdown, Preventive, Predictive, and Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** strategies. For a 50-tonne gantry crane handling molten metal in a steel plant, which strategy would you recommend and why? **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) – TPM and its 8 Pillars (7 Marks):**

**Definition of TPM (1 Mark):**
Total Productive Maintenance (TPM) is a **holistic, employee-driven maintenance philosophy** developed by **Seiichi Nakajima** (JIPM, Japan, 1971) with the goal of achieving *zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents, and zero pollution* through the **total participation of all employees** – from operators to top management.

**The 8 Pillars of TPM (6 × 1 Mark each, brief):**

1. **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators take ownership of their machines through routine cleaning, lubrication, tightening, and inspection. This builds early-fault-detection capability, reducing safety incidents.

2. **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen):** A structured, time-based preventive maintenance schedule targeting *zero unplanned breakdowns* through systematic intervention.

3. **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen):** Defect prevention through condition monitoring and *Poka-Yoke* (mistake-proofing) devices. Critical for cranes handling molten metal where a defect = a fatality.

4. **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen):** Cross-functional teams tackle the **six big losses** (breakdown, setup, minor stop, reduced speed, defect, startup) using PDCA cycles.

5. **Early Management of New Equipment:** Design new equipment with maintainability, operability, and safety built in (Design for Maintenance, *DfM*).

6. **Education & Training:** Multi-skill development through *S-Kill Matrix* – operators trained to identify hazards, technicians cross-trained on multiple equipment.

7. **Safety, Health & Environment:** Zero-accident culture through Job Safety Analysis (JSA), near-miss reporting, Kaizen on unsafe conditions, and 5S housekeeping.

8. **TPM in Administration:** Apply TPM principles to office and support functions (purchase, finance, HR) to remove administrative losses.

**Safety Impact (Embedded in pillars 1, 6, 7):** In material handling, TPM's autonomous maintenance pillar ensures operators visually inspect wire ropes, brakes, and hooks *daily*, catching defects before they cause dropped loads. The Safety pillar mandates JSA before every non-routine lifting task.

#### **Part (b) – Maintenance Strategy Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Strategy | Trigger | Strength | Weakness | Suitability for 50T Gantry Crane |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Breakdown (Reactive)** | Failure occurs | Zero upfront cost | Unplanned downtime, secondary damage, **catastrophic risk** | ❌ **Prohibited** – molten metal drop = mass fatality |
| **Preventive (Time-based)** | Calendar / hours | Predictable budget, simple | Over-maintenance, under-detection of incipient faults | ⚠️ **Adequate baseline only** |
| **Predictive (Condition)** | Sensor thresholds | Maximizes life, early fault detection | High sensor cost, skilled analysts required | ✅ **Recommended** |
| **RCM (Logic-driven)** | FMECA-driven | Optimizes strategy to failure mode | Resource-intensive, requires data | ✅✅ **Best – mandated for hazardous service** |

**Recommendation (3 Marks):**

For a 50-tonne gantry crane handling **molten metal in a steel plant**, I recommend a **hybrid RCM + Predictive Maintenance** strategy for the following reasons:

1. **Safety-critical consequence:** Failure results in a dropped load of molten metal (~$200 °C, ~50 tonnes) – a single failure can cause mass-casualty events. The *consequence cost* justifies the highest investment in maintenance.

2. **High equipment criticality:** The crane is a single-point-of-failure for an entire steel-making process. Any unplanned downtime directly hits production of **crores of rupees per day**.

3. **Continuous-duty operation:** Gantry cranes operate in harsh, high-temperature environments with high cycle counts – condition monitoring (vibration on hoist gearbox, thermography on motors, wire-rope NDT, brake-lining wear sensors) provides the *real-time intelligence* that time-based PM cannot.

4. **Failure modes are mixed:** Wire ropes degrade gradually (predictable by visual + NDT), while electrical faults can be sudden (suitable for thermography + MCSA). RCM's logic-driven mix of strategies is therefore superior to a single strategy.

5. **Compliance:** Major HazardFactories under the Manufacture, Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules require RCM-style documented maintenance for safety-critical equipment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Trap (Part b):** Students often present the table without the *recommendation rationale*. KTU examiners award marks for the *engineering judgment* – "Why this strategy for *this* equipment?" Always end with a clear, justified recommendation tied to the equipment's specific risks and operating context.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Module 4 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Inspection & Maintenance"**

### **Core Concepts to Memorize**
- [x] Three-tier inspection: *Daily (operator) → Monthly (technician) → Annual (Competent Person).*
- [x] Four maintenance strategies: *Breakdown, Preventive, Predictive, RCM.*
- [x] TPM: *Nakajima, JIPM, 1971; 8 pillars; zero-loss philosophy.*
- [x] Statutory basis: *Factories Act 1948, Sections 28 & 29; annual fitness certificate; Form 27.*
- [x] Load test: *1.25 × SWL (existing) or 1.5 × SWL (new installation); 5-min hold.*

### **Formula Sheet (must remember verbatim)**
- [x] $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$
- [x] $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$
- [x] $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- [x] $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$
- [x] $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ *(World-class ≥ 85%)*

### **Diagrams to Draw in Exam (Practice Before Exam)**
- [x] Bathtub Curve (Infant Mortality → Useful Life → Wear-out).
- [x] 8 Pillars of TPM (hub-and-spoke).
- [x] RCM Decision Logic Flowchart.
- [x] Inspection Hierarchy (Daily / Monthly / Annual).

### **Common Numerical Pitfalls**
- ⚠️ Always convert minutes to hours.
- ⚠️ Use **MTBF** (not MTTF) for *repairable* systems like cranes, hoists, conveyors.
- ⚠️ Exponential reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid only in the *useful life* phase.
- ⚠️ OEE factors are *multiplied* (not averaged) – quality, performance, availability are all percentages of the same base.

### **Mandatory One-Liners for Theory Answers**
- *"Inspection is the eyes; Maintenance is the hands."*
- *"Inspection without maintenance is documentation of failure; Maintenance without inspection is blind repair."*
- *"The 1-10-100 rule: 1 rupee on prevention saves 10 on repair, 100 on accident."*
- *"Daily by eyes, Monthly by hands, Yearly by experts."*
- *"TPM = Total participation, not just Total maintenance."*
- *"For lifting equipment, breakdown maintenance is not a strategy – it is a hazard."*

### **Likely KTU Question Patterns to Expect**
1. Define + list 3-tier inspection *(3 marks – Part A).*
2. Differentiate PM vs PdM with examples *(3 marks – Part A).*
3. Calculate MTBF, MTTR, Availability from given data *(7 marks – Part B sub-question).*
4. OEE calculation for a conveyor / packaging line *(7 marks – Part B sub-question).*
5. Describe TPM pillars and explain its role in safety *(7 marks – Part B).*
6. Recommend maintenance strategy for a specific equipment with justification *(7 marks – Part B).*
7. Prepare an inspection checklist for a given material handling equipment *(7 marks – Part B).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Trucks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric Trucks

# Electric Trucks in Material Handling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Electric Truck** is a battery-powered, self-propelled industrial material handling vehicle used for the short-distance transportation, lifting, stacking, and order-picking of unit loads within enclosed or semi-enclosed environments such as warehouses, manufacturing plants, cold-storage facilities, and distribution centres. According to KTU 2024 Scheme (OESFT833) terminology, electric trucks form the **Class I (Counterbalanced Electric Rider), Class II (Narrow-Aisle Electric Rider), and Class III (Electric Hand-Rider / Hand-Held) categories** as broadly adopted from the ISO 5053-1 and OSHA 1910.178 classifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An electric truck is a non-combustion, electrically driven (DC/AC traction motor) material handling equipment whose prime mover, lift mechanism, and steering functions are energised from an on-board rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device — predominantly **Lead-Acid (VRLA/Flooded)** or **Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄ / NMC)** battery packs.

The dominant safety and engineering performance parameters include:

- **Rated Capacity (Q):** expressed in **kilograms (kg)** or **tonnes (t)**, typically at a standard **500 mm** or **600 mm load centre distance**.
- **Service Weight (W):** the un-laden self-weight of the truck in **kg**.
- **Lift Height (h):** the maximum vertical fork travel, generally **3.0 m to 12.0 m** for high-reach models.
- **Travel Speed (v):** laden / un-laden speed in **km/h** or **m/s**.
- **Battery Voltage (V):** typically **24 V, 36 V, 48 V, 72 V, or 80 V DC** traction packs.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Industrial Elevator-Car":** Think of an electric truck as a "moving elevator car" parked on the warehouse floor. Just as an elevator (a) carries a payload vertically between floors using a hoisted cable and (b) has a maximum permissible load posted on its door, an electric truck (a) carries a load horizontally across the floor using traction motors and (b) has a **rated capacity plate** stating the maximum load at a specific **load-centre distance**. Just as overloading an elevator triggers an alarm and brake, exceeding the truck's rated capacity causes **forward tip-over** — the single most fatal accident category in material handling.

A simpler, everyday analogue is a **battery-powered golf cart crossed with a hydraulic car jack**: the cart's chassis provides mobility, while the jack's hydraulic cylinder lifts the load. Both motions are powered by a rechargeable battery. The safety philosophy revolves around **three pillars** — *machine integrity, operator behaviour, and environmental layout.*

### 1.3 Why Electric Trucks? The Engineering & Safety Rationale

| Parameter | Electric Truck | Internal-Combustion (Diesel/LPG) Truck |
|---|---|---|
| **Emissions at point of use** | **Zero** (no CO, CO₂, NOₓ, particulate) | High; hazardous indoors |
| **Noise (dBA)** | **65–75 dBA** | 90–100 dBA |
| **Energy Efficiency** | **~80–90 %** (electric drivetrain) | ~25–35 % (Otto/Diesel cycle) |
| **Indoor Air Quality** | Safe (no fumes) | Requires ventilation |
| **Operating Cost (Energy)** | Lower | Higher (fuel cost) |
| **Maintenance Complexity** | Lower (fewer moving parts) | Higher (engine, exhaust) |
| **Cold-Storage Suitability** | **Excellent** | Poor (engine starting issues) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Safety Driver:** Electric trucks are the **default choice for indoor material handling** because they eliminate toxic exhaust emissions, reduce fire-risk from fuel spills, and operate at lower noise levels (a critical factor in **hearing-conservation compliance** per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95).

### 1.4 Classification of Electric Trucks (Class I, II, III)

The KTU syllabus (aligned with ISO 5053-1 / ANSI/ITSDF B56.1) categorises electric trucks as:

1. **Class I — Electric Motor Rider Trucks (Counterbalanced)**
   - Stand-on or sit-down counterbalanced forklift with electric drive.
   - Capacities: 1,000 kg – 8,000 kg.

2. **Class II — Electric Motor Narrow-Aisle Trucks (Reach / Straddle / VNA)**
   - Operates in aisles ≤ 2.5 m wide.
   - Includes **Reach Trucks** and **Turret Trucks** (Very Narrow Aisle, VNA).

3. **Class III — Electric Motor Hand-Rider Trucks (Hand-Held / Walkie)**
   - Operator walks behind; includes **Powered Pallet Trucks**, **Pallet Jacks**, **Stackers**, **Order Pickers**, and **Tow Tractors**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture of an Electric Truck

A modern electric truck is a **multi-domain mechatronic system** composed of the following major sub-systems:

1. **Energy Storage Sub-system (Battery Pack):**
   - Flooded Lead-Acid (FLA), Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid (VRLA), or Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄ / NMC).
   - Nominal voltage: 24 V, 36 V, 48 V, 72 V, or 80 V DC.
   - Capacity: 200 Ah – 1,200 Ah.

2. **Traction Sub-system (Drive Motor + Controller):**
   - Series-wound DC motor (older designs) or **AC induction motor** (modern, with regenerative braking).
   - Power: 5 kW – 50 kW.
   - Controlled by MOSFET / IGBT-based PWM inverter (Variable Frequency Drive, VFD).

3. **Hydraulic Sub-system (Lift Motor + Pump + Cylinder):**
   - DC or AC motor driving a gear pump.
   - Pressure: 140 bar – 200 bar.
   - Cylinders: single-stage (simplex) or multi-stage (telescopic for high lift).

4. **Steering Sub-system:**
   - Mechanical (older), Hydrostatic, or **Electric Power Steering (EPS)**.

5. **Braking Sub-system:**
   - Regenerative (motor acts as generator on deceleration).
   - Friction (disc/drum, mechanically or hydraulically actuated).
   - **Electromagnetic parking brake** (spring-applied, electrically released — fail-safe).

6. **Control & Safety Sub-system:**
   - Seat-switch (operator-presence sensor).
   - Speed limiter (cornering speed reduction).
   - Load-Weight Indicator (LWI) / Overload Cut-out.
   - **Deadman switch**, **Anti-Roll-Back** on ramps, **Blue Spot / Red-Zone pedestrian warning lights**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Cycle of an Electric Counterbalanced Truck:**
> 1. **Operator On-Boarding:** Seat-switch closes circuit, energising the master controller.
> 2. **Direction Selection:** Forward / Reverse selected via the directional control lever (interlocked with seat-switch).
> 3. **Acceleration:** Accelerator pedal modulates the VFD frequency; motor torque proportional to pedal deflection.
> 4. **Travel:** Truck traverses aisle at limited speed (typically **8–12 km/h laden / 12–18 km/h un-laden**).
> 5. **Load Engagement:** Forks advanced under pallet via hydraulic side-shift carriage.
> 6. **Lift:** Hydraulic pump pressurises cylinder; load raised to required height.
> 7. **Manoeuvring & Placement:** Operator drives to destination, lowers load smoothly, retracts forks.
> 8. **Battery Discharge Monitoring:** State-of-Charge (SoC) displayed; truck derates or shuts off at low SoC.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-relevant for the KTU 2024 ESE & Series Test** of OESFT833.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Battery Capacity (Charge)** | $C = I \times t$ | Charge stored in a battery (Ampere-hours) | Ah |
| 2 | **Battery Energy Stored** | $E = V \times C = V \times I \times t$ | Total energy in battery | Wh or kWh |
| 3 | **Traction Power Required** | $P_{t} = \frac{F \times v}{\eta_{d}}$ | Power to overcome tractive force at speed v | W or kW |
| 4 | **Tractive Force (Rolling)** | $F_{r} = \mu_{r} \times W_{total}$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 5 | **Tractive Force (Grade)** | $F_{g} = W_{total} \times g \times \sin(\theta)$ | Grade-climbing force (θ = grade angle) | N |
| 6 | **Lift Motor Power** | $P_{L} = \frac{Q \times g \times v_{L}}{\eta_{h}}$ | Hydraulic lifting power | W or kW |
| 7 | **Load Capacity Reduction** | $Q_{a} = Q_{r} \times \dfrac{L - c_{a}}{L - c_{r}}$ | Adjusted capacity for new load centre $c_{a}$ | kg |
| 8 | **Tipping-Over Moment (Load)** | $M_{tip} = W_{load} \times L_{load}$ | Overturning moment about front axle | N·m |
| 9 | **Restoring Moment (Truck)** | $M_{res} = W_{truck} \times d$ | Counter-moment due to truck weight & wheelbase | N·m |
| 10 | **Stability Condition (Static)** | $M_{res} \geq M_{tip} \times k$ | k = safety factor (≥ 1.5) | — |
| 11 | **Battery Runtime** | $t_{run} = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V}{P_{total}}$ | Continuous operation time | hours |
| 12 | **Energy Cost per Shift** | $Cost = kWh \times rate$ | Operating expense | ₹ or $ |
| 13 | **Hydraulic Cylinder Force** | $F_{hyd} = P_{gauge} \times A_{piston}$ | Lifting force = pressure × piston area | N |
| 14 | **Mast Deflection (Safety)** | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$ | Maximum mast bending | m |
| 15 | **Braking Distance** | $d_{b} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | Stopping distance on level surface | m |

> **Notation:** $V$ = battery voltage (V), $I$ = current (A), $t$ = time (h), $F$ = force (N), $v$ = velocity (m/s), $\eta$ = efficiency (decimal), $\mu_r$ = rolling-resistance coefficient, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $Q$ = load (kg), $L$ = wheelbase (mm), $c$ = load centre (mm), $P$ = pressure (Pa), $A$ = area (m²), $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa), $I$ = second moment of area (m⁴).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse Management:** Electric trucks are the **backbone of modern automated warehouses (AS/RS hybrid systems)** where they interface with conveyors, WMS software, and AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) fleets.
- **Cold Storage:** Operate reliably at **-25 °C to -30 °C** without engine warm-up; lead-acid and Li-ion chemistries both qualified, with Li-ion offering superior cold performance.
- **Pharma & Food Industry:** Zero emission, no fuel contamination — compliant with **GMP, HACCP, and ISO 22000** hygiene norms.
- **E-Commerce Fulfilment Centres:** High-throughput narrow-aisle reach trucks and order pickers maximise cubic storage density.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **rigorous, fully-worked, and aligned with KTU 2024 ESE expected-solution patterns.** Every step is shown; no algebraic jumps.

## 3.1 Derivation — Load Capacity Adjustment for Non-Standard Load Centre

### Statement
A truck rated for **Q_r = 2,000 kg at a load centre c_r = 600 mm** must lift a load whose actual centre of gravity is **c_a = 800 mm** from the fork face. Determine the **permissible new load capacity Q_a**, given the wheelbase **L = 1,400 mm** and the front-axle to fork-face distance a = 400 mm.

### Step 1 — Establish the Geometric Reference

For a counterbalanced truck, the **rated capacity** is established by the manufacturer at a **standard load centre (c_r = 600 mm)** measured horizontally from the **fork face** (vertical plane of the forks) to the **centre of gravity (CG) of the load**. When the actual CG lies further out, the **overturning moment** increases linearly with the moment-arm, so the safe load must be reduced **inversely**.

### Step 2 — Define the Two Pivot Distances

The effective distance from the **front axle** to the load CG is:
- At rated condition: $L_r = a + c_r$
- At actual condition: $L_a = a + c_a$

Substituting:
- $L_r = 400 + 600 = 1{,}000$ mm
- $L_a = 400 + 800 = 1{,}200$ mm

### Step 3 — Apply the Inverse Proportionality Rule

The maximum overturning moment (load × front-axle distance) must remain constant for a given truck. Therefore:

$$
Q_r \times L_r = Q_a \times L_a
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Adjusted Capacity Q_a

$$
Q_a = Q_r \times \dfrac{L_r}{L_a}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
Q_a = 2{,}000 \times \dfrac{1{,}000}{1{,}200}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Final Numerical Result

$$
Q_a = 2{,}000 \times 0.8333 = 1{,}666.67 \text{ kg}
$$

### Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 25 % increase in load-centre distance (600 → 800 mm) causes a **16.7 % reduction in safe capacity** (2,000 → 1,666.67 kg). This is a **frequently tested 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE — students often forget the "+a" term and lose 2–3 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation — Battery Runtime of an Electric Truck

### Statement
An electric counterbalanced truck has a **48 V, 600 Ah** lead-acid battery. The combined average load on the battery is **120 A** (continuous traction + lift duty cycle, with regenerative recovery reducing net draw). Calculate the **runtime per full charge**.

### Step 1 — Recall the Battery Capacity Equation

$$
C_{Ah} = I \times t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify the Variables

- $C_{Ah} = 600$ Ah
- $I = 120$ A

### Step 3 — Compute the Theoretical Runtime

$$
t = \dfrac{600}{120} = 5.0 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Practical Derating Factor (Peukert Correction)

Lead-acid batteries suffer **capacity loss at high discharge rates.** The **Peukert equation** captures this:

$$
t = \dfrac{C_{n}}{(I / I_{n})^{k-1} \times I}
$$

where $k$ is the Peukert exponent (≈ **1.1 – 1.3** for VRLA; ≈ **1.05 – 1.15** for LiFePO₄). Take $k = 1.2$ and rated current $I_n = 100$ A, $C_n = 600$ Ah.

### Step 5 — Compute the Peukert-Adjusted Runtime

$$
t = \dfrac{600}{(120/100)^{1.2-1} \times 120}
$$

$$
(120/100)^{0.2} = (1.2)^{0.2} \approx 1.0371
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{600}{1.0371 \times 120} = \dfrac{600}{124.45} \approx 4.82 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply an Additional Reserve Factor (80 % Depth of Discharge)

To preserve battery life, discharge only to **80 % DoD**:

$$
t_{usable} = 4.82 \times 0.80 = 3.86 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Answer

> [!NOTE]
> **The truck can operate approximately 3.86 hours (≈ 4 hours) per charge under the stated duty cycle.** This is a **direct KTU 2024 ESE long-answer derivation**, frequently appearing in Module 4 as a 7-mark question.

## 3.3 Derivation — Tipping-Over Stability Check

### Statement
A 3,000 kg electric truck (including battery) is to lift a 1,500 kg load. The wheelbase is **L_w = 1,500 mm**, and the front-axle to fork-face is a = 400 mm. The load centre is c = 600 mm. Determine whether the truck is **statically stable against forward tip-over.**

### Step 1 — Compute Overturning Moment M_tip

$$
M_{tip} = W_{load} \times (a + c) = 1{,}500 \times 9.81 \times (0.400 + 0.600)
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to N·m

$$
M_{tip} = 14{,}715 \times 1.0 = 14{,}715 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Restoring Moment M_res (Truck CG at Centre of Wheelbase)

$$
M_{res} = W_{truck} \times \dfrac{L_w}{2} = 3{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 0.750
$$

### Step 4 — Compute M_res

$$
M_{res} = 29{,}430 \times 0.750 = 22{,}072.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 5 — Apply Stability Criterion

$$
M_{res} \geq M_{tip} \times k, \quad k = 1.5 \text{ (safety factor)}
$$

$$
M_{tip} \times k = 14{,}715 \times 1.5 = 22{,}072.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

### Step 6 — Compare and Conclude

$$
M_{res} = 22{,}072.5 \text{ N·m} \; = \; M_{tip} \times k = 22{,}072.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion: The truck is at the *exact stability limit*. Any dynamic loading (braking, acceleration, turning, uneven floor, or load-shift) will cause forward tip-over.** Either reduce the load to ≤ 1,000 kg or increase the wheelbase. **Always maintain $M_{res} \geq 1.5 \times M_{tip}$** for an operational margin.

## 3.4 Derivation — Lift Motor Power Requirement

### Statement
Calculate the hydraulic motor power required to lift a **2,000 kg** load at a speed of **0.4 m/s**, assuming a hydraulic system efficiency of **85 %**.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Lifting Force

$$
F = m \times g = 2{,}000 \times 9.81 = 19{,}620 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Hydraulic Power Output

$$
P_{out} = F \times v_{L} = 19{,}620 \times 0.4 = 7{,}848 \text{ W} \approx 7.85 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 3 — Account for System Efficiency

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta_h} = \dfrac{7.85}{0.85} = 9.24 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Answer

> [!NOTE]
> **Lift motor must deliver at least 9.24 kW (≈ 12.4 HP) input power** to lift 2 t at 0.4 m/s. In KTU 2024, this appears as a standard 3-mark numerical in the *Part A* section.

## 3.5 KTU Numerical Practice Matrix (Quick-Reference)

| Problem Type | Key Formula | Typical Marks | Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Load centre adjustment | $Q_a = Q_r \times (L_r / L_a)$ | 7 | High |
| Battery runtime | $t = C / I$ (with Peukert) | 7 | High |
| Tipping stability | $M_{res} \geq 1.5 \times M_{tip}$ | 7 | Medium |
| Lift motor power | $P = mgv / \eta$ | 3 | High |
| Hydraulic cylinder size | $A = F / P$ | 3 | Medium |
| Braking distance | $d = v² / (2\mu g)$ | 3 | Low |
| Energy cost per shift | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t \cdot rate$ | 3 | Low |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an electric truck. List the major classes of electric trucks as per ISO 5053-1 classification, with one example for each class.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

An **electric truck** is a battery-powered, self-propelled industrial vehicle used to transport, lift, and stack unit loads in indoor / warehouse environments. The prime mover is an electric motor (AC induction or DC series) energised by a rechargeable on-board battery.

**Three Classes (with examples):**

| Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Class I** | Electric Motor Rider — Counterbalanced | 2 t Electric Counterbalanced Forklift |
| **Class II** | Electric Motor Narrow-Aisle Rider | Reach Truck / Turret VNA |
| **Class III** | Electric Motor Hand-Rider (Walkie) | Powered Pallet Truck / Walkie Stacker |

> *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for class list + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)*

### Question 2 **[KTU Series Test 2 – 2024]**
**State any three safety features mandatory on a battery-electric counterbalanced forklift as per OSHA 1910.178.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

1. **Operator-Presence Seat Switch (1 mark):** Truck cannot be operated unless the operator is seated; disengages traction and lift on exit.
2. **Load-Weight Indicator (LWI) with Overload Cut-out (1 mark):** Audible/visual warning and hydraulic cut-out if load exceeds rated capacity.
3. **Spring-Applied, Electrically-Released Parking Brake (1 mark):** Fail-safe brake automatically engages on power loss or operator exit.

> *(Examiner allocates 1 mark per feature with brief justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Two Part-B questions per module, with **internal choice (A or B)** at the ESE. Each Part-B carries 14 marks, broken as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.** Both alternatives are presented below for parallel preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** A 2,500 kg-rated electric counterbalanced truck is rated at a load centre of **600 mm**. The wheelbase is **1,400 mm**, and the front-axle to fork-face distance is **400 mm**. Calculate the **safe load capacity** if the actual load centre is shifted to **900 mm**. Comment on the safety implications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a neat block diagram the **major sub-systems of a modern battery-electric forklift**. Highlight the role of the **VFD controller** and **regenerative braking** in operator safety. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution — Load Capacity Adjustment (7 marks)**

Given: $Q_r = 2{,}500$ kg; $c_r = 600$ mm; $c_a = 900$ mm; $L_w = 1{,}400$ mm; $a = 400$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute front-axle to rated-load-CG distance:** $L_r = a + c_r = 400 + 600 = 1{,}000$ mm.

**Step 2 — Compute front-axle to actual-load-CG distance:** $L_a = a + c_a = 400 + 900 = 1{,}300$ mm.

**Step 3 — Apply inverse-proportionality (constant overturning moment):**

$$
Q_a \times L_a = Q_r \times L_r
$$

$$
Q_a = 2{,}500 \times \dfrac{1{,}000}{1{,}300}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute:**

$$
Q_a = 2{,}500 \times 0.7692 \approx 1{,}923.1 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Safety comment (2 marks):**
- A 50 % increase in load-centre distance caused a **23.1 % reduction in safe capacity** (2,500 → 1,923 kg).
- Operationally, the operator must be trained to **read the load-centre sticker** on the pallet, and to reduce load whenever the pallet is longer, wider, or asymmetric.
- Exceeding the rated configuration can cause **forward tip-over** — the leading cause of fatal forklift accidents worldwide.

**(b) Solution — Block Diagram & Safety Features (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (drawn by student):**
- Battery (1 mark) → Master Controller / VFD (1 mark) → Traction Motor → Drive Wheels (1 mark)
- Battery → Hydraulic Motor Controller → Hydraulic Pump → Lift Cylinder (1 mark)
- Seat Switch / Brakes / Steering Sensors all feed into Master Controller (1 mark)
- Regenerative Braking shown as a feedback loop from motor back to battery (1 mark)
- VFD role: smooth torque, anti-roll-back, speed-limiting on cornering (1 mark)
- Regenerative braking role: recovers kinetic energy, reduces brake wear, prevents uncontrolled descent on ramps (1 mark)

> *(Distribute 7 marks across: 4 for diagram blocks, 2 for VFD, 1 for regen-braking or equivalent.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** A 48 V, 720 Ah lead-acid battery powers an electric forklift. The average current draw is **150 A**, with intermittent peak loads up to **300 A** (lift). Using the **Peukert equation (k = 1.25)**, calculate the **practical runtime per charge**, applying an 80 % depth-of-discharge limit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the **battery-charging safety procedure** for a lead-acid traction battery in a warehouse. Mention **three specific hazards** and their controls. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Solution — Battery Runtime with Peukert (7 marks)**

Given: $V = 48$ V, $C_n = 720$ Ah, $I_n = 100$ A (rated), $I = 150$ A (actual), $k = 1.25$.

**Step 1 — Peukert-adjusted time at rated current:**
$$
t = \dfrac{C_n}{I_n} = \dfrac{720}{100} = 7.2 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Peukert exponent for actual current:**
$$
t_{actual} = t \times \left(\dfrac{I_n}{I}\right)^{k-1} = 7.2 \times \left(\dfrac{100}{150}\right)^{0.25}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**
$$
\left(\dfrac{100}{150}\right)^{0.25} = (0.6667)^{0.25} \approx 0.9036
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the corrected time:**
$$
t_{actual} = 7.2 \times 0.9036 = 6.51 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 80 % DoD limit:**
$$
t_{usable} = 6.51 \times 0.80 = 5.21 \text{ h}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The forklift can operate **≈ 5.2 hours per charge** under the stated load profile, with the 80 % DoD margin preserving battery cycle life.

**(b) Solution — Charging Safety Procedure (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step charging protocol (4 marks):**
1. Park truck in **designated charging bay** with ventilation.
2. Inspect battery case, cables, connectors — **no cracks, no exposed terminals.**
3. Ensure **eye-wash station and spill kit** are accessible.
4. Connect DC cable to battery first, then plug AC into mains.
5. Set correct voltage and current per battery nameplate.
6. Monitor **temperature rise** and **hydrogen gas evolution** (acid fumes).
7. After full charge, **disconnect AC first, then DC cable.**
8. **Log the charge cycle** in the maintenance register.

**Three specific hazards and controls (3 marks):**

| Hazard | Control |
|---|---|
| **Hydrogen explosion during gassing** | Forced ventilation; LEL detectors; no naked flames; charging area isolated from ignition sources |
| **Sulphuric acid splash / burn** | PPE (acid-resistant gloves, goggles, apron); neutralising soda-ash spill kit; eye-wash within 10 m |
| **Electrocution from 48–80 V DC** | Lockout-tagout during cable maintenance; insulated tools; trained personnel only; never touch with wet hands |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Electric-Truck Questions (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+a" term in load-centre calculations** — students write $L_r = c_r$ instead of $L_r = a + c_r$. *Cost: 2–3 marks per question.*
> 2. **Ignoring the Peukert correction** — KTU now expects Peukert-adjusted runtime for lead-acid batteries, not just $t = C/I$. *Cost: 2 marks.*
> 3. **Stating the safety factor incorrectly** — the correct value is **k ≥ 1.5** (not 1.0 or 1.1). Examiners deduct for "k = 1" answers.
> 4. **Conflating electric truck classes** — Class II is **Narrow-Aisle Rider**, not Hand-Rider. Mixing up Class II and Class III loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the 80 % Depth-of-Discharge (DoD) step** in battery runtime problems — examiners want a practical, not theoretical, answer.
> 6. **Failing to mention the hydrogen / acid hazards** specifically in charging questions — generic "be careful" answers score zero.
> 7. **Not converting units** — Load-centre is in **mm** in the formula but the final load should be reported in **kg** with no unit confusion. Examiners specifically check unit consistency.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Bullets for the Night-Before Exam Revision:**

- **Definition:** An electric truck is a battery-powered, self-propelled material handling vehicle, classified into **Class I (Counterbalanced Rider)**, **Class II (Narrow-Aisle Rider)**, and **Class III (Hand-Rider / Walkie)**.
- **Battery chemistries:** **Lead-Acid (FLA/VRLA)** and **Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄ / NMC)**. Li-ion is preferred for cold storage and opportunity charging.
- **Typical voltages:** **24 V, 36 V, 48 V, 72 V, 80 V** DC traction packs.
- **Key formula for load capacity adjustment:** $Q_a = Q_r \times \dfrac{a + c_r}{a + c_a}$ — *do not forget the "a" term.*
- **Peukert equation** is mandatory for accurate lead-acid battery runtime calculations.
- **Stability criterion:** $M_{res} \geq 1.5 \times M_{tip}$ — the safety factor **k = 1.5** is non-negotiable.
- **Lift-motor power** $P = \dfrac{m g v_L}{\eta_h}$ — with efficiency ≈ **0.85** for hydraulic systems.
- **Mandatory safety devices (OSHA 1910.178):**
  - Seat switch (operator-presence).
  - LWI (Load-Weight Indicator) with overload cut-out.
  - Spring-applied, electrically-released parking brake.
  - Horn, reverse alarm, blue/red pedestrian-warning lights.
  - Anti-rollback device on ramps.
  - Speed limiter for cornering.
- **Battery-charging hazards (must memorise):** **Hydrogen explosion (LEL 4 %), Sulphuric acid burns, DC electrocution (48–80 V).**
- **PPE for charging operations:** acid-resistant gloves, face shield / goggles, apron, closed-toe rubber-soled boots.
- **Daily inspection checklist:** Tires → Forks/Mast → Hydraulics → Battery → Brakes → Steering → Horn/Lights → Safety interlocks → Cabin.
- **Regenerative braking** converts kinetic energy back to electrical energy; reduces brake wear and prevents runaway on ramps.
- **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** controls AC traction motor; provides smooth acceleration, anti-roll-back, and programmable speed limits.
- **KTU 2024 favourite question:** "Compare lead-acid vs lithium-ion batteries for electric trucks" — learn **cycle life, energy density, charging time, and cold-storage performance.**
- **Forensic number to remember:** Standard load centre = **500 mm (European) or 600 mm (ANSI/ISO 5053-1)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Gasoline Operated Trucks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gasoline Operated Trucks (GOTs):** A class of **internal combustion engine (ICE) powered industrial lift trucks** classified under **ASME B56.1** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178**, where the prime mover is a **spark-ignition gasoline engine** (typically 4-stroke, liquid-cooled, fueled by petrol/gasoline of octane rating **RON 91 or higher**). These trucks fall under **Class IV (Internal Combustion Engine Trucks – Cushion Tires)** and **Class V (Internal Combustion Engine Trucks – Pneumatic Tires)** of the **OSHA Industrial Truck Classification System**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **Safety in Material Handling (OESFT833)**, gasoline operated trucks are studied as a critical category of **powered industrial trucks (PITs)** whose handling, refueling, exhaust emission, and operator-safety protocols form a major assessment area.

## 1.2 Key Technical Characteristics

| Parameter | Typical Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Power Source** | 4-Stroke SI Gasoline Engine |
| **Fuel Type** | Unleaded Petrol (Octane RON 91) |
| **Power Output Range** | **30 kW – 75 kW** |
| **Operating Voltage (Electrical)** | **12 V DC** (Starting & Auxiliaries) |
| **Fuel Tank Capacity** | **40 L – 90 L** |
| **Load Capacity Range** | **1,000 kg – 15,000 kg** |
| **Lift Height (Standard Mast)** | **3.0 m – 6.0 m** |
| **Travel Speed (Loaded)** | **15 km/h – 25 km/h** |
| **Standard Reference** | **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178**, **ANSI/ITSDF B56.1** |

## 1.3 OSHA Classification of Industrial Trucks (Context)

> [!NOTE]
> The U.S. **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA)** classifies all industrial trucks into **seven (7) classes**. Gasoline operated trucks specifically belong to:
> - **Class IV** — Internal Combustion Engine Trucks (Cushion Tires) — Indoor use on smooth floors
> - **Class V** — Internal Combustion Engine Trucks (Pneumatic Tires) — Outdoor & rough terrain use

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Heavy-Duty Shopping Cart with a Brain"**
> Imagine a shopping cart at a supermarket. Now amplify its strength **100 times**, give it a powerful engine, a hydraulic lifting arm, and strict government safety certification — you essentially get a **gasoline operated forklift truck**.
>
> - **Engine** = the "muscle" that moves it
> - **Hydraulic Mast** = the "arm" that lifts the load
> - **Stability Triangle** = the "feet" keeping it from tipping over
> - **Fuel Tank** = its "stomach" (must be refilled carefully to avoid fire)
>
> Just as you wouldn't let a child drive a heavy car, **only a trained, certified operator** is legally permitted to drive an industrial truck.

## 1.5 Why Study Gasoline Trucks in Material Handling Safety?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **OSHA statistics**, **forklift-related accidents cause approximately 85 fatal injuries and 94,500 injuries annually** in industrial workplaces. Gasoline operated trucks contribute a significant portion because of:
> 1. **Fire & explosion hazards** from fuel handling
> 2. **Carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning** in enclosed warehouses
> 3. **Tipping accidents** due to load instability
> 4. **Noise-induced hearing loss** (>90 dB operational noise)
> 5. **Burns** from hot engine components and exhaust manifold

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Gasoline Operated Truck — Component Architecture

A gasoline operated truck consists of **seven major subsystems**, each of which has its own safety considerations:

### 2.1.1 Power Plant (Engine Assembly)
- **4-stroke spark-ignition engine** with carburetor or electronic fuel injection (EFI)
- Operating speed: **1,800 – 2,800 RPM** (governed)
- Multi-cylinder configuration: typically **4 cylinders inline**
- Engine cooling: **Liquid-cooled** (ethylene glycol radiator) or **air-cooled**

### 2.1.2 Fuel System
- Fuel tank (steel or HDPE)
- Fuel lines (high-pressure resistant rubber)
- Fuel filter & fuel pump
- **Vapor recovery system** to control hydrocarbon emissions
- **Firewall** between engine and operator compartment (OSHA mandatory)

### 2.1.3 Transmission & Drivetrain
- **Hydrostatic transmission** (most common in modern trucks)
- Single-speed forward/reverse with infinite variable control
- Drive axle with differential
- Service brake: **hydraulic, dual-circuit** (mandatory safety feature)

### 2.1.4 Hydraulic Lifting System
- Hydraulic pump driven by engine
- Hydraulic cylinders (lift, tilt, side-shift)
- Control valve bank
- Maximum working pressure: **175 – 210 bar**

### 2.1.5 Mast Assembly
- Two or three-stage mast
- Lift chains (load-bearing)
- Hydraulic lift cylinders
- Forks (standard: **1,070 mm long × 100 mm wide**)

### 2.1.6 Operator Cabin & Safety Features
- **Overhead Guard (OHG)** — protects operator from falling objects (mandatory per **ANSI B56.1**)
- **Load Backrest Extension** — prevents load from falling rearward onto mast
- **Seat Belt** — operator restraint (mandatory since 1992)
- **Rear-view mirrors** and **backup alarm** (>102 dB)
- **Warning beacon / strobe light**

### 2.1.7 Wheels, Tires & Chassis
- **Cushion tires** (Class IV — solid rubber, indoor smooth surfaces)
- **Pneumatic tires** (Class V — air-filled, outdoor rough terrain)
- Counterweight at rear for stability

## 2.2 The Stability Triangle — The Most Critical Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stability Triangle (or Stability Pyramid)** is the single most important safety concept in any lift truck operation. It represents the **three-point contact zone** formed by the **two front drive wheels** and the **center of the steer axle (or single rear wheel)**.

When a forklift lifts a load, the **combined center of gravity (CG)** of the truck-plus-load must remain **inside** this triangle at all times, otherwise the truck will tip over.

```
        FRONT
         △
        / \
       /   \
      /     \
     /  CG   \
    /_________\
   Rear Pivot Point
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **examinable** in KTU University examinations for OESFT833. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M_{load} = W_{load} \times L_{horiz}$ | **Load Moment** (Tipping Moment) | $M_{load}$ in N·m; $W_{load}$ in N; $L_{horiz}$ in m |
| 2 | $M_{resist} = W_{truck} \times L_{CG,truck}$ | **Resisting Moment** (Stabilizing Moment) | $M_{resist}$ in N·m |
| 3 | $SF = \dfrac{M_{resist}}{M_{load}}$ | **Stability Factor** (must be $\geq 1.0$) | Dimensionless; **SF ≥ 1.0 for safe operation** |
| 4 | $W_{combined} = W_{truck} + W_{load}$ | **Combined Weight** | N (Newtons) |
| 5 | $L_{CG,combined} = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot L_{CG,truck} + W_{load} \cdot L_{load}}{W_{combined}}$ | **Combined CG Location** | m (measured from rear axle) |
| 6 | $Cap_{actual} = Cap_{rated} \times \dfrac{L_{std}}{L_{actual}}$ | **Capacity Derating** (with increased load center) | kg; $L_{std}$ = standard load center (**500 mm** or **600 mm** typically) |
| 7 | $Cap_{actual} = Cap_{rated} \times \dfrac{H_{std}}{H_{actual}}$ | **Capacity Reduction at Elevated Heights** | kg |
| 8 | $P_{engine} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_{mech}}$ | **Engine Power Requirement** | W; $\eta_{mech}$ = mechanical efficiency (~0.85) |
| 9 | $V_{CO} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \times EF_{CO}}{\rho_{CO}}$ | **Carbon Monoxide Emission Rate** | m³/s; relevant for ventilation design |
| 10 | $L_{PA} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{0}}\right)$ | **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | dB; $I_{0}$ = **10⁻¹² W/m²** (reference intensity) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Rule in Tables:** For absolute-value expressions inside formula tables, always use **\vert** or **\mid** (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Stability Triangle and Load Moment formulas are not merely academic — they are **directly used in industry** to:
- Design **load capacity charts** printed on every forklift
- Conduct **safety audits** and **risk assessments**
- Train **operators for certification** (mandatory under OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178(l))
- Develop **load-handling procedures** for warehouses and ports
- Determine **aisle widths** in warehouse design (typically **truck width + 1.0 m clearance**)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Combined Center of Gravity (CG) of Truck + Load

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a gasoline operated forklift with the following parameters:

- Truck weight (including counterweight): $W_{truck} = 5{,}000$ kg
- Truck CG location from rear axle: $L_{CG,truck} = 0.80$ m
- Load weight: $W_{load} = 1{,}500$ kg
- Load CG location (from rear axle, on forks at standard 500 mm load center): $L_{load} = 1.50$ m

Determine the **combined center of gravity** location.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert Weights to Forces (SI Units)**

For each mass, the gravitational force is given by Newton's Second Law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{truck,N} &= m_{truck} \cdot g = 5{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
            &= 49{,}050 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{load,N} &= m_{load} \cdot g = 1{,}500 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \\
           &= 14{,}715 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Explanation:** We convert masses (in kg) to weights (in Newtons) because the moment equation requires **force × distance**, not mass × distance. The constant $g = 9.81$ m/s² is the **standard gravity** adopted by KTU examination papers unless otherwise stated.

---

**Step 2 — Apply the Principle of Moments**

By the **principle of moments** (also called the **static equilibrium of moments**), the **moment of the combined system about the rear axle** equals the **sum of individual moments**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(W_{truck,N} + W_{load,N}) \cdot L_{CG,combined} = W_{truck,N} \cdot L_{CG,truck} + W_{load,N} \cdot L_{load}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Explanation:** This equation comes from the principle that **no net torque** acts on a stationary body. The sum of clockwise moments equals the sum of counter-clockwise moments about any reference point — here, the **rear axle pivot** is chosen because it is the tipping fulcrum.

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Combined Weight**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{combined} &= W_{truck,N} + W_{load,N} \\
             &= 49{,}050 + 14{,}715 \\
             &= 63{,}765 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Sum of Individual Moments**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma M &= W_{truck,N} \cdot L_{CG,truck} + W_{load,N} \cdot L_{load} \\
         &= (49{,}050)(0.80) + (14{,}715)(1.50) \\
         &= 39{,}240 + 22{,}072.5 \\
         &= 61{,}312.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Explanation:** Each term is a **first-moment of weight** about the rear axle. The total is the **first moment of the combined system**.

---

**Step 5 — Solve for the Combined CG Location**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{CG,combined} &= \dfrac{\Sigma M}{W_{combined}} \\
                 &= \dfrac{61{,}312.5}{63{,}765} \\
                 &= 0.9615 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Verify Against Stability Triangle**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a typical sit-down counterbalanced forklift, the **front wheels are approximately 1.20 m** from the rear axle. The **stability triangle** boundary requires that $L_{CG,combined}$ be **less than the front-axle distance (≈ 1.20 m)**.

Since $L_{CG,combined} = 0.9615$ m $< 1.20$ m, the combined CG **lies safely inside the stability triangle**, and the truck **will not tip forward** under this load.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stability Factor Calculation

Using the same example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{load} &= W_{load,N} \times L_{load} = 14{,}715 \times 1.50 = 22{,}072.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{resist} &= W_{truck,N} \times L_{CG,truck} = 49{,}050 \times 0.80 = 39{,}240 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF &= \dfrac{M_{resist}}{M_{load}} = \dfrac{39{,}240}{22{,}072.5} = 1.778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Since $SF = 1.778 > 1.0$, the truck is **stable** with a **77.8% safety margin** over the minimum stability requirement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity Derating with Increased Load Center

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A truck has a rated capacity of **2,500 kg** at a standard load center of **500 mm**. The operator must lift a load whose CG is at **700 mm** from the fork face. Determine the **new safe capacity**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cap_{actual} &= Cap_{rated} \times \dfrac{L_{std}}{L_{actual}} \\
              &= 2{,}500 \times \dfrac{500}{700} \\
              &= 2{,}500 \times 0.7143 \\
              &= 1{,}785.7 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Explanation:** The relationship is **inversely proportional**. As the load center increases (load is shifted forward), the **effective capacity decreases**. This is the foundation of every **load capacity plate** mounted on industrial trucks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students frequently compute $\dfrac{L_{actual}}{L_{std}}$ (which gives a value >1) and **incorrectly multiply**, instead of dividing. **Always divide the rated by the actual load center ratio**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ventilation Requirement for Indoor Gasoline Truck Operation

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

A warehouse uses one gasoline forklift consuming fuel at $\dot{m}_{fuel} = 4.5$ L/h. The CO emission factor is **$EF_{CO} = 240$ g/L** (typical for an SI engine without catalyst). Determine the **required fresh-air ventilation rate** to keep CO below the **OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) of 50 ppm**.

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Mass Emission Rate of CO**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{CO} &= \dot{V}_{fuel} \times EF_{CO} \\
             &= 4.5 \text{ L/h} \times 240 \text{ g/L} \\
             &= 1{,}080 \text{ g/h} \\
             &= 1{,}080 / 3{,}600 \text{ g/s} \\
             &= 0.300 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Convert OSHA PEL to Mass Concentration**

CO PEL = 50 ppm = 50 parts per million by volume.
Molar mass of CO = 28 g/mol.
At NTP (25 °C, 1 atm), molar volume = 24.45 L/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PEL} &= 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{28}{24.45} \\
        &= 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.145 \\
        &= 5.725 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/L} \\
        &= 5.725 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mg/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute Required Ventilation Flow Rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{vent} &= \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{C_{PEL}} \\
               &= \dfrac{0.300 \text{ g/s}}{5.725 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/L}} \\
               &= 5{,}240 \text{ L/s} \\
               &= 188.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step Explanation:** The required ventilation rate is **~189 m³/min** to dilute CO to safe levels. This is why **NFPA 505** mandates that gasoline trucks **must NOT be used indoors** unless adequate mechanical ventilation is provided.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Gasoline Operated Truck: Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Operator Cabin] --> nodeB[Safety Systems]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Controls]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Overhead Guard]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Seat Belt]

    nodeB --> nodeG[Load Backrest Extension]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Steering Wheel]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Hydraulic Levers]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Brake Pedal]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Accelerator Pedal]
    nodeL[Engine Compartment] --> nodeM[4-Stroke SI Engine]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Fuel Tank 40 to 90 L]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Exhaust System with Muffler]
    nodeL --> nodeP[Radiator Cooling System]
    nodeL --> nodeQ[12 V Battery]
    nodeR[Hydraulic System] --> nodeS[Hydraulic Pump]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Control Valve Bank]
    nodeR --> nodeU[Lift and Tilt Cylinders]
    nodeV[Mast Assembly] --> nodeW[Forks 1070 mm]
    nodeV --> nodeX[Lift Chains]
    nodeV --> nodeY[Two Stage Mast]
    nodeZ[Drivetrain] --> nodeAA[Hydrostatic Transmission]
    nodeZ --> nodeAB[Drive Axle Front]
    nodeZ --> nodeAC[Steer Axle Rear]
    nodeZ --> nodeAD[Cushion or Pneumatic Tires]

    nodeAE --> nodeAG[Wheelbase Base]
    nodeL -. powers .-> nodeR
    nodeL -. powers .-> nodeZ
    nodeR -. actuates .-> nodeV
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Operational Stability Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Operator Receives Load Instruction] --> startB[Identify Load Weight and CG]
    startB --> startC[Compare with Capacity Plate]
    startC --> startD{Capacity OK?}
    startD -->|No| startE[STOP - Use Larger Truck or Reduce Load]
    startD -->|Yes| startF[Check Load Center Distance]
    startF --> startG[Apply Capacity Derating Formula]
    startG --> startH{Derated Capacity OK?}
    startH -->|No| startE
    startH -->|Yes| startI[Position Forks Under Load]
    startI --> startJ[Lift Load 100 mm Above Floor]
    startJ --> startK[Tilt Mast Back Slightly]
    startK --> startL[Travel with Load Low and Tilted Back]
    startL --> startM[Monitor Stability Triangle Continuously]
    startM --> startN{Tip-Over Risk?}
    startN -->|Yes| startO[STOP - Lower Load - Reverse Direction]
    startN -->|No| startP[Reach Destination]
    startP --> startQ[Lower Load Fully Before Disengaging]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** | **CO4 | Bloom Level: Remember**

### Question A1
**Define the term "Stability Triangle" as applied to a gasoline operated forklift. State its significance in material handling safety.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Stability Triangle** is an imaginary triangle formed by connecting the **two front drive wheels** and the **center of the rear steer axle**. It represents the lateral three-point contact zone on which the truck rests. (1.5 marks)

**Significance:**
1. It defines the **safe zone** within which the combined center of gravity (CG) of the truck and load must remain. (1 mark)
2. If the combined CG moves **outside** the triangle, the truck **tips over** — a major cause of fatalities. (0.5 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** | **CO4 | Bloom Level: Understand**

### Question A2
**List any SIX safety features that are mandatory on a modern gasoline operated industrial truck as per ANSI/ITSDF B56.1.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Overhead Guard (OHG)** — protects operator from falling objects
2. **Load Backrest Extension** — prevents load rearward shift
3. **Seat Belt with Retractor** — operator restraint system
4. **Backup Alarm** (>102 dB) — reverse motion warning
5. **Service Brake System** — dual-circuit hydraulic brakes
6. **Parking Brake** — automatically engages when operator leaves seat

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Model Question Paper]** | **CO4, CO5 | Bloom Level: Apply / Analyze**

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**A gasoline operated forklift has the following data:**
- Total truck weight (with counterweight): $W_{truck} = 6{,}500$ kg
- CG of truck from rear axle: $L_{CG,truck} = 0.90$ m
- Load weight: $W_{load} = 1{,}800$ kg
- Load CG from rear axle: $L_{load} = 1.40$ m
- Rated capacity of truck: $2{,}000$ kg at standard load center $500$ mm
- Actual load center: $650$ mm

#### (a) Calculate the combined center of gravity of the truck and load. Determine whether the combined CG lies within the stability triangle, given that the front axle is $1.15$ m from the rear axle. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert masses to weights:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{truck,N} &= 6{,}500 \times 9.81 = 63{,}765 \text{ N} \\
W_{load,N}  &= 1{,}800 \times 9.81 = 17{,}658 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Combined weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{combined} &= 63{,}765 + 17{,}658 = 81{,}423 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum of individual moments about rear axle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma M &= (63{,}765)(0.90) + (17{,}658)(1.40) \\
         &= 57{,}388.5 + 24{,}721.2 \\
         &= 82{,}109.7 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined CG location:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{CG,combined} &= \dfrac{82{,}109.7}{81{,}423} \\
                 &= 1.0084 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with front axle distance (1.15 m):**

Since $L_{CG,combined} = 1.0084 \text{ m} < 1.15 \text{ m}$ (the front axle boundary), the combined CG **lies inside the stability triangle**. (1 mark for conclusion)

**Stability Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SF &= \dfrac{M_{resist}}{M_{load}} = \dfrac{57{,}388.5}{24{,}721.2} = 2.321
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $SF > 1.0$, the truck is **stable** with a **132.1% safety margin**. (1 mark for SF comment)

#### (b) Calculate the derated safe capacity of the truck for the actual load center. State whether the given load of 1,800 kg can be safely lifted. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply capacity derating formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cap_{actual} &= Cap_{rated} \times \dfrac{L_{std}}{L_{actual}} \\
              &= 2{,}000 \times \dfrac{500}{650} \\
              &= 2{,}000 \times 0.7692 \\
              &= 1{,}538.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with load weight:**

Since actual load $W_{load} = 1{,}800$ kg $>$ $Cap_{actual} = 1{,}538.5$ kg, the **load CANNOT be safely lifted** with the given truck configuration. (1 mark for comparison)

**Step 3 — Required alternative:**

Either use a **larger capacity truck** OR **reduce load** to within safe limits, OR shift load to reduce the actual load center distance. (1 mark for recommendation)

**Step 4 — Effect of lift height (additional consideration):**

At elevated heights, capacity decreases further. If lift height = 3.5 m (standard = 3.0 m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cap_{elevated} &= 1{,}538.5 \times \dfrac{3.0}{3.5} = 1{,}318.7 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark for elevation derating logic)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Do not** forget to convert kg to N before using the moment equation if the question asks for moment in N·m. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Do not** confuse the stability triangle check: the boundary is the **front axle distance**, not a fixed value. **(−2 marks)**
> - **Always** state the **Stability Factor** in your answer — examiners reward this explicitly. **(−1 mark)**
> - The derating formula is **inversely proportional**; reversing the ratio gives a **wrong answer**. **(−2 marks)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**A warehouse uses a single gasoline operated forklift for indoor pallet movement. The forklift consumes fuel at 5.2 L/h. The emission factor of CO is 220 g/L of fuel. The OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for CO is 50 ppm. The warehouse dimensions are 50 m × 30 m × 8 m (height).**

#### (a) Calculate the mass emission rate of CO in g/s and the required fresh-air ventilation rate in m³/min to maintain CO below the PEL. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass emission rate of CO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{CO} &= 5.2 \text{ L/h} \times 220 \text{ g/L} \\
             &= 1{,}144 \text{ g/h} \\
             &= \dfrac{1{,}144}{3{,}600} \\
             &= 0.3178 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert PEL to mass concentration:**

CO PEL = 50 ppm. Molar mass of CO = 28 g/mol. Molar volume at NTP = 24.45 L/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{PEL} &= 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{28}{24.45} \\
        &= 5.725 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Required ventilation flow:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{vent} &= \dfrac{0.3178}{5.725 \times 10^{-5}} \\
               &= 5{,}551 \text{ L/s} \\
               &= 333.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against warehouse volume:**

Warehouse volume $V_{wh} = 50 \times 30 \times 8 = 12{,}000 \text{ m}^3$.
Number of air changes per hour required:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ACH &= \dfrac{333.1 \times 60}{12{,}000} = 1.665 \text{ per hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **insufficient** for safe continuous indoor operation. NFPA recommends a **minimum of 6 ACH** for gasoline truck indoor use.

#### (b) Discuss FIVE critical safety precautions for operating gasoline trucks indoors and outline a refueling safety procedure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer — 5 Indoor Safety Precautions (3.5 marks):**
1. **Adequate Mechanical Ventilation** — minimum 6 ACH, per NFPA 505
2. **CO Detectors with Alarms** — set at 25 ppm (alarm) and 50 ppm (evacuation)
3. **Catalytic Converter Installation** — reduces CO emission by 70-90%
4. **Restricted Operating Hours** — limit continuous engine runtime
5. **Periodic Emission Testing** — quarterly CO measurement at exhaust

**Model Answer — Refueling Procedure (3.5 marks):**
1. **Park the truck in a designated refueling area**, away from ignition sources
2. **Shut off the engine** and engage parking brake before refueling
3. **Ground the truck** with a bonding cable to prevent static discharge
4. **Use only approved fuel containers** with proper labeling
5. **Do not overfill** — leave 5% air space in tank for vapor expansion
6. **Replace fuel cap securely** and clean up any spillage immediately
7. **No smoking or open flames** within 15 m of refueling zone

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - **Failure to convert g/h to g/s** is the most common unit error in emission problems. **(−2 marks)**
> - Students often forget to **verify against warehouse volume** — examiners look for this engineering judgment step. **(−1 mark)**
> - In refueling procedure questions, **do not skip the bonding/grounding step** — it's the most testable safety point. **(−2 marks)**
> - Mentioning **NFPA 505 / OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178** references carries extra credit. **(bonus)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gasoline Operated Trucks (Module 4)**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- [ ] **Gasoline Operated Truck** = Spark-ignition ICE forklift (Classes IV & V per OSHA)
- [ ] **Stability Triangle** = Triangle connecting front wheels + rear steer pivot
- [ ] **Combined CG** = Weighted average location of truck+load CG
- [ ] **Load Center** = Horizontal distance from fork face to load CG (standard: 500/600 mm)
- [ ] **Rated Capacity** = Maximum load at standard load center and standard lift height

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize These)
- [ ] **Combined CG:** $L_{CG,combined} = \dfrac{W_{truck} \cdot L_{CG,truck} + W_{load} \cdot L_{load}}{W_{truck} + W_{load}}$
- [ ] **Capacity Derating:** $Cap_{actual} = Cap_{rated} \times \dfrac{L_{std}}{L_{actual}}$
- [ ] **Stability Factor:** $SF = \dfrac{M_{resist}}{M_{load}} \geq 1.0$
- [ ] **Ventilation:** $\dot{Q}_{vent} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{C_{PEL,CO}}$

### ✅ Mandatory Safety Features (ANSI B56.1)
- [ ] Overhead Guard (OHG), Load Backrest, Seat Belt, Backup Alarm, Dual-circuit Brakes, Parking Brake

### ✅ Critical Safety Numbers
- [ ] CO PEL = **50 ppm** (OSHA)
- [ ] Backup alarm = **≥ 102 dB**
- [ ] Minimum indoor ventilation = **6 ACH** (NFPA 505)
- [ ] Stability Factor **must be ≥ 1.0**

### ✅ Top Causes of Gasoline Truck Accidents
- [ ] **Tip-overs** (load instability)
- [ ] **Pedestrian strikes** (poor visibility)
- [ ] **Falls from truck** (operator not wearing seat belt)
- [ ] **CO poisoning** (indoor use without ventilation)
- [ ] **Fires during refueling** (static discharge, sparks)

### ✅ Standards to Remember for Exam
- [ ] **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178** — Operator training & truck operation
- [ ] **ANSI/ITSDF B56.1** — Safety standard for industrial trucks
- [ ] **NFPA 505** — Fire safety standard for powered industrial trucks

### ✅ Sub-Parts Likely in Exam
- [ ] Calculation problems (Combined CG, Derating, SF, Ventilation) = **guaranteed 7-mark question**
- [ ] Safety procedure / pre-operation checklist = **3-mark question** (Part A)
- [ ] Definition / classification = **3-mark question** (Part A)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
