# Module 4 | LPG Trucks & Power Elevators — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 LPG Trucks (Liquefied Petroleum Gas Transport Vehicles)

### Formal Definition
An **LPG Truck** is a specialized hazardous-materials (HazMat) transport vehicle designed, fabricated, and certified under the **Indian Petroleum Rules 2002, Gas Cylinder Rules 2004, and OISD (Oil Industry Safety Directorate) Standard 149** for the safe road transportation of Liquefied Petroleum Gas — a mixture primarily of **butane (C₄H₁₀)** and **propane (C₃H₈)** — either in bulk (road tankers) or in pressurized cylinders (cylinder trucks). The vehicle must be registered as a **"EX"/FLAMMABLE** category vehicle under Rule 129 of the Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR) and bear **Class 2.1 (Flammable Gas)** UN hazmat placards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LPG trucks are covered in OESFT833 under the unit "Transportation of Hazardous Materials — Bulk & Cylinder Carriers". The mandatory Indian standards are **IS 3196 (Welded Low Carbon Steel Cylinders)** and **OISD-149 (Road Transportation of LPG)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Floating Bomb on Wheels"
Think of an LPG truck as a **pressurized thermos flask driving down the highway**. The pressurized vessel inside (operating at roughly **7 bar to 17 bar gauge** at 60 °C) is no different from a shaken soda bottle — except the contents are highly flammable and odorized with **Ethyl Mercaptan (C₂H₅SH)** for leak detection. A single spark from a loose bolt grounding the chassis or a road accident turning a valve into a projectile can turn the truck into a BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion). Therefore, every safety feature — from the **emergency shut-off valve (ESDV)** to the **pressure relief valve (PRV)** — is a redundant layer of "defense in depth", exactly like the multiple safety interlocks in a nuclear reactor.

### Physical & Design Constants
- **Design pressure (tank):** **17.5 kg/cm² (g)** — per IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII Div 1
- **Test pressure (hydraulic):** **1.5 × Design Pressure = 26.25 kg/cm² (g)**
- **Filling Density (Propane):** **425 kg/m³** | **Filling Density (Butane):** **510 kg/m³**
- **Boiling Point (Propane):** **−42 °C** | **Boiling Point (Butane):** **−0.5 °C**
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (Propane):** **450 °C**
- **LEL / UEL (Propane in air):** **2.1 % / 9.5 %** by volume
- **Marking color on cylinder body:** **Red (IS 4379)** with "LPG" written in white.

---

## 1.2 Power Elevators (Mechanical Vertical Material/Personnel Handling)

### Formal Definition
A **Power Elevator** is a mechanically powered, fixed-installation lifting appliance that hoists a platform (car or cage) along a vertical or inclined guide path between two or more landings, used for transporting **personnel, materials, or both**. Under KTU's OESFT833 syllabus, elevators are studied under **"Powered Industrial Trucks & Vertical Lifting Equipment"** and are governed by the **Indian Standard IS 14665 (Safety Requirements for Lifts/Elevators)** and the **Factories Act 1948, Section 28 & Schedule VII**.

### Types of Power Elevators
| Type | Drive Mechanism | Typical Capacity | Application |
|------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| **Traction (Electric) Elevator** | Geared/Gearless motor + steel ropes over sheave | 4 – 20 persons | Buildings, hospitals |
| **Hydraulic Elevator** | Piston ram in cylinder (oil-actuated) | Up to 25 000 kg | Goods lifts, low-rise |
| **Scissor Lift Elevator** | Hydraulic/pneumatic scissor linkage | 500 – 10 000 kg | Warehouses, loading docks |
| **Freight Elevator (Goods Lift)** | Geared motor + counterweight | 1000 – 5000 kg | Industrial plants |
| **Pneumatic (Vacuum) Elevator** | Air-pressure differential | 1 – 2 persons | Residential, retrofits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Vertical Train"**
> Imagine an elevator as a **train that runs vertically inside a shaft**. The "engine" (motor + sheave or hydraulic ram) provides lifting force, the "brake" (rope gripper + counterweight) provides fail-safe stopping, the "doors" (interlocked landing/car doors) prevent falls, and the "track" (guide rails + T-rails) keeps the car from swinging. If any of these four "railway components" fails, the system MUST default to a safe state — this is the essence of elevator safety engineering.

### Key Performance Constants (IS 14665 Compliant)
- **Maximum rated speed (passenger lift):** **1.0 – 2.5 m/s**
- **Maximum rated speed (high-rise):** **up to 8 m/s**
- **Hoistway overtravel (top clearance):** **≥ 0.3 m + 0.035 v²** (where v = rated speed)
- **Buffer stroke:** **0.067 v²** (minimum)
- **Safety gear tripping speed:** **1.15 × rated speed**
- **Car door closing force limit:** **≤ 150 N**
- **Counterweight ratio:** typically **40 – 50 % of car weight + 50 – 60 % of rated load**

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LPG Truck — Engineering Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Why a "Tanker-Truck" is NOT an Ordinary Container
LPG in liquid form occupies only **1/260th** the volume of its gaseous form, which is why it is transported as a pressurized liquid. However, the moment the liquid leaks, it flash-vaporizes into a flammable cloud roughly **270× larger**, creating a vapor-cloud explosion risk. The truck tank must therefore be:
- A **pressure vessel** (IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII Div 1)
- Equipped with **two independent PRVs** (one operational, one standby)
- Mounted on a chassis with **non-sparking ferrous / aluminium alloy fittings**
- Fitted with a **roll-over protection cage** and **rear under-run guard (RU-G)**

### 2.1.2 Critical Components & Their Safety Function
| Component | Function | Standard |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Emergency Shut-off Valve (ESDV)** | Remotely closes liquid/vapor line from outside the vehicle | OISD-149 |
| **Internal Valve (Excess Flow Valve)** | Auto-closes if flow exceeds 150 % of normal (e.g., on pipe rupture) | OISD-149 |
| **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** | Opens at set pressure; vents to safe location | ASME Sec VIII |
| **Roto-gauge / Float gauge** | Indicates % liquid fill (must never exceed 85 % volumetric) | IS 3196 |
| **Thermometer well** | Reads bulk liquid temperature | IS 3196 |
| **Earth-continuity strap** | Static-electricity dissipation (resistance < 10 Ω) | IS 1912 |
| **Fire extinguisher (DCP, 9 kg × 2 nos.)** | First-attack fire suppression | OISD-117 |

### 2.1.3 Operational Hazards
1. **BLEVE** — Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (tank rupture under fire)
2. **Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — released LPG mixes with air and ignites
3. **Roll-over** — valve damage, hose shear
4. **Static discharge** — ignition during loading/unloading
5. **Overfilling** — hydrostatic rupture of the vessel

---

## 2.2 Power Elevator — Engineering Logic Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Why Every Elevator is a "Forgiving System"
A falling elevator is the engineering equivalent of a free-fall from a height. The standard "defense in depth" is:
1. **Primary suspension** (steel wire ropes, 4–8 strands) — supports car weight
2. **Counterweight + sheave traction** — provides mechanical advantage & balance
3. **Over-speed governor (OSG)** — detects car speed > 115 % of rated speed
4. **Safety gear (parachute brake)** — wedges car into guide rails within 1 m of travel
5. **Buffers (oil/spring type)** — absorb residual kinetic energy at shaft extremes
6. **Final limit switches** — cut motor power at top/bottom overtravel
7. **Emergency brake on motor** — friction disc/band brake

### 2.2.2 Safety Logic Flowchart
- Door closed + interlocks made + car at landing → **"Run-permissive"** signal sent to controller
- Any door interlock broken → car **cannot move** (interlock logic)
- Cable slack detected → rope gripper **engages immediately** (no power required — fail-safe)
- Power failure → gravity drop initiated → OSG detects overspeed → safety gear wedge into rails

---

## 2.3 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### LPG Truck Formulas
| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Filling Density (FD)** | $FD = \dfrac{M_{LPG}}{V_{water\, displaced}}$ | **kg/m³** | Must follow IS 3196 |
| 2 | **Test Pressure** | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_{design}$ | **kg/cm² (g)** | Hydrostatic test |
| 3 | **Vapor Cloud Volume (LPG leak)** | $V_{vapour} = V_{liquid} \times \dfrac{\rho_{L}}{\rho_{V}}$ | **m³** | 270× expansion typical |
| 4 | **Cylinder Burst Pressure (thin-wall approx.)** | $P = \dfrac{2 \times t \times \sigma_{t}}{D}$ | **N/mm²** | Hoop stress formula |
| 5 | **Grounding Resistance** | $R_g \le 10 \,\Omega$ | **Ohm** | Static dissipation |
| 6 | **% Volumetric Filling Limit** | $V_{fill} \le 85\,\%$ | **%** | IS 3196 / OISD-149 |

### Power Elevator Formulas (IS 14665)
| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Top Car Clearance (PIT-OVERHEAD)** | $C_{top} \ge 0.3 + 0.035\,v^{2}$ | **m** | $v$ = rated speed (m/s) |
| 2 | **Bottom Pit Depth** | $D_{pit} \ge 0.5 + 0.035\,v^{2}$ | **m** | For buffers |
| 3 | **Buffer Stroke (energy balance)** | $S_{buffer} \ge 0.067\,v^{2}$ | **m** | Oleo/oil buffer |
| 4 | **Over-speed Governor trip** | $v_{trip} = 1.15\,v_{rated}$ | **m/s** | Triggers safety gear |
| 5 | **Counterweight Mass** | $M_{cw} = M_{car} + 0.5\,Q_{rated}$ | **kg** | Approx. balance |
| 6 | **Rope Safety Factor** | $SF \ge 12$ | **dimensionless** | IS 14665 Part 3 |
| 7 | **Hoist Motor Power** | $P = \dfrac{(M_{car}+Q_{rated})\,g\,v}{\eta}$ | **W** | $\eta$ = 0.75–0.85 |
| 8 | **Rope Tension per Strand** | $T = \dfrac{(M_{car}+Q_{rated})\,g}{n_{ropes}}$ | **N** | $n_{ropes}$ = number of ropes |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the "0.035 v²" formula** — it appears in nearly every KTU elevator numerical problem (top clearance, pit depth, buffer stroke derivation). Master it!

- **Power Elevator Design:** L&T (OTIS joint venture), KONE India, Schindler India, Johnson Lifts. Compliance with **Chief Inspector of Factories (Kerala)** annual inspection mandates.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LPG Cylinder Wall Thickness (Hoop Stress)

### Problem Context
A commercial LPG cylinder must be designed for an internal design pressure of $P_d = 17.5 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ (gauge). The outer diameter is $D_o = 320 \text{ mm}$. The cylinder is made of **IS 6240 hot-rolled steel** with ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u = 410 \text{ N/mm}^2$ and yield strength $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Compute the **required wall thickness** and the **hydrostatic test pressure**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert design pressure to SI (N/mm²)**
$$
1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 = 0.0981 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
P_d = 17.5 \times 0.0981 = 1.717 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Select allowable stress (per IS 2825)**
$$
\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{1.6} = \dfrac{250}{1.6} = 156.25 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Apply thin-cylinder hoop-stress formula**
$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P_d \, D_i}{2\,t} \le \sigma_{allow}
$$

Solving for $t$:
$$
t = \dfrac{P_d \, D_i}{2 \,\sigma_{allow}}
$$

**Step 4: Approximate inner diameter** (assume $t \ll D_o$ for thin-wall check first)
$$
D_i \approx D_o - 2t \approx D_o \;\;(\text{iterative})
$$

$$
t = \dfrac{1.717 \times 320}{2 \times 156.25} = \dfrac{549.4}{312.5} = 1.758 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Iterate to refine (set $D_i = 320 - 2(1.76) = 316.5$ mm)**
$$
t_{refined} = \dfrac{1.717 \times 316.5}{2 \times 156.25} = \dfrac{543.5}{312.5} = 1.74 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Apply corrosion allowance (CA = 1.0 mm for LPG)**
$$
t_{design} = 1.74 + 1.0 = 2.74 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{final} \approx 3.0 \text{ mm} \;(\text{standardized to next higher gauge})}
$$

**Step 7: Hydrostatic Test Pressure (per IS 2825)**
$$
P_{test} = 1.5 \times P_d = 1.5 \times 1.717 = 2.576 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{test} \approx 26.25 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \,(g)}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Elevator Top Car Clearance (IS 14665)

### Problem Context
A passenger elevator has a **rated speed $v = 1.75$ m/s**. Determine:
1. Top car clearance (overhead)
2. Bottom pit depth
3. Buffer stroke requirement

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Top Car Clearance (per IS 14665 Part 2, Cl. 5.7)**
$$
C_{top} = 0.3 + 0.035 \,v^{2}
$$

$$
C_{top} = 0.3 + 0.035 \times (1.75)^{2}
$$

$$
C_{top} = 0.3 + 0.035 \times 3.0625
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{top} = 0.3 + 0.1072 = 0.4072 \text{ m} \approx 410 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 2: Bottom Pit Depth (per IS 14665 Part 2, Cl. 5.8)**
$$
D_{pit} = 0.5 + 0.035\,v^{2}
$$

$$
D_{pit} = 0.5 + 0.1072
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{pit} = 0.6072 \text{ m} \approx 610 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3: Buffer Stroke (Oleo type, IS 14665 Part 4)**
$$
S_{buffer} = 0.067\,v^{2}
$$

$$
S_{buffer} = 0.067 \times 3.0625
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{buffer} = 0.2052 \text{ m} \approx 210 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vapor Cloud Volume from LPG Cylinder Leak

### Problem Context
A 14.2 kg domestic LPG cylinder contains 26.2 litres of liquid LPG. If the entire contents leak and vaporize completely (worst-case), find the **flammable vapor volume at 1 atm and 25 °C**.

**Given:**
- Density of liquid LPG: $\rho_L = 510 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Density of LPG vapor at 25 °C, 1 atm: $\rho_V = 1.85 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Volume of liquid: $V_L = 0.0262 \text{ m}^3$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Mass of LPG leaked**
$$
M = \rho_L \times V_L = 510 \times 0.0262 = 13.36 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Vapor volume expansion**
$$
V_{vapour} = \dfrac{M}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{13.36}{1.85} = 7.22 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 3: Expansion Ratio (verification)**
$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\rho_L}{\rho_V} = \dfrac{510}{1.85} = 275.7 \approx 276
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{vapour} \approx 7.22 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (from one 14.2 kg cylinder)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-world significance:** A single 14.2 kg domestic cylinder, if fully leaked indoors, can fill a typical **10 m × 10 m × 3 m** room (300 m³) up to an LPG concentration of **2.4 % by volume** — which lies **above the LEL of 2.1 %**, making the room IMMEDIATELY EXPLOSIVE on any spark.

| **Inspection Authority** | PESO + RTO | Chief Inspector of Factories (Kerala) |
| **Inspection Frequency** | Every 3 years (hydro-test) | Annually + 6-monthly for safety gear |

### Q1. Define a "Power Elevator" as per IS 14665. List any four mandatory safety components. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A power elevator is a permanently installed, motor-driven lifting appliance that transports personnel or materials between floors of a building along fixed vertical guides. The four mandatory safety components as per IS 14665 are:
1. **Over-Speed Governor (OSG)** — trips at 1.15 × rated speed
2. **Safety Gear (Car-mounted)** — wedges car into guide rails on overspeed
3. **Buffers (Oleo/Spring type)** — absorb residual kinetic energy at shaft extremes
4. **Door Interlocks (Car + Landing)** — prevent car movement unless all doors are closed

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **safety features and essential components of an LPG road tanker-truck** as per **OISD-149** and **IS 3196**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LPG cylinder of **14.2 kg water capacity** is filled with **11.0 kg of LPG (propane)**. Calculate:
- (i) The **filling density** in kg/m³
- (ii) The **mass of vapor** that would be released if the cylinder leaks completely at 25 °C
- (iii) The **volumetric expansion ratio** of liquid to vapor **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The LPG road tanker-truck must include the following safety features (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **Pressure Vessel Tank** — designed per IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII Div 1, hydrostatically tested at 1.5 × design pressure.
2. **Two Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs)** — primary + standby, set at 17.5 kg/cm².
3. **Emergency Shut-off Valve (ESDV)** — remote-operable from outside the vehicle.
4. **Internal Excess-Flow Valve** — auto-closes if flow > 150 % normal.
5. **Earth-continuity strap** — resistance ≤ 10 Ω, prevents static spark.
6. **Two 9 kg DCP fire extinguishers** in quick-release brackets.
7. **Reflective UN 1075 placards** on all four sides.
8. **Anti-spark wheel chocks**, **rear under-run guard (RU-G)**, and **side under-run rails**.
9. **PESO license, MSDS, and route permit** carried in cab.
10. **Speed governor on vehicle** — max 60 km/h on highways, 40 km/h in city limits (OISD-149).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Water capacity of cylinder: $V_w = 14.2 \text{ kg water} = 0.0142 \text{ m}^3$ (since 1 kg water = 0.001 m³)
- Mass of LPG filled: $M_{LPG} = 11.0 \text{ kg}$

**(i) Filling Density:**
$$
FD = \dfrac{M_{LPG}}{V_w} = \dfrac{11.0}{0.0142}
$$

$$
\boxed{FD = 774.6 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

**Sanity check:** IS 3196 allows max 425 kg/m³ for propane. The result of 774.6 kg/m³ is physically impossible (it exceeds density of pure liquid propane at 15 °C ≈ 510 kg/m³). Therefore, this represents a hypothetical scenario OR the cylinder is overfilled (which is illegal). The KTU answer is to **report the calculation** and **flag it as a non-compliant overfill**.

**(ii) Mass of vapor released (complete leak):**
$$
M_{vapour} = M_{LPG} = 11.0 \text{ kg}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric expansion ratio:**
Liquid volume = $V_L = 0.0142 \text{ m}^3$
Vapor volume at 25 °C, 1 atm, with $\rho_V = 1.85 \text{ kg/m}^3$:
$$
V_{vapour} = \dfrac{11.0}{1.85} = 5.95 \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Expansion Ratio} = \dfrac{5.95}{0.0142} \approx 419 \text{ times}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often forget the unit-conversion of water capacity (1 kg water = 1 litre = 0.001 m³) — losing 1 mark.
> - Students skip the "sanity check" of FD vs IS 3196 limit — losing 1 mark for not identifying that the problem describes an overfill.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **construction and working of a traction-type power elevator**, listing **at least six safety features** as per **IS 14665**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A freight elevator has the following specifications:
- Car weight: $M_{car} = 1500$ kg
- Rated load: $Q = 3000$ kg
- Rated speed: $v = 1.5$ m/s
- Number of wire ropes: $n = 4$
- Drive efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

Calculate:
- (i) The **counterweight mass** (assume 50 % of (car + load))
- (ii) The **tension in each rope**
- (iii) The **hoist motor power** required **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The traction-type power elevator consists of:

1. **Hoist Motor** (geared/gearless AC motor with VVVF drive)
2. **Traction Sheave** — grooved pulley that grips the ropes via friction
3. **Steel Wire Ropes** (4–8 strands), each with safety factor ≥ 12
4. **Car** — suspended on one side of the sheave
5. **Counterweight** — on the opposite side, balances 40–50 % of (car + 50 % load)
6. **Guide Rails** — T-section steel rails in the hoistway
7. **Controller** — PLC-based, with motor drive logic

**Six Safety Features (IS 14665):**
- Over-speed governor (OSG) — trips at 1.15 v
- Progressive safety gear on car
- Counterweight safety gear
- Final limit switches (top & bottom)
- Buffers (Oleo type) in pit
- Door interlock circuit
- Slack rope detector
- Emergency alarm & intercom

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Counterweight mass:**
$$
M_{cw} = M_{car} + 0.5 \times Q = 1500 + 0.5 \times 3000
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{cw} = 3000 \text{ kg}}
$$

**(ii) Tension in each rope (when fully loaded at standstill):**

Total suspended load on car side = $M_{car} + Q = 1500 + 3000 = 4500$ kg
Force = $W_{car} = 4500 \times 9.81 = 44145$ N

If the counterweight balances exactly 50 %, then net unbalanced load at standstill = 0 N. But during **lifting at full load, the counterweight pulls down and the car pulls up**. The "loaded rope tension" (worst case, single-roped, fully loaded) is:
$$
T_{rope} = \dfrac{(M_{car} + Q) \times g}{n} = \dfrac{4500 \times 9.81}{4}
$$

$$
T_{rope} = \dfrac{44145}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{rope} = 11036 \text{ N} \approx 11.04 \text{ kN per rope}}
$$

**(iii) Hoist motor power:**

Net force to lift (counterweight balances 50 %, so net load = 50 % of rated):
$$
F_{net} = (0.5 \times Q) \times g = (0.5 \times 3000) \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{F_{net} \times v}{\eta} = \dfrac{14715 \times 1.5}{0.85} = \dfrac{22072.5}{0.85}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 25967 \text{ W} \approx 25.97 \text{ kW} \approx 26 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Failing to include gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s²** will cost 1 mark.
> - Using **load weight instead of half-load** in the net-force calculation is the most common error. The counterweight cancels **half** the load, not the full load.
> - **Power should be in kW (not W)** — students often write the final answer as 25 967 W and lose the 1 mark for "unit awareness".

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

### LPG Trucks
- ✅ UN hazard class is **2.1 (Flammable Gas)**, UN number **1075** (Propane) / **1965** (Butane)
- ✅ Design pressure = **17.5 kg/cm²**, Test pressure = **1.5 × Design = 26.25 kg/cm²**
- ✅ Filling density: Propane **≤ 425 kg/m³**, Butane **≤ 510 kg/m³**
- ✅ Volumetric fill must **never exceed 85 %** of vessel capacity
- ✅ At least **two PRVs** (one operational + one standby) and one **ESDV** mandatory
- ✅ Earth-continuity resistance **≤ 10 Ω**, test before any hose connection
- ✅ Hydrostatic test of cylinders every **3 years** (PESO rule)
- ✅ Mandatory: **OISD-149** for transport, **IS 3196** for cylinder design
- ✅ Expansion ratio of liquid→vapor LPG ≈ **270–280 times**
- ✅ LEL of propane = **2.1 %**, UEL = **9.5 %** in air
- ✅ Two 9 kg DCP extinguishers on every LPG truck

### Power Elevators
- ✅ Governing standard: **IS 14665** (Parts 1–5)
- ✅ Top clearance formula: $C_{top} = 0.3 + 0.035 v^2$
- ✅ Pit depth formula: $D_{pit} = 0.5 + 0.035 v^2$
- ✅ Buffer stroke: $S = 0.067 v^2$
- ✅ OSG trip speed: **1.15 × rated speed**
- ✅ Rope safety factor: **≥ 12**
- ✅ Counterweight mass: **$M_{car} + 0.5 \times Q_{rated}$**
- ✅ Door closing force limit: **≤ 150 N**
- ✅ Annual inspection by **Chief Inspector of Factories** mandatory
- ✅ Defense-in-depth: Ropes → Counterweight → OSG → Safety gear → Buffers → Limit switches
- ✅ Interlock rule: **Car cannot move if ANY door is open** (this is the single most important safety feature)

### KTU Exam Tips
- 📌 Memorize the **0.035 v²** and **0.067 v²** formulas (used in 60 % of elevator problems).
- 📌 Always convert **kg/cm² → N/mm²** in LPG pressure vessel problems (1 kg/cm² = 0.0981 N/mm²).
- 📌 For comparison questions, **always use a two-column table** — KTU examiners reward structured answers.
- 📌 Include a **labelled block diagram** (Mermaid-style or hand-drawn) in any 14-mark question worth > 7 marks.
- 📌 Remember the **UN hazmat placard code** for LPG (Class 2.1 / UN 1075).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of material handling engineering, a **Drive** is defined as an integrated power transmission system that accepts energy from a prime mover (electric motor, internal combustion engine, hydraulic pump, or pneumatic compressor) and delivers it — in a controlled form of torque, speed, and direction — to the working mechanism of a material handling equipment such as a conveyor, crane, hoist, lift, elevator, forklift, or industrial robot.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, drives in material handling are formally classified into **five (5) primary families** based on the energy medium employed for power transmission:

1. **Mechanical Drives** — Solid elements (gears, belts, chains, ropes) transmit power through direct contact.
2. **Electrical Drives** — Electric motors coupled with electronic controllers transmit power through electromagnetic interaction.
3. **Hydraulic Drives** — Pressurized incompressible fluid (oil) transmits power through Pascal's principle.
4. **Pneumatic Drives** — Pressurized compressible gas (air) transmits power through expansion.
5. **Hybrid / Combination Drives** — Two or more drive technologies integrated to exploit the advantages of each.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The classification of drives, their operating principles, comparative merits, demerits, and the **safety interlocks / guarding** associated with each drive type form the core examinable content. Students must be able to *select* a drive for a given material handling application and justify the choice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Body's Power Transmission" Metaphor

Think of a material handling drive exactly like the **human muscular-skeletal system**:

| Human Body | Material Handling Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Brain (decision)** | Control panel / PLC | Sends the command |
| **Heart (prime mover)** | Electric motor / Engine | Generates the raw energy |
| **Arteries (transmission)** | Belt, Chain, Gear, Hydraulic line | Carries the power |
| **Muscles (actuator)** | Hoist drum, Conveyor pulley, Hydraulic cylinder | Does the work |
| **Joints (couplings)** | Couplings, Clutches, Brakes | Connect and decouple motion |
| **Skin & reflexes (safety)** | Guards, Limit switches, Overload relays | Protect against harm |

Just as a doctor must understand **cardiology** (hydraulic-like blood flow) **and neurology** (electrical nerve signals) to treat a patient, a material handling safety engineer must understand **all five drive types** — because each carries its **own unique safety hazards** (e.g., a chain drive risks whip-lash failure; a hydraulic drive risks hose burst and oil injection injuries; an electrical drive risks arc-flash and short-circuit).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following values are universally used in KTU-level material handling calculations:
>
> - **Standard motor supply frequencies:** $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$ (India, Europe, most of Asia) and $f = 60\ \text{Hz}$ (USA, Japan, parts of Middle East)
> - **Standard supply voltages (India, IS 12360):** $230\ \text{V}$ (1-$\phi$), $415\ \text{V}$ (3-$\phi$)
> - **Standard hydraulic working pressures:** Low pressure $\leq 70\ \text{bar}$, Medium $70\text{–}210\ \text{bar}$, High $210\text{–}420\ \text{bar}$, Ultra-high $> 420\ \text{bar}$
> - **Standard pneumatic working pressure:** $4\text{–}8\ \text{bar}$ (typical), maximum $10\ \text{bar}$
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Standard load factors (IS 3177 / IS 807):** Hoists $1.25\times\text{SWL}$ static test, $1.5\times\text{SWL}$ dynamic test

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Drives

The KTU 2024 module structures drives as follows:

### A. Mechanical Drives
- **A1. Belt Drives** — Flat belts, V-belts, timing (synchronous) belts
- **A2. Chain Drives** — Roller chains, silent chains, leaf chains
- **A3. Gear Drives** — Spur, helical, bevel, worm & wheel
- **A4. Rope Drives** — Wire ropes (used in cranes/hoists)
- **A5. Friction Drives** — Wheel-on-wheel, cone-pulley

### B. Electrical Drives
- **B1. AC Motors** — Squirrel-cage induction, slip-ring induction, synchronous
- **B2. DC Motors** — Shunt, series, compound
- **B3. Special Motors** — Servo, stepper, linear induction

### C. Hydraulic Drives
- **C1. Pump–Motor Pair** — Fixed or variable displacement
- **C2. Actuator-based** — Hydraulic cylinder (linear), hydraulic motor (rotary)
- **C3. Hydrostatic Transmission** — Closed-loop systems

### D. Pneumatic Drives
- **D1. Compressed-Air Cylinders** — Single-acting, double-acting
- **D2. Pneumatic Motors** — Vane, piston, turbine type
- **D3. Vacuum Systems** — For sheet/lifting of smooth surfaces

### E. Hybrid Drives
- **E1. Electro-Hydraulic** — Servo-valve controlled
- **E2. Electro-Mechanical** — Motor + gearbox integrated
- **E3. Hydro-Mechanical** — Torque converter + gear train

## 2.2 Operating Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### A. Mechanical Drives
- **Why?** Solid elements can transmit very high torque densities with predictable behaviour and are inherently reversible.
- **How?** Contact friction (belts, friction wheels), positive meshing (gears, chains, timing belts), or wrap friction (ropes on sheaves) transmit force.
- **Speed ratio** is set by the geometry of the elements (diameter or pitch).
- **Safety concern:** Catastrophic failure (belt whip, chain snap) — requires full enclosure guarding per **IS 4772 / OSHA 1910.219**.

### B. Electrical Drives
- **Why?** Clean, controllable, easy to reverse, easy to interlock, high efficiency ($\eta$ up to **95–97%** for modern VFD-controlled induction motors).
- **How?** Electromagnetic induction (AC) or Lorentz force (DC) converts electrical to mechanical energy.
- **Control hierarchy:** Motor → Contactor / VFD → PLC → HMI.
- **Safety concern:** Arc-flash, electric shock, regeneration over-voltage — requires **IP54 minimum enclosure, ELCB / RCCB, earthing per IS 3043**.

### C. Hydraulic Drives
- **Why?** Highest power density of any drive (up to **10×** that of equivalent electric motor), self-lubricating, smooth at low speeds, inherent overload protection via relief valve.
- **How?** Pascal's law — pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.
- **Safety concern:** High-pressure oil injection injuries (can penetrate skin at > 100 bar), hose whip, fire risk from oil leaks — requires **hoses rated 4× working pressure, whip-check cables, lockout/tagout (LOTO)**.

### D. Pneumatic Drives
- **Why?** Clean (no leak contamination), fast, simple, intrinsically safe in flammable atmospheres (no sparks), cheap.
- **How?** Compressed air expansion in a chamber does work.
- **Safety concern:** Hose whip, projectile risk from valve failure, noise (> 85 dB → hearing protection mandatory), freezing of moisture-laden air at expansion.
- **Limitation:** Cannot be precisely positioned without adding closed-loop control — air is compressible.

### E. Hybrid Drives
- **Why?** Combines precision of one medium with power density of another.
- **Example:** Servo-hydraulic press — electrical command signal drives a servo valve, which positions a high-power hydraulic ram. Used in modern injection moulding, forging, robotic lifts.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **minimum arsenal** required to solve any numerical KTU problem on Types of Drives. All are dimensionally consistent (SI units).

**Table 2.1 — Universal Power, Torque, Speed Relationships**

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P$ | Power transmitted | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | W (watt) |
| $P$ | Power in rotational motion | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W |
| $T$ | Torque | $T = \dfrac{P}{\omega} = \dfrac{60\,P}{2\pi N}$ | N·m |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | $N = \dfrac{60\,f}{p}$ (synchronous) | rpm |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s |
| $i$ | Speed ratio (gear/belt) | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | — |

**Table 2.2 — Mechanical Drive Equations**

| Drive | Key Equation | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| Belt drive length (open) | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(D_1 + D_2) + \dfrac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$ | $C$ = centre distance, $D$ = pulley dia. |
| Belt tension ratio | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ (capstan eq.) | $\mu$ = friction coeff., $\theta$ = wrap angle |
| Chain pitch | $p = \dfrac{\pi D}{N}$ | $D$ = sprocket pitch dia., $N$ = teeth |
| Gear train ratio | $i_{total} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{N_{d_k}}{N_{dr_k}}$ | $d$ = driven, $dr$ = driver |
| Wire rope factor of safety | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate tensile strength}}{\text{Working load}}$ | Typical FoS for hoists $= 3.5\text{ to } 5$ |

**Table 2.3 — Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic Drive Equations**

| System | Key Equation | Variables |
|---|---|---|
| Hydraulic power | $P = p \cdot Q = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600}$ (kW if $p$ in bar, $Q$ in L/min) | $p$ = pressure, $Q$ = flow rate |
| Hydraulic cylinder force | $F = p \cdot A = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $D$ = piston dia. |
| Hydraulic cylinder velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $Q$ = flow, $A$ = piston area |
| Pneumatic force | $F = p \cdot A \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency ($\approx 0.8$) |
| AC induction motor speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{p}$ | $f$ = freq., $p$ = no. of poles |
| Motor slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | $N_r$ = rotor speed, $N_s$ = synchronous |
| Drive efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | — |

**Table 2.4 — Hoisting / Lifting Drive Equations (Critical for Safety Calculations)**

| Quantity | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Hoist drum torque | $T = (W - m a) \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | $W$ = load, $a$ = accel., $D$ = drum dia. |
| Hoist motor power (lifting) | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $v$ = lift velocity, $\eta$ = overall efficiency |
| Braking torque needed | $T_b = (W + m a) \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot k$ | $k$ = safety factor ($\geq 1.5$) |
| Crane stability check | $M_{overturning} < M_{restoring}$ | Must include dynamic effects |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Domains

- **Cranes & EOTs (Electric Overhead Travelling):** Hybrid drives — squirrel-cage induction motor (long travel) + slip-ring induction with resistance control (hoist) + electric disc brake.
- **Mobile Cranes:** Diesel engine (prime mover) + torque converter (hydraulic-mechanical) + multiple gear stages + hydraulic outrigger cylinders.
- **Conveyors:** Squirrel-cage induction motor + helical-bevel gearbox + V-belt or chain coupling to drum.
- **Forklifts:** Lead-acid battery + DC series motor (historically) / AC induction + MOSFET inverter (modern).
- **Hydraulic Presses:** Servo-hydraulic hybrid — electrical control, hydraulic power.
- **Pneumatic Conveyors (food/pharma):** Compressed air + venturi ejectors — intrinsically safe, oil-free.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I — Speed and Torque Relationships in a Belt Drive

**Given:** A flat belt drive connects a driving pulley of diameter $D_1$ rotating at $N_1$ rpm to a driven pulley of diameter $D_2$ rotating at $N_2$ rpm. Belt thickness is $t$ and there is no slip.

**Assumption:** Linear belt speed is identical on both pulleys (no slip condition).

### Step 1: Write the linear belt speed at each pulley

The linear speed at the surface of the driving pulley is:

$$
v_1 = \pi D_1 N_1 / 60 \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

The linear speed at the surface of the driven pulley is:

$$
v_2 = \pi D_2 N_2 / 60 \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the no-slip condition

Since the belt does not slip, the surface speed of both pulleys is equal:

$$
v_1 = v_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\pi D_1 N_1 / 60 = \pi D_2 N_2 / 60
$$

### Step 3: Cancel common factors $\pi$ and $60$

The $\pi$ and $60$ terms cancel on both sides:

$$
D_1 N_1 = D_2 N_2
$$

### Step 4: Solve for the speed ratio $i$

Rearranging for the ratio of driven to driver speed:

$$
i = N_2 / N_1 = D_1 / D_2
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The smaller pulley rotates *faster* (driver), and the larger pulley rotates *slower* (driven). Speed ratio is the inverse of the diameter ratio.

### Step 5: Torque transmission

The tangential force on the pulley rim is $F_t$ (the net belt tension difference, $F_t = T_1 - T_2$, where $T_1$ is the tight side and $T_2$ the slack side). The torque on the driver and driven pulleys are:

$$
T_{driver} = F_t \cdot (D_1 / 2) \quad \text{and} \quad T_{driven} = F_t \cdot (D_2 / 2)
$$

The torque ratio is therefore:

$$
T_{driven} / T_{driver} = D_2 / D_1 = N_1 / N_2 = 1/i
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** A belt drive that *reduces* speed ($i < 1$) *increases* torque proportionally. This is the same law that applies to all gear-based mechanical transmissions.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Hydraulic Cylinder Lifting Force and Motor Power

**Given:** A hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D_p = 100\ \text{mm}$ lifts a load $W = 5000\ \text{N}$ at a velocity $v = 0.1\ \text{m/s}$. System pressure is to be determined, and the motor power required.

### Step 1: Hydraulic force equation

The lifting force produced by a hydraulic cylinder is:

$$
F = p \cdot A_p
$$

where $A_p$ is the piston area. With $D_p = 100\ \text{mm} = 0.1\ \text{m}$:

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi D_p^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

### Step 2: Solve for the required pressure

Setting $F = W$ for a stationary hold (no acceleration), the pressure is:

$$
p = \dfrac{W}{A_p} = \dfrac{5000}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.366 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}
$$

Converting to bar (1 bar = $10^5$ Pa):

$$
p \approx 6.37\ \text{bar}
$$

> **Note:** In practice, a pressure of 8–10 bar would be specified to provide a margin for dynamic loads and to overcome friction.

### Step 3: Required flow rate

Flow rate to achieve the lift velocity:

$$
Q = A_p \cdot v = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 7.854 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Converting to litres per minute (1 m³/s = 60 000 L/min):

$$
Q = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \times 60\,000 = 47.12\ \text{L/min}
$$

### Step 4: Required hydraulic power

The theoretical hydraulic power is:

$$
P_{th} = p \cdot Q = 6.366 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-4} = 500\ \text{W}
$$

This equals $W \cdot v$ as expected (work rate of lifting) — a useful consistency check.

### Step 5: Motor power considering overall efficiency

Assuming overall efficiency $\eta = 0.7$ (typical for a hydraulic system including pump, motor, valves, and cylinder):

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{th}}{\eta} = \dfrac{500}{0.7} \approx 714\ \text{W} \approx 0.95\ \text{HP}
$$

> **Practical selection:** A standard $1\ \text{HP}$ (0.75 kW) electric motor with a 4-pole ($N_s = 1500$ rpm) induction rating would be selected, coupled to a gear pump of ~14 cc/rev displacement.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Crane Hoist Motor Power and Drum Torque

**Given:** A hoist must lift a maximum load of $W = 20\ \text{kN}$ at a velocity $v = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$. The drum diameter is $D = 0.4\ \text{m}$. Assume overall efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, and a dynamic factor of 1.25.

### Step 1: Useful power output (lifting)

$$
P_{out} = W \cdot v = 20\,000 \times 0.5 = 10\,000\ \text{W} = 10\ \text{kW}
$$

### Step 2: Motor input power

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{10}{0.85} \approx 11.76\ \text{kW}
$$

### Step 3: Drum torque (static + dynamic component)

For a vertically lifting load with a small acceleration allowance, the effective load is $W_{eff} = W \times 1.25 = 25\ \text{kN}$. The torque on the drum is:

$$
T_{drum} = W_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = 25\,000 \times \dfrac{0.4}{2} = 5\,000\ \text{N·m}
$$

### Step 4: Motor speed required

The drum peripheral speed must equal the hoist rope speed $v$:

$$
N_{drum} = \dfrac{60\,v}{\pi D} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.5}{\pi \times 0.4} \approx 23.87\ \text{rpm}
$$

### Step 5: Required gearbox ratio

Assuming a 4-pole motor ($N_s = 1500$ rpm), and accounting for slip ($s \approx 4\%$, so $N_r \approx 1440$ rpm):

$$
i_{gearbox} = \dfrac{N_{motor}}{N_{drum}} = \dfrac{1440}{23.87} \approx 60.3
$$

A two-stage helical or worm-gear reducer with ratio 60 would be selected.

> **Safety check:** The motor must also develop the *holding* torque against gravity when the brake is released — and the brake itself must be capable of holding $1.5 \times W$ dynamically.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram of Drives

The following Mermaid block provides the complete functional taxonomy of drives in material handling.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DRIVES IN MATERIAL HANDLING] --> B[MECHANICAL DRIVE]
    A --> C[ELECTRICAL DRIVE]
    A --> D[HYDRAULIC DRIVE]
    A --> E[PNEUMATIC DRIVE]
    A --> F[HYBRID DRIVE]

    B --> B1[BELT DRIVE]
    B --> B2[CHAIN DRIVE]
    B --> B3[GEAR DRIVE]
    B --> B4[ROPE DRIVE]
    B --> B5[FRICTION DRIVE]

    B1 --> B1A[FLAT BELT]
    B1 --> B1B[V BELT]
    B1 --> B1C[TIMING BELT]
    B3 --> B3A[SPUR]
    B3 --> B3B[HELICAL]
    B3 --> B3C[BEVEL]
    B3 --> B3D[WORM]

    C --> C1[AC MOTOR]
    C --> C2[DC MOTOR]
    C --> C3[SPECIAL MOTOR]
    C1 --> C1A[INDUCTION]
    C1 --> C1B[SYNCHRONOUS]
    C2 --> C2A[SHUNT]
    C2 --> C2B[SERIES]
    C2 --> C2C[COMPOUND]
    C3 --> C3A[SERVO]
    C3 --> C3B[STEPPER]

    D --> D1[PUMP MOTOR PAIR]
    D --> D2[HYDRAULIC CYLINDER]
    D --> D3[HYDROSTATIC TRANSMISSION]

    E --> E1[AIR CYLINDER]
    E --> E2[PNEUMATIC MOTOR]
    E --> E3[VACUUM LIFT]

    F --> F1[ELECTRO HYDRAULIC]
    F --> F2[ELECTRO MECHANICAL]
    F --> F3[HYDRO MECHANICAL]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of an Electric Hoist Drive

The following block diagram maps the **functional flow** of a typical electric wire-rope hoist, the most common material handling drive combination.

```mermaid
graph LR
    PWR[3 PHASE 415 V SUPPLY] --> MCB[MCCB / CIRCUIT BREAKER]
    MCB --> CTR[CONTACTOR AND OVERLOAD RELAY]
    CTR --> VFD[VARIABLE FREQUENCY DRIVE]
    VFD --> MTR[3 PHASE INDUCTION MOTOR 4 POLE]
    MTR --> BRK[FAIL SAFE DISC BRAKE]
    MTR --> GBX[HELICAL GEAR REDUCER]
    GBX --> DRM[HOIST DRUM]
    DRM --> ROP[WIRE ROPE]
    ROP --> HK[HOOK BLOCK]
    HK --> LOAD[LOAD BEING LIFTED]
    MTR -.-> SENSOR[ENCODER FEEDBACK]
    SENSOR -.-> VFD
    CTR -.-> LMT1[UPPER LIMIT SWITCH]
    CTR -.-> LMT2[LOWER LIMIT SWITCH]
    CTR -.-> OVL[OVERLOAD LIMITER]
```

## 4.3 Hydraulic Drive Architecture for a Lift Table

```mermaid
graph TD
    EM[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> PUMP[GEAR PUMP]
    PUMP --> RSV[RELIEF VALVE SET 200 BAR]
    PUMP --> DCV[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE 4 3]
    DCV --> CYL[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    CYL --> TABLE[SCISSOR TABLE]
    CYL -.-> PI[PRESSURE GAUGE]
    DCV -.-> FLV[FLOW CONTROL VALVE]
    RSV -.-> TANK[OIL TANK]
    DCV -.-> TANK
    PUMP -.-> SUC[STRAINER AND SUCTION LINE]
    SUC -.-> TANK
    DCV -.-> LVDT[DISPLACEMENT SENSOR]
    LVDT -.-> SOL[DCV SOLENOID CONTROLLER]
```

## 4.4 Safety Interlock Hierarchy in a Modern Drive System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SAFETY INTEGRITY] --> B[LEVEL 1 HARDWARE]
    A --> C[LEVEL 2 CONTROL]
    A --> D[LEVEL 3 SUPERVISORY]
    B --> B1[EMERGENCY STOP BUTTON HARD WIRED]
    B --> B2[GUARD INTERLOCK SWITCH]
    B --> B3[MECHANICAL LIMIT STOPS]
    C --> C1[SPEED MONITORING RELAY]
    C --> C2[OVERCURRENT PROTECTION]
    C --> C3[OVERLOAD SENSOR]
    C --> C4[BRAKE WEAR MONITOR]
    D --> D1[PLC SAFETY LOGIC]
    D --> D2[SCADA ALARM]
    D --> D3[DATA LOGGER]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a 'Drive' in the context of material handling equipment. List any four major classifications of drives. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **drive** is an integrated power transmission system that receives energy from a prime mover and delivers it in a controlled form (torque, speed, direction) to the working element of material handling equipment such as a conveyor, hoist, crane, or lift.

Four major classifications of drives used in material handling are:

1. **Mechanical drives** (belt, chain, gear, rope, friction)
2. **Electrical drives** (AC, DC, special motors)
3. **Hydraulic drives** (pump-motor, cylinder, hydrostatic)
4. **Pneumatic drives** (air cylinder, pneumatic motor, vacuum)

> *(A fifth category — hybrid drives — is also widely accepted in modern texts.)*

---

### Q2. State any three safety hazards specifically associated with chain drives. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Three safety hazards specific to chain drives are:

1. **Catastrophic chain whip and snap** — A broken chain end can lash out at high velocity (up to 60 m/s) and cause fatal injuries. Requires full chain enclosure per **IS 4772**.
2. **Pinch-point injuries** — Sprocket mesh points can sever fingers or catch loose clothing.
3. **Elongation and fatigue failure** — Chain stretches with use; ignoring wear leads to jumping sprocket teeth and sudden release of stored energy.

> *(Additional marks can be awarded for mentioning lubrication failures, overloading, and misalignment.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** Each Part B question in Module 4 is internally split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students may attempt **either** the full Q-A or the full Q-B but not mix between them.

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Compare mechanical, electrical, hydraulic, and pneumatic drives in a material handling context. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Mechanical | Electrical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Power density** | Medium | High | Very high | Low–medium |
| **Speed control** | Stepped (gears) | Smooth (VFD) | Smooth (throttle/servo) | Limited (on-off) |
| **Overload protection** | Shear pin / slip | Overload relay | Relief valve | Pressure regulator |
| **Efficiency** | 85–95% | 90–97% | 60–75% | 30–50% |
| **Fire/explosion risk** | Low | High (arc) | Medium (oil) | Low (clean) |
| **Noise** | High (gears) | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Precision positioning** | Poor (backlash) | Excellent | Excellent (servo) | Poor |
| **Self-lubrication** | No (needs grease) | Yes (bearings) | Yes (oil bath) | N/A |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent (wear) | Lowest | Moderate (filters) | Moderate (filters) |
| **Best application** | Constant speed | Variable speed | High force | Light, fast, clean |

**Conclusion:** No single drive is universally best; selection is based on the application envelope. For example, an EOT crane hoist uses a **hybrid** approach — electrical for power, mechanical gearbox for reduction, and electrical/mechanical disc brake for safety.

#### (b) A wire-rope hoist is to lift a load of 15 kN at a speed of 0.4 m/s. The hoist drum diameter is 350 mm. Compute (i) the useful power output, (ii) the motor power assuming an overall efficiency of 80%, and (iii) the drum torque. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**(i) Useful power output:**

$$
P_{out} = W \cdot v = 15\,000 \times 0.4 = 6\,000\ \text{W} = 6\ \text{kW}
$$

**(ii) Motor input power (assuming $\eta = 0.8$):**

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{6\,000}{0.8} = 7\,500\ \text{W} = 7.5\ \text{kW}
$$

> A standard **7.5 kW (10 HP)** squirrel-cage induction motor with 4 poles would be the most common selection.

**(iii) Drum torque (static, with dynamic factor 1.25 for safety):**

$$
W_{eff} = 1.25 \times 15\,000 = 18\,750\ \text{N}
$$

$$
T_{drum} = W_{eff} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} = 18\,750 \times \dfrac{0.35}{2} = 3\,281.25\ \text{N·m} \approx 3.28\ \text{kN·m}
$$

> **Examiner's tip:** Many students forget the dynamic factor. The rated static torque is $T = 15\,000 \times 0.35/2 = 2\,625\ \text{N·m}$, but a $25\%$ overload margin is mandatory for any material handling drive per **IS 3177**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the working principle of a hydraulic drive system. With the help of a sketch, describe the components of a typical hydraulic circuit used in a material handling application. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

A hydraulic drive works on **Pascal's Law**: *Pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in every direction.* The system consists of:

- An **electric motor** drives a **hydraulic pump**.
- The pump draws oil from a **reservoir** through a **strainer/filter**.
- Oil is pressurised and delivered through **hoses/piping** to a **directional control valve (DCV)**.
- The DCV routes oil to a **hydraulic cylinder or motor**, which produces linear or rotary motion.
- A **relief valve** protects the system from over-pressure.
- **Flow control valves** regulate speed.
- **Check valves** prevent backflow.
- Return oil is filtered and sent back to the tank.

**Key components (refer to the Mermaid block 4.3 above for the architecture):**

1. Prime mover (electric motor)
2. Hydraulic pump (gear/vane/piston)
3. Reservoir (tank) with breather
4. Strainer (suction side) and filter (return side)
5. Relief valve (pressure safety)
6. Directional control valve (4/3 manually or solenoid operated)
7. Hydraulic cylinder (double-acting, with piston, rod, seals)
8. Flow control valve (for speed regulation)
9. Pressure gauge
10. Hose and fittings (rated for 4× working pressure, with whip-check cables)

**Safety features:** Pressure relief, hose burst protection, emergency stop, mechanical lock-out of cylinder (for maintenance), and oil-level monitoring.

#### (b) A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 40 mm. The system pressure is 120 bar. Calculate (i) the extending force, (ii) the retracting force, and (iii) the ratio of the two. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Piston dia $D = 80\ \text{mm} = 0.08\ \text{m}$
- Rod dia $d = 40\ \text{mm} = 0.04\ \text{m}$
- Pressure $p = 120\ \text{bar} = 120 \times 10^5\ \text{Pa} = 12\ \text{MPa}$

**Step 1: Piston full area (extending):**

$$
A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Annular area (retracting):**

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi ((0.08)^2 - (0.04)^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.0016)}{4}
$$

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.0048}{4} = 3.770 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Extending force (assuming 100% efficiency for the force calculation):**

$$
F_{ext} = p \cdot A_1 = 12 \times 10^6 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 60\,320\ \text{N} \approx 60.3\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4: Retracting force:**

$$
F_{ret} = p \cdot A_2 = 12 \times 10^6 \times 3.770 \times 10^{-3} = 45\,240\ \text{N} \approx 45.2\ \text{kN}
$$

**Step 5: Ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \dfrac{5.027 \times 10^{-3}}{3.770 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.333
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The cylinder is **33% stronger in extension** than in retraction — a critical design consideration for asymmetric loads (e.g., a scissor lift platform with a motor on one side).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and How Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the dynamic factor in hoist/hoisting calculations.** Always apply a $1.25$ to $1.5$ factor for acceleration, wind, and shock loads. *Loss: 1–2 marks per part.*
> 2. **Mixing up units in hydraulic power formula.** $P\ (\text{kW}) = \dfrac{p\ (\text{bar}) \times Q\ (\text{L/min})}{600}$. A student who writes $P = p \cdot Q$ without the **600** divisor (or in wrong units) loses full marks. *Loss: 2–3 marks.*
> 3. **Not stating efficiency assumptions.** Numerical answers without a stated $\eta$ are incomplete. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Confusing $N_s = 120f/p$ (synchronous) with $N_r = N_s(1-s)$ (rotor).** In an induction motor problem, this distinction is critical. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 5. **Forgetting safety devices.** A diagram of any drive system without an **emergency stop, overload protection, and limit switch** will be marked incomplete in the "safety in material handling" course. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Writing "hydraulic drive = high pressure = good" without quantitative backing.** Examiners expect a numerical example or formula justification.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Drive = Prime Mover + Transmission + Control + Safety**. Memorise this 4-block structure for any drive question.
- **Five drive families:** Mechanical, Electrical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Hybrid. Be ready to give one merit, one demerit, and one application of each.
- **Power equation is universal:** $P = T \omega = 2\pi N T / 60$. Always work in SI units.
- **Belt/chain drive ratio:** $i = N_1/N_2 = D_2/D_1$ (no slip). Torque ratio is the inverse.
- **Hydraulic force:** $F = p \times A$; **Power:** $P = p \times Q$ (watch the $600$ divisor for kW/bar/L·min⁻¹).
- **Pneumatic force:** $F = p \times A \times \eta$. Pneumatic drives are **not** self-locking — they need a brake or spring return.
- **AC induction motor synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / p$. Rotor runs at $N_r = N_s(1-s)$, where $s = 2$–$5\%$ typical.
- **Wire rope factor of safety (FoS)** for hoists = **3.5 to 5**. For lifts (human carriage) = **8 to 12**.
- **Hydraulic hose rating** = 4× working pressure minimum. Always specify **whip-check cables** on all flexible hose connections.
- **Emergency stop (E-stop)** must be **hard-wired** (not software-based) for SIL-2/3 compliance.
- **Guarding requirement (IS 4772 / OSHA 1910.219):** All rotating shafts, belts, chains, gears, and couplings within **2.1 m of floor** must be fully guarded.
- **Brake selection rule:** The brake must be capable of holding **at least 1.5× the rated load** dynamically and **1.25× statically**.
- **Always mention 3 safety devices** in any diagram: Emergency stop, Overload protector, Limit switch.
- **Hybrid drives dominate modern MH equipment** — expect a question on servo-hydraulic or VFD-driven systems.
- **For numerical problems:** state given data, draw a free-body or schematic, write the governing equation, substitute, box the answer with units.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** The "Safety in Material Handling" paper specifically tests your ability to *link* a drive selection to a **safety implication**. A standalone mechanical answer without safety commentary will score 60–70%. A safety-integrated answer will score 85–100%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hoist Way and Machine Room Emergency Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hoist Way and Machine Room Emergency Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Hoist Way and Machine Room Emergency Procedure

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (OESFT833 / Module 4):** This module deals with the safe design, layout, and emergency operating procedures associated with the **hoist way (lift well)**, the **machine room**, and the **pit** of vertical material/people handling systems such as lifts, elevators, freight hoists, and dumb waiters. It is the single most important module from an accident-prevention standpoint.

### 1.1 Hoist Way — Formal Definition

The **hoist way** (also called the **lift well**, **shaft**, or **hoistway**) is the totally enclosed, fire-resistant vertical passage inside a building in which the elevator/lift car, counterweight, guide rails, buffers, and traveling cables move. It extends from the floor of the **pit** at the bottom to the **overhead** (machine-room floor slab or roof slab) at the top.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):**
> *“A hoist way is a vertical, enclosed runway that provides a safe, fire-rated, and structurally isolated corridor for the movement of an elevator car, counterweight, and associated suspension means between the lowest and highest landing levels of a building.”*

**Key physical parameters** of a typical hoist way (per IS 14665 / NBC 2016):
- **Minimum clear internal depth:** 1.8 m for passenger lifts
- **Minimum clear internal width:** 1.5 m for passenger lifts
- **Ventilation opening:** minimum **0.3 m²** per hoist way
- **Fire resistance rating:** **2 hours** (4 hours if > 24 m building height)
- **Pit depth:** minimum **1.4 m** (1.5 m for speed > 1.0 m/s)
- **Headroom (overhead clearance):** minimum **4.5 m** above top landing

### 1.2 Machine Room — Formal Definition

The **machine room** is a dedicated, separate, lockable room (or enclosure) at the top of the hoist way that houses the **traction machine (motor + sheave/gear)**, the **hoist motor controller**, the **drive mechanism**, the **governor**, the **over-speed detection devices**, the **main electrical switchgear**, and sometimes the **floor-level selector** and the **emergency rescue panel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):**
> *“A machine room is an enclosed, ventilated, illuminated, and fire-separated space that accommodates the driving machinery, the controller, and the associated safety components of a lift, located either directly above the hoist way, below the hoist way (basement), or in a side-adjacent enclosure as per MRL (Machine-Room-Less) modern architecture.”*

**Mandatory characteristics of a machine room:**
- **Floor area:** at least the projected area of the machine + **0.5 m** working space on every side
- **Floor load-bearing capacity:** minimum **5 kN/m²**
- **Headroom inside:** minimum **2.2 m** clear
- **Ambient temperature:** maintained between **+5 °C and +40 °C**
- **Access:** only through a **self-closing, self-locking, fire-rated door** (1-hour fire rating)
- **Ventilation:** minimum **6 air changes per hour** to dissipate motor heat

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — “Building the Vertical Highway”

Imagine the **hoist way as a tall, vertical highway tunnel** that runs through a building. The **lift car is the “vehicle,”** the **ropes are the “cables pulling the vehicle,”** the **guide rails are the “lane markers,”** the **buffers at the bottom are the “crumple zones,”** and the **machine room on top is the “engine control cabin”** of this highway.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of the famous **Burj Khalifa elevator system**. A 57-storey vertical tunnel, 3.3 m wide, with a dedicated temperature-controlled control room on the 165th floor — that is a hoist way + machine room in action. Every emergency procedure you study here is designed so that if *anything* goes wrong in that tunnel, the occupants can be rescued without the firefighter having to “free-climb” the shaft.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this module is critical in OESFT833:** Approximately **60% of all material-handling fatalities** (globally, per ILO statistics) occur in or around vertical handling equipment. A student who masters this module can directly contribute to safer design and rescue planning.

---

### 1.4 Emergency Procedure — Formal Definition

A **hoist way / machine room emergency procedure** is the **pre-planned, documented, and rehearsed set of actions** to be executed by trained personnel (lift mechanics, fire service, building emergency team) when the lift car is immobilised between floors due to:
- Power failure (most common, ~70% of all entrapments)
- Over-speed / governor tripping
- Rope / belt failure
- Door interlock failure (door opens when car is not at floor)
- Fire in the building
- Earthquake / seismic event
- Flooding of the pit
- Mechanical seizure of the traction sheave

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Lift Rescue (NFPA-101, IS 14665):**
> ***“Persons shall be released from a stalled lift car before the car is moved to the nearest landing, except where the car is in the landing zone and the doors can be opened directly.”***
> In simple words: **DO NOT move the car with passengers inside. First stabilise, then rescue from the safe landing zone.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Hoist Way — Sub-systems

The hoist way is **not just a hole in the building**. It is a multi-component engineered system. Each sub-system has a specific safety role.

**1. Structural Shell (Shaft Walls)**
- Built of **reinforced concrete (min. 150 mm thick)** or brick masonry (230 mm).
- Must resist **2-hour fire** (4-hour for high-rise).
- Smooth, plumb (verticality tolerance **± 25 mm over full height**).
- No external openings except **ventilation louvres** and **access doors**.

**2. Guide Rails (Car & Counterweight)**
- Cold-drawn steel **T-section rails**, typically 8–16 kg/m weight.
- **Car guide length = full travel + buffer stroke + 0.3 m.**
- Function: keep the car from swinging, tilting, or rotating.

**3. Buffers (Energy-Absorbing Devices)**
- **Spring buffers:** for speeds ≤ 1.0 m/s.
- **Oil (hydraulic) buffers:** for speeds > 1.0 m/s; stroke = **0.067 v²** (where v is in m/s).
- Positioned at pit floor below car and counterweight.

**4. Pit**
- **Pit depth** must accommodate: buffer fully compressed + 0.5 m working space.
- **Pit stop switch (red)**: cuts power when pit accessed.
- **Ladder** for pit access from lowest landing door.
- **Drainage sump** to prevent flooding.

**5. Overhead / Headroom**
- Minimum clearance for counterweight runby and car runby.
- **Car runby** = top floor to car roof striking device = **min 1.0 m + 0.035 v².**
- **Counterweight runby** = top floor to counterweight striking device.

**6. Travelling Cables**
- Carry electrical signals and power to the car.
- **Length = travel distance + 5 m** slack for car movement + 2 m service loop in pit.

**7. Ventilation**
- **0.3 m² per shaft** free opening at top OR mechanical extraction.
- Prevents heat build-up from car machinery (MRL) and smoke accumulation.

**8. Fire-Floor Signalling**
- For buildings > 24 m: each landing must have a **fireman’s switch** (red, break-glass type).

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Machine Room — Sub-systems

**1. Traction Machine**
- **Geared (worm-gear)**: 0.5–2.5 m/s, low-rise buildings.
- **Gearless (permanent-magnet synchronous)**: 2.5–10 m/s, high-rise.

**2. Sheave / Pulley**
- Diameter ≥ **40 × rope diameter** (traction sheave).
- Groove angle matched to rope (typically **35° – 40°**).

**3. Governor**
- Mechanical centrifugal device that triggers the **safety gear** on the car if the car speed exceeds **115% of rated speed**.

**4. Controller**
- Modern: **VVVF (Variable Voltage Variable Frequency)** drive.
- Houses relays, contactors, PCBs.

**5. Over-Speed Detection & Final Limit Switches**
- **Final limits** in hoist way cut power at both extreme ends.

**6. Emergency Power Supply (UPS / DG backup)**
- **Minimum 5 lux** illumination in car during power failure.
- Auto-dialler / intercom to manned location.

**7. Fireman’s Lift Switch (Phase II)**
- Returns the lift to the **designated evacuation floor** (usually ground or 1st).

### 2.3 Emergency Scenarios & Logical Response Steps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following step-by-step logic is the **most scoring content** for KTU 14-mark questions. Memorise the *order* of actions.

**Scenario A — Loss of Mains Power (Most Common, ~70%)**

| Step | Action | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Confirm power failure (check mains, DG, UPS) | Differentiate from internal fault |
| 2 | Establish communication with trapped occupants (intercom / phone) | Calm them, gather medical info |
| 3 | Locate the exact floor position of the car (via floor indicator in MR) | Plan rescue route |
| 4 | Switch controller to **Emergency Power Mode** | Resumes control if ARD (Automatic Rescue Device) fitted |
| 5 | If ARD fitted: it auto-creeps the car to **nearest landing** at **< 0.3 m/s** | Safest, fastest |
| 6 | If no ARD: trained mechanic uses **manual winding** (hand-winch) on the traction sheave | Slow, but safe |
| 7 | Open car door using **emergency door-release key** in landing door | Only at floor-level position |
| 8 | Evacuate passengers | Through landing door, NOT through car top hatch |
| 9 | Lock-out the car electrically (main switch OFF, brakes applied) | Prevent unexpected movement |
| 10 | Log the incident and inform maintenance company | Statutory requirement |

**Scenario B — Fire in the Building**

| Step | Action | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | **Fireman’s switch (Phase I)** activated at ground floor | Returns car to **evacuation floor** automatically |
| 2 | Doors open at evacuation floor and stay open for **30 s**, then close | Allows trapped people to escape |
| 3 | Lift then parks with doors closed and is **out of service** | Prevents firemen being called to wrong lift |
| 4 | Fire service uses **separate fireman’s lift** (Phase II switch) | This lift is dedicated for fire service use |
| 5 | Phase II lift has **fireman’s controls inside car** (single-button per floor) | Allows one-handed operation wearing gloves |
| 6 | On lobby, fireman can override other lifts | Centralised control |

**Scenario C — Car Immobilised Between Floors (Doors Closed)**

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Establish voice contact (intercom) |
| 2 | Reassure occupants (max anxiety peaks at 20 min) |
| 3 | Authorised mechanic enters **from top landing** (climbs car roof via top-hatch or pit ladder) |
| 4 | Manually energise the **brake-release lever** on the machine |
| 5 | Use the **hand-wind facility** (crank/wheel) to move car to nearest landing **at creep speed < 0.3 m/s** |
| 6 | Stop when car sill is **level with the floor** (door zone) |
| 7 | Apply parking brake, lock-out main switch |
| 8 | Open landing door with **emergency release key** (triangular / square) |
| 9 | Open car door manually, evacuate occupants |
| 10 | Lock landing door, post **“OUT OF SERVICE”** notice |

**Scenario D — Door Opens When Car Is Not at Floor (Door-Zone Failure)**

This is the **most dangerous** lift accident. If the interlock fails, a person could fall into the shaft.

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | **STOP** all rescue attempts involving shaft entry. |
| 2 | Establish voice contact. Tell occupants to **sit on car floor, back to door, do not move.** |
| 3 | Use **emergency ventilation**, light, and water if heat stress. |
| 4 | Wait for **trained rescue team with full body harness & rope access** if vertical gap is > 1.5 m. |
| 5 | Rescue from **above** (top hatch) using **rope-and-harness transfer** by trained personnel. |
| 6 | If car is just **< 1.0 m below floor**, trained person can build a step using a ladder and escort occupants out. |
| 7 | Door is **NOT closed mechanically** during the rescue — keep it propped and guarded. |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER** attempt to “pull” occupants out through a door that is open in mid-shaft. Falls from even 2 m can be fatal. Always use the **top-hatch / rope rescue** protocol.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> These formulas are the most commonly tested numericals in KTU ESE for OESFT833. Memorise the *units* and *boundary conditions* alongside the formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Buffer stroke} \; S_{buf} &= 0.067 \cdot v^2 \quad [\text{m}, \; v \text{ in m/s}] \\
\text{Car runby} \; R_{car} &= 1.0 + 0.035 \cdot v^2 \quad [\text{m}] \\
\text{Counterweight runby} \; R_{cw} &= 1.0 + 0.035 \cdot v^2 \quad [\text{m}] \\
\text{Governor trip speed} \; v_{gov} &= 1.15 \cdot v_{rated} \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
\text{Safety gear trip speed} \; v_{sg} &= 1.20 \cdot v_{rated} \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
\text{Pit depth} \; D_{pit} &= h_{buf} + 0.5 \quad [\text{m}] \\
\text{Headroom (top)} \; H_{top} &= R_{car} + 1.0 \quad [\text{m}] \\
\text{Vent area} \; A_{vent} &\geq 0.30 \quad [\text{m}^2 \text{ per shaft}] \\
\text{Machine room temp rise} \; \Delta T &= \frac{P_{motor}}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p} \quad [\text{K}] \\
\text{Emergency creep speed} \; v_{rescue} &\leq 0.30 \quad [\text{m/s}] \\
\text{Time to wind manually} \; t &= \frac{\text{Travel distance}}{0.15} \quad [\text{s}]
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Range (Passenger Lift) | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|--------|------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| Rated speed | v | m/s | 0.5 – 2.5 | v > 1.0 → oil buffer mandatory |
| Governor trip speed | v_gov | m/s | 1.15 v | Cannot exceed 1.40 v_escape_velocity |
| Buffer stroke (oil) | S_buf | m | 0.067 v² | Min 0.15 m, max 1.20 m |
| Car runby | R_car | m | 1.0 + 0.035 v² | Min 1.8 m |
| Pit depth | D_pit | m | 1.4 – 2.5 | Plus buffer stroke if spring |
| Overhead clearance | H_top | m | 4.5 – 6.0 | Plus counterweight runby |
| Vent area | A_vent | m² | 0.3 – 0.5 | Per hoist way |
| Machine-room area | A_mr | m² | 10 – 40 | Min 2 × car footprint |
| Working load | P | kN | 4 – 16 kN (4–16 persons) | 0.75 kN/person × 75 kg |
| Traction sheave dia | D_sh | mm | 40 × rope dia | Min 320 mm |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Real World

- **Architects** use the buffer stroke and runby formulas to size the **floor-to-floor heights** of a building **before construction** (you cannot shorten a hoist way after the building is built).
- **Lift consultants** use the ventilation formula to design **smoke-control systems** integrated with the building HVAC.
- **Fire services** rely on the **fireman’s switch logic** to decide which lift is usable during a fire.
- **Insurance companies** audit these procedures to decide the **liability premium** for high-rise buildings.
- **Rescue teams** use the **door-zone failure protocol** to avoid becoming a second casualty.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are the **most frequently asked numericals** in KTU ESE. Practise them until the substitution step is automatic.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Oil Buffer Stroke for a Given Rated Speed

**Given:** A passenger lift has rated speed **v = 2.5 m/s**. Determine the **oil buffer stroke** required and verify compliance with IS 14665.

**Solution:**

The kinetic energy of the fully-loaded car (plus counterweight imbalance) at rated speed must be **fully absorbed** by the buffer when the car strikes it from free-fall at governor-trip speed.

**Step 1 — Energy of the moving system at impact**

$$
E_{kin} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m_{eq} \cdot v_{gov}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed by oil buffer**

An oil buffer is essentially a **dashpot**. The pressure developed in the oil forces fluid through an orifice, dissipating energy. The standard IS 14665 empirical formula (derived from energy balance with a typical orifice coefficient) gives:

$$
S_{buf} = 0.067 \cdot v_{rated}^{2} \quad [\text{m}]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute v = 2.5 m/s**

$$
S_{buf} = 0.067 \times (2.5)^2
$$

$$
S_{buf} = 0.067 \times 6.25
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{buf} = 0.41875 \approx 0.42 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Check against boundary condition**

IS 14665 specifies the oil-buffer stroke must be:
- **≥ 0.15 m** (lower limit, to prevent rigid impact), and
- **≤ 1.20 m** (upper limit, to prevent oil-spill overflow).

**Conclusion:** 0.15 ≤ 0.42 ≤ 1.20 → **Design is compliant.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Pit Depth

**Given:** Spring buffer (only for v ≤ 1.0 m/s) has a maximum compression of **h_buf = 0.30 m**. Determine the **minimum pit depth** D_pit.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the IS 14665 formula**

$$
D_{pit} = h_{buf,\,max} + 0.5
$$

The 0.5 m is the **working space** for a person to stand crouched for inspection and rescue.

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
D_{pit} = 0.30 + 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{pit} = 0.80 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify against absolute minimum**

IS 14665 mandates an **absolute minimum pit depth = 1.40 m** even if the formula gives less (for human-entry safety and counterweight runby clearance).

**Conclusion:** Use **D_pit = 1.40 m** (the larger value governs).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Overhead Clearance (Headroom)

**Given:** A lift with rated speed v = 1.5 m/s. Find the total headroom above the top landing.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate car runby**

$$
R_{car} = 1.0 + 0.035 \cdot v^2
$$

$$
R_{car} = 1.0 + 0.035 \times (1.5)^2
$$

$$
R_{car} = 1.0 + 0.035 \times 2.25
$$

$$
R_{car} = 1.0 + 0.0788
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{car} = 1.0788 \approx 1.08 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Add safety clearance above car roof**

Per IS 14665, an additional **0.60 m minimum** clearance is required between car roof striking device and the overhead structure (so the mechanic standing on the car roof does not hit his head).

**Step 3 — Total headroom from top-finished-floor level**

$$
H_{top} = R_{car} + 0.60
$$

$$
H_{top} = 1.08 + 0.60
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{top} = 1.68 \text{ m}}
$$

**Step 4 — Check absolute minimum**

IS 14665 specifies **H_top ≥ 4.5 m** for any car with a roof capable of being stood upon (which is all modern lifts).

**Conclusion:** Use **H_top = 4.5 m** (the absolute minimum governs).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time Required for Manual Winding (Rescue)

**Given:** A lift car is stuck **6.0 m** below the nearest landing. The mechanic uses a hand-crank that moves the car at approximately **0.15 m/s** (half the ARD speed). Calculate the time to reach the landing.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Formula**

$$
t = \frac{\text{distance}}{v_{crank}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute**

$$
t = \frac{6.0}{0.15}
$$

$$
\boxed{t = 40 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Step 3 — Add communication pauses**

Per rescue SOP, allow for **2 voice-contact pauses × 30 s = 60 s** for reassurance and confirmation.

**Step 4 — Total rescue time**

$$
t_{total} = 40 + 60
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{total} \approx 100 \text{ seconds} \approx 1.67 \text{ min}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** The KTU examiner often uses this number to test the **human factor** — trapped occupants panic after 20 minutes, and the rescue team should ideally complete evacuation within **30 minutes** (European standard EN 81-20 soft target).

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ventilation Air-Change Calculation

**Given:** A machine room has volume V = 60 m³ (e.g., 5 m × 4 m × 3 m). The motor dissipates P = 4 kW continuously. The desired temperature rise ΔT must be ≤ 10 K above ambient. Air specific heat c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. Air density ρ = 1.2 kg/m³. Find the required air-flow rate (m³/h) and confirm it meets **6 air changes/h** minimum.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{P_{motor}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{4.0}{1.005 \times 10}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.398 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volume flow rate**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{0.398}{1.2}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 0.332 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per hour**

$$
\dot{V}_{h} = 0.332 \times 3600
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{V}_{h} = 1195 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}
$$

**Step 4 — Air changes per hour (ACH)**

$$
ACH = \frac{\dot{V}_{h}}{V_{room}}
$$

$$
ACH = \frac{1195}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{ACH \approx 19.9 \text{ per hour}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with minimum**

19.9 ≥ 6 → **Design is compliant.** The 6-ACH rule is automatically satisfied; the 10 K temperature rise is the governing design parameter here.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Rescue Methods

| Method | Speed | Skill Level | Equipment | Risk to Rescuer | Risk to Passenger |
|--------|-------|-------------|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| ARD (Automatic) | 0.3 m/s | Operator only | None extra | Very low | Very low |
| Manual hand-wind from machine room | 0.15 m/s | Trained mechanic | Crank/wheel | Low | Low |
| Pit-ladder rescue (only if car at bottom floor) | Static | Trained mechanic | Ladder | Low | Low |
| Top-hatch rope rescue (door-zone failure) | Static | Rope-access certified | Harness, rope | Medium | Medium |
| Hydraulic jack release (for hydraulic lifts) | Static | Trained mechanic | Jack | Low | Low |

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Emergency Devices on a Modern Lift

| Device | Location | Function | Activated When |
|--------|----------|----------|----------------|
| Governor | Machine room | Detects over-speed | v > 1.15 v_rated |
| Safety gear | On car | Clamps guide rail | Governor trips |
| Buffer | Pit | Absorbs impact | Car overruns downward |
| Final limit switch | Top & bottom of shaft | Cuts power | Car reaches extreme end |
| Emergency stop (in car) | Below COP | Cuts power | Passenger presses |
| Fireman’s switch (Phase I) | Ground landing | Returns car to lobby | Building fire alarm |
| Fireman’s switch (Phase II) | In designated fire lift | Allows one-button recall | Fire service arrives |
| ARD (Automatic Rescue Device) | Controller + battery | Drives car to nearest floor | Power failure |
| Intercom | Car ↔ machine room ↔ building office | Voice contact | Any time |
| Emergency lighting | Car roof | Min 5 lux for 1 h | Power failure |
| Battery-operated alarm bell | Car roof | Audible signal | Power failure + bell button |
| Pit stop switch | Pit wall | Cuts power when pit entered | Manual |
| Car-top stop switch | Car roof | Cuts power when roof stood upon | Manual |
| Emergency door key | Landing door (triangular) | Manual door opening | Rescue scenario |
| Two-way ventilation | Car roof vent | Air circulation | Any entrapment |
