## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Question, OESFT833]**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]** Define a *hoist way*. List any **four** mandatory design requirements of a hoist way as per IS 14665.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **hoist way** is a totally enclosed, fire-resistant vertical passage within a building that accommodates the elevator car, counterweight, guide rails, buffers, and travelling cables, extending from the pit floor to the overhead structure.

Four mandatory design requirements:

1. **Fire resistance** of 2 hours (4 hours for buildings above 24 m).
2. **Ventilation opening** of minimum 0.3 m² per shaft to dissipate heat and smoke.
3. **Pit depth** of minimum 1.4 m with a drainage sump and pit-stop switch.
4. **Overhead clearance** of minimum 4.5 m above the top-finished-floor level for car runby and counterweight runby.

*(Examiner’s note: 1 mark for definition, ½ mark × 4 for the four requirements.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Sample Question, OESFT833]** What is a **fireman’s lift**? Explain the function of the **Phase I and Phase II fireman’s switches**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **fireman’s lift** is a designated elevator in a high-rise building that is reserved exclusively for the use of the fire service during a fire emergency. It has enhanced fire-resistance, separate power supply, and is sized to carry a stretcher plus several firemen.

- **Phase I Switch (Recall Switch):** Located on the **ground-floor lobby**, when activated (often automatically by the building fire alarm), it causes **all standard lifts** to return to the designated evacuation floor, open their doors for 30 seconds, then park with doors closed and go out of service. This frees the fireman’s lift for exclusive use.
- **Phase II Switch (In-Car Switch):** Located **inside the fireman’s lift car**, it gives the fireman **direct one-button-per-floor control** so that the lift can be operated with one hand (the other holding a hose or breathing apparatus), even when wearing firefighting gloves.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester, Module 4 Coverage]**

---

#### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **various components of a machine room** of a passenger lift. State any **four safety devices** that must be installed inside the machine room. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger lift has a rated speed of **2.0 m/s**. Calculate:
- (i) the **oil buffer stroke** required,
- (ii) the **car runby**,
- (iii) the **governor trip speed**,
- (iv) the **time to manually wind the car** from a position 5 m below the nearest landing at 0.15 m/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Machine Room Components and Safety Devices (7 marks):**

The machine room is a dedicated enclosure above (or beside, in MRL designs) the hoist way, housing the driving and control machinery of the lift. The standard layout includes:

1. **Traction Machine** – a gearless PM synchronous motor (for high-speed lifts) or worm-geared motor (for low-speed lifts), mounted on an isolated concrete plinth to reduce vibration transmission to the building.
2. **Traction Sheave / Deflector Sheave** – the grooved pulley over which the suspension ropes pass; the groove angle is matched to the rope (typically 35°–40°).
3. **Controller (VVVF Drive)** – the electrical panel containing relays, contactors, PCBs, and the variable-voltage-variable-frequency drive for smooth acceleration and deceleration.
4. **Governor** – a centrifugal device whose flyweights swing outwards as speed rises; at 115% of rated speed it triggers the safety gear on the car.
5. **Main Isolator Switch** – a lockable, heavy-duty switch (≥ 5 kA fault rating) that isolates the entire lift from the building mains.
6. **Emergency Power Equipment** – UPS/battery for the Automatic Rescue Device (ARD), emergency car lighting (≥ 5 lux for 1 h), and battery-operated alarm bell.
7. **Ventilation System** – exhaust fan or louvres providing ≥ 6 air changes/hour.

**Four Mandatory Safety Devices in the Machine Room:**

1. **Governor** – over-speed detection (trips at 1.15 v_rated).
2. **Final limit switches** – in the shaft, electrically cut power at the extreme ends of travel.
3. **Main isolator** – permits lock-out during maintenance.
4. **Emergency stop button** – on the controller, cuts all motion instantly.
5. *(Bonus)* Phase I fireman’s switch on the lobby, wired back to the controller.

*(Examiner allocates 1 mark for sketch, 1 for component identification × 4, 1 for safety devices × 2, and 1 for overall presentation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** v = 2.0 m/s, distance = 5.0 m, v_crank = 0.15 m/s.

**(i) Oil buffer stroke (2 marks)**

$$
S_{buf} = 0.067 \times v^2 = 0.067 \times (2.0)^2 = 0.067 \times 4
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{buf} = 0.268 \text{ m}}
$$

**(ii) Car runby (2 marks)**

$$
R_{car} = 1.0 + 0.035 \times v^2 = 1.0 + 0.035 \times 4 = 1.0 + 0.14
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{car} = 1.14 \text{ m}}
$$

**(iii) Governor trip speed (1.5 marks)**

$$
v_{gov} = 1.15 \times v_{rated} = 1.15 \times 2.0
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{gov} = 2.30 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(iv) Manual winding time (1.5 marks)**

$$
t = \frac{d}{v_{crank}} = \frac{5.0}{0.15}
$$

$$
\boxed{t = 33.33 \text{ seconds} \approx 33.3 \text{ s}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Alert — Part B (b):**
> - Students often **forget to square the speed** in the buffer and runby formulas. Half-mark deduction per error.
> - Some students compute **time in hours** because the distance is in metres — remember: distance is in **m**, speed in **m/s**, so time is in **seconds**.
> - Always state the **boundary check** (e.g., “0.15 m ≤ 0.268 m ≤ 1.20 m, hence design is compliant”) to fetch the last ½ mark.

---

#### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step emergency procedure** to be followed when a passenger lift car is **immobilised between floors** due to complete power failure. Mention the role of the **Automatic Rescue Device (ARD)** and the **manual hand-wind facility**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A freight lift machine room has dimensions **5 m × 4 m × 3 m** (L × W × H). The motor dissipates **5 kW**. The air specific heat is **1.005 kJ/kg·K** and density is **1.2 kg/m³**. Calculate the **required ventilation air-flow rate (m³/h)** to keep the temperature rise ≤ 8 K. Verify whether the design satisfies the **minimum 6 air changes per hour** requirement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Emergency Procedure for Power-Failure Entrapment (7 marks):**

**Step 1 – Confirm the nature of failure (½ mark):** Verify the failure is from mains power loss (and not internal electrical fault). Check the mains indicator, DG status, and UPS lights.

**Step 2 – Establish voice contact (½ mark):** Use the car intercom to speak with the trapped occupants, reassure them, and ask about any medical conditions. Continuous communication is essential to control panic, which typically peaks after 20 minutes of entrapment.

**Step 3 – Locate the car position (½ mark):** The building engineer checks the floor-position indicator in the machine room to determine exactly where the car is stuck.

**Step 4 – Activate the Automatic Rescue Device (ARD) (1½ marks):** If the lift is fitted with an ARD (battery + controller logic), the ARD automatically detects the power failure, switches to battery mode, and **creeps the car to the nearest landing at a controlled speed of ≤ 0.3 m/s**. The doors open automatically once the car is in the door zone. This is the **safest and fastest** method.

**Step 5 – Use the manual hand-wind facility (1½ marks):** If no ARD is fitted, a **trained mechanic** must:
- Enter the machine room using the access key.
- Engage the **hand-wind crank** on the traction sheave.
- Release the motor **brake manually** (using the brake-release lever).
- Wind the car to the **nearest landing at ≤ 0.15 m/s** (half the ARD speed for safety margin).
- Stop the moment the car sill is level with the landing (door zone).

**Step 6 – Open the doors (½ mark):** Apply the **parking brake**, switch off the main isolator (lock-out-tag-out), and use the **emergency triangular/square key** to manually open the landing door. Open the car door manually.

**Step 7 – Evacuate and secure (½ mark):** Help the occupants out through the landing door, lock the landing door, post an “OUT OF SERVICE” notice, and inform the service company.

**Step 8 – Documentation (½ mark):** Log the incident with time, location, and duration, and file a statutory report with the lift inspector if the duration exceeded 30 minutes.

*(Examiner allocates ~½–1 mark per major step, with extra credit for the ARD vs. hand-wind contrast.)*

---

**(b) Ventilation Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** Room volume V = 5 × 4 × 3 = **60 m³**, P_motor = 5 kW, ΔT_max = 8 K, c_p = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, ρ = 1.2 kg/m³.

**Step 1 – Mass flow rate required (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{P_{motor}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{5.0}{1.005 \times 8}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{5.0}{8.04} = 0.622 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 – Volume flow rate (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.622}{1.2}
$$

$$
\dot{V} = 0.518 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 – Convert to per hour (1 mark)**

$$
\dot{V}_h = 0.518 \times 3600
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{V}_h = 1866 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}
$$

**Step 4 – Air changes per hour (ACH) (1 mark)**

$$
ACH = \frac{\dot{V}_h}{V} = \frac{1866}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{ACH = 31.1 \text{ per hour}}
$$

**Step 5 – Compliance check (1 mark)**

Since **31.1 ≥ 6** (the IS 14665 minimum), the design is **comfortably compliant**. The temperature-rise criterion (8 K) governs, not the 6 ACH rule.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Alert — Part B (b):**
> - Common error: students use **ΔT in °C** correctly but forget to **convert seconds to hours** in the final step. Always write “× 3600” explicitly.
> - Some students calculate ACH as **V_vent / V_room** using litres or cm³ — make sure **all units are SI** (m³).
> - Don’t forget the **compliance statement** — examiners award the last ½ mark for explicitly saying “**satisfies**” or “**does not satisfy**” the code requirement.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-hour revision table — read this 3 times before the exam.**

- [ ] **Hoist way** = vertical fire-rated shaft from pit floor to overhead; **fire rating = 2 h** (4 h for > 24 m buildings).
- [ ] **Pit depth minimum = 1.4 m**; pit-stop switch, sump, and ladder are mandatory.
- [ ] **Headroom minimum = 4.5 m** above top landing.
- [ ] **Vent area = 0.3 m² per shaft**; **6 air changes/hour** in machine room.
- [ ] **Buffer stroke (oil) = 0.067 v²**; for **v > 1.0 m/s**, oil buffer is **mandatory**.
- [ ] **Car runby = 1.0 + 0.035 v²**; **counterweight runby** uses the same formula.
- [ ] **Governor trip = 1.15 v_rated**; **safety gear trip = 1.20 v_rated**.
- [ ] **Machine room** must be **lockable, fire-rated, ventilated, illuminated**, and accessible **only by authorised personnel**.
- [ ] **Phase I fireman’s switch** is on the **ground-floor lobby**; **Phase II** is **inside the fireman’s lift car**.
- [ ] **Power-failure rescue:** confirm → communicate → locate → ARD or hand-wind → door-zone stop → manual door key → evacuate → lock-out → log.
- [ ] **Door-zone failure** = **most dangerous** scenario; rescue from **top hatch** using rope-and-harness, never from the door.
- [ ] **NEVER** move the car with passengers inside unless you are in the door zone and doors can be opened directly (NFPA-101 golden rule).
- [ ] **ARD speed ≤ 0.30 m/s**; **manual crank speed ≤ 0.15 m/s**.
- [ ] **Trapped-occupant panic peaks at 20 min**; aim to complete rescue within **30 minutes**.
- [ ] **Emergency lighting in car ≥ 5 lux for 1 hour** on battery backup.
- [ ] **Always apply lock-out-tag-out** after a rescue before any maintenance work begins.
- [ ] **Standards to remember for KTU viva:** IS 14665 (India), EN 81-20 (Europe), NFPA-101 (USA), NBC 2016 (India – fire & lift).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory aid (mnemonic for the rescue sequence):**
> ***“C³ L² A S M O E L”***
> **C**onfirm → **C**ommunicate → **C**heck car position → **L**ocate floor → **L**ock-out plan ready → **A**RD / **A**lternate crank → **S**top in door zone → **M**anual door open → **O**ut via landing → **E**vacuate → **L**og incident.

**End of Module 4 — Hoist Way and Machine Room Emergency Procedure Notes.**

# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Foundational Definitions (Aligned with WHO & RPwD Act 2016)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "Requirements for the Handicapped" module focuses on designing workplaces, equipment, and material-handling systems so that differently-abled persons can operate safely, ergonomically, and with dignity, in compliance with the **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act)** and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**.

**Disability (WHO ICF Model, 2001):** *Disability* is an umbrella term for **impairments** (loss of body function/structure), **activity limitations** (difficulty in executing a task), and **participation restrictions** (problems in involvement in life situations). It is the interaction between a person's health condition and their **contextual/environmental factors**.

**Impairment vs. Handicap vs. Disability (Three-Tier Distinction — Examiner's Favourite):**

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Impairment** | Loss or abnormality of psychological, physiological, or anatomical structure/function | Loss of one leg, partial blindness |
| **Disability** | Restriction or lack (resulting from impairment) of ability to perform an activity in the manner considered normal | Cannot climb stairs, cannot read fine print |
| **Handicap** | A disadvantage resulting from impairment or disability that limits/prevents fulfilment of a normal social role | Unemployed because workplace has no ramp |

> [!NOTE]
> The modern, socially accepted umbrella term is **"Persons with Disabilities (PwD)"**. The archaic term "handicapped" is retained in some KTU legacy questions but is discouraged in formal engineering documentation.

## 1.2 Classification of Disabilities (As Per RPwD Act, 2016 — Schedule Specified Disabilities)

The **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016** recognizes **21 categories** of disabilities, broadly grouped as follows:

1. **Physical Disabilities**
   - Locomotor Disability (Leprosy-cured, Cerebral Palsy, Dwarfism, Muscular Dystrophy, Acid Attack victims)
   - Visual Impairment (Blindness, Low Vision)
   - Hearing Impairment (Deaf, Hard of Hearing)
   - Speech and Language Disability
2. **Intellectual Disabilities**
   - Specific Learning Disabilities, Autism Spectrum Disorder
3. **Mental Illness / Behavioural Disabilities**
4. **Disabilities caused by Neurological Conditions** (Multiple Sclerosis, Parkinson's, Stroke)
5. **Blood Disorders** (Haemophilia, Thalassemia, Sickle Cell Disease)
6. **Multiple Disabilities** (combination of two or more)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Universal-Doorway Principle"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are designing a hotel. If the door, ramp, and bathroom are built *only* for an able-bodied adult of average height, an elderly person with a wheelchair, a blind guest, or a mother with a stroller will all struggle. **Universal Design** says: *design once for everyone*. A ramp that helps a wheelchair user also helps a delivery person with a trolley, a patient on crutches, and a parent pushing a pram. Hence, **"requirements for the handicapped" are not special provisions — they are the baseline of safe, inclusive, ergonomic design** that benefits *every* worker in material-handling industries.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Material Handling Safety

In industries where manual and mechanical material handling is routine (warehousing, manufacturing, construction, logistics), workers with disabilities form a significant portion of the workforce. KTU emphasises that the **employer must**:

- Provide **accessible workplaces** (ramps, lifts, wide aisles).
- Offer **adapted tools and PPE** (e.g., one-handed lifting devices, vibrating-alarm fire alarms for the hearing impaired, tactile flooring for the visually impaired).
- Reassign heavy lifting tasks to **able-bodied workers** while retaining PwDs in safe, modified roles.
- Train supervisors on the **Rights of PwD Act, 2016** and emergency-evacuation protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic (Exam-Relevant):** As per the RPwD Act 2016, every establishment with **20 or more employees must reserve 4% of vacancies for PwDs**. Establishments employing **100+ persons on any day in the preceding 12 months** are designated as **"establishments"** under Section 2(t).

# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — Workplace Architectural Requirements

### 2.1.1 Ramps (Most Frequently Asked Sub-Topic)

A ramp is an inclined walking surface. For accessibility:

- **Maximum Gradient (Slope):**
  - 1 in 12 (≈ 8.33% grade) — preferred for public buildings.
  - 1 in 15 (≈ 6.67%) — for steeper but safer situations.
  - **Never exceed 1 in 12** for unassisted wheelchair users.
- **Minimum Width:** **1200 mm** (1.2 m) for one-way wheelchair traffic; **1800 mm** for two-way.
- **Handrails:** Required on both sides at heights of **760 mm and 900 mm** from ramp surface.
- **Landing:** Rest platform every **9 m** of ramp run (max) and at every change in direction.
- **Surface:** Slip-resistant, non-glare.

### 2.1.2 Doors and Corridors

| Element | Minimum Specification |
|---|---|
| Door clear opening width | **900 mm** (for wheelchair) |
| Door threshold height | Not to exceed **12 mm** |
| Corridor width (single wheelchair) | **1200 mm** |
| Corridor width (two wheelchairs / passing) | **1800 mm** |
| Turning radius for wheelchair | **1500 mm** minimum |
| Maximum handle operating force | **22.2 N (2.27 kgf)** |

### 2.1.3 Stairways and Steps

- Tread depth ≥ **300 mm**, riser height ≤ **150 mm**.
- Nosing must be rounded (no sharp projections).
- Tactile warning strip **300 mm** before top and bottom step.
- Continuous handrails both sides, projecting **300 mm** beyond top and bottom riser.

### 2.1.4 Toilets, Drinking Fountains, and Rest Areas

- At least **one accessible toilet per floor** with grab bars (40–45 mm dia.), 900 mm height, and 1500 mm turning radius.
- Drinking fountain spout at **760–900 mm** with knee clearance for wheelchair users.
- Rest seating every **30–50 m** in long corridors.

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Visual Impairment Adaptations

- **Tactile Paving:** Truncated domes/blister surface **300 mm before** stairs, platforms, and hazards.
- **Audible Signals:** Fire alarms ≥ **75 dB at 3 m**, with **low-frequency (≤ 500 Hz)** signals for the hearing impaired.
- **High-Contrast Colours:** Yellow on black for stairs; contrasting nosings.
- **Braille Signage:** Mandatory on lift buttons, room numbers, and exit signs.
- **Lighting:** Minimum **100 lux** on work surfaces, **200 lux** for visually impaired workstations (avoid glare).

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Hearing Impairment Adaptations

- **Visual Alert Systems:** Strobe lights in fire alarms, flashing doorbells.
- **Vibration Alert Devices** for emergency signals.
- **Sign Language Interpreters** during safety training sessions.
- **Written/Visual Instructions** instead of verbal-only briefings.
- **Hearing-loop (Induction Loop)** systems in training rooms.

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Locomotor Disability & Material Handling

For PwDs working in warehousing, packaging, or assembly:

- **Maximum Manual Lifting Limit (Healthy Adult):** 25 kg (male), 15 kg (female) per ISO 11228 and Indian Factory Rules.
- **Recommended Reduction for PwDs (Locomotor):** Typically **50%** of able-bodied limit, subject to medical fitness certification.
- **Replaced by Mechanical Aids:** Conveyors, lift tables, pallet jacks, hoists.
- **PPE Adaptations:** Anti-vibration gloves for one-handed grip; reinforced braces; custom-fit harnesses.
- **Workstation Height Adjustment:** Sit-stand stations (650–1100 mm adjustable) for wheelchair users.

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Emergency Evacuation for PwDs

- **Areas of Rescue Assistance:** Identified safe zones on each floor, with two-way communication.
- **Evacuation Chairs:** Stair-climber chairs for non-ambulant persons.
- **Buddy System:** Pair each PwD with a trained colleague.
- **Visual Alarm Strobes:** 1 Hz to 2 Hz flash rate, 75 candela minimum intensity.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Dimensions Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Numbers Table** — These values are **repeatedly tested** in KTU ESE. Memorise the **bold** ones.

| Parameter | Symbol/Value | Standard/Source |
|---|---|---|
| Ramp gradient (preferred) | $G = \dfrac{1}{12}$ | NBC 2016, IS 4960 |
| Ramp gradient (steep limit) | $G = \dfrac{1}{15}$ | IS 4960 |
| Ramp width (one-way) | $w = 1200 \, \text{mm}$ | NBC 2016 |
| Ramp width (two-way) | $w = 1800 \, \text{mm}$ | NBC 2016 |
| Handrail height (lower) | $h_1 = 760 \, \text{mm}$ | IS 4960 |
| Handrail height (upper) | $h_2 = 900 \, \text{mm}$ | IS 4960 |
| Door clear width | $W_d = 900 \, \text{mm}$ | NBC 2016 |
| Corridor width (single) | $W_c = 1200 \, \text{mm}$ | NBC 2016 |
| Turning radius (wheelchair) | $R = 1500 \, \text{mm}$ | IS 4960 |
| Force on door handle (max) | $F = 22.2 \, \text{N}$ | NBC 2016 |
| Stair tread (min) | $T_{min} = 300 \, \text{mm}$ | IS 4960 |
| Stair riser (max) | $R_{max} = 150 \, \text{mm}$ | IS 4960 |
| Handrail projection beyond stair | $p = 300 \, \text{mm}$ | IS 4960 |
| Reserved vacancies for PwDs | $4\%$ of workforce | RPwD Act 2016, Sec 34 |
| Threshold establishment size | $n \geq 20$ employees | RPwD Act 2016 |
| Fire alarm sound level | $L \geq 75 \, \text{dB at 3 m}$ | NBC 2016 |

## 2.7 Engineering Significance

The requirements for the handicapped are the **cornerstone of inclusive safety engineering**. By internalising anthropometric and ergonomic constraints of disabled workers, an engineer can design:
- Workplaces that comply with the **RPwD Act 2016** and avoid legal penalties.
- Material-handling stations with **50% fewer manual handling injuries** (OSHA statistics).
- Spaces that are **universally usable** (Universal Design Principle — Ronald Mace, 1997).

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ramp Length Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A warehouse entrance has a vertical rise (height difference) of $H = 750 \, \text{mm}$ between the ground and the loading platform. Design the ramp length using the preferred gradient of **1 in 12**.

### Mathematical Deduction

**Step 1 — Define the gradient.**
The gradient $G$ is the ratio of vertical rise to horizontal run.

$$G = \frac{\text{Vertical Rise}}{\text{Horizontal Run}} = \frac{H}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the preferred gradient.**

$$\frac{1}{12} = \frac{H}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for horizontal run $L$.**

$$L = 12 \times H$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $H = 750 \, \text{mm}$.**

$$L = 12 \times 750 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute.**

$$L = 9000 \, \text{mm} = 9.0 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of intermediate landings.**
According to NBC 2016, a rest landing is required every **9 m** of horizontal run. Since the run is exactly $9.0 \, \text{m}$, **one intermediate landing** must be provided at $4.5 \, \text{m}$ to split the climb into two $4.5 \, \text{m}$ runs of $375 \, \text{mm}$ rise each.

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The ramp length is **9.0 m**, requiring **one intermediate landing** for compliance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reduced Lifting Load for a Locomotor-Disabled Worker

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** The safe manual lifting limit for an able-bodied male worker in India is $W_{ab} = 25 \, \text{kg}$. Calculate the recommended maximum lifting load $W_{d}$ for a male worker with **40% locomotor disability**, assuming the standard occupational safety reduction factor.

### Mathematical Deduction

**Step 1 — Apply the disability reduction factor $k_d$.**
Industry practice (and KTU reference) recommends reducing the manual lifting limit for locomotor-disabled workers.

$$k_d = 0.50 \quad \text{(50% of able-bodied limit)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the modified load.**

$$W_d = k_d \times W_{ab}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$W_d = 0.50 \times 25 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$W_d = 12.5 \, \text{kg}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Newtons (for engineering rigour).**

$$W_d = 12.5 \times 9.81 \, \text{N} = 122.625 \, \text{N}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The recommended maximum lift for a locomotor-disabled male worker is **12.5 kg (≈ 122.6 N)**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wheelchair Turning Space (T-Space)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Calculate the minimum floor area needed for a standard manual wheelchair to complete a 180° turn in a confined corridor.

### Mathematical Deduction

**Step 1 — Standard wheelchair dimensions.**
A standard adult manual wheelchair has the following dimensions:
- Length $L_w = 1100 \, \text{mm}$
- Width $W_w = 650 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 2 — Turning circle diameter $D$.**
The minimum turning diameter is given by:

$$D = 2 \times R_w + W_w$$

where $R_w$ is the radius of the rear wheel.

**Step 3 — Assume $R_w = 300 \, \text{mm}$ for a standard wheelchair.**

$$D = 2 \times 300 + 650 = 600 + 650 = 1250 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the safety factor (NBC 2016) for the turning T-space.**
NBC 2016 mandates a **minimum clearance of 1500 mm diameter** for a complete 180° turn.

$$D_{design} = 1250 \, \text{mm} + 2 \times 125 \, \text{mm (margin)} = 1500 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the floor area $A$.**

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D_{design}^2}{4}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute.**

$$A = \frac{\pi \times (1500)^2}{4} \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 7 — Compute.**

$$A = \frac{3.14159 \times 2{,}250{,}000}{4} \, \text{mm}^2 \approx 1{,}767{,}143 \, \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 8 — Convert to square metres.**

$$A \approx 1.77 \, \text{m}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The minimum wheelchair turning T-space is a **1500 mm diameter circle** (≈ **1.77 m²**).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Number of Accessible Toilets Required

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A four-storey office building has 80 occupants per floor. Determine the minimum number of accessible (PwD-friendly) toilets required per the RPwD Act 2016.

### Mathematical Deduction

**Step 1 — Apply the standard rule.**

$$N_{toilets} = \left\lceil \frac{N_{floors}}{N_{floors per unit}} \right\rceil$$

where one accessible toilet is required **per floor** for buildings with mixed occupancy.

**Step 2 — Direct rule.**
NBC 2016 mandates at least **one accessible toilet per floor** where public access is permitted.

$$N_{toilets} = 1 \times \text{Number of floors}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$N_{toilets} = 1 \times 4 = 4$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The building requires **4 accessible toilets**, one per floor.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Computed Value | KTU Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Ramp horizontal run (750 mm rise) | $9.0 \, \text{m}$ | Gradient 1:12 rule |
| Lift load (disabled male, 40% loco) | $12.5 \, \text{kg} = 122.6 \, \text{N}$ | 50% reduction factor |
| Wheelchair T-space | $\approx 1.77 \, \text{m}^2$ | 1500 mm dia. circle |
| Accessible toilets (4-storey) | $4$ | 1 per floor rule |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

### Question A1. [3 Marks]
**Differentiate between *impairment*, *disability*, and *handicap* with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Impairment:** Loss or abnormality of body structure or function (e.g., loss of a leg in an accident).
- **Disability:** Restriction of ability to perform a normal activity due to impairment (e.g., inability to climb stairs).
- **Handicap:** A disadvantage that prevents fulfilment of a normal social role because of impairment/disability (e.g., unemployment due to inaccessible workplace).

### Question A2. [3 Marks]
**List any three architectural requirements in workplaces for persons with locomotor disabilities.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Ramps with maximum gradient **1 in 12**, minimum width **1200 mm**, and handrails at **760 mm and 900 mm** heights on both sides.
2. Doors with clear opening width of at least **900 mm** and operating force not exceeding **22.2 N**.
3. Corridors of minimum **1200 mm** width with a wheelchair turning circle of **1500 mm** diameter at intersections.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

---

### **Question B1(a). [7 Marks]**
**Explain the Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016. List the categories of disabilities recognised and discuss the employer's obligations in a material-handling industry.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Overview of the Act.**
The **Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, 2016 (RPwD Act)** replaced the Persons with Disabilities Act, 1995. It is the primary legislation safeguarding the rights of PwDs in India and is aligned with the **UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD), 2007**.

**Step 2 — Recognised Categories of Disabilities.**
The Act expanded the list from **7 (in 1995) to 21 categories**, including:
- Locomotor Disability, Visual Impairment, Hearing Impairment, Speech and Language Disability
- Intellectual Disability, Autism Spectrum Disorder
- Mental Illness
- Disabilities due to neurological conditions (Parkinson's, Multiple Sclerosis)
- Blood disorders (Haemophilia, Thalassemia, Sickle Cell Disease)
- Acid Attack Victims, Dwarfism
- Multiple Disabilities

**Step 3 — Employer Obligations.**
Under **Section 34** of the Act:
- Every establishment with **20 or more employees** must reserve **4% of vacancies** for PwDs.
- The Government must designate **4% of vacancies** in identified establishments as **backlog vacancies**.
- Establishments must ensure **non-discrimination** in employment.
- Provisions for **reasonable accommodation** must be made (accessible premises, adapted equipment).

**Step 4 — Application to Material Handling Industry.**
- Lifting limits must be **reduced by 50%** for locomotor-disabled workers.
- **Mechanical aids** (conveyors, hoists) must replace manual handling wherever feasible.
- **PPE** (gloves, harnesses) must be customised to individual worker needs.
- **Accessible routes** must connect storage zones, loading docks, and emergency exits.
- **Safety training** must be delivered in accessible formats (sign language, Braille).

**Step 5 — Penalties.**
Non-compliance attracts fines up to **₹25,000 for first offence** and up to **₹1 lakh for repeat offences**, plus cancellation of registration under Section 89.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write only the *number* of disabilities (21) without naming the Act section (Sec 34) or the percentage (4%). This costs 2 marks.

---

### **Question B1(b). [7 Marks]**
**Design a ramp for a public building with a vertical rise of 900 mm. Determine the ramp length, gradient, number of intermediate landings, and handrail specifications as per NBC 2016.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Given Data.**
- Vertical rise $H = 900 \, \text{mm} = 0.9 \, \text{m}$
- Preferred gradient $G = \dfrac{1}{12}$

**Step 2 — Calculate the horizontal run.**

$$L = 12 \times H = 12 \times 900 \, \text{mm} = 10{,}800 \, \text{mm} = 10.8 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Number of Intermediate Landings.**
Per NBC 2016, a rest landing is required every **9 m** of horizontal run.

$$N_{landings} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{9 \, \text{m}} \right\rceil - 1 = \left\lceil \frac{10.8}{9} \right\rceil - 1 = 2 - 1 = 1$$

Thus, **1 intermediate landing** is required, placed at **5.4 m** to split the run into two equal segments of $5.4 \, \text{m}$ each (rise $450 \, \text{mm}$ per segment).

**Step 4 — Ramp Width.**
- One-way wheelchair: $w = 1200 \, \text{mm}$
- Two-way (preferred for public buildings): $w = 1800 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 5 — Handrail Specifications.**
- **Two handrails**, one on each side.
- Heights: **760 mm** (lower) and **900 mm** (upper) from ramp surface.
- Handrail grip diameter: **40–45 mm**.
- Handrails must extend **300 mm beyond the top and bottom** of the ramp horizontally.
- Ends must be rounded back to the wall (no protruding hooks).

**Step 6 — Surface and Edging.**
- Slip-resistant surface (carborundum inserts or chequered plate).
- Edge protection (curb) of **100 mm height** to prevent wheel slippage.

**Step 7 — Sketches (to be drawn in exam).**
A schematic sketch showing the ramp, landings, handrails, and edge curbs must be included.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students compute the ramp length correctly but forget to specify the **landing placement**. A $10.8 \, \text{m}$ ramp without a landing **loses full marks** as it violates NBC 2016.

---

### **Question B2(a). [7 Marks]**
**Discuss in detail the workplace requirements for persons with visual and hearing impairments in a material-handling industry.**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Visual Impairment Requirements.**
Visually impaired workers rely heavily on tactile, auditory, and high-contrast cues.

| Requirement | Specification |
|---|---|
| Tactile flooring | Truncated domes (blister surface) 300 mm before hazards |
| Tactile signage | Braille labels on storage racks, doors, lifts |
| Lighting | 200 lux minimum; no glare |
| Audible alarms | ≥ 75 dB at 3 m with low-frequency (≤ 500 Hz) signals |
| High-contrast colours | Yellow nosings on stairs, contrasting edges |
| Voice-enabled equipment | Barcode scanners with audio feedback |
| Mobility canes and guide pathways | Designated tactile routes in warehouses |

**Step 2 — Hearing Impairment Requirements.**
Hearing-impaired workers depend on visual and vibrotactile signals.

| Requirement | Specification |
|---|---|
| Visual alarm strobes | 1–2 Hz flash rate, 75 candela minimum |
| Vibration pagers | Personal vibrating receivers for emergency alerts |
| Written safety instructions | Pictograms, multilingual text |
| Sign language interpreters | During toolbox talks |
| Induction loop systems | In training rooms |
| Buddy system | Trained colleague for verbal cues |

**Step 3 — Practical Application in Material Handling.**
- Forklifts equipped with **reverse alarms with visual indicators** (flashing lights) for deaf operators.
- Conveyor emergency-stop cords at **chest height** with red-coloured, textured pull handles for blind operators.
- Pallet racks labelled with **Braille and tactile numbers**.
- Inspection checklists in **audio or pictogram form** for the visually impaired.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write generic safety rules without specifying **exact numerical values** (e.g., "75 dB at 3 m", "1–2 Hz strobe"). Always quote the standard value.

---

### **Question B2(b). [7 Marks]**
**Explain the design requirements for an accessible toilet for persons with disabilities as per NBC 2016. Why is this important in a material-handling industry?**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Importance in Material Handling Industry.**
Accessible toilets ensure **dignity, hygiene, and 24-hour usability** of facilities for PwD workers on long shifts. Inadequate toilets are a recognised cause of absenteeism and occupational health complaints among disabled workers.

**Step 2 — Dimensional Requirements.**
| Element | Specification |
|---|---|
| Overall room size | ≥ **2200 mm × 1700 mm** |
| Turning circle (T-space) | **1500 mm diameter** |
| Door clear width | **900 mm**, outward-opening or sliding |
| WC centre-line from side wall | **480 mm** |
| WC seat height | **450–480 mm** |
| Grab bar (rear wall, side wall) | Length **600 mm**, dia. **40–45 mm**, height **900 mm** from floor |
| Washbasin height | **760–800 mm** with knee clearance |
| Mirror height | Bottom edge ≤ **1000 mm** from floor |
| Emergency call bell | At **900 mm** height with pull-cord to floor |

**Step 3 — Material and Surface.**
- Anti-skid tiles.
- Rounded corners (no sharp edges).
- Lever-type taps (not knob-type).

**Step 4 — Signage.**
- Pictogram of a wheelchair user at **1500–1800 mm** height on the door.
- Braille sign next to the door handle.

**Step 5 — Quantity.**
At least **one accessible toilet per floor** of any public/industrial building.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently miss the **WC centre-line distance (480 mm)** and the **grab-bar height (900 mm)**. These are tested routinely.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Consolidated Pitfall List

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in this Module:**
> 1. **Mixing up "impairment" and "disability"** — they are not synonyms.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4% reservation figure** under the RPwD Act 2016.
> 3. **Not specifying units** (mm vs cm vs m) in ramp calculations.
> 4. **Omitting handrail projections** (300 mm) and **landing intervals** (every 9 m).
> 5. **Ignoring emergency evacuation** adaptations (strobe, evacuation chair).
> 6. **Writing generic safety rules** instead of citing **NBC 2016 / IS 4960 numbers**.
> 7. **Skipping the medical fitness certification** for PwD manual handling workers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorise These Before the Exam:**

- **Disability = WHO ICF** model (Impairment → Disability → Handicap chain).
- **RPwD Act 2016** recognises **21 categories** of disability.
- **4% reservation** for PwDs in establishments with **20+ employees**.
- **Ramp gradient:** preferred **1:12**, steep limit **1:15**; width **1200 mm** (single) / **1800 mm** (two-way).
- **Handrail heights:** **760 mm and 900 mm**, projection **300 mm** beyond stair.
- **Door width:** minimum **900 mm**; operating force ≤ **22.2 N**.
- **Wheelchair turning T-space:** **1500 mm diameter** circle.
- **Tactile paving:** **300 mm before** stairs and hazards.
- **Fire alarm:** ≥ **75 dB at 3 m**; visual strobe **1–2 Hz**.
- **Manual lift reduction:** **50%** of able-bodied limit for locomotor PwDs.
- **Accessible toilet:** **one per floor**, **1500 mm T-space**, grab bar at **900 mm** height.
- **Landing interval:** every **9 m** of ramp run.
- **Stair:** tread ≥ **300 mm**, riser ≤ **150 mm**, rounded nosings.
- **Tactile and Braille signage** mandatory for lifts, doors, racks, and exits.
- **Evacuation aids:** evacuation chairs, two-way communication, buddy system.
- **Universal Design Principle** (Ronald Mace, 1997) underpins all requirements.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Requirements for the Handicapped**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol for OESFT833, 2024 Scheme.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Escalator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types - Escalator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Escalator (As per KTU / IS 4591 / EN 115):** A *power-driven, continuously moving stairway* — comprising an *endless chain of moving steps* guided on tracks, used for transporting passengers between *two (or more) floors* in an *inclined* orientation. It is a Class of *Mechanical Conveyor* (technically a "people conveyor") that falls under the broader domain of **continuous material/people handling equipment**.

An escalator is formally classified as a **continuous-flow vertical transport device**, distinct from elevators because:
- It never stops moving (in normal operation).
- It does not have a confined cabin.
- It is intended for **high-traffic, short vertical distances** (typically **3 m to 15 m** rise).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a *conveyor belt* in a supermarket checkout — except instead of carrying tomatoes, it carries **people standing on steps**. The belt tilts upward (or downward) at an angle, and the steps appear to *emerge from the floor at the bottom* and *vanish into the floor at the top*, like a never-ending moving staircase. The whole loop is hidden inside the truss (the metal "skeleton" of the escalator).

A simpler way to think of it: **An escalator is a *looping staircase in motion* — passengers step onto a moving tread and are carried to the next floor without pressing a button.**

### 1.3 Standard Reference Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU / IS 4591) | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Inclination Angle | **30°** (standard) or **35°** (transit) | $\theta$ |
| Step Width | **600 mm / 800 mm / 1000 mm** | $w$ |
| Rated Speed | **0.5 m/s** (commercial) / **0.65 m/s** (transit) | $v$ |
| Vertical Rise Range | **3 m to 15 m** (typical) | $H$ |
| Step Depth (tread) | **0.4 m** (standard) | $t_d$ |
| Handrail Speed | **Equal to step speed** (synchronous) | $v_h$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Safety Engineering:** The escalator is governed by the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 4591)**, and the **European EN 115-1:2017** standard. Any deviation from these parameters directly impacts **occupational safety** in malls, metros, and airports.

---

### 1.4 Why Escalators Are Used Instead of Lifts

| Criteria | Escalator Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Continuous Flow** | No waiting time — passengers board instantly. |
| **High Throughput** | Theoretical capacity up to **13,000 persons/hour**. |
| **Visibility** | Shoppers see all floors (marketing benefit in malls). |
| **No Door Dependency** | Cannot be "stuck" inside a cabin. |
| **Low Mechanical Failure** | Fewer hydraulics / cables than a lift. |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Escalators — The Core Topic

Escalators are **typed** along **four primary classification axes** as required by the KTU OESFT833 syllabus (Module 4: *Types*). Master these four categories:

### 🔷 Axis 1: Based on Step/Running Surface Construction

#### **A. Step-Type Escalator (Most Common)**
- Uses **rigid metal treads** (aluminium or stainless steel) connected to a roller chain.
- Each step has **grooves** (horizontal ridges) for **anti-slip** and **combs** at entry/exit.
- Operates safely up to **0.65 m/s** and angles up to **35°**.

#### **B. Belt-Type Escalator (Belt Palate)**
- Uses a **continuous rubber/PVC belt** (no discrete steps).
- Limited to **incline angles ≤ 30°** because passengers cannot maintain balance on a smooth moving surface at higher angles.
- Lower capacity (~50% of step-type) and rarely used in modern installations.

### 🔷 Axis 2: Based on Directional Configuration

#### **1. Parallel (Side-by-Side) Escalator** ⬆️ ⬇️
- Two escalators side by side — one **UP**, one **DOWN**.
- Most common in **shopping malls, airports, railway stations**.
- **Advantage:** Simple, low-cost, easy to maintain.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires **2× floor footprint**.

#### **2. Criss-Cross (Cross-Over / Scissor) Escalator** ✖️
- The UP and DOWN escalators are **interleaved** — they share the same vertical shaft.
- The down-going escalator's bottom landing is at the **top of the up-going escalator** and vice versa.
- **Advantage:** **Single shaft footprint** — saves ~40% floor area.
- **Disadvantage:** Passengers must walk *across* the floor level to find their direction.

#### **3. Spiral / Helical Escalator** 🌀
- The step path follows a **curved (helical) trajectory** — not straight.
- Used architecturally in **premium malls, museums, hotels** (aesthetic appeal).
- **Disadvantage:** Very expensive, complex chain mechanics, lower capacity.

#### **4. Multiple Flat (Multi-Floor) Escalator** 🏢
- Serves **more than two floors** in one continuous loop (rare, used in some metro stations).

### 🔷 Axis 3: Based on Environmental Application

| Type | Environment | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Indoor Escalator** | Malls, offices, airports | Standard IP rating, no weatherproofing. |
| **Outdoor Escalator** | Metro stations, foot-over-bridges | **IP65 waterproofing**, corrosion-resistant coating, anti-skid steps. |
| **Marine Escalator** | Cruise ships | **Fire-retardant materials**, gimbal-mounted to handle ship roll. |

### 🔷 Axis 4: Based on Operational Mode

- **One-Way (Fixed Direction):** Permanent UP or DOWN. Used in metro exits.
- **Reversible:** Can flip direction based on time of day (morning = UP, evening = DOWN).

---

## 2.2 Key Mathematical Formulas (The "Cheat Sheet")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** — these are the *most-tested equations* in ESE for the topic "Escalator":

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Capacity | $C = \dfrac{3600 \times v \times k \times w_{\text{coeff}}}{t_d}$ | persons/hour | $v$ = speed, $k$ = step-fill factor, $t_d$ = step depth |
| 2 | Step Fill Factor (k) | $k = \dfrac{\text{Actual Pax per step}}{\text{Max Pax per step}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $0.5$ to $0.8$ |
| 3 | Step Coefficient | $k_w = 0.5$ (600mm), $0.75$ (800mm), $1.0$ (1000mm) | dimensionless | Empirical width constant |
| 4 | Horizontal Length (Run) | $L_h = \dfrac{H}{\tan(\theta)}$ | metres | $H$ = vertical rise, $\theta$ = angle |
| 5 | Diagonal Length (Slope) | $L_d = \dfrac{H}{\sin(\theta)}$ | metres | Actual truss length |
| 6 | Travel Time | $T = \dfrac{L_d}{v}$ | seconds | Time to traverse one-way |
| 7 | Motor Power | $P = \dfrac{W \times v \times g}{\eta \times 1000}$ | kW | $W$ = total load (people + steps), $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 8 | Handrail Speed | $v_h = v_{\text{step}}$ (synchronous) | m/s | Always matches step speed |

---

## 2.3 Why Escalator Types Matter in Safety Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> 1. **Crowd Flow Modeling:** Choosing parallel vs criss-cross affects evacuation time during fire drills (a KTU safety-critical scenario).
> 2. **Ergonomics:** Criss-cross escalators have **higher fall risk** because the handrail at the landing creates a "gap perception" issue.
> 3. **Maintenance Strategy:** Spiral escalators have **3× maintenance cost** — the safety engineer's inspection schedule must reflect this.
> 4. **Load Capacity:** Belt-type escalators have **50% lower capacity** than step-type — wrong selection in metro stations can cause **stampede risk**.

### 2.4 Safety Components Common to All Escalator Types

1. **Emergency Stop Button** — at both landings (red, mushroom-head).
2. **Comb Plate Safety Switch** — detects foreign objects at the skirt/ comb interface.
3. **Step Chain Safety Switch** — triggers on chain elongation.
4. **Handrail Speed Sensor** — cuts power if $v_h \neq v_{\text{step}}$.
5. **Skirt Deflector** — prevents loose clothing from being trapped.
6. **Anti-Static Brushes** — at top/bottom transitions.
7. **Step-Levelling Sensors** — at landings to prevent the "step-drop" hazard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Hourly Capacity of an Escalator

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Theoretical Capacity* is the **maximum number of passengers** an escalator can theoretically transport in **one hour**, assuming the steps are **perfectly and continuously filled**.

The *theoretical capacity* ($C$) depends on three things:
- How **fast** the steps move ($v$).
- How **deep** each step is ($t_d$).
- How **many people** can fit on each step (governed by step width $w$).

### 3.1.2 The Derivation

**Step 1 — Number of steps passing a fixed point per second:**

The steps are spaced by the **step depth** $t_d$ along the direction of motion. Therefore, the number of steps that cross a fixed point in **one second** is:

$$
N_{1s} = \frac{v}{t_d}
$$

**Explanation of logic:** If the steps move at $v$ m/s and each step is $t_d$ m long, then a "new step" arrives every $\frac{t_d}{v}$ seconds. So in 1 second, the number of new steps is $\frac{v}{t_d}$. ✅

**Step 2 — Number of steps passing per hour:**

Multiply $N_{1s}$ by the number of seconds in an hour (3600):

$$
N_{1h} = N_{1s} \times 3600 = \frac{3600 \times v}{t_d}
$$

**Step 3 — People per step (width-dependent):**

The standard step widths and their maximum occupancy (one person per step "row" in a 600 mm step, two rows in 1000 mm):

$$
n = k_w \times w_{\text{unit}}
$$

where $k_w$ is the empirical step-width coefficient:
- $k_w = 0.5$ for $w = 600\,\text{mm}$
- $k_w = 0.75$ for $w = 800\,\text{mm}$
- $k_w = 1.0$ for $w = 1000\,\text{mm}$

**Step 4 — Final theoretical capacity formula:**

Multiplying steps-per-hour by people-per-step:

$$
\boxed{C = \frac{3600 \times v \times k_w}{t_d}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **[Problem]:** An escalator has a step depth of **0.4 m**, a step width of **1000 mm**, and a rated speed of **0.5 m/s**. Calculate the theoretical hourly capacity.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $v = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$
- $t_d = 0.4\,\text{m}$
- $k_w = 1.0$ (for 1000 mm width)

Substitute into the formula:

$$
C = \frac{3600 \times 0.5 \times 1.0}{0.4}
$$

Evaluating numerator first:

$$
C = \frac{1800}{0.4}
$$

Final answer:

$$
\boxed{C = 4500 \,\text{persons/hour}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Note:** Always include **units in the final answer** and show the **substitution step** — markers deduct up to 1 mark for omitting units.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diagonal Length of the Escalator Truss

The escalator truss is the structural framework that supports the entire mechanism. Its **diagonal (sloped) length** is required for installation and material-cost estimation.

### 3.2.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a right triangle:
- **Vertical side** = rise height $H$.
- **Hypotenuse** = diagonal truss length $L_d$.
- **Angle** between hypotenuse and horizontal = inclination angle $\theta$.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

From trigonometry:

$$
\sin(\theta) = \frac{H}{L_d}
$$

Rearranging for $L_d$:

$$
\boxed{L_d = \frac{H}{\sin(\theta)}}
$$

The **horizontal run** is:

$$
\boxed{L_h = \frac{H}{\tan(\theta)}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example

> **[Problem]:** A mall escalator rises $H = 6\,\text{m}$ at $\theta = 30°$. Find the diagonal truss length.

**Solution:**

$$
L_d = \frac{H}{\sin(\theta)} = \frac{6}{\sin(30°)} = \frac{6}{0.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_d = 12 \,\text{m}}
$$

The horizontal run is:

$$
L_h = \frac{H}{\tan(30°)} = \frac{6}{0.5774} = 10.39 \,\text{m}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Motor Power Requirement

The motor must overcome the **gravitational load** of the loaded steps and overcome **friction**.

The total weight to lift per second:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{steps+people}} \times g
$$

Power (in Watts):

$$
P_W = \frac{W_{\text{total}} \times v \times \sin(\theta)}{\eta}
$$

Converting to kW:

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{W_{\text{total}} \times v \times \sin(\theta)}{\eta \times 1000}}
$$

where $\eta$ is the drive efficiency (typically 0.85 to 0.92).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Insight:** The motor is typically **oversized by 1.5×** to handle peak load (e.g., 100% loaded steps during a stadium exit event).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — Escalator Subsystems

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional interaction** between the major subsystems of a step-type escalator. Each block represents a real component, and the arrows show the **flow of mechanical power and safety signal feedback**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Mains Power Supply 3 Phase 415V] --> B[Motor Drive Unit]
    B --> C[Gear Reducer Box]
    C --> D[Main Drive Sprocket]
    D --> E[Step Chain - Endless Loop]
    E --> F[Step Treads with Rollers]
    F --> G[Top Track - Return Curve]
    F --> H[Bottom Track - Return Curve]
    D --> I[Handrail Drive Wheel]
    I --> J[Continuous Handrail Loop]
    K[Safety Controller PLC] --> L[Emergency Stop Switch Top]
    K --> M[Emergency Stop Switch Bottom]
    K --> N[Comb Plate Sensor]
    K --> O[Handrail Speed Sensor]
    K --> P[Step Chain Stretch Sensor]
    L --> B
    M --> B
    N --> B
    O --> B
    P --> B
    Q[Comb Plates Top and Bottom] --> F
    R[Skirt Panels Both Sides] --> F
    S[Balustrade and Glass Panels] --> J
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Power path:** A → B → C → D → E → F (motor drives the step chain).
- **Handrail path:** D → I → J (handrail moves in sync with the steps).
- **Safety path:** K is the central PLC; all emergency sensors (L, M, N, O, P) feed back to K, which can **cut motor power** (the lines from sensors to B) in a fault condition.

---

## 4.2 Functional Comparison Block Diagram — Types of Escalators

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Step Type Escalator] --> T1A[Rigid Metal Treads]
    T1 --> T1B[Angle up to 35 degrees]
    T1 --> T1C[High Capacity 4500 pax/hr]

    T2[Belt Type Escalator] --> T2A[Continuous Rubber Belt]
    T2 --> T2B[Angle limited to 30 degrees]
    T2 --> T2C[Lower Capacity 2200 pax/hr]

    T3[Parallel Escalator] --> T3A[Two Trusses Side by Side]
    T3 --> T3B[Simple Maintenance]

    T4[Criss Cross Escalator] --> T4A[Interleaved in One Shaft]
    T4 --> T4B[Space Saving Design]

    T5[Spiral Escalator] --> T5A[Helical Curved Path]
    T5 --> T5B[Aesthetic Premium Design]

    T6[Indoor Escalator] --> T6A[Standard IP Rating]
    T7[Outdoor Escalator] --> T7A[IP65 Waterproof]
    T7 --> T7B[Corrosion Resistant Coating]

    T8[Reversible Escalator] --> T8A[Direction Flipped by Timer]
    T9[Fixed Direction Escalator] --> T9A[Always UP or Always DOWN]
```

**Reading guide:**
- The left blocks represent the **Type** (parent).
- The right blocks (`--> T1A`) represent the **defining property** of that type.
- For the KTU exam, you may be asked to "**List 4 types of escalators and their key feature**" — this diagram is the ready answer.

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Block Diagram of a Step

```mermaid
graph TD
    STEP[Step Tread Surface] --> GROOVE1[Anti Slip Groove 1]
    STEP --> GROOVE2[Anti Slip Groove 2]
    STEP --> GROOVE3[Anti Slip Groove 3]
    STEP --> R1[Riser - Vertical Front]
    R1 --> CHAIN[Connecting Point to Main Chain]
    STEP --> SIDE1[Side Roller Left]
    STEP --> SIDE2[Side Roller Right]
    SIDE1 --> TRACK[Track Guide Rail]
    SIDE2 --> TRACK
    CHAIN --> PIN[Chain Pin and Roller]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between a step-type and a belt-type escalator. List any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Step-Type Escalator | Belt-Type Escalator |
|---|---|---|
| **Running Surface** | Discrete metal treads (steps) connected to a chain. | Continuous rubber/PVC belt (no separate steps). |
| **Maximum Incline Angle** | Up to **35°** (safe for passenger balance). | Limited to **30°** (no ridges to grip). |
| **Theoretical Capacity** | Higher — up to **4500 persons/hour** (1000 mm, 0.5 m/s). | Lower — about **50% of step-type** at the same speed. |
| **Safety** | Comb plates at landings engage with grooves — high safety. | Comb plates less effective — higher entrapment risk. |
| **Application** | Malls, metro stations, airports (most common). | Older installations, low-traffic sites. |

> **Any three of the above points = 3 marks** (1 mark each).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: What is a criss-cross escalator? State its main advantage and disadvantage.**

**Model Answer:**

A **criss-cross (cross-over) escalator** is a configuration in which the **UP and DOWN escalators are mounted in a single vertical shaft, interleaved** such that the **lower landing of the down-escalator aligns with the upper landing of the up-escalator**, and vice versa.

- **Main Advantage (1.5 marks):** It saves **approximately 40% of floor footprint** compared to a parallel side-by-side configuration. This is critical in **high-rent commercial spaces** (metros, airports).

- **Main Disadvantage (1.5 marks):** Passengers must **walk across the floor level** at the mid-landing to find the correct direction, which:
  - Causes **wayfinding confusion** during emergencies.
  - Increases **fall risk** at the landing edge (gap perception hazard).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule (As per 2024 Scheme):** Answer **any ONE** of the following two questions. Each carries **14 marks** with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Show **all derivations** and **diagram with labelling** for full marks.

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Classify escalators based on **(i) step construction** and **(ii) directional configuration**, listing at least **two types under each** with a **defining feature** of each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An escalator in a metro station has the following specifications:
- Vertical rise: $H = 7.2\,\text{m}$
- Inclination angle: $\theta = 30°$
- Step width: **1000 mm**
- Step depth: $t_d = 0.4\,\text{m}$
- Rated speed: $v = 0.65\,\text{m/s}$

Calculate:
1. The **diagonal length** of the truss.
2. The **theoretical hourly capacity**.
3. The **travel time** for a single journey. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**(i) Classification by Step Construction (3.5 marks):**

1. **Step-Type Escalator:** Uses **discrete rigid metal treads** (aluminium/stainless steel) connected to a **roller chain**. Each step has anti-slip grooves and comb plates at landings. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Belt-Type Escalator:** Uses a **continuous rubber/PVC belt** instead of discrete steps. Limited to inclines **≤ 30°** and has lower capacity. *(1.5 marks)*

3. **Bonus mention:** Step-type is the **industry standard** for metro and airport use because of higher safety and capacity. *(0.5 marks)*

**(ii) Classification by Directional Configuration (3.5 marks):**

1. **Parallel (Side-by-Side):** Two independent escalators, one UP and one DOWN, placed side by side. Most common. *(1 mark)*

2. **Criss-Cross (Cross-Over):** UP and DOWN escalators share a single vertical shaft, interleaved. Saves footprint. *(1 mark)*

3. **Spiral / Helical:** The step path follows a **curved helical trajectory**, used for architectural aesthetics. *(1 mark)*

4. **Multi-Floor (Multiple-Flat):** Serves **more than two floors** in one continuous loop. *(0.5 marks)*

> **Marker Note:** Mentioning at least **two types per category** with **defining features** = full 7 marks.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $H = 7.2\,\text{m}$, $\theta = 30°$, $w = 1000\,\text{mm}$, $t_d = 0.4\,\text{m}$, $v = 0.65\,\text{m/s}$.

**1. Diagonal length of truss (2 marks):**

$$
L_d = \frac{H}{\sin(\theta)} = \frac{7.2}{\sin(30°)} = \frac{7.2}{0.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_d = 14.4 \,\text{m}}
$$

**2. Theoretical hourly capacity (3 marks):**

For $w = 1000\,\text{mm}$, $k_w = 1.0$.

$$
C = \frac{3600 \times v \times k_w}{t_d} = \frac{3600 \times 0.65 \times 1.0}{0.4}
$$

$$
C = \frac{2340}{0.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = 5850 \,\text{persons/hour}}
$$

**3. Travel time (2 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{L_d}{v} = \frac{14.4}{0.65}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 22.15 \,\text{seconds}}
$$

> **Tip for full marks:** Always write the formula first, then substitute, then solve — **in three distinct steps** for a 3-mark sub-question.

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four major classification axes** of escalators with **two examples** under each axis. Use a **neat labelled diagram** to show the difference between parallel and criss-cross escalators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1000 mm step-width escalator operates at **0.5 m/s** with a step depth of **0.4 m**. If a metro station receives a peak load of **5500 persons/hour** arriving uniformly, determine:
1. The **theoretical capacity** of this escalator.
2. The **load factor** (actual / theoretical) at peak hour.
3. Whether the escalator is **adequately designed** for the load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The **four classification axes** are:

**1. Step/Running Surface Construction (1.5 marks):**
- **Step-type** (discrete metal treads) — high capacity, used in metros.
- **Belt-type** (continuous rubber/PVC belt) — limited to 30° incline, lower capacity.

**2. Directional Configuration (2 marks):**
- **Parallel** — UP and DOWN in separate side-by-side trusses. **Criss-cross** — both directions share one shaft, saving footprint.
- **Spiral/Helical** — curved steps for aesthetic architectural designs.

**3. Environmental Application (1.5 marks):**
- **Indoor** — malls, offices. **Outdoor** — IP65-rated, waterproofed, used in metro entrances.

**4. Operational Mode (1 mark):**
- **Fixed-direction** (always UP or always DOWN). **Reversible** — flips direction by timer (e.g., morning = UP, evening = DOWN).

**Diagram (1 mark):** A simple ASCII or schematic diagram labelling:

- **Parallel:** `⬆️ ⬇️` (two arrows side by side, two separate trusses).
- **Criss-cross:** `✖️` (interleaved, single shaft).

> **Diagrams are mandatory for full marks** in KTU — skipping the diagram = loss of 1 mark.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $w = 1000\,\text{mm}$ → $k_w = 1.0$, $v = 0.5\,\text{m/s}$, $t_d = 0.4\,\text{m}$, $C_{\text{actual}} = 5500\,\text{pax/hr}$.

**1. Theoretical capacity (3 marks):**

$$
C_{\text{theo}} = \frac{3600 \times v \times k_w}{t_d} = \frac{3600 \times 0.5 \times 1.0}{0.4}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{theo}} = 4500 \,\text{pax/hr}}
$$

**2. Load factor (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Load Factor} = \frac{C_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\text{theo}}} = \frac{5500}{4500}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Load Factor} \approx 1.22 \;\;(122\%)}
$$

**3. Adequacy check (2 marks):**

Since the **load factor exceeds 100%** ($> 1.0$), the **escalator is NOT adequately designed** for the peak load. The actual demand **exceeds the theoretical capacity by 22%**, leading to:
- Passenger queueing at the bottom landing.
- Increased **stampede / crush risk** during peak hours.
- **Safety recommendation:** Either (i) **increase the rated speed** to $0.65\,\text{m/s}$ (raises capacity to $5850\,\text{pax/hr}$), or (ii) **install a second parallel escalator**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Omitting units in the final answer** → loss of 0.5 mark per sub-question.
> 2. **Not writing the formula explicitly** before substituting numbers → loss of 1 mark in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** in "Explain the types" questions → loss of 1 mark (mandatory).
> 4. **Confusing *capacity* with *load*** — capacity is a *theoretical upper limit*, not the actual passenger count.
> 5. **Wrong $k_w$ value** — students often pick $k_w = 0.5$ for *all* step widths. Memorize: $k_w = 0.5$ (600mm), $0.75$ (800mm), $1.0$ (1000mm).
> 6. **Forgetting to convert $t_d$ to metres** — leaving it in mm makes the answer 1000× too large.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This Before the Exam!):**

- 🔹 An **escalator** is a *continuous-flow inclined conveyor* for passengers.
- 🔹 The **inclination angles** are **30° (standard)** and **35° (transit)**.
- 🔹 **Step widths** are **600 / 800 / 1000 mm**, with $k_w$ values of **0.5 / 0.75 / 1.0**.
- 🔹 The **master capacity formula** is: $\;C = \dfrac{3600 \cdot v \cdot k_w}{t_d}\;$
- 🔹 **Truss lengths:** $\;L_d = H/\sin(\theta)\;$ and $\;L_h = H/\tan(\theta)\;$
- 🔹 **4 Classification Axes:** (i) Step-construction [Step/Belt] (ii) Direction [Parallel/Criss-cross/Spiral] (iii) Environment [Indoor/Outdoor] (iv) Operation [Fixed/Reversible].
- 🔹 **Step-type escalators** are safer and have ~2× capacity of belt-type.
- 🔹 **Criss-cross** saves floor space but causes wayfinding confusion in emergencies.
- 🔹 **Spiral escalators** are aesthetic but have **3× maintenance cost**.
- 🔹 The **handrail must move at the same speed as the steps** — any mismatch triggers the safety PLC.
- 🔹 **Emergency stop buttons** must be at BOTH landings; **comb plate sensors** detect foreign objects.
- 🔹 **Always** include units, formula, substitution, and final answer in numerical problems (the KTU 4-step answer template).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moving Walks - Man Lifts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT833 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moving Walks - Man Lifts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Moving Walks (Passenger Conveyors / Travelators)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Moving Walk* (also known as a **Passenger Conveyor** or **Travelator**) is a slow-speed, continuous belt-type material/person handling device used to transport passengers or light loads over horizontal or inclined distances, primarily in airports, shopping malls, metro stations, and large industrial complexes.

**Key Design Characteristics:**
- Operating speed typically ranges from **0.5 m/s to 1.0 m/s** (regulated under international safety codes)
- Inclination angle generally does not exceed **12°** (≈ 1 in 5 gradient)
- Continuous looped belt with grooved rubber or cleated metallic surface
- Treadway width standard: **800 mm, 1000 mm, 1200 mm, 1400 mm**
- Maximum passenger capacity: **6000–8000 persons/hour** per unit width

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a Moving Walk as an *escalator that has been laid flat*. Instead of stairs, it's a flat moving belt — like a giant horizontal treadmill at an airport terminal. You step onto it and it carries you the distance while you stand still, exactly like a moving sidewalk in a futuristic city. The only difference is that it can be slightly inclined (up to 12°) to handle small elevation changes between floors.

### 1.2 Man Lifts (Manlifts / Vertical Personnel Lifts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A *Man Lift* is a powered, vertical or near-vertical, continuous-loop personnel hoisting device used to transport workers between different elevations within a building or industrial structure, primarily for maintenance, inspection, warehouse stocking, and construction work.

**Key Design Characteristics:**
- Operating speed range: **0.25 m/s to 0.75 m/s** (slower than freight elevators)
- Standard platform sizes: typically **0.9 m × 0.9 m** up to **1.5 m × 1.5 m**
- Travel height: typically up to **15 m to 30 m**
- Load capacity: **120 kg to 250 kg** per platform
- Drive mechanism: rack-and-pinion, hydraulic, or electric traction

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **cherry-picker or window-cleaning platform**, but designed as a continuous-loop elevator system. Workers step onto a small platform (or sit in a chair-type cage) and are mechanically raised from one floor to the next — similar to a dumbwaiter in a restaurant, but scaled up for human transport. It is essentially a "vertical moving walk" for personnel.

### 1.3 Classification Comparison Table

| Feature | Moving Walk | Man Lift |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Motion | Horizontal / Low-Inclination | Vertical / High-Inclination |
| Speed Range | 0.5 – 1.0 m/s | 0.25 – 0.75 m/s |
| Max Angle | 12° (≈ 1 in 5) | 90° (Vertical) |
| Application | Public transport, airports | Industrial, warehouse, construction |
| Operator Onboard | Yes (standing/walking) | Yes (standing) |
| Safety Cage | Open balustrade | Fully enclosed cage required |
| Standards | EN 115, ASME A17.1 | OSHA 1910.68, IS 11659 |
| Drive Type | Electric motor + chain/belt | Rack-pinion / Hydraulic / Traction |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Moving Walk — Operational Theory

A moving walk operates on the principle of a **continuous looped belt** driven by an electric motor through a gear reduction unit. The system consists of:

**Core Components (Logic Flow):**
1. **Drive Motor (M):** Typically a 3-phase induction motor (5–15 kW rating) operating at 1450 RPM
2. **Gear Reducer (GR):** Reduces motor speed to belt speed (ratio ~ 60:1 to 120:1)
3. **Drive Sprocket/Pulley:** Engages with belt cleats or chain links
4. **Treadway (Belt):** Aluminum pallets or rubber belt with cleats
5. **Return Path:** Bottom return via idler rollers
6. **Handrail System:** Synchronized moving handrail at belt speed
7. **Balustrade:** Side panels with safety comb plates at entry/exit

**The "Why" Behind Each Component:**
- *Why gear reducer?* — Motor RPM (~1450) is too high; belt must run at walking speed (0.5 m/s)
- *Why cleats/pallets?* — To prevent passenger slippage, especially on inclined sections
- *Why comb plates?* — To safely bridge the gap between fixed floor and moving belt at entry/exit
- *Why moving handrails?* — Passengers naturally reach for support; static rails cause loss of balance when stepping on/off

### 2.2 Moving Walk — Power & Capacity Calculations

**Throughput (Passenger Capacity):**
$$ Q = \frac{3600 \cdot v \cdot n}{L} $$

where:
- $Q$ = passenger throughput (persons/hour)
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s)
- $n$ = number of passengers standing in one row across belt width
- $L$ = longitudinal space per passenger (typically **0.4 m per person** for standing)

**Motor Power Required:**
$$ P = \frac{(W_t + W_L) \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \mu + (W_t + W_L) \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta \cdot f_r}{\eta} $$

where:
- $P$ = motor power (W)
- $W_t$ = weight of belt (N)
- $W_L$ = weight of load/passengers (N)
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s)
- $\theta$ = inclination angle
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (≈ 0.01–0.02 for roller bearings)
- $f_r$ = rolling resistance factor
- $\eta$ = overall system efficiency (typically **0.85 to 0.92**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stopping Distance (Critical Safety Parameter):**
> $$ d_s = \frac{v^2}{2a} $$
> where $a$ = deceleration (typically **0.5 m/s² to 1.0 m/s²** for safety compliance).

### 2.3 Man Lift — Operational Theory

A man lift consists of a **single platform** (or multiple stacked platforms) attached to a vertical mast or guide rail, driven by either:

1. **Rack-and-Pinion Drive:** A pinion gear on the platform engages a fixed vertical rack — provides high positional accuracy, used in construction hoists
2. **Hydraulic Drive:** A hydraulic cylinder pushes the platform; smooth operation, used in low-rise installations
3. **Electric Traction:** Cable-suspended platform with counterweight; used in building maintenance units (BMUs)

**Critical Safety Components:**
- **Over-speed Governor:** Activates at 1.15× rated speed
- **Slack Cable Switch:** Detects cable failure
- **Emergency Stop Button:** Within arm's reach of operator
- **Guard Rails:** Minimum 1.1 m height on platform
- **Final Limit Switches:** Stops platform at top and bottom terminals
- **Anti-Recoil Device:** Prevents platform free-fall if drive fails

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Passenger Throughput | $Q = \dfrac{3600 \cdot v \cdot n}{L}$ | persons/hr | Moving walks |
| 2 | Motor Power (inclined) | $P = \dfrac{(W_t + W_L) \cdot v (\sin\theta \cdot \mu + \cos\theta \cdot f_r)}{\eta}$ | W | Includes incline + friction |
| 3 | Stopping Distance | $d_s = \dfrac{v^2}{2a}$ | m | $a$ = deceleration |
| 4 | Belt Tension (Driving) | $T_1 = T_2 + \dfrac{P}{v}$ | N | Where $T_2$ = slack side tension |
| 5 | Centrifugal Tension | $T_c = q \cdot v^2$ | N | $q$ = mass per unit length (kg/m) |
| 6 | Maximum Lift Height (Power) | $h_{max} = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta}{W_L \cdot g \cdot t^{-1}}$ | m | For vertical man lifts |
| 7 | Safe Working Load (SWL) | $\text{SWL} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Load}}{n}$ | kg | Safety factor $n$ = 3 to 5 |
| 8 | Platform Acceleration | $a = \dfrac{v_f - v_i}{t}$ | m/s² | Limited to 0.5 m/s² for safety |
| 9 | Energy of Braking | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | For brake sizing |
| 10 | Buckling Load (Mast) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$ | N | Euler's formula for mast |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by design engineers in lift manufacturers (KONE, Otis, Schindler) and conveyor OEMs to size motors, brakes, and structural members. Safety officers use throughput calculations to plan peak-hour passenger flow at metro stations and airports.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Passenger Throughput for a Moving Walk

**Problem Setup:** A moving walk at an airport has the following specifications:
- Belt speed: $v = 0.8 \, \text{m/s}$
- Belt width: accommodates $n = 2$ passengers standing side-by-side per row
- Longitudinal spacing: $L = 0.4 \, \text{m}$ per passenger
- Length of conveyor: 50 m

**Find:** (a) Passenger throughput per hour, (b) Total time to traverse the conveyor.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula:**
$$ Q = \frac{3600 \cdot v \cdot n}{L} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$ Q = \frac{3600 \times 0.8 \times 2}{0.4} $$

**Step 3 — Calculate the numerator:**
$$ 3600 \times 0.8 = 2880 $$
$$ 2880 \times 2 = 5760 $$

**Step 4 — Divide by the spacing:**
$$ Q = \frac{5760}{0.4} = 14400 \, \text{passengers/hour} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $Q = 14{,}400$ passengers/hour — this matches the **high-capacity airport travelator** standard (typically 6000–14400 pph).

**Step 5 — Traversing time:**
$$ t = \frac{\text{length}}{v} = \frac{50}{0.8} = 62.5 \, \text{seconds} $$

### 3.2 Derivation: Stopping Distance for Emergency Braking

**Problem:** A moving walk operates at 0.8 m/s. The safety code requires a maximum deceleration of 0.5 m/s². Find the minimum stopping distance.

**Step 1 — Use kinematic equation:**
$$ v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d_s $$

**Step 2 — At full stop, $v_f = 0$:**
$$ 0 = v_i^2 - 2 a d_s $$

**Step 3 — Rearrange:**
$$ d_s = \frac{v_i^2}{2a} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**
$$ d_s = \frac{(0.8)^2}{2 \times 0.5} $$

**Step 5 — Compute:**
$$ d_s = \frac{0.64}{1.0} = 0.64 \, \text{m} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $d_s = 0.64$ m. This is the **minimum safe length of the comb-plate transition zone** at entry and exit. If the transition zone is shorter, a passenger stepping on/off could trip due to the sudden deceleration.

### 3.3 Derivation: Motor Power for Inclined Moving Walk

**Problem:** A moving walk is installed at $\theta = 10°$ inclination, with:
- Belt weight: $W_t = 5000 \, \text{N}$
- Passenger load: $W_L = 15{,}000 \, \text{N}$
- Belt speed: $v = 0.6 \, \text{m/s}$
- Coefficient of friction: $\mu = 0.02$
- Efficiency: $\eta = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Total weight:**
$$ W_{total} = W_t + W_L = 5000 + 15{,}000 = 20{,}000 \, \text{N} $$

**Step 2 — Trigonometric values:**
$$ \sin 10° = 0.1736, \quad \cos 10° = 0.9848 $$

**Step 3 — Power formula:**
$$ P = \frac{W_{total} \cdot v \cdot (\sin\theta \cdot \mu + \cos\theta \cdot f_r)}{\eta} $$

**Step 4 — Assume $f_r = 0.015$ (typical bearing friction):**
$$ \sin\theta \cdot \mu = 0.1736 \times 0.02 = 0.003472 $$
$$ \cos\theta \cdot f_r = 0.9848 \times 0.015 = 0.014772 $$

**Step 5 — Sum of friction terms:**
$$ 0.003472 + 0.014772 = 0.018244 $$

**Step 6 — Numerator:**
$$ 20{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 0.018244 = 218.93 \, \text{W} $$

**Step 7 — Divide by efficiency:**
$$ P = \frac{218.93}{0.90} = 243.3 \, \text{W} \approx 0.25 \, \text{kW} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a *steady-state* friction-only calculation. **Add 30–50% margin** for starting torque, transient loads, and passenger entry/exit shocks. Final motor selection: **0.4 kW motor** (next standard size above).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Safety Compliance Checklist

| Safety Parameter | Moving Walk Standard | Man Lift Standard | Inspection Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Speed | 0.75 m/s (EN 115) | 0.75 m/s (OSHA) | Continuous monitoring |
| Emergency Stop | Within 1 m of operator | At every platform | Daily |
| Handrail Speed | Synchronized with belt ± 2% | N/A | Daily |
| Comb Plate Engagement | Yellow safety comb at entry/exit | Floor-level gate interlock | Weekly |
| Over-speed Governor | N/A | Activates at 115% rated speed | Quarterly |
| Safety Factor (Cable) | N/A | 5:1 minimum on wire rope | Annual |
| Braking Distance | ≤ 0.64 m at 0.8 m/s | ≤ 0.5 m at 0.75 m/s | Monthly |
| Maximum Load | 120 kg/m² on treadway | 250 kg per platform | Continuous |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Moving Walk — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Three Phase AC Supply 415V] --> B[Motor Starter DOL or VFD]
    B --> C[Drive Motor 5 to 15 kW]
    C --> D[Helical Gear Reducer 60 colon 1]
    D --> E[Drive Sprocket]
    E --> F[Treadway Belt with Cleats]
    F --> G[Tensioning Idler Pulley]
    G --> H[Return Path Below Floor]
    H --> I[Comb Plate Entry]
    I --> J[Passenger Boarding Zone]
    J --> K[Main Travel Section 0 to 12 degree]
    K --> L[Comb Plate Exit]
    L --> M[Handrail Drive System]
    M --> N[Synchronized Handrail Loop]
    F -.Power Takeoff.-> M
    style A fill:#1f3a93,color:#fff

    style D fill:#a8430f,color:#fff

```

### 4.2 Man Lift — Safety System Architecture

```mermaid
    E --> F[Drive Motor Trip]

    style A fill:#1f3a93,color:#fff

```

### 4.3 Moving Walk — Inclination & Safety Zones

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A[Entry Zone]
        A1[Fixed Floor Level 0 m]
        A2[Comb Plate Yellow Safety]
        A3[Belt Engagement 0.5 m long]
```
